topics.title: Introduction to Geotechnical Engineering - Soil Types - Major Soil Deposits of India - 3 Phase System - Basic Soil Properties: Void Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Geotechnical Engineering - Soil Types - Major Soil Deposits of India - 3 Phase System - Basic Soil Properties: Void Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Geotechnical Engineering — Formal Definition

**Geotechnical Engineering** is the specialized branch of civil engineering that deals with the engineering behavior of earth materials (soils and rocks). It applies the principles of soil mechanics and rock mechanics to investigate subsurface conditions, determine the relevant physical/mechanical/hydraulic properties, design foundations, retaining structures, earthworks, and slopes, and evaluate the stability of natural and engineered ground.

**Soil Mechanics** is the theoretical and applied sub-discipline of geotechnical engineering concerned with the study of the physical properties of soil, the stresses and strains within a soil mass, and the resulting deformation and failure behavior under applied loads. It is also termed **"Geotechnics"** in many international curricula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The course PCCET402 — Soil Mechanics forms the foundation for PCCET403 (Geotechnical Engineering-II) and the design of shallow/deep foundations. A strong command of the **3-phase system, index properties, and phase relationships** is non-negotiable for all subsequent modules.

### 1.2 Role of a Geotechnical Engineer in the Industry

A geotechnical engineer is involved in:
- Site investigation and subsoil exploration.
- Design of **shallow foundations** (isolated, combined, raft, strap) and **deep foundations** (piles, piers, caissons, well foundations).
- Earth-retaining structures (gravity walls, cantilever, sheet pile, anchored bulkheads, braced cuts).
- Stability of natural slopes and engineered earthworks (embankments, dams).
- Pavement design (subgrade characterization).
- Ground improvement techniques (compaction, stabilization, grouting, vibro-flotation, preloading, geosynthetics).
- Underground structures (tunnels, shafts, underground tanks).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Soil = A Three-Ingredient Cake"**
> Imagine you are baking a sponge cake. The cake has three components: **solid flour particles (skeleton)**, **water/moisture in the pores (pore fluid)**, and **air trapped in the unfilled pores (pore air)**. The "flavor" and "texture" of the cake — i.e., the **strength**, **compressibility**, and **permeability** of a soil — depend entirely on the *relative proportions* of these three phases.
>
> - If you add too much water → the cake becomes weak and slumps (loose, saturated soil).
> - If you compress the cake → pores shrink, solids pack tighter (compaction, void ratio decreases).
> - If you bake it dry → all water evaporates, only solids + air remain (dry soil).
>
> **Geotechnical engineering is essentially the science of measuring, predicting, and manipulating this 3-phase "recipe" to ensure the ground safely supports whatever we build on it.**

### 1.4 Why Is Soil Mechanics So Critical in Civil Engineering?

Soil is the *only* construction material that is **naturally occurring**, **highly variable**, and **inherently heterogeneous and anisotropic**. Every structure — a residential building, a 300 m skyscraper, a 5 km tunnel, a 10 km highway, an offshore wind turbine — ultimately transfers its load to the **soil** or **rock** through its foundation. A small error in characterizing the soil can lead to:
- Excessive settlement (e.g., the tilting of the Leaning Tower of Pisa).
- Bearing capacity failure.
- Slope failures and landslides.
- Liquefaction during earthquakes.
- Heave in expansive soils.

Hence, every civil engineer must understand soil behavior at a fundamental level.

### 1.5 Key Engineering Standards and Physical Constants (Highlighted)

The following physical constants and standard conditions are used globally and are **assumed in KTU numerical problems unless stated otherwise**:

- Unit weight of water: **$\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$** (often rounded to **$10\ \text{kN/m}^3$** in textbook problems).
- Density of water: **$\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1\ \text{g/cm}^3$**.
- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$**.
- Specific gravity of water: **$G_w = 1.0$** (dimensionless).
- Specific gravity of soil solids ($G$ or $G_s$): typically **2.65 to 2.75** for most mineral soils (quartz ≈ 2.65, mica ≈ 2.80–3.20, iron-rich soils > 3.00).
- Standard temperature for reporting $\gamma_w$: **$4\,^\circ\text{C}$** (where water attains its maximum density of $1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$).

---

## 1.6 Soil Types — A Comprehensive Overview

Soil is broadly classified by **grain size (textural classification)** and by **engineering behavior (IS, USCS, AASHTO classifications)**. KTU Module 1 emphasizes the **textural (grain-size) classification** which feeds into the Indian Standard (IS 1498 – 1970) system.

#### A. Based on Grain Size (IS 1498 / IS 2720)

| Soil Type | IS Sieve Size | Size Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Boulder / Cobble** | — | $> 300\ \text{mm}$ |
| **Gravel** | $4.75\ \text{mm}$ to $80\ \text{mm}$ | Coarse-grained |
| **Sand** | $75\ \mu\text{m}$ ($0.075\ \text{mm}$) to $4.75\ \text{mm}$ | Coarse-grained |
| **Silt** | $2\ \mu\text{m}$ to $75\ \mu\text{m}$ | Fine-grained |
| **Clay** | $< 2\ \mu\text{m}$ | Fine-grained (colloidal) |

**Sub-divisions of Sand (per IS 1498):**
- Coarse sand: **2.0 mm – 4.75 mm**
- Medium sand: **0.425 mm – 2.0 mm**
- Fine sand: **0.075 mm – 0.425 mm**

**Sub-divisions of Gravel:**
- Coarse gravel: **20 mm – 80 mm**
- Medium gravel: **4.75 mm – 20 mm**
- Fine gravel: **< 4.75 mm**

#### B. Coarse-Grained vs. Fine-Grained Soils

> [!NOTE]
> **The $75\ \mu\text{m}$ sieve (IS Standard Sieve No. 200 / ASTM Sieve No. 200)** is the *critical divider*. More than **50% retained** on the $75\ \mu\text{m}$ sieve = **coarse-grained soil**; more than **50% passing** = **fine-grained soil**.

- **Coarse-grained soils** (Gravel + Sand): Friction-dominated, high permeability, low compressibility, good drainage. Used as subgrade, filter media, railway ballast, concrete aggregate.
- **Fine-grained soils** (Silt + Clay): Cohesion-dominated (especially clay), low permeability, high compressibility, problematic in construction.

#### C. Special Soil Types Encountered in Practice

- **Organic soils**: Peat, muck, gyttja. Highly compressible, low shear strength. Must be removed or pre-consolidated.
- **Expansive soils** (e.g., Black Cotton Soil of central India): Contain the mineral **montmorillonite**; exhibit severe **swelling–shrinkage** behavior with moisture changes.
- **Lateritic soils** (Kerala, Karnataka, Goa): Highly weathered, rich in iron and aluminum oxides; usually reddish-brown; problematic for foundations.
- **Loess**: Wind-blown silt deposits (e.g., parts of the Indo-Gangetic plain); collapsible upon wetting.
- **Calcareous soils**: Contain calcium carbonate; found in parts of Gujarat and Rajasthan.
- **Marine clays** (e.g., Kochi backwaters, Mumbai coastal belt): Soft, sensitive, often thixotropic; very low shear strength.
- **Bentonite**: A special clay (montmorillonite-rich), used in drilling mud and as a barrier material in landfills.

---

## 1.7 Major Soil Deposits of India

India has a remarkably diverse geology. KTU Module 1 specifically asks for awareness of the **major soil deposits** of the country, which influence regional construction practices.

#### A. Indo-Gangetic Alluvial Plains

- Extends across the states of Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, West Bengal, and parts of Assam.
- Composed of **alluvial deposits** (transported by the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra river systems).
- Typically **silty sands, clays of low to medium plasticity**, with occasional kankar (calcareous nodules).
- The **Northern plains** are very fertile but pose **seismic liquefaction risk** (Zone IV–V).

#### B. Black Cotton Soil (Regur) — Central and Western India

- Found extensively in **Maharashtra, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu**.
- Derived from the weathering of **basalt and trap rocks (Deccan Traps)**.
- Rich in **montmorillonite clay mineral**, giving it high **swelling potential**.
- Exhibits **high plasticity, low permeability, and high compressibility**.
- Foundation design requires special treatment: **under-reamed piles, soil replacement, lime/cement stabilization, moisture control**.

#### C. Lateritic Soil — Western and Southern India

- Common in **Kerala, Karnataka, Goa, Maharashtra, Odisha, and the Western Ghats**.
- Formed by intense **tropical weathering under alternating wet–dry cycles**.
- Rich in **iron and aluminum sesquioxides**; reddish-brown to yellowish in color.
- Typically **coarse-grained, porous, low density, and low strength when saturated**.
- Used in low-cost building blocks (laterite bricks) but problematic as foundation soil when wet.

#### D. Coastal and Marine Deposits

- Found along the **eastern (Bengal, Odisha, Andhra) and western (Kerala, Karnataka, Goa, Maharashtra, Gujarat) coasts**.
- Comprise **soft marine clays, silts, and recent alluvium**.
- Possess **high compressibility, low shear strength, and high salt content** (which affects cement stabilization).
- **Kochi, Mangalore, Mumbai, and Chennai** all have extensive marine clay deposits.

#### E. Desert / Aeolian Deposits — Western Rajasthan

- **Thar Desert** — predominantly **sandy soils** transported and deposited by wind.
- Predominantly **fine to medium sand, uniformly graded, with low density and high permeability**.
- **Loess-like silt deposits** are also present.

#### F. Hilly and Mountainous Soils

- Found in the **Himalayan ranges, Western Ghats, Vindhyan and Aravalli ranges**.
- Highly variable, ranging from **boulders and coarse gravels to residual silts and clays**.
- Often associated with **landslide-prone slopes**, requiring slope-stability analysis.

#### G. Permafrost and Cold-Region Soils

- Found in **Ladakh, parts of Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh, and Jammu & Kashmir** at high altitudes.
- Permanently frozen ground with **ice lenses**, posing unique challenges: **frost heave and thaw weakening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** Questions on "Major Soil Deposits of India" typically expect students to list **3 to 4 deposits** with their location, parent rock/genesis, characteristic mineralogy, and a *key engineering problem* associated with each. Memorize in tabular form.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase System of Soil — Conceptual Foundation

A soil mass in nature is a **dispersed, three-phase particulate system**. Unlike steel or concrete (which are essentially single-phase or two-phase), soil is a **multi-phase porous medium**. The three phases are:

1. **Solid phase (S)** — mineral particles (soil grains), quantified by **mass $M_s$** and **volume $V_s$**.
2. **Liquid phase (L)** — pore water (with dissolved salts), quantified by **mass $M_w$** and **volume $V_w$**.
3. **Gas phase (G)** — pore air and water vapor, quantified by **mass $M_a \approx 0$** (negligible) and **volume $V_a$**.

#### 2.1.1 Phase Diagram Representation

A soil sample is conventionally represented as an **idealized three-phase diagram** (proposed by Karl Terzaghi) consisting of:
- A **left block** representing the **volume** ($V$) of the soil subdivided into $V_s$, $V_v$, $V_w$, $V_a$.
- A **right block** representing the **mass** ($M$) of the soil subdivided into $M_s$, $M_w$, and (negligibly) $M_a$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **total volume** $V$ = volume of voids $V_v$ + volume of solids $V_s$. The total volume of voids $V_v$ = volume of water $V_w$ + volume of air $V_a$. The **total mass** $M$ = $M_s + M_w$ (air mass is conventionally taken as zero).

---

### 2.2 The Eight Fundamental Volume/Mass Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Total volume of soil mass | $V = V_s + V_v = V_s + V_w + V_a$ |
| $V_s$ | Volume of solids | — |
| $V_v$ | Volume of voids | $V_v = V_w + V_a$ |
| $V_w$ | Volume of water | — |
| $V_a$ | Volume of air | — |
| $M$ | Total mass of soil | $M = M_s + M_w$ |
| $M_s$ | Mass of solids | — |
| $M_w$ | Mass of water | $M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w$ |

---

### 2.3 Basic Soil Properties (Index Properties) — Definitions and Formulas

#### A. Void Ratio ($e$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Void Ratio ($e$)** is defined as the **ratio of the volume of voids to the volume of solids** in a given soil mass.
> $$e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$$

- It is a **dimensionless** quantity.
- Since $V_s \le V$ (solids are never more than the total), $V_v \ge 0$, so $e \ge 0$ (theoretically).
- For most natural soils: $e$ ranges between **0.3 and 1.5**.
- For loose, organic, or peaty soils: $e$ can exceed **5 or even 10**.

**Why is void ratio so important?**
- $e$ is **independent of the stress state** when expressed in terms of solids (it changes only with the volume of solids, which is invariant).
- The **shear strength, compressibility, and permeability** of a soil are all directly correlated with $e$.
- All volume-change calculations (consolidation, compaction) are written in terms of $e$.

#### B. Porosity ($n$)

> **Porosity ($n$)** is the ratio of the volume of voids to the total volume of the soil mass.
> $$n = \dfrac{V_v}{V}$$

- Expressed as a **percentage** in practice (e.g., $n = 35\%$), but dimensionless in formula form.
- Range: **0 ≤ $n$ ≤ 1** (or 0% to 100%).
- Typical values: **30% – 60%** for granular soils, **40% – 90%** for clays.

#### C. Degree of Saturation ($S$ or $S_r$)

> **Degree of Saturation ($S$)** is the ratio of the volume of water to the volume of voids.
> $$S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}$$

- Range: **0 ≤ $S$ ≤ 1** (or 0% to 100%).
- $S = 0$: **Dry soil**.
- $S = 1$ (or 100%): **Fully saturated soil** (all voids filled with water; $V_a = 0$).
- $0 < S < 1$: **Partially saturated / moist soil** (three-phase system with air present).

#### D. Water Content / Moisture Content ($w$)

> **Water Content ($w$)** is the ratio of the mass of water to the mass of solids, expressed as a percentage.
> $$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100\%$$

- Range: **0% to several hundred percent** (very high for organic clays/peat).
- Determined experimentally by **oven-drying** at $105\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $110\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 24 hours (IS 2720 Part 2).

#### E. Air Content ($a_c$)

> **Air Content ($a_c$)** is the ratio of the volume of air to the volume of voids.
> $$a_c = \dfrac{V_a}{V_v} = 1 - S$$

- Range: **0 to 1**.

#### F. Specific Gravity of Soil Solids ($G$ or $G_s$)

> **Specific Gravity ($G$)** is the ratio of the mass of solids to the mass of an equal volume of water at $4\,^\circ\text{C}$.
> $$G = \dfrac{M_s / V_s}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w}$$

- Dimensionless.
- For most mineral soils, $G$ ≈ **2.65 to 2.75**.
- Determined by **pycnometer test** (IS 2720 Part 3 / Part 14).

#### G. Unit Weight ($\gamma$) and Density ($\rho$)

> **Bulk Unit Weight ($\gamma$)** is the weight per unit total volume.
> $$\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{V}$$

> **Dry Unit Weight ($\gamma_d$)** is the weight of solids per unit total volume.
> $$\gamma_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V} = \dfrac{M_s \cdot g}{V}$$

> **Saturated Unit Weight ($\gamma_{sat}$)** is the bulk unit weight when all voids are filled with water ($S = 1$).
> $$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{W_s + W_w}{V}\bigg\vert_{S=1}$$

> **Submerged / Buoyant Unit Weight ($\gamma'$)** is the effective unit weight of soil below the water table (after subtracting buoyancy).
> $$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Range of Unit Weights:**
> - $\gamma_d$ for granular soils: **14 – 20 kN/m³**.
> - $\gamma_{sat}$ for clays: **18 – 22 kN/m³**.
> - $\gamma'$ (submerged): typically **8 – 12 kN/m³**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — Phase Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important set of formulas in KTU Soil Mechanics Module 1. Memorize ALL of them.** Use $\vert$ and $\mid$ notation for absolute / conditional value to avoid breaking the table.

| # | Property / Relationship | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Void Ratio | $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$ | Dimensionless, $\ge 0$ |
| 2 | Porosity | $n = \dfrac{V_v}{V}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le n < 1$ |
| 3 | $e$ in terms of $n$ | $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$ | Derived relation |
| 4 | $n$ in terms of $e$ | $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | Derived relation |
| 5 | Degree of Saturation | $S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}$ | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 6 | Water Content | $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s}$ | Expressed as % |
| 7 | Air Content | $a_c = 1 - S$ | $0 \le a_c \le 1$ |
| 8 | Specific Gravity | $G = \dfrac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w}$ | $\approx 2.65$–$2.75$ |
| 9 | Bulk Unit Weight | $\gamma = \dfrac{W_s + W_w}{V}$ | — |
| 10 | Dry Unit Weight | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V}$ | — |
| 11 | $\gamma_d$ in terms of $G$, $e$, $\gamma_w$ | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Crucial formula |
| 12 | $\gamma$ in terms of $G$, $S$, $e$, $w$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{(G + S\,e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | General |
| 13 | $\gamma$ in terms of $G$, $e$, $w$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{G\,(1+w)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | When $S$ unknown |
| 14 | Saturated Unit Weight | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G+e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $S = 1$ |
| 15 | Submerged Unit Weight | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Below WT |
| 16 | $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ | $S\,e = w\,G$ | Master phase relation |
| 17 | Mass of solids per unit volume | $M_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$ | — |
| 18 | Saturated water content | $w_{sat} = \dfrac{e}{G}$ | When $S=1$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the 3-Phase System

The phase diagram and its derived formulas are the **algebraic engine room** of soil mechanics. They enable engineers to:

- Convert between **in-situ** measured properties (e.g., weight of a soil core) and **index properties** (e.g., $e$, $n$, $S$).
- Predict the **degree of saturation** of a soil sample recovered from the field.
- Compute **settlements** (volume change = $V_s \cdot \Delta e$).
- Estimate the **liquefaction potential** of sands (loose sands with high $e$ and $S=1$ are most vulnerable).
- Determine **compaction specifications** (e.g., Maximum Dry Density, MDD, and Optimum Moisture Content, OMC).
- Convert between laboratory mass-based measurements and field volume-based measurements (e.g., sand cone test, core cutter method).

---

## 2.6 Significance of Void Ratio in Engineering Practice

Void ratio is more fundamental than porosity because it uses the **invariable** $V_s$ (solids volume) as the reference. The volume of solids $V_s$ remains constant for a given soil mass (it changes only if the soil physically loses or gains solid material, which does not occur in normal mechanical loading). Therefore:

- During **1-D consolidation**, only $V_v$ changes → $\Delta e$ is the *direct measure of compression*.
- During **compaction**, reduction in $e$ indicates densification.
- During **shear** of dense sands, $e$ may initially *decrease* (contraction) and then *increase* (dilation) — the *critical void ratio* $e_{crit}$ marks the transition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Void Ratio ($e_c$):** The void ratio at which a saturated sand can undergo continuous deformation at constant volume (and hence constant pore-water pressure) under shear. If $e < e_c$ → dilative behavior; if $e > e_c$ → contractive behavior. This concept is fundamental to **liquefaction analysis**.

---

## 2.7 Practical Applications — Worked Theoretical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1 (Theoretical Framework):** A soil sample has $V = 100\ \text{cm}^3$, $M = 200\ \text{g}$, $w = 20\%$, and $G = 2.70$. Find $e$, $n$, $S$, and $\gamma_d$.
>
> - $M_s = M / (1+w) = 200 / 1.20 = 166.67\ \text{g}$
> - $M_w = 200 - 166.67 = 33.33\ \text{g}$
> - $V_w = M_w / \rho_w = 33.33\ \text{cm}^3$
> - $V_s = M_s / (G \cdot \rho_w) = 166.67 / 2.70 = 61.73\ \text{cm}^3$
> - $V_v = 100 - 61.73 = 38.27\ \text{cm}^3$
> - $e = 38.27 / 61.73 = 0.620$
> - $n = 38.27 / 100 = 0.383 = 38.3\%$
> - $S = 33.33 / 38.27 = 0.871 = 87.1\%$
> - $\gamma_d = 166.67 \cdot 9.81 / (100 \cdot 10^{-6}) / 1000 = 16.35\ \text{kN/m}^3$
>
> This pattern is the standard approach to all 3-phase problems in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section is the heart of the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus.** You will be expected to derive (or reproduce) every relation shown below. We present each derivation with full algebraic transparency.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation Between Void Ratio ($e$) and Porosity ($n$)

**Given:**
$$e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s} \quad\quad (1)$$
$$n = \dfrac{V_v}{V} \quad\quad (2)$$
$$V = V_s + V_v \quad\quad (3)$$

**Step 1 — Express $V$ in terms of $V_s$ and $e$.**

From (1): $V_v = e \cdot V_s$

Substituting in (3):
$$V = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s(1 + e)$$

**Step 2 — Express $V_v/V$ in terms of $e$.**

$$V_v = e \cdot V_s$$
$$V = V_s (1+e)$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{V_v}{V} = \dfrac{e \cdot V_s}{V_s (1+e)} = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$$

But $V_v / V = n$ (from equation 2):

$$\boxed{\,n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to get $e$ in terms of $n$.**

Starting from $n = e/(1+e)$:
$$n(1+e) = e$$
$$n + ne = e$$
$$n = e - ne = e(1 - n)$$
$$\boxed{\,e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}\,}$$

**Verification of limiting behavior:**
- If $n \to 0$ (no voids), $e \to 0$ ✓
- If $n \to 1$ (all voids, no solids), $e \to \infty$ ✓ (physically: solids volume tends to zero, so $V_v/V_s$ blows up)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Master Phase Relation: $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$

This is the **single most important equation** in KTU Module 1. It is derived below step-by-step.

**Given quantities:**
- $S = V_w / V_v$
- $e = V_v / V_s$
- $w = M_w / M_s$
- $G = M_s / (V_s \cdot \rho_w)$

**Step 1 — Express $V_w$ and $V_s$ in terms of $V_v$.**

From the definition of $S$: $V_w = S \cdot V_v$

From the definition of $e$: $V_s = V_v / e$

**Step 2 — Express $M_w$ and $M_s$ in terms of $V_w$ and $V_s$.**

$$M_w = \rho_w \cdot V_w = \rho_w \cdot S \cdot V_v$$
$$M_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{V_v}{e}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $w = M_w / M_s$.**

$$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot S \cdot V_v}{G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_v / e} = \dfrac{S \cdot V_v \cdot e}{G \cdot V_v}$$

Cancel $V_v$ and $\rho_w$:

$$w = \dfrac{S \cdot e}{G}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,S \cdot e = w \cdot G\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** This equation links **all four** index properties of a soil. Given any three of $S$, $e$, $w$, $G$, the fourth can always be found. This is the equation that students use to "fill in the blanks" in every KTU numerical.

**Special Case — Fully Saturated Soil ($S = 1$):**

When $S = 1$:
$$e = w \cdot G \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_{sat} = \dfrac{e}{G}$$

This gives the **maximum possible water content** for a given soil (since $S \le 1$ by definition).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Unit Weight $\gamma$ in terms of $G$, $S$, $e$, $\gamma_w$

**Given:** $\gamma = W / V$ where $W = W_s + W_w$.

**Step 1 — Express $W_s$ and $W_w$.**

$$W_s = G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$
$$W_w = \gamma_w \cdot V_w = \gamma_w \cdot S \cdot V_v = \gamma_w \cdot S \cdot e \cdot V_s$$

(Using $V_v = e V_s$.)

**Step 2 — Sum them.**

$$W = W_s + W_w = G\,\gamma_w\,V_s + \gamma_w\,S\,e\,V_s = \gamma_w\,V_s\,(G + S\,e)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $V$.**

$$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e V_s = V_s (1 + e)$$

Therefore:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{\gamma_w\,V_s\,(G + S\,e)}{V_s(1+e)} = \dfrac{(G + S\,e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma = \dfrac{(G + S\,e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dry Unit Weight $\gamma_d$ in terms of $G$, $e$, $\gamma_w$

When the soil is **dried** in the oven, all water is removed but $V_s$ remains the same (in a drying test, the volume of solids doesn't change, though the *total* volume may shrink slightly). For the **dry state**:

$$W = W_s = G\,\gamma_w\,V_s \quad\quad \text{(no water)}$$

Therefore:
$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{W_s}{V} = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w\,V_s}{V_s(1+e)} = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma_d = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

This is the **most important formula for compaction problems** (Module 3 of KTU syllabus).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Saturated Unit Weight $\gamma_{sat}$

For **saturated soil**, $S = 1$ and $V_a = 0$. All voids are filled with water.

Setting $S = 1$ in the general formula:
$$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

**Alternative derivation** (using $V_w = V_v = e V_s$):
$$W_s = G\,\gamma_w\,V_s$$
$$W_w = \gamma_w \cdot V_w = \gamma_w \cdot e \cdot V_s$$
$$W = (G + e)\,\gamma_w\,V_s$$
$$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{(G+e)\,\gamma_w\,V_s}{V_s(1+e)} = \dfrac{(G+e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Submerged Unit Weight $\gamma'$

By **Archimedes' principle**, a submerged body loses weight equal to the weight of the displaced water. For a saturated soil mass of total volume $V$, the **buoyant force** per unit volume is $\gamma_w$.

Therefore:
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

Substituting $\gamma_{sat}$:
$$\gamma' = \dfrac{(G+e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w = \dfrac{(G+e)\,\gamma_w - \gamma_w(1+e)}{1+e}$$

Expand the numerator:
$$(G+e)\,\gamma_w - \gamma_w - e\,\gamma_w = G\,\gamma_w + e\,\gamma_w - \gamma_w - e\,\gamma_w = (G-1)\,\gamma_w$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\,\gamma' = \dfrac{(G-1)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

**Sanity check:** For $G = 2.65$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$, $e = 0.7$:
$$\gamma' = \dfrac{(2.65 - 1) \cdot 9.81}{1 + 0.7} = \dfrac{1.65 \cdot 9.81}{1.7} = \dfrac{16.1865}{1.7} = 9.52\ \text{kN/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

This is a typical value for submerged clays.

---

### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Mass-Based Form of Bulk Unit Weight

A frequently used form in KTU numericals is:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{G(1+w)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

**Derivation:**

We know $w = M_w/M_s \Rightarrow M_w = w \cdot M_s$, so:
$$M = M_s + M_w = M_s(1 + w)$$

Also, $M_s = G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s$, and $V = V_s(1+e)$.

Therefore:
$$\gamma = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{V} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_s \cdot g \cdot (1+w)}{V_s(1+e)} = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (1+w)}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\,\gamma = \dfrac{G(1+w)\,\gamma_w}{1+e}\,}$$

This formula is useful when $S$ is not given but $w$ is known.

---

### 3.8 Derivation 8 — Density Form of Void Ratio

Sometimes, KTU questions give the **density** ($\rho$) of a soil. We have:
$$\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}, \quad \rho_s = G \cdot \rho_w$$

In terms of densities:
$$e = \dfrac{G\,\rho_w - \rho}{\rho - \rho_w} \cdot \text{(derived from 3-phase algebra; provided here for reference)}$$

**Derivation:**

Start with:
$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + e = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{\gamma_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1 = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w - \gamma_d}{\gamma_d}$$

In density form (divide all by $g$):
$$e = \dfrac{G\,\rho_w - \rho_d}{\rho_d} \quad\quad \text{where } \rho_d = \dfrac{M_s}{V}$$

This relation is used in **sand replacement (sand cone) tests** where $\rho_d$ is directly measured.

---

### 3.9 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — Inter-Relationships of Index Properties

The following master map shows how each index property can be computed from any other combination, given the master relation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$.

| **Given →** \ **Find ↓** | $e, w, G$ | $e, G, S$ | $n, w, G$ | $\gamma_d, G$ | $\gamma, w, G$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $\dfrac{wG}{e}$ | $S$ | $\dfrac{wG(1-n)}{n}$ | (Use $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ after finding $e$) | $\dfrac{\gamma(1+e) - G\gamma_w}{e\,\gamma_w}$ |
| $e$ | $e$ | $e$ | $\dfrac{n}{1-n}$ | $\dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | $\dfrac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{\gamma} - 1$ |
| $n$ | $\dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | $n$ | $\dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{e}{1+e}$ |
| $w$ | $w$ | $\dfrac{S\,e}{G}$ | $w$ | $\dfrac{S\,e}{G}$ | $w$ |
| $\gamma$ | $\dfrac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{(G+Se)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | (combined) | $\dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\gamma$ |
| $\gamma_d$ | $\dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{G\,\gamma_w(1-n)}{1}$ | $\gamma_d$ | $\dfrac{\gamma}{1+w}$ |
| $\gamma_{sat}$ | $\dfrac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | (combined) | (combined) | (combined) |
| $\gamma'$ | $\dfrac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | $\dfrac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | (combined) | $\dfrac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | (combined) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Master trick for KTU numericals:** Almost every question reduces to filling in the 3-phase diagram with $V_s = 1$ (unit solids volume assumption) or $V = 1$ (unit total volume assumption). Once any one assumption is made, all other quantities can be back-calculated from the given data.

---

### 3.10 Worked Derivation — Illustrative Numerical (3-Phase Filling Method)

**Problem:** A soil sample in its natural state has $V = 200\ \text{cm}^3$, $M = 350\ \text{g}$, $w = 25\%$, and $G = 2.70$. Determine $e$, $n$, $S$, $\gamma$, $\gamma_d$, $\gamma_{sat}$, and $\gamma'$.

**Step 1 — Compute mass quantities.**

$$M_s = \dfrac{M}{1 + w} = \dfrac{350}{1.25} = 280\ \text{g}$$
$$M_w = M - M_s = 350 - 280 = 70\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume quantities.**

$$V_w = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{70}{1} = 70\ \text{cm}^3$$
$$V_s = \dfrac{M_s}{G \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{280}{2.70 \cdot 1} = 103.70\ \text{cm}^3$$
$$V_v = V - V_s = 200 - 103.70 = 96.30\ \text{cm}^3$$
$$V_a = V_v - V_w = 96.30 - 70 = 26.30\ \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute index properties.**

$$e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s} = \dfrac{96.30}{103.70} = 0.929$$
$$n = \dfrac{V_v}{V} = \dfrac{96.30}{200} = 0.482 = 48.2\%$$
$$S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v} = \dfrac{70}{96.30} = 0.727 = 72.7\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute unit weights (using $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$).**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{V} = \dfrac{350 \cdot 9.81 / 1000}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{3.4335}{0.0002} = 17.17\ \text{kN/m}^3$$
$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{M_s \cdot g}{V} = \dfrac{280 \cdot 9.81 / 1000}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 13.73\ \text{kN/m}^3$$
$$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \dfrac{(2.70 + 0.929) \cdot 9.81}{1.929} = \dfrac{35.61}{1.929} = 18.46\ \text{kN/m}^3$$
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 18.46 - 9.81 = 8.65\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$:**
> LHS: $S \cdot e = 0.727 \cdot 0.929 = 0.6754$.
> RHS: $w \cdot G = 0.25 \cdot 2.70 = 0.675$. ✓ (Matches to rounding error.)

This kind of cross-check is what differentiates a topper from an average student in KTU exams.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Classic 3-Phase Phase Diagram (Schematic)

The Terzaghi phase diagram is the standard visual representation of the three-phase system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VOL["VOLUME BLOCK (Total Volume V)"]
        direction TB
        Vs["Vs\nVolume of\nSolids"]
        Vv["Vv = Vw + Va\nVolume of Voids"]
        Vv1["Vw\nVolume of Water"]
        Vv2["Va\nVolume of Air"]
    end
    subgraph MASS["MASS BLOCK (Total Mass M)"]
        direction TB
        Ms["Ms\nMass of Solids"]
        Mw["Mw\nMass of Water"]
    end
    VOL --- MASS
    Vs --- Ms
    Vv1 --- Mw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left block tracks **volume** (decomposed into solid and void sub-volumes, with voids further split into water and air). The right block tracks **mass** (solids and water only; air has negligible mass). The two blocks share the *same soil sample*, just showing different attribute decompositions.

---


### 4.4 Flowchart — Solving a 3-Phase Numerical in KTU Exams

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q --> R["Step 2: Choose ASSUMPTION\nEither V = 1 OR Vs = 1"]

```

### 4.5 The Phase Relationship Wheel (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e["Void Ratio e"] --- n["Porosity n"]
    e --- S["Saturation S"]
    e --- w["Water Content w"]
    e --- G["Specific Gravity G"]
    e --- gamma_d["Dry Unit Weight gamma_d"]
    e --- gamma_sat["Saturated gamma_sat"]
    e --- gamma_prime["Submerged gamma_prime"]
    n --- S
    n --- w
    n --- G
    S --- w
    S --- G
    w --- G
    gamma_d --- G
    gamma_sat --- e

```

> [!NOTE]
> **The wheel shows that every index property is interconnected with every other one through $V_s$ and $V_v$.** This is the conceptual reason the master relation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ and the family of $\gamma$ formulas exist. KTU 2024 problems are designed to test whether you can navigate this wheel confidently.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and is expected to be answered in **80 to 100 words** with a crisp definition, classification, or derivation snippet. Diagrams and equations are encouraged.

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Void ratio, (ii) Porosity, (iii) Degree of saturation.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Void ratio ($e$)** is the ratio of the volume of voids ($V_v$) to the volume of solids ($V_s$) in a soil mass: $e = V_v / V_s$. It is dimensionless and is typically between 0.3 and 1.5 for most natural soils. Example: A loose, sandy soil may have $e \approx 0.8$.

(ii) **Porosity ($n$)** is the ratio of the volume of voids ($V_v$) to the total volume ($V$) of the soil: $n = V_v / V$. Expressed as a percentage, it usually ranges from 30% to 60% in granular soils. Example: A well-graded sand may have $n \approx 35\%$.

(iii) **Degree of saturation ($S$)** is the ratio of the volume of water ($V_w$) to the volume of voids ($V_v$): $S = V_w / V_v$. It ranges from 0 (dry soil) to 1 (fully saturated). Example: A soil just below the water table is fully saturated with $S = 1$ (or 100%).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
List the major soil deposits of India and explain any two with their engineering significance.

**Model Answer:**

The major soil deposits of India are:

1. **Indo-Gangetic alluvial plains** (Punjab, UP, Bihar, West Bengal)
2. **Black Cotton Soil (Regur)** (Maharashtra, Gujarat, MP)
3. **Lateritic soil** (Kerala, Karnataka, Goa, Odisha)
4. **Coastal marine deposits** (Kochi, Mumbai, Chennai)
5. **Desert / Aeolian sands** (Rajasthan)
6. **Himalayan mountain soils** (J&K, HP, Ladakh)

**Engineering significance of two:**

- **Black Cotton Soil:** Rich in montmorillonite clay, exhibits high **swelling-shrinkage behavior** with moisture changes, causing severe damage to lightly loaded structures. Special foundations (under-reamed piles) and ground improvement (lime stabilization) are required.

- **Lateritic Soil:** Highly porous with low dry density; loses strength rapidly upon wetting. Used in low-cost housing as building blocks but is a **poor foundation material** when saturated, often requiring soil replacement or compaction.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **one** of the two internal choices provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Three-Phase System and Index Properties

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Define the three-phase system of soil with a neat phase diagram. Derive the relationship between void ratio ($e$) and porosity ($n$). (7 marks)

**(b)** A saturated soil sample has a water content of 40% and specific gravity of solids as 2.70. Calculate its void ratio, porosity, saturated unit weight, and dry unit weight. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Three-Phase System Definition and Diagram:**

A soil mass consists of three phases: **solid** (mineral grains), **liquid** (pore water), and **gas** (pore air). This is conventionally represented by a **phase diagram** (Terzaghi), with one block showing the decomposition of total volume $V$ into $V_s$ (solids), $V_w$ (water), and $V_a$ (air), and another block showing the decomposition of total mass $M$ into $M_s$ and $M_w$.

(Refer to **SECTION 4.1** for the labeled phase diagram.)

**Derivation of $e$ vs. $n$:**

$$V = V_s + V_v$$
$$V_v = e \cdot V_s \quad \text{(from definition of $e$)}$$
$$V = V_s + e V_s = V_s(1+e)$$

Now:
$$n = \dfrac{V_v}{V} = \dfrac{e V_s}{V_s(1+e)} = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$$

Inverting: $n(1+e) = e \Rightarrow n + ne = e \Rightarrow n = e(1-n) \Rightarrow e = n/(1-n)$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $w = 40\% = 0.40$, $G = 2.70$, **saturated** ($S = 1$), $\gamma_w = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Find void ratio using the master relation.**

For saturated soil, $S = 1$, so $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ becomes:
$$e = w \cdot G = 0.40 \cdot 2.70 = 1.08$$

**Step 2 — Find porosity.**

$$n = \dfrac{e}{1+e} = \dfrac{1.08}{1+1.08} = \dfrac{1.08}{2.08} = 0.5192 = 51.92\%$$

**Step 3 — Find saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat}$.**

$$\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e)\,\gamma_w}{1+e} = \dfrac{(2.70 + 1.08) \cdot 9.81}{1 + 1.08} = \dfrac{3.78 \cdot 9.81}{2.08} = \dfrac{37.08}{2.08} = 17.83\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Find dry unit weight $\gamma_d$.**

$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{G\,\gamma_w}{1+e} = \dfrac{2.70 \cdot 9.81}{1 + 1.08} = \dfrac{26.487}{2.08} = 12.73\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Cross-check using $\gamma_d = \gamma_{sat}/(1+w)$:**

$$\gamma_d = \dfrac{17.83}{1 + 0.40} = \dfrac{17.83}{1.40} = 12.74\ \text{kN/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- $e = 1.08$
- $n = 51.92\%$
- $\gamma_{sat} = 17.83\ \text{kN/m}^3$
- $\gamma_d = 12.73\ \text{kN/m}^3$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Soil Types, Indian Deposits, and Phase Calculations

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Classify soils based on grain size as per IS 1498. List the major soil deposits of India and explain the engineering problems associated with any two of them. (7 marks)

**(b)** A soil sample in its natural state weighs 425 g. Its volume is 220 cm³. The oven-dried mass is 350 g, and the specific gravity of solids is 2.68. Compute the void ratio, porosity, degree of saturation, and bulk unit weight. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Soils as per IS 1498:**

| Soil | Size Range |
|---|---|
| Boulder | $> 300\ \text{mm}$ |
| Gravel | $4.75\ \text{mm}$–$80\ \text{mm}$ |
| Sand | $75\ \mu\text{m}$–$4.75\ \text{mm}$ |
| Silt | $2\ \mu\text{m}$–$75\ \mu\text{m}$ |
| Clay | $< 2\ \mu\text{m}$ |

**Major Soil Deposits of India and Engineering Problems:**

- **Black Cotton Soil (Regur)** — Found in Maharashtra, Gujarat, MP, and parts of Karnataka. Contains **montmorillonite** mineral, undergoes severe **swelling–shrinkage cycles** with seasonal moisture variation, causing cracking of foundations, roads, and walls. Mitigation: under-reamed piles, lime stabilization, moisture barriers.

- **Lateritic Soil** — Found in Kerala, Karnataka, Goa. Highly **porous, low dry density, low bearing capacity when saturated**. Problematic for foundations in monsoon season; needs replacement with better soil or compaction.

- **Marine Clays** (Kochi, Mumbai coast) — **Soft, sensitive, thixotropic** clays with very low shear strength and high compressibility, posing severe settlement and stability issues for heavy structures.

- **Alluvial Soils** (Indo-Gangetic Plain) — Variable, often **loose saturated sands** with high liquefaction potential in seismic zones.

- **Desert Sands** (Rajasthan) — **Uniformly graded, loose, wind-deposited**; very low density, high permeability, prone to erosion.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $M = 425\ \text{g}$, $V = 220\ \text{cm}^3$, $M_d = 350\ \text{g}$ (oven-dried), $G = 2.68$, $\rho_w = 1\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Find $M_s$ and $M_w$.**

$$M_s = M_d = 350\ \text{g}$$
$$M_w = M - M_s = 425 - 350 = 75\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Find water content $w$ (for verification).**

$$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} = \dfrac{75}{350} = 0.2143 = 21.43\%$$

**Step 3 — Find $V_w$, $V_s$, $V_v$.**

$$V_w = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{75}{1} = 75\ \text{cm}^3$$
$$V_s = \dfrac{M_s}{G \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{350}{2.68 \cdot 1} = 130.60\ \text{cm}^3$$
$$V_v = V - V_s = 220 - 130.60 = 89.40\ \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute index properties.**

$$e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s} = \dfrac{89.40}{130.60} = 0.6845$$
$$n = \dfrac{V_v}{V} = \dfrac{89.40}{220} = 0.4064 = 40.64\%$$
$$S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v} = \dfrac{75}{89.40} = 0.8390 = 83.90\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute bulk unit weight $\gamma$.**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{M \cdot g}{V} = \dfrac{425 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 9.81}{220 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{4.169}{0.00022} = 18.95\ \text{kN/m}^3$$

**Cross-check using $\gamma = G(1+w)\gamma_w/(1+e)$:**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{2.68 \cdot (1 + 0.2143) \cdot 9.81}{1 + 0.6845} = \dfrac{2.68 \cdot 1.2143 \cdot 9.81}{1.6845} = \dfrac{31.93}{1.6845} = 18.95\ \text{kN/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- $e = 0.685$
- $n = 40.64\%$
- $S = 83.9\%$
- $\gamma = 18.95\ \text{kN/m}^3$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Easy Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $\text{g/cm}^3$ with $\text{kN/m}^3$ without conversion. The conversion is $\gamma (\text{kN/m}^3) = \rho (\text{g/cm}^3) \times 9.81$. Many students forget this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ (or 10):** Always state the assumed value of $\gamma_w$ in your answer. KTU allows either 9.81 or 10 kN/m³ (check the question); consistent usage is key.
>
> 3. **Confusing $e$ and $n$ in the formula for $\gamma_d$:** A very common error is to use $\gamma_d = G \gamma_w / n$ instead of $\gamma_d = G \gamma_w / (1 + e)$. Both formulas are **wrong** without the $V = V_s(1+e)$ derivation step.
>
> 4. **Mixing $w$ as a fraction vs. percentage:** If $w = 20\%$ but you plug in $w = 20$ (i.e., 20.0 instead of 0.20) into $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$, you will get an answer that is 100× too large. **Always convert percentages to decimals** in formulas.
>
> 5. **Assuming $S = 1$ (saturated) without justification:** The problem must explicitly state "saturated" or "below the water table." A *moist* soil with $w = 20\%$ and $G = 2.65$ is **not** saturated.
>
> 6. **Neglecting to draw the phase diagram in 3-mark and 7-mark answers:** A simple 3-block phase diagram is worth 1 to 2 marks by itself and is essentially free marks. Always include one.
>
> 7. **Errors in $M_s$ calculation from $M$ and $w$:** It is $M_s = M/(1+w)$, not $M_s = M(1-w)$ or $M - w$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Geotechnical engineering** is the branch of civil engineering dealing with soil and rock behavior; **soil mechanics** is its theoretical foundation.
- [x] Soils are classified by grain size per **IS 1498**: Boulder, Gravel, Sand, Silt, Clay; the **75 μm sieve** separates coarse from fine fractions.
- [x] **Major soil deposits of India:** Alluvial (Indo-Gangetic), Black Cotton (Regur), Lateritic, Marine, Desert (Aeolian), Himalayan.
- [x] **Black Cotton Soil** → montmorillonite → swelling–shrinkage problem.
- [x] **Lateritic Soil** → iron/aluminum-rich → low strength when wet.
- [x] **Marine Clay** → thixotropic, low strength, high compressibility.
- [x] **Soil is a 3-phase system:** Solid ($S$), Liquid ($L$), Gas ($G$).
- [x] **Void ratio** $e = V_v / V_s$ — uses solids as reference, invariant under volume change.
- [x] **Porosity** $n = V_v / V$ — uses total volume as reference.
- [x] $e = n / (1 - n)$ and $n = e / (1 + e)$.
- [x] **Degree of saturation** $S = V_w / V_v$; $0 \le S \le 1$.
- [x] **Water content** $w = M_w / M_s$ (always as percentage in lab, decimal in formulas).
- [x] **Master Phase Relation:** $\boxed{S \cdot e = w \cdot G}$ — *the most important equation in Module 1*.
- [x] For **saturated** soil ($S = 1$): $w_{sat} = e/G$.
- [x] **Specific gravity** $G = \gamma_s / \gamma_w \approx 2.65$–$2.75$ for mineral soils.
- [x] **Dry unit weight:** $\gamma_d = G \gamma_w / (1 + e)$.
- [x] **Bulk unit weight:** $\gamma = (G + Se)\gamma_w / (1 + e) = G(1+w)\gamma_w / (1 + e)$.
- [x] **Saturated unit weight:** $\gamma_{sat} = (G + e)\gamma_w / (1 + e)$.
- [x] **Submerged unit weight:** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = (G - 1)\gamma_w / (1 + e)$.
- [x] **Standard values:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ or $10\ \text{kN/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1\ \text{g/cm}^3$.
- [x] **Always draw a 3-block phase diagram** in numericals for clarity and partial marks.
- [x] **Always cross-check** with the master relation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ at the end of every numerical.
- [x] **Typical ranges to memorize:** $e$ (0.3–1.5), $n$ (30%–60% granular, 40%–90% clay), $G$ (2.65–2.75), $\gamma_d$ (14–20 kN/m³), $\gamma_{sat}$ (18–22 kN/m³).
- [x] **Volume of solids is constant** for a given soil mass → it is the *natural reference quantity* for all phase relationships.
- [x] The phase diagram and its formulas are the **gateway** to all subsequent KTU Soil Mechanics modules (compaction, consolidation, shear strength, earth pressure, bearing capacity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Porosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Porosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Porosity ($n$)** is defined as the ratio of the **volume of voids** ($V_v$) in a soil mass to the **total volume** ($V_t$) of the soil mass. It is a fundamental index property that quantifies the empty space available within the soil skeleton for the storage and flow of water and air.

$$ n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} = \frac{V_v}{V_s + V_v} $$

where $V_s$ is the volume of solids and $V_v$ is the volume of voids. Porosity is dimensionless and is conventionally expressed as a **decimal fraction** (e.g., $0.40$) or a **percentage** (e.g., $40\%$). Its theoretical bounds are $0 < n < 1$, since $V_v$ is always strictly positive in a real soil but can never equal $V_t$ (the solids always occupy some finite volume).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Porosity is listed as a foundational index property in *Module 1: Introduction & Index Properties of Soils*. It forms the prerequisite for all subsequent derivations involving void ratio, degree of saturation, relative density, and permeability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honeycomb Model

Imagine a **glass jar filled with marbles and sand**, then shaken until the particles settle tightly. Now imagine the same jar is filled with **sugar cubes stacked in a loose, jumbled pile**.

- The **marble jar** has very little empty space between perfectly round, tightly-packed particles → **Low Porosity** (e.g., $n \approx 0.36$ for uniform spheres).
- The **sugar cube jar** has large interconnected gaps and air pockets between the angular crystals → **High Porosity** (e.g., $n \approx 0.45$ to $0.50$).

Porosity is, simply put, a measure of **"how hollow"** a soil is. A sponge has very high porosity; a solid steel ball has zero porosity. Soil lies somewhere in between, and the exact value controls its **water-holding capacity, settlement behaviour, and seepage flow**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Range in Natural Soils |
|---|---|---|
| Porosity $n$ | Dimensionless (decimal) or % | **$0.25$ to $0.70$** |
| Porosity $n$ (sands) | Decimal | **$0.30$ to $0.45$** |
| Porosity $n$ (clays) | Decimal | **$0.40$ to $0.70$** |
| Theoretical max (uniform spheres) | Decimal | **$0.476$** (cubic packing) – **$0.395$** (rhombic packing) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why clays have higher porosity than sands:** Clay particles are plate-shaped (flaky) and create intricate, multi-scale pore networks. Sands are bulky grains that nest more efficiently, leaving less void space.

## 1.4 Types of Porosity

Geotechnical engineering distinguishes between several sub-categories of porosity, each relevant in different contexts:

- **Total Porosity ($n$)**: Includes *all* voids — both interconnected and isolated (sealed) air pockets. This is the standard textbook definition.
- **Effective Porosity ($n_e$)**: Only the *interconnected* voids that contribute to fluid flow and drainage. It is always less than or equal to total porosity: $n_e \le n$.
- **Primary Porosity**: The original inter-particle voids formed during soil deposition and sedimentation.
- **Secondary Porosity**: Voids created *post-deposition* by processes such as root decay, burrowing organisms, fractures, or chemical leaching. Important in residual soils and weathered rock.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Understand the Three-Phase Soil System
A saturated or partially saturated soil is conceptually divided into three phases:
1. **Solid phase** → mineral grains (volume $V_s$).
2. **Liquid phase** → pore water (volume $V_w$).
3. **Gas phase** → pore air (volume $V_a$).

The voids are the sum of liquid and gas: $V_v = V_w + V_a$.

### Step 2 — Establish the Reference Volume
The **total volume** of the soil mass is:
$$ V_t = V_s + V_w + V_a = V_s + V_v $$

### Step 3 — Formulate the Porosity Ratio
Porosity is *always* a void-to-total ratio, never a void-to-solid ratio (that is the void ratio $e$):
$$ n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} = \frac{V_v}{V_s + V_v} $$

### Step 4 — Why the Denominator Matters
A critical conceptual point: because the denominator is **always larger** than the numerator ($V_t > V_v$), porosity is **always less than 1**, whereas void ratio can exceed 1 (in highly compressible peats and organic clays).

### Step 5 — Convert Between Porosity and Void Ratio
This is the **single most important relationship** in KTU problems. The derivation is in Section 3, but the results are:
$$ n = \frac{e}{1 + e} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad e = \frac{n}{1 - n} $$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Why does porosity matter?** It directly controls how much water a soil can store, how quickly water flows through it (hydraulic conductivity), and how much it will compress under load.
- **Why is porosity bounded by 1 and not infinity?** A real soil always contains *some* solids. Even in a loose, flocculated clay, the platelets themselves occupy volume.
- **Why is it more convenient than void ratio in some cases?** Porosity is a *probability-like* quantity (fraction of the whole), making it natural for mass-balance calculations and for expressing the volumetric water content $\theta = n \cdot S$ (where $S$ is degree of saturation).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{V_v}{V_t}$ | Basic definition of porosity | Decimal (or %) |
| 2 | $n = \dfrac{e}{1 + e}$ | Porosity from void ratio | Decimal |
| 3 | $e = \dfrac{n}{1 - n}$ | Void ratio from porosity | Decimal |
| 4 | $n \cdot S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_t}$ | Volumetric water content relation | Decimal |
| 5 | $\theta = n \cdot S$ | Volumetric water content $\theta$ | Decimal |
| 6 | $n = 1 - \dfrac{V_s}{V_t} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e}$ | Porosity in terms of solid fraction | Decimal |
| 7 | $0 \le n_e \le n \le 1$ | Effective porosity bound | Decimal |
| 8 | Theoretical max $n$ (uniform spheres) | $= 0.476$ (cubic) / $0.395$ (rhombic) | Decimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Alert:** Many students confuse **void ratio ($e$)** with **porosity ($n$)**. They are *not* interchangeable! $e$ can be $>1$; $n$ cannot. Always check which one the question is asking for.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Porosity is a controlling parameter in:

- **Earth dam design:** Seepage losses through the embankment are estimated using effective porosity and Darcy's law.
- **Landfill liner design:** Low-porosity compacted clays are specified to minimize leachate migration.
- **Groundwater hydrology:** Aquifer storage and yield are directly tied to effective porosity.
- **Foundation engineering:** Settlement calculations on loose sands require the initial (in-situ) porosity to estimate compressibility.
- **Geotechnical site characterization:** Standard Penetration Test (SPT) and Cone Penetration Test (CPT) correlations with relative density use porosity as an input.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Porosity ($n$) in Terms of Void Ratio ($e$)

This is the **core derivation** every KTU Soil Mechanics student must know.

### Starting Point
The two index properties are defined as:
$$ e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} $$

### Step 1 — Express the total volume in terms of $V_s$ and $V_v$
By definition of the three-phase system:
$$ V_t = V_s + V_v $$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the porosity expression
$$ n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} = \frac{V_v}{V_s + V_v} $$

### Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by $V_s$
This is the key algebraic move that introduces the void ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{V_v / V_s}{(V_s + V_v) / V_s} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{V_v / V_s}{1 + V_v / V_s} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{e}{1 + e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of Step 3:** Dividing by $V_s$ normalizes the expression to a dimensionless per-unit-solid-volume form. The numerator becomes $e$ (by definition) and the denominator becomes $1 + e$ because $V_s / V_s = 1$ and $V_v / V_s = e$.

### Step 4 — Derive the inverse relation
Starting from $n = e/(1+e)$, solve for $e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n(1 + e) &= e \\[4pt]
n + n \cdot e &= e \\[4pt]
n &= e - n \cdot e \\[4pt]
n &= e(1 - n) \\[4pt]
e &= \frac{n}{1 - n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of Step 4:** Cross-multiply, collect the $e$ terms on one side, and factor out $e$. The result is the standard inverted form used whenever porosity is given and void ratio is required.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Porosity from the Degree of Saturation

The degree of saturation $S$ is defined as:
$$ S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_w = S \cdot V_v $$

The volumetric water content $\theta$ is:
$$ \theta = \frac{V_w}{V_t} $$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{V_w}{V_t} = \frac{S \cdot V_v}{V_t} = S \cdot \frac{V_v}{V_t} = S \cdot n
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$ \boxed{\theta = n \cdot S} $$

This is widely used in **agricultural soil science** and **unsaturated soil mechanics**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Porosity from Specific Gravity and Unit Weight

A frequently tested KTU problem gives the **specific gravity of solids ($G_s$)**, the **bulk unit weight ($\gamma$)**, and the **water unit weight ($\gamma_w$)**, and asks for porosity. The required formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{W_t}{V_t} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s + \gamma_w \cdot V_w}{V_t} \\[4pt]
\gamma_d &= \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \quad \text{(dry unit weight)} \\[4pt]
n &= 1 - \frac{\gamma_d}{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The dry unit weight is the weight of solids per total volume. Dividing by $G_s \cdot \gamma_w$ gives the volumetric solids fraction $V_s / V_t = 1/(1+e)$. The complement is porosity.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A soil sample has a void ratio $e = 0.65$ and a degree of saturation $S = 0.80$. Compute the porosity, the volumetric water content, and the air content.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Porosity
$$ n = \frac{e}{1 + e} = \frac{0.65}{1.65} = 0.3939 \approx 0.394 $$

### Step 2 — Volumetric water content
$$ \theta = n \cdot S = 0.394 \times 0.80 = 0.3152 \approx 0.315 $$

### Step 3 — Air content ($a_c$)
By definition, $S + a_c = 1$ (when expressed as fractions of void volume):
$$ a_c = 1 - S = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 $$

The **volumetric air content** is:
$$ n_a = n \cdot a_c = 0.394 \times 0.20 = 0.0788 \approx 0.079 $$

### Step 4 — Verification using three-phase volumes
Assume $V_s = 1 \text{ m}^3$ (any convenient unit works). Then:
$$ V_v = e \cdot V_s = 0.65 \text{ m}^3 $$
$$ V_t = V_s + V_v = 1.65 \text{ m}^3 $$
$$ V_w = S \cdot V_v = 0.80 \times 0.65 = 0.52 \text{ m}^3 $$
$$ V_a = V_v - V_w = 0.65 - 0.52 = 0.13 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Re-check porosity:** $V_v / V_t = 0.65 / 1.65 = 0.394$ ✓
**Re-check $\theta$:** $V_w / V_t = 0.52 / 1.65 = 0.315$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase Soil Diagram (Block Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block renders the classical **three-phase block diagram** of a soil mass, showing how the total volume partitions into solids, water, and air, and how porosity emerges as the void-to-total ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TOTAL VOLUME V_t"]:::tot --> B["SOLID PHASE V_s"]:::solid
    A --> C["VOID PHASE V_v"]:::void
    C --> D["WATER PHASE V_w"]:::water
    C --> E["AIR PHASE V_a"]:::air
    B -. "V_s" .-> F["Porosity n = V_v / V_t"]:::formula
    C -. "V_v" .-> F
    F -. "n" .-> G["VOID RATIO e = n / (1 - n)"]:::formula
    D -. "V_w" .-> H["Degree of Saturation S = V_w / V_v"]:::formula
    C -. "V_v" .-> H
    H -. "S" .-> I["Volumetric Water Content theta = n * S"]:::formula
    F -. "n" .-> I
    classDef tot fill:#2C3E50,stroke:#1A252F,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef solid fill:#8B4513,stroke:#5C2C0C,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef void fill:#ECF0F1,stroke:#7F8C8D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef water fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef air fill:#BDC3C7,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef formula fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#B7950B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow: How Porosity is Determined in the Lab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1: Obtain undisturbed soil sample"]:::step --> P2["Step 2: Measure total volume V_t by mercury displacement or caliper"]:::step
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Oven-dry at 105 to 110 deg C for 24 hours"]:::step
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Measure mass of solids Ms, then compute Vs = Ms / Gs * rho_w"]:::step
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Compute Vv = Vt - Vs"]:::step
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Compute n = Vv / Vt"]:::step
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Cross-check with e = Vv / Vs using n = e / (1+e)"]:::step
    classDef step fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Porosity vs. Void Ratio — Comparative Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R["VOLUMETRIC INDEX PROPERTIES OF SOIL"]:::root --> N["POROSITY n"]:::branch1
    R --> E["VOID RATIO e"]:::branch2
    N --> N1["Range: 0 to 1"]:::leaf
    N --> N2["Cannot exceed 1"]:::leaf
    N --> N3["Used in water content and degree of saturation calcs"]:::leaf
    E --> E1["Range: 0 to infinity"]:::leaf
    E --> E2["Can exceed 1 in organic clays and peats"]:::leaf
    E --> E3["Used in compressibility and shear strength calcs"]:::leaf
    N -. "n = e / (1+e)" .-> E
    classDef root fill:#1B2631,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef branch1 fill:#2874A6,stroke:#1A5276,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef branch2 fill:#A93226,stroke:#641E16,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef leaf fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#B9770E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** The yellow nodes represent derived formulas, the blue/red nodes represent the two competing index properties (porosity and void ratio), and the green flow nodes represent the laboratory procedure. Together they give a complete mental model of how porosity fits into the broader geotechnical framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Define porosity and effective porosity. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Porosity ($n$) is the ratio of the volume of voids to the total volume of the soil mass:
$$ n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} $$
Effective porosity ($n_e$) is the ratio of the volume of *interconnected* voids (those that allow fluid flow) to the total volume. It excludes sealed, isolated air pockets. The relationship is:
$$ n_e \le n $$
with equality only when the soil is fully interconnected and saturated or when all voids are continuous.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**The void ratio of a soil sample is 0.85. Compute its porosity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Using the standard conversion:
$$ n = \frac{e}{1 + e} = \frac{0.85}{1 + 1.85} = \frac{0.85}{1.85} = 0.4595 \approx 0.46 $$
Therefore the porosity is **0.46 or 46%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

#### (a) Derive the relationship between porosity ($n$) and void ratio ($e$). A soil sample has $e = 0.75$; determine its porosity and the porosity of a similar soil whose void ratio is half the original. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation**
Starting with the definitions:
$$ e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}, \qquad n = \frac{V_v}{V_t} = \frac{V_v}{V_s + V_v} $$
Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_s$:
$$ n = \frac{V_v / V_s}{1 + V_v / V_s} = \frac{e}{1 + e} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $n$ for $e = 0.75$**
$$ n = \frac{0.75}{1.75} = 0.4286 \approx 0.429 \text{ or } 42.9\% $$

**Step 3 — Compute $n$ for $e/2 = 0.375$**
$$ n_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.375}{1.375} = 0.2727 \approx 0.273 \text{ or } 27.3\% $$

**Conclusion:** Halving the void ratio does *not* halve the porosity; the relationship is non-linear. Porosity drops from **42.9% to 27.3%** (a relative reduction of ~36%).

---

#### (b) A saturated soil has $G_s = 2.70$ and bulk unit weight $\gamma = 19.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Taking $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$, determine the porosity, void ratio, and water content. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the saturated unit weight relation**
For a saturated soil ($S = 1$):
$$ \gamma_{\text{sat}} = \frac{(G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for $e$**
$$ 19.5 = \frac{(2.70 + e)(9.81)}{1 + e} $$
$$ 19.5(1 + e) = 9.81(2.70 + e) $$
$$ 19.5 + 19.5 e = 26.487 + 9.81 e $$
$$ 9.69 e = 6.987 $$
$$ e = 0.721 $$

**Step 3 — Porosity**
$$ n = \frac{e}{1 + e} = \frac{0.721}{1.721} = 0.419 \approx 0.42 $$

**Step 4 — Water content**
For a saturated soil, $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$, so:
$$ w = \frac{S \cdot e}{G_s} = \frac{1 \times 0.721}{2.70} = 0.267 = 26.7\% $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified Pattern]**

#### (a) Explain with a neat phase diagram the concept of porosity. A soil mass in its natural state has a total volume of $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ and weighs $22.5 \text{ N}$. After oven-drying, it weighs $19.0 \text{ N}$. The specific gravity of solids is $2.65$. Compute the porosity. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute weights and water content**
- Total weight $W_t = 22.5$ N; Dry weight $W_s = 19.0$ N.
- Water weight $W_w = W_t - W_s = 3.5$ N.
- Water content $w = W_w / W_s = 3.5 / 19.0 = 0.1842 = 18.42\%$.

**Step 2 — Compute volumes**
- Volume of water: $V_w = W_w / \gamma_w = 3.5 / 9810 = 3.568 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$ (using $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$).
- Volume of solids: $V_s = W_s / (G_s \cdot \gamma_w) = 19.0 / (2.65 \times 9810) = 7.309 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$.

**Step 3 — Compute volume of voids and porosity**
- $V_v = V_t - V_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} - 7.309 \times 10^{-4} = 4.691 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$.
- $n = V_v / V_t = 4.691 \times 10^{-4} / 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.391$.

**Answer:** **Porosity = 0.391 or 39.1%.**

---

#### (b) Define void ratio. Show that for a soil sample, $n = e/(1+e)$ and $e = n/(1-n)$. A soil has $n = 0.45$. If it is compressed so that its void ratio reduces by 30%, find the new porosity. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of void ratio**
Void ratio ($e$) is the ratio of the volume of voids to the volume of solids:
$$ e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} $$

**Step 2 — Prove $n = e/(1+e)$**
Starting from $n = V_v / (V_s + V_v)$ and dividing both terms by $V_s$:
$$ n = \frac{V_v/V_s}{V_s/V_s + V_v/V_s} = \frac{e}{1 + e} $$

**Step 3 — Prove $e = n/(1-n)$**
Starting from $n(1+e) = e \Rightarrow n + ne = e \Rightarrow n = e(1-n) \Rightarrow e = n/(1-n)$.

**Step 4 — Original void ratio from $n = 0.45$**
$$ e_1 = \frac{0.45}{1 - 0.45} = \frac{0.45}{0.55} = 0.8182 $$

**Step 5 — Reduce void ratio by 30%**
$$ e_2 = 0.7 \times e_1 = 0.7 \times 0.8182 = 0.5727 $$

**Step 6 — New porosity**
$$ n_2 = \frac{e_2}{1 + e_2} = \frac{0.5727}{1.5727} = 0.3642 $$

**Answer:** **New porosity = 0.364 or 36.4%** (down from 45%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in the $\gamma_w$ step.** KTU examiners frequently use $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ in one sub-question and $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ in another. Mixing units (e.g., dividing kN by N/m³) is the **#1 source of full-mark losses** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $w$ to a fraction.** Writing $w = 18.42$ instead of $w = 0.1842$ in $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ will silently give the wrong void ratio.
> 3. **Reporting porosity as $> 1$ or void ratio as $> e$ at given $n$.** Always sanity-check the result: if $n > 0.5$, then $e > 1$; if $n < 0.5$, then $e < 1$.
> 4. **Skipping the phase diagram.** KTU expects a labelled three-phase block diagram in any 14-mark question on index properties. Omitting it costs **1 to 2 marks** even if the algebra is perfect.
> 5. **Inverting the formula.** A surprisingly common error is writing $n = (1+e)/e$ instead of $n = e/(1+e)$. Always derive from the definition under exam pressure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Porosity ($n$)** = Volume of voids / Total volume. Dimensionless. Range $0 < n < 1$.
- **Void ratio ($e$)** = Volume of voids / Volume of solids. Range $0 \le e < \infty$.
- **Master formula:** $n = e/(1+e)$ and its inverse $e = n/(1-n)$.
- **Volumetric water content:** $\theta = n \cdot S$, where $S$ is the degree of saturation.
- **For a fully saturated soil ($S = 1$):** $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s \Rightarrow w = e/G_s$.
- **Porosity is *not* a constant** for a given soil — it changes with compaction, consolidation, and swelling. Only the **specific gravity of solids ($G_s$)** is a near-constant mineral property.
- **Typical porosity ranges:** Sands ≈ 0.30–0.45; Clays ≈ 0.40–0.70; Uniform spheres (loosest) ≈ 0.476; Uniform spheres (densest) ≈ 0.395.
- **Effective porosity ($n_e$)** is always $\le n$, and is the relevant quantity for **seepage and groundwater flow** problems.
- **Clays > Sands** in porosity because plate-like particles form a more open, intricate fabric than bulky grains.
- **Lab determination** of porosity requires measuring $V_t$ and $V_s$ independently — typically $V_t$ by caliper/mercury displacement and $V_s$ by oven-dried mass and $G_s$.
- **KTU exam tip:** In any 14-mark question on index properties, **always draw the three-phase diagram** and **always state the units explicitly** for any numerical quantity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Degree of Saturation # 🌊 DEGREE OF SATURATION — CORE FOUNDATIONS ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Degree of Saturation (S)** is a fundamental phase-relationship index in Soil Mechanics that quantifies the proportion of the void space in a soil mass that is actually filled with water. It is defined as the **ratio of the volume of water present in the soil voids to the total volume of voids**, expressed as a decimal fraction or a percentage. Mathematically, in standard KTU 2024 Scheme notation: $$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v}$$ Where: - $V_w$ = Volume of water - $V_v$ = Volume of voids - The result is dimensionless (a pure ratio) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Degree of Saturation is a **purely volumetric** index. It tells us *how full* the voids are, but says nothing directly about the *weight* of water or *mass* relationships. This distinction is frequently tested in the university exam. ### 1.2 Boundary (Limit) Values | Soil Condition | Value of S | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Completely Dry Soil** | $S = 0$ (i.e., **0%**) | Voids are entirely filled with air | | **Partially Saturated Soil** | $0 < S < 1$ (i.e., **0% to 100%**) | Voids contain both water and air | | **Fully Saturated Soil** | $S = 1$ (i.e., **100%**) | Voids are completely filled with water (e.g., below the water table) | > [!NOTE] > In nature, $S$ rarely equals exactly 0% because most soils retain some moisture. Values approaching 0% are seen in oven-dried laboratory samples, while values approaching 100% are typical of soils below the groundwater table or in capillary-saturated zones. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Sponge Analogy:** Imagine a dry kitchen sponge held under a running tap. At first, only a few drops of water seep into the sponge — the *voids* (pores) are mostly filled with *air*. This is **low saturation** ($S \approx 20\text{–}30\%$). As you keep squeezing water in, more voids get filled. When the sponge is dripping wet and you cannot squeeze in any more water, every void is full — this is **full saturation** ($S = 100\%$). The Degree of Saturation simply tells you *what percentage* of those internal "holes" are currently holding water versus air. In geotechnical terms: the **voids in soil** act exactly like the **pores in a sponge**, and water progressively displaces air as the degree of saturation rises. ### 1.4 Classification of Soils Based on S While KTU primarily focuses on the quantitative use of $S$, a practical qualitative classification is: | Saturation Range | Common Term | |---|---| | $S = 0\%$ | Oven-dry / Bone-dry | | $S < 50\%$ | Dry to moist | | $50\% \le S < 85\%$ | Moist to wet | | $85\% \le S < 100\%$ | Nearly saturated | | $S = 100\%$ | Saturated | --- # 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Why Is Degree of Saturation Important? The Degree of Saturation is **not a curiosity index** — it is a direct measure of how much free water is available within the soil's pore network. This single ratio controls several critical engineering behaviors: - **Shear Strength:** A dry soil derives its strength entirely from interparticle friction and interlocking. As $S$ increases, pore-water pressure develops, reducing effective stress and hence reducing strength. - **Compressibility (Settlement):** Saturated soils undergo consolidation (time-dependent settlement) when loaded, whereas dry or partially saturated soils settle almost immediately. - **Permeability:** A fully saturated soil transmits water far more easily than a partially saturated one (air pockets block flow). - **Volume Change Behavior:** Expansive clays show heave or shrinkage cycles directly tied to seasonal variations in $S$. - **Frost Heave and Pore-Water Pressure:** Only soils with high $S$ are susceptible to frost damage in cold climates. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework The following chain of logic underpins the most important formula in this module: **Step 1 — The defining equation:** Start with the direct volumetric definition. $$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v}$$ **Step 2 — Express the water content ($w$) in terms of volumes and specific gravity:** Water content by definition is the ratio of weight of water to weight of solids. $$w = \frac{W_w}{W_s}$$ Using $\gamma_w$ (unit weight of water) and $G$ (specific gravity of solids), we expand: $$w = \frac{V_w \cdot \gamma_w}{V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w} = \frac{V_w}{V_s \cdot G}$$ **Step 3 — Recall the void ratio definition:** $$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s}$$ **Step 4 — Combine the expressions to eliminate the volume terms:** $$S \cdot e = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \cdot \frac{V_v}{V_s} = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$ Equating the two expressions for $\frac{V_w}{V_s}$: $$S \cdot e = w \cdot G$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important relationship** in the entire Degree of Saturation topic for KTU examinations. Commit it to memory. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S = \dfrac{V_w}{V_v}$ | **Definition** of Degree of Saturation | Dimensionless | | 2 | $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ | **Most-tested** phase relationship | Dimensionless | | 3 | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{e}$ | Rearranged form to find $S$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | $e = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{S}$ | Find void ratio from $S$ and $w$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | $w = \dfrac{S \cdot e}{G}$ | Find water content from $S$ and $e$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | Porosity from void ratio | Dimensionless | | 7 | $V_w = S \cdot V_v$ | Volume of water in terms of voids | Volume ($\text{m}^3$) | | 8 | $V_a = (1 - S) \cdot V_v$ | Volume of air in voids | Volume ($\text{m}^3$) | | 9 | $V_v = V_a + V_w$ | Partitioning of void volume | Volume ($\text{m}^3$) | | 10 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Dry unit weight | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | | 11 | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Saturated unit weight (when $S = 1$) | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | | 12 | $\gamma = \gamma_d (1 + w)$ or $\gamma = \gamma_d + S \cdot n \cdot \gamma_w$ | Bulk unit weight (general) | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | > [!NOTE] > **Note on symbols:** $e$ = void ratio, $n$ = porosity, $w$ = water content, $G$ = specific gravity of solids, $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water = **$9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$** (KTU standard). All other Greek symbols retain standard meaning. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The relationship $S \cdot e = w \cdot G$ is the **bridge** that connects: - **Volumetric quantities** ($S$, $e$, $V_w$) - **Gravimetric / weight quantities** ($w$) - **Material property** ($G$) In practice, geotechnical engineers rarely measure $S$ directly. Instead, they: 1. Measure water content $w$ in the lab (oven-drying method). 2. Assume $G$ from index tests (typically $G \approx 2.65$ to $2.75$ for mineral soils). 3. Measure $e$ from phase diagrams or using unit weight data. 4. Compute $S = \frac{w \cdot G}{e}$ to back-calculate the saturation state. This makes the formula indispensable in **field compaction control, earthwork design, and slope-stability analysis**. --- # 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Master Relationship $\mathbf{S \cdot e = w \cdot G}$ This is the cornerstone derivation of the module. We will expand it in meticulous detail. ### Given Data A three-phase soil system with: - $V_s$ = Volume of solids - $V_w$ = Volume of water - $V_a$ = Volume of air - $V_v = V_w + V_a$ = Volume of voids - $V = V_s + V_v$ = Total volume - $W_w = V_w \cdot \gamma_w$ = Weight of water - $W_s = V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w$ = Weight of solids - $W_a \approx 0$ (weight of air is negligibly small) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Write the formal definition of Degree of Saturation:** $$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v}$$ **Step 2 — Write the formal definition of Void Ratio:** $$e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_v = e \cdot V_s$$ **Step 3 — Multiply $S$ and $e$ together:** $$S \cdot e = \frac{V_w}{V_v} \cdot \frac{V_v}{V_s}$$ The $V_v$ terms cancel algebraically: $$S \cdot e = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$ **Step 4 — Write the formal definition of Water Content:** $$w = \frac{W_w}{W_s} = \frac{V_w \cdot \gamma_w}{V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w}$$ The $\gamma_w$ terms cancel: $$w = \frac{V_w}{V_s \cdot G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_w}{V_s} = w \cdot G$$ **Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $\frac{V_w}{V_s}$:** $$\boxed{S \cdot e = w \cdot G}$$ This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The entire derivation rests on the **cancellation of $V_v$ in Step 3** and the **cancellation of $\gamma_w$ in Step 4**. These two algebraic simplifications are the heart of the relationship. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Saturated Unit Weight from Phase Relations When the soil is fully saturated, $S = 1$, so $V_w = V_v$ and $V_a = 0$. **Total weight:** $$W = W_w + W_s = V_w \cdot \gamma_w + V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w$$ Using $V_w = V_v = e \cdot V_s$: $$W = (e \cdot V_s) \cdot \gamma_w + V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w = V_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (G + e)$$ **Total volume:** $$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e \cdot V_s = V_s (1 + e)$$ **Saturated unit weight:** $$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{V_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (G + e)}{V_s (1 + e)}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G + e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: General Bulk Unit Weight with Degree of Saturation For a partially saturated soil, the volume of water is $V_w = S \cdot V_v = S \cdot e \cdot V_s$. **Total weight:** $$W = W_s + W_w = V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w + (S \cdot e \cdot V_s) \cdot \gamma_w = V_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (G + S \cdot e)$$ **Total volume:** $$V = V_s (1 + e)$$ **Bulk unit weight:** $$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{V_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot (G + S \cdot e)}{V_s (1 + e)}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{(G + S \cdot e) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}}$$ > [!NOTE] > When $S = 1$, this equation gracefully reduces to the saturated unit weight formula derived above, confirming internal consistency. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style) ### 🔢 Example 1: Basic Saturation Calculation **Problem:** A soil sample has a void ratio $e = 0.65$ and water content $w = 18\%$. The specific gravity of solids is $G = 2.70$. Determine the degree of saturation $S$. **Model Solution:** Using the master relationship: $$S \cdot e = w \cdot G$$ Substituting the given values (taking $w$ in decimal form: $w = 0.18$): $$S \cdot 0.65 = 0.18 \cdot 2.70$$ $$S \cdot 0.65 = 0.486$$ $$S = \frac{0.486}{0.65} = 0.7477$$ Expressed as a percentage: $$\boxed{S \approx 74.77\%}$$ The soil is therefore in a **moist to wet** state, with voids roughly three-quarters filled with water. --- ### 🔢 Example 2: Back-Calculating Water Content **Problem:** A sandy soil has void ratio $e = 0.55$, specific gravity $G = 2.65$, and is found to be $60\%$ saturated. Calculate the water content. **Model Solution:** Rearranging the master relationship: $$w = \frac{S \cdot e}{G}$$ Substituting ($S = 0.60$): $$w = \frac{0.60 \cdot 0.55}{2.65} = \frac{0.33}{2.65} = 0.1245$$ $$\boxed{w \approx 12.45\%}$$ --- ### 🔢 Example 3: Identifying the Saturation State **Problem:** A clay sample in the laboratory has $w = 32\%$, $G = 2.72$, and $e = 0.90$. Is the sample fully saturated? Justify. **Model Solution:** $$S = \frac{w \cdot G}{e} = \frac{0.32 \cdot 2.72}{0.90} = \frac{0.8704}{0.90} = 0.9671$$ $$\boxed{S \approx 96.71\%}$$ The sample is **not fully saturated** ($S \neq 1$), though it is very close to saturation. The remaining $3.29\%$ of void space is occupied by air (entrapped or dissolved air). > [!TIP] > A common KTU trick: if your calculated $S > 100\%$, the input data is **physically impossible** (super-saturation cannot occur naturally). Always cross-check the assumption $S \le 1$ after every calculation. --- ### 🔢 Example 4: Computing Bulk Unit Weight from S **Problem:** Given $G = 2.70$, $e = 0.70$, $S = 50\%$, and $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
