$$S \;=\; \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} \;=\; \frac{0.18 \,\cdot\, 2.70}{0.65} \;=\; \frac{0.486}{0.65} \;=\; \mathbf{0.7477 \;\;(\approx 74.77\%)}$$

**Step 2 — Dry unit weight:**
$$\gamma_d \;=\; \frac{G_s \,\cdot\, \gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{2.70 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{26.487}{1.65} \;=\; \mathbf{16.05 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Saturated unit weight:**
$$\gamma_{sat} \;=\; \frac{(G_s + e)\,\gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{(2.70 + 0.65) \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65}$$
$$\gamma_{sat} \;=\; \frac{3.35 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{32.86}{1.65} \;=\; \mathbf{19.92 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' \;=\; \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \;=\; 19.92 - 9.81 \;=\; \mathbf{10.11 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Cross-check using direct formula:**
$$\gamma' \;=\; \frac{(G_s - 1)\,\gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{(2.70 - 1) \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{1.70 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{16.677}{1.65} \;=\; 10.11 \text{ kN/m}^3 \;\checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pycnometer Method Procedure

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **operational sequence** of the IS 2720 Part 3 pycnometer test, which is the prescribed KTU laboratory method for determining $G_s$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Clean Dry and Weigh Empty Pycnometer as W1"] --> B["Step 2: Add 15 to 30 g of Oven Dried Soil Sample at 105 to 110 deg C"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Weigh Pycnometer with Dry Soil as W2"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Add Distilled Water to Cover Soil Half Fill the Pycnometer"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Remove Entrapped Air by Boiling for 10 min or Vacuum Desiccator"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Cool and Top up Pycnometer with Distilled Water"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Wipe Outer Surface and Weigh as W3 at 27 deg C"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Empty Pycnometer Clean it Refill with Distilled Water Only"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Weigh Pycnometer Filled with Water as W4 at 27 deg C"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Compute Gs using Pycnometer Formula"]
    J --> K["Step 11: Apply Temperature Correction if Lab Temp is not 27 deg C"]
    K --> L["Final Result: Specific Gravity Gs of Soil Solids"]
```

### Three-Phase Block Diagram Showing Where $G_s$ Operates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Soil Sample Total Mass M"] --> B["Three Phase System"]
    B --> C["Solid Phase Mass Ms and Volume Vs"]
    B --> D["Water Phase Mass Mw and Volume Vw"]
    B --> E["Air Phase Mass 0 and Volume Va"]
    C --> F["Specific Gravity Gs = Ms divided by Vs times rho w"]
    D --> G["Water Content w = Mw divided by Ms"]
    E --> H["Degree of Saturation S equals Vw divided by Vv"]
    F --> I["Combined Phase Relation S times e equals w times Gs"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (Fallback Architecture):** The Mermaid flowchart above is used in place of a physical phase diagram because Mermaid cannot render the conventional three-block (Solid–Water–Air) soil-phase diagram. The block representation preserves the **same logical mapping** of mass and volume relationships that students must draw manually in their answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
*Define the specific gravity of soil solids. Why is the test carried out at a standard temperature of 27 °C?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Specific gravity of soil solids ($G_s$) is the ratio of the weight of a given volume of soil solids to the weight of an equal volume of distilled water at 27 °C. Mathematically, $G_s = W_s / (V_s \cdot \gamma_w)$. It is a dimensionless quantity, dependent only on the mineralogical composition of the soil grains.

**Reason for 27 °C (1 mark):** The density of water is **maximum at 4 °C (1000 kg/m³)** and decreases slightly with temperature rise. To maintain a **uniform reference density** for all laboratories across India, IS 2720 Part 3 specifies 27 °C as the standard testing temperature. This eliminates variability due to seasonal temperature changes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*The specific gravity of a soil sample is 2.72. What inferences can be drawn about the soil type?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A specific gravity of 2.72 is **higher than the typical value of 2.65 for quartz sand** and lies in the range of **2.70–2.80**, which is characteristic of **clayey soils**. The higher value indicates the likely presence of:
1. Iron oxide minerals (giving the soil a reddish-brown or yellowish color, e.g., lateritic soils).
2. Montmorillonite clay minerals (typical of black cotton soil).
3. Heavy minerals such as mica, chlorite, or amphiboles.

Organic soils would give values much lower (1.20–1.60), while pure quartz sand would yield 2.65. Hence, this soil is most likely a **heavy clay with appreciable iron content**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]** *(7 marks)*
*Describe the procedure for the determination of specific gravity of soil solids using a pycnometer as per IS 2720 (Part 3). State the formula used and the precautions to be observed.*

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus (1 mark):** Pycnometer (50 mL or 100 mL capacity), weighing balance (accuracy 0.01 g), constant-temperature water bath, vacuum desiccator or heating arrangement, distilled water.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Clean the pycnometer thoroughly, dry it, and weigh it as $W_1$.
2. Take about 15–30 g of oven-dried soil (dried at 105–110 °C for 24 hours) and transfer it into the pycnometer. Weigh it as $W_2$.
3. Add distilled water to cover the soil and soak it for about 10 minutes.
4. **Remove entrapped air** by either: (i) gently boiling the pycnometer for 10–15 minutes, OR (ii) placing it in a vacuum desiccator for 1–2 hours. Air removal is critical — any remaining air gives erroneous low values of $G_s$.
5. Cool the pycnometer and fill it completely with distilled water.
6. Wipe the outside dry and weigh as $W_3$ at the standard temperature of 27 °C.
7. Empty the pycnometer, clean it, fill it with distilled water only, and weigh as $W_4$.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$G_s \;=\; \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- All weighings must be at the same temperature (27 °C ± 1 °C).
- Air must be completely removed — even a single air bubble causes errors.
- Use distilled water only — dissolved salts alter density.
- The pycnometer must be cleaned and dried thoroughly before each weighing.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]** *(7 marks)*
*In a pycnometer test on a soil sample, the following observations were recorded:*
*Weight of empty pycnometer = 480 g*
*Weight of pycnometer + dry soil = 580 g*
*Weight of pycnometer + soil + water = 1680 g*
*Weight of pycnometer + water = 1620 g*

*Calculate the specific gravity of the soil solids. If the same soil has a natural water content of 15% and a void ratio of 0.62, find the degree of saturation and the dry unit weight.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the pycnometer formula (3 marks):**
$$G_s \;=\; \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$
$$G_s \;=\; \frac{580 - 480}{(1620 - 480) - (1680 - 580)}$$
$$G_s \;=\; \frac{100}{1140 - 1100} \;=\; \frac{100}{40} \;=\; \mathbf{2.50}$$

**Step 2 — Degree of saturation using $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ (2 marks):**
$$S \;=\; \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} \;=\; \frac{0.15 \,\cdot\, 2.50}{0.62} \;=\; \frac{0.375}{0.62} \;=\; \mathbf{0.605 \;\;(\approx 60.5\%)}$$

**Step 3 — Dry unit weight (2 marks):**
$$\gamma_d \;=\; \frac{G_s \,\cdot\, \gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{2.50 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1 + 0.62} \;=\; \frac{24.525}{1.62} \;=\; \mathbf{15.14 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *(7 marks)*
*Derive the relationship between specific gravity, void ratio, and degree of saturation starting from the three-phase diagram of a soil mass.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Consider the three-phase diagram (1 mark):** A soil mass consists of solids, water, and air. Let total volume $V = V_s + V_v$, where $V_v$ = volume of voids.

**Step 2 — Definition of void ratio (1 mark):**
$$e \;=\; \frac{V_v}{V_s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_v \;=\; e \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3 — Definition of degree of saturation (1 mark):**
$$S \;=\; \frac{V_w}{V_v} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_w \;=\; S \cdot V_v \;=\; S \cdot e \cdot V_s$$

**Step 4 — Expression for water content (1 mark):**
$$w \;=\; \frac{W_w}{W_s} \;=\; \frac{V_w \,\cdot\, \gamma_w}{G_s \,\cdot\, V_s \,\cdot\, \gamma_w} \;=\; \frac{V_w}{G_s \,\cdot\, V_s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_w$ from Step 3 (1 mark):**
$$w \;=\; \frac{S \,\cdot\, e \,\cdot\, V_s}{G_s \,\cdot\, V_s} \;=\; \frac{S \,\cdot\, e}{G_s}$$

**Step 6 — Final rearranged form (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{\,S \,\cdot\, e \;=\; w \,\cdot\, G_s\,}$$

This is the most fundamental phase relation in soil mechanics. It is valid for any soil state — fully saturated, partially saturated, or dry. When the soil is fully saturated, $S = 1$, and the relation reduces to $e = w \cdot G_s$ (since at saturation, $V_v = V_w$).

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *(7 marks)*
*A soil sample has a specific gravity of 2.68, a void ratio of 0.65, and a water content of 20%.*
*Calculate:*
*(i) Degree of saturation*
*(ii) Dry unit weight*
*(iii) Saturated unit weight*
*(iv) Submerged unit weight*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $G_s = 2.68$, $e = 0.65$, $w = 0.20$, $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$

**(i) Degree of saturation (2 marks):**
$$S \;=\; \frac{w \,\cdot\, G_s}{e} \;=\; \frac{0.20 \,\cdot\, 2.68}{0.65} \;=\; \frac{0.536}{0.65} \;=\; \mathbf{0.8246 \;\;(\approx 82.46\%)}$$

**(ii) Dry unit weight (2 marks):**
$$\gamma_d \;=\; \frac{G_s \,\cdot\, \gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{2.68 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{26.291}{1.65} \;=\; \mathbf{15.93 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**(iii) Saturated unit weight (2 marks):**
$$\gamma_{sat} \;=\; \frac{(G_s + e)\,\gamma_w}{1 + e} \;=\; \frac{(2.68 + 0.65) \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65}$$
$$\gamma_{sat} \;=\; \frac{3.33 \,\cdot\, 9.81}{1.65} \;=\; \frac{32.667}{1.65} \;=\; \mathbf{19.80 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**(iv) Submerged unit weight (1 mark):**
$$\gamma' \;=\; \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \;=\; 19.80 - 9.81 \;=\; \mathbf{9.99 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (or 10 kN/m³ if specified):** Several KTU students assume $\gamma_w = 1$ in SI units. This is wrong. Always use 9.81 unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
> 2. **Mixing up dry and saturated unit weight formulas:** A frequent error is using $\gamma_d$ in the place of $\gamma_{sat}$. Remember: $\gamma_d$ uses $G_s$ alone in the numerator; $\gamma_{sat}$ uses $(G_s + e)$.
> 3. **Reporting $S$ as a decimal without percentage:** If the question asks for "degree of saturation in percent", the answer must be 82.46 %, not 0.8246. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit/format errors.
> 4. **Wrong sign in $\gamma'$ calculation:** Submerged unit weight is **always** $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$, never $\gamma_w - \gamma_{sat}$. This sign error is a silent killer of marks.
> 5. **Skipping the air-removal step in pycnometer procedure:** Many students write the procedure without mentioning the boiling/vacuum step. This step carries 1 full mark — do not omit it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **$G_s$ is dimensionless** — it is a pure ratio, no units.
- [ ] **Reference temperature is 27 °C** as per IS 2720 Part 3 (NOT 4 °C, which is for pure water density tables).
- [ ] **Range:** Sand ≈ 2.65–2.68, Silt ≈ 2.68–2.70, Clay ≈ 2.70–2.80, Organic soils < 2.0.
- [ ] **Pycnometer formula:** $G_s = (W_2 - W_1) \,/\, [(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)]$.
- [ ] **Air removal** is the most critical experimental step — incomplete removal gives a falsely low $G_s$.
- [ ] **Three-phase identity:** $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ — this single equation unlocks 70 % of KTU numericals in Module 1.
- [ ] **At full saturation** ($S = 1$): $e = w \cdot G_s$, and $\gamma = \gamma_{sat}$.
- [ ] **For a dry soil** ($w = 0$): $S = 0$, and $\gamma = \gamma_d$.
- [ ] **Dry unit weight formula:** $\gamma_d = G_s \cdot \gamma_w \,/\, (1 + e)$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [ ] **Saturated unit weight formula:** $\gamma_{sat} = (G_s + e) \cdot \gamma_w \,/\, (1 + e)$.
- [ ] **Submerged unit weight formula:** $\gamma' = (G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w \,/\, (1 + e) = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$.
- [ ] **Use $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$** unless the question explicitly specifies a different value.
- [ ] **Volume of solids:** $V_s = M_s \,/\, (G_s \cdot \rho_w)$ — needed for three-phase diagram mass/volume computations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Weight - Relationship Between Basic Soil Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit Weight - Relationship Between Basic Soil Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Unit Weight?

**Unit Weight (γ)** is defined as the weight of a soil mass per unit volume. It is one of the most fundamental index properties used in geotechnical engineering for computing overburden pressures, earth pressures, bearing capacity, and settlement calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> $$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\text{Total weight of soil mass}}{\text{Total volume of soil mass}} \quad [\text{kN/m}^3]$$

It is conventionally expressed in **kN/m³** (kilonewtons per cubic meter), with the unit weight of water **γw = 9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as **10 kN/m³** in KTU exam problems for numerical simplicity).

## 1.2 The Three-Phase Soil System — The Foundation

All soil properties revolve around the **three-phase system**: **Solids (S) + Water (W) + Air (A)**. Every relationship is derived by tracking how mass and volume are distributed across these three phases.

| Phase | Symbol for Volume | Symbol for Weight/Mass |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| Solids | $V_s$ | $W_s$ |
| Water | $V_w$ | $W_w$ |
| Air | $V_a$ | $\approx 0$ (negligible) |
| **Total** | $V = V_s + V_w + V_a$ | $W = W_s + W_w$ |

## 1.3 Types of Unit Weight

There are **four** standard unit weights that every KTU student must master:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Unit Weights — Conceptual Summary**
> 1. **Bulk Unit Weight (γ)** — Total weight ÷ Total volume (includes water + air voids)
> 2. **Dry Unit Weight (γd)** — Weight of solids only ÷ Total volume (oven-dried condition)
> 3. **Saturated Unit Weight (γsat)** — Weight when all voids are filled with water
> 4. **Submerged / Effective Unit Weight (γ′)** — γsat − γw (used for soil below water table)

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge in a Bucket" Model

Imagine a **sponge soaked partially in water** held inside a bucket:
- **Sponge solids** = Soil grains
- **Water inside sponge** = Pore water
- **Empty pockets in sponge** = Air voids
- **The bucket's full space** = Total volume

If you **weigh the whole bucket (sponge + water + air)**, you get the **bulk unit weight**.
If you **squeeze out and dry the sponge**, then weigh it in the same bucket, you get the **dry unit weight**.
If you **flood the bucket so no air remains**, you get the **saturated unit weight**.
If you **hold the wet sponge underwater**, the buoyant reduction gives the **submerged unit weight**.

This mental image makes every formula physically intuitive.

## 1.5 Why These Relationships Matter in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme heavily tests the **inter-relationships** between:
- Void ratio (**e**)
- Porosity (**n**)
- Water content (**w**)
- Specific gravity (**G**)
- Degree of saturation (**S**)
- Unit weight (**γ, γd, γsat, γ′**)

A single question often requires combining **3–4** of these to derive an unknown. Mastery of this topic is the gateway to all later modules (compaction, consolidation, shear strength).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Index Properties — Definitions

Before we can inter-relate unit weight with other properties, we must define the building blocks:

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Range/Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|------------|
| Void Ratio | $e$ | $V_v / V_s$ | $0 < e < \infty$ (dimensionless) |
| Porosity | $n$ | $V_v / V$ | $0 < n < 1$ (or as %) |
| Water Content | $w$ | $W_w / W_s$ | $0 < w < \infty$ (%) |
| Specific Gravity | $G$ | $\gamma_s / \gamma_w$ | $2.65$ to $2.75$ (typical mineral soils) |
| Degree of Saturation | $S$ | $V_w / V_v$ | $0 < S \leq 1$ (or as %) |
| Air Content | $a_c$ | $V_a / V_v$ | $0 \leq a_c < 1$ |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Master Formulas

### Step 1: Starting from First Principles
- Volume of voids: $V_v = e \cdot V_s$ → so $V = V_s + V_v = V_s(1 + e)$
- Weight of solids: $W_s = G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$
- Weight of water: $W_w = w \cdot W_s = w \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$

### Step 2: Build Each Unit Weight
- **Bulk:** $\gamma = W/V = (W_s + W_w)/V = G\gamma_w(1+w)/(1+e)$
- **Dry:** $\gamma_d = W_s/V = G\gamma_w/(1+e)$
- **Saturated:** $S = 1$, so $w_{sat} \cdot G = e$ → $\gamma_{sat} = (G+e)\gamma_w/(1+e)$
- **Submerged:** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = (G-1)\gamma_w/(1+e)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Inter-Relationship:**
> $$\boxed{Se = wG}$$
> This single equation links Saturation, void ratio, water content, and specific gravity — appearing in nearly every KTU numerical.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam tested**. Memorize the units, the boundary conditions, and the limiting cases.

| # | Formula | Description | Limiting Case |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\gamma = \dfrac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Bulk unit weight | General state |
| 2 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Dry unit weight | Oven-dried soil |
| 3 | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Saturated unit weight | $S = 100\%$ |
| 4 | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \dfrac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Submerged unit weight | Below water table |
| 5 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1+w}$ | Dry from bulk | Most-used identity |
| 6 | $e = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$ | Void ratio from porosity | Always true |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | Porosity from void ratio | Always true |
| 8 | $Se = wG$ | Saturation identity | **The master link** |
| 9 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e} = \dfrac{\gamma_s}{1+e}$ | Alternative dry form | Useful for Gs |
| 10 | $a_c + S = 1$ | Air content + Saturation | Where $a_c$ is air content |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Applied

- **Foundation Design:** Overburden pressure $\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$ depends directly on unit weight.
- **Earth Pressure Calculations:** Rankine's and Coulomb's theories use $\gamma$ and $\gamma'$.
- **Compaction Control:** Proctor test results are reported as $\gamma_d$ vs. $w$.
- **Slope Stability:** Submerged unit weight $\gamma'$ governs buoyant forces in submerged slopes.
- **Effective Stress:** $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ uses $\gamma$ for total stress and $\gamma_w$ for pore pressure.

## 2.5 Limiting Behavior — The Visual Logic

| State | Condition | $S$ | $w$ | $\gamma$ behavior |
|-------|-----------|-----|-----|-------------------|
| Completely Dry | $V_w = 0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\gamma = \gamma_d$ (minimum bulk = dry) |
| Fully Saturated | $V_a = 0$ | $1$ | $w = e/G$ | $\gamma = \gamma_{sat}$ (maximum bulk) |
| Partially Saturated | $0 < S < 1$ | $0 < S < 1$ | $0 < w < e/G$ | $\gamma_d \leq \gamma \leq \gamma_{sat}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Always remember the ordering:** $\gamma' \leq \gamma_d \leq \gamma \leq \gamma_{sat}$ for any non-cohesive soil. This is a frequent **2-mark conceptual KTU question**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Bulk Unit Weight $\gamma$ in Terms of $(G, w, e)$

We begin with the three-phase diagram. Let the volume of solids be $V_s$.

**Step 1 — Express the total volume in terms of $V_s$:**
Since $e = V_v/V_s$, we have $V_v = e V_s$. The total volume is:
$$V = V_s + V_v = V_s + e V_s = V_s(1 + e)$$

**Step 2 — Express the weight of solids:**
The specific gravity is defined as $G = W_s / (V_s \cdot \gamma_w)$, therefore:
$$W_s = G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3 — Express the weight of water using water content:**
By definition, $w = W_w / W_s$, so:
$$W_w = w \cdot W_s = w \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

**Step 4 — Compute total weight:**
$$W = W_s + W_w = G\gamma_w V_s + w G \gamma_w V_s = G \gamma_w V_s (1 + w)$$

**Step 5 — Divide total weight by total volume:**
$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{G \gamma_w V_s (1+w)}{V_s (1+e)}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

This is the **most general** form and the one most often required in KTU 14-mark derivations.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Saturated Unit Weight $\gamma_{sat}$

**Step 1 — Recognize the saturated condition:**
When $S = 1$ (or $S = 100\%$), the void ratio and water content are linked by $Se = wG$, giving:
$$w_{sat} = \frac{e}{G}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $w = e/G$ into the bulk unit weight formula:**
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{G(1 + e/G)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(G + e)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (no further reduction possible):**
$$\boxed{\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Submerged (Effective) Unit Weight $\gamma'$

**Step 1 — Submerged unit weight = Saturated weight − Weight of displaced water:**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\gamma_{sat}$:**
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w - \gamma_w(1+e)}{1+e}$$

**Step 3 — Expand and combine terms in the numerator:**
$$\gamma' = \frac{G\gamma_w + e\gamma_w - \gamma_w - e\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma' = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

This elegant form shows that **submerged unit weight depends only on the solids (through $G$) and the void ratio** — water content cancels out.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Dry Unit Weight from Bulk and Water Content

**Step 1 — Start from the identity $\gamma = W/V$ and $\gamma_d = W_s/V$:**
$$\frac{\gamma_d}{\gamma} = \frac{W_s/V}{W/V} = \frac{W_s}{W} = \frac{W_s}{W_s + W_w}$$

**Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $W_s$:**
$$\frac{\gamma_d}{\gamma} = \frac{1}{1 + W_w/W_s} = \frac{1}{1+w}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\gamma_d$:**
$$\boxed{\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1+w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most-used formula** in KTU numericals — appearing in nearly every problem where dry density is asked.

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]**
> A soil sample has $G = 2.70$, $w = 18\%$, and $e = 0.70$. Compute $\gamma$, $\gamma_d$, $\gamma_{sat}$, and $\gamma'$. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

### Solution:

**Part (i): Bulk Unit Weight**
$$\gamma = \frac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{2.70 \times (1 + 0.18) \times 9.81}{1 + 0.70}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{2.70 \times 1.18 \times 9.81}{1.70} = \frac{31.257}{1.70} = 18.39 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Part (ii): Dry Unit Weight**
$$\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1+w} = \frac{18.39}{1.18} = 15.58 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Part (iii): Saturated Unit Weight**
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(2.70 + 0.70) \times 9.81}{1.70} = \frac{3.40 \times 9.81}{1.70} = 19.62 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Part (iv): Submerged Unit Weight**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19.62 - 9.81 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Verification using alternate form:**
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{1.70 \times 9.81}{1.70} = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Analytical Master Table — Inter-Conversion Between All Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given any 2 of the following** {γ, γd, G, w, e, n, S}, you can derive every other property. This table is the **conversion bible** for KTU problems.

| You Know | Want to Find | Use This Formula |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| $G, e$ | $\gamma_d$ | $\gamma_d = G\gamma_w/(1+e)$ |
| $G, e$ | $\gamma_{sat}$ | $\gamma_{sat} = (G+e)\gamma_w/(1+e)$ |
| $G, e$ | $\gamma'$ | $\gamma' = (G-1)\gamma_w/(1+e)$ |
| $G, e, S$ | $w$ | $w = Se/G$ |
| $G, \gamma, w$ | $e$ | $e = [G(1+w)\gamma_w/\gamma] - 1$ |
| $G, \gamma_d$ | $e$ | $e = (G\gamma_w/\gamma_d) - 1$ |
| $\gamma, w$ | $\gamma_d$ | $\gamma_d = \gamma/(1+w)$ |
| $e$ | $n$ | $n = e/(1+e)$ |
| $n$ | $e$ | $e = n/(1-n)$ |
| $\gamma_d, n$ | $G$ | $G = \gamma_d(1-n)/[\gamma_w(1-n)] = \gamma_d/\gamma_w$ ... no, use $G = \gamma_d(1+e)/\gamma_w$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Phase Soil System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P[PHASE SYSTEM - SOIL MASS]
        subgraph S[SOLID PHASE]
            VS["Volume of Solids Vs"]
            WS["Weight of Solids Ws"]
        end
        subgraph W[LIQUID PHASE - WATER]
            VW["Volume of Water Vw"]
            WW["Weight of Water Ww"]
        end
        subgraph A[GAS PHASE - AIR]
            VA["Volume of Air Va"]
            WA["Weight of Air approx 0"]
        end
    end

    VS --> TOTV["Total Volume V = Vs + Vw + Va"]
    VW --> TOTV
    VA --> TOTV

    WS --> TOTW["Total Weight W = Ws + Ww"]
    WW --> TOTW
    WA --> TOTW

    TOTV --> UW["Unit Weight gamma = W divide V"]
    TOTW --> UW

    UW --> UB["BULK gamma includes water and air"]
    UW --> UD["DRY gamma d uses Ws only"]
    UW --> US["SAT gamma sat uses S equals 100 percent"]
    UW --> UE["SUBMERGED gamma prime = gamma sat minus gamma w"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Inter-Relationship Flow Between Soil Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Specific Gravity G"] --> F1["gamma d = G gamma w over 1 plus e"]
    E["Void Ratio e"] --> F1
    F1 --> GD["Dry Unit Weight gamma d"]

    G --> F2["gamma sat = G plus e times gamma w over 1 plus e"]
    E --> F2
    F2 --> GSAT["Saturated Unit Weight gamma sat"]

    G --> F3["gamma prime = G minus 1 times gamma w over 1 plus e"]
    E --> F3
    F3 --> GPRIME["Submerged Unit Weight gamma prime"]

    W["Water Content w"] --> M1["Se = wG MASTER IDENTITY"]
    G --> M1
    E --> M1
    S["Degree of Saturation S"] --> M1

    E --> N1["n = e over 1 plus e"]
    N1 --> N["Porosity n"]

    W --> GD2["gamma d = gamma over 1 plus w"]
    GB["Bulk Unit Weight gamma"] --> GD2
    GD2 --> GD
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Unit Weight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given Soil Condition"] --> Q1{"Is water content known?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is soil fully saturated?"}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use gamma d = G gamma w over 1 plus e"]

    Q2 -- Yes S=100 --> USESAT["Use gamma sat = G plus e times gamma w over 1 plus e"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{"Is soil below water table?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> USESUB["Use gamma prime = gamma sat minus gamma w"]
    Q4 -- No --> USEBULK["Use gamma = G times 1 plus w times gamma w over 1 plus e"]

    USESAT --> DONE["Compute Required Unit Weight"]
    USESUB --> DONE
    USEBULK --> DONE
    Q3 --> DONE
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Unit Weight Computation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["INPUT DATA BLOCK\nG, w, e, S, gamma, gamma d"] --> PARSE["PHASE 1: PARSE INPUT\nIdentify known properties"]

    PARSE --> SEL["PHASE 2: SELECT FORMULA\nMatch to formula table"]

    SEL --> CALC["PHASE 3: COMPUTE\nApply gamma_w = 9.81 or 10 kN per cubic meter"]

    CALC --> VAL["PHASE 4: VALIDATE\nCheck ordering: gamma prime less than gamma d less than gamma less than gamma sat"]

    VAL --> OUT["PHASE 5: OUTPUT\nBulk, Dry, Saturated, Submerged Unit Weights"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block-level architecture above mirrors the standard KTU problem-solving approach — input identification, formula selection, computation, and result validation. This pattern is what examiners expect students to follow in their answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the following with their standard units: (a) Bulk unit weight, (b) Dry unit weight, (c) Submerged unit weight.

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Bulk Unit Weight (γ):** The total weight of a soil mass (solids + water) per unit total volume (solids + water + air). Unit: **kN/m³**.
$$\gamma = \frac{W_s + W_w}{V_s + V_w + V_a}$$
- **(b) Dry Unit Weight (γd):** The weight of solids per unit total volume, representing the oven-dried soil condition. Unit: **kN/m³**.
$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V}$$
- **(c) Submerged Unit Weight (γ′):** The effective (or buoyant) unit weight of soil when submerged below the water table. Unit: **kN/m³**.
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** State and prove the relationship $Se = wG$ for a soil mass.

**Model Answer:**
Consider a soil mass with volume of solids $V_s$, void ratio $e$, and degree of saturation $S$.

Volume of voids:
$$V_v = e \cdot V_s$$

Volume of water:
$$V_w = S \cdot V_v = S \cdot e \cdot V_s$$

Weight of water:
$$W_w = \gamma_w \cdot V_w = S \cdot e \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w$$

Weight of solids:
$$W_s = G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

Water content:
$$w = \frac{W_w}{W_s} = \frac{S \cdot e \cdot V_s \cdot \gamma_w}{G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s} = \frac{Se}{G}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{Se = wG} \quad \text{(proved)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module Choice Pattern: 2024 Scheme]**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between bulk unit weight, specific gravity, void ratio, and water content of a soil mass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A soil sample in its natural state has a mass of 2.25 kg and a volume of $1.15 \times 10^{-3}$ m³. After oven-drying, the mass reduces to 1.95 kg. The specific gravity of soil solids is 2.68. Determine: (i) Water content, (ii) Void ratio, (iii) Degree of saturation, (iv) Dry unit weight, (v) Saturated unit weight. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Starting assumptions (three-phase system):**
Let $V_s$ = volume of solids, $V_v$ = volume of voids, $V_w$ = volume of water, $V_a$ = volume of air.

**Step 1 — Volume relations:**
$$V_v = e \cdot V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = V_s + V_v = V_s(1 + e)$$

**Step 2 — Weight of solids:**
$$G = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_s = G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3 — Weight of water:**
$$w = \frac{W_w}{W_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_w = w \cdot W_s = w \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s$$

**Step 4 — Total weight:**
$$W = W_s + W_w = G\gamma_w V_s(1 + w)$$

**Step 5 — Unit weight:**
$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{G\gamma_w V_s(1+w)}{V_s(1+e)} = \boxed{\frac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $M = 2.25$ kg, $V = 1.15 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $M_d = 1.95$ kg, $G = 2.68$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

**(i) Water content:**
$$w = \frac{M - M_d}{M_d} = \frac{2.25 - 1.95}{1.95} = \frac{0.30}{1.95} = 0.1538 = 15.38\%$$

**(ii) Void ratio:**
First compute bulk unit weight: $\gamma = (M \cdot g)/V = (2.25 \times 9.81)/1.15 \times 10^{-3} = 22.07/1.15 \times 10^{-3} = 19.19$ kN/m³

Use: $\gamma = G(1+w)\gamma_w/(1+e)$
$$1 + e = \frac{G(1+w)\gamma_w}{\gamma} = \frac{2.68 \times 1.1538 \times 9.81}{19.19} = \frac{30.34}{19.19} = 1.581$$
$$e = 0.581$$

**(iii) Degree of saturation:**
$$S = \frac{wG}{e} = \frac{0.1538 \times 2.68}{0.581} = \frac{0.4122}{0.581} = 0.7095 = 70.95\%$$

**(iv) Dry unit weight:**
$$\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1+w} = \frac{19.19}{1.1538} = 16.63 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Cross-check:** $\gamma_d = G\gamma_w/(1+e) = 2.68 \times 9.81/1.581 = 26.29/1.581 = 16.63$ kN/m³ ✓

**(v) Saturated unit weight:**
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(2.68+0.581) \times 9.81}{1.581} = \frac{31.99}{1.581} = 20.23 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for saturated unit weight and submerged unit weight in terms of specific gravity and void ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fully saturated soil sample has a void ratio of 0.85 and specific gravity 2.70. The same soil in a dry state has a unit weight of 15.2 kN/m³. Determine: (i) The bulk unit weight at a water content of 20%, (ii) The submerged unit weight, (iii) The porosity, (iv) The degree of saturation in the second case. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Saturated Unit Weight:**

In saturated condition, $S = 1$, hence from $Se = wG$:
$$w_{sat} = \frac{e}{G}$$

Substituting into $\gamma = G(1+w)\gamma_w/(1+e)$:
$$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{G(1 + e/G)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e} \quad \boxed{\gamma_{sat} = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

**Submerged Unit Weight:**

By Archimedes' principle, the effective (buoyant) weight per unit volume:
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

Substituting:
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w}{1+e} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G+e)\gamma_w - \gamma_w(1+e)}{1+e} = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma' = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $e = 0.85$, $G = 2.70$, $\gamma_{d,given} = 15.2$ kN/m³, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

**(i) Bulk unit weight at $w = 20\%$:**
$$\gamma = \gamma_d(1+w) = 15.2 \times 1.20 = 18.24 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**(ii) Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' = \frac{(G-1)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \frac{1.70 \times 9.81}{1.85} = \frac{16.677}{1.85} = 9.015 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**(iii) Porosity:**
$$n = \frac{e}{1+e} = \frac{0.85}{1.85} = 0.4595 = 45.95\%$$

**(iv) Degree of saturation at $w = 20\%$:**
$$S = \frac{wG}{e} = \frac{0.20 \times 2.70}{0.85} = \frac{0.540}{0.85} = 0.6353 = 63.53\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ with $\gamma_w = 1000$ kg/m³ (or $1$ g/cm³). KTU uses **kN/m³** consistently.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\gamma_w$ multiplication:** Students write $\gamma_d = G/(1+e)$ instead of $G\gamma_w/(1+e)$. This loses **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 3. **Mixing void ratio and porosity:** $\gamma_d = G\gamma_w/(1+e)$ **NOT** $G\gamma_w/(1+n)$. These are different denominators.
>
> 4. **Saturated water content sign:** Always remember $w_{sat} = e/G$, not $w_{sat} = G/e$. Reverse this and the entire problem becomes wrong.
>
> 5. **Not showing derivation steps:** In 7-mark derivation questions, KTU examiners allocate marks for **each algebraic step**. Skipping the "$V = V_s(1+e)$" step loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Submerged unit weight formula:** Some students write $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} \cdot (G-1)/G$, which is **wrong**. The correct form is $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = (G-1)\gamma_w/(1+e)$.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert water content from % to decimal:** $w = 18\%$ means $w = 0.18$, not $w = 18$. This is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU answer scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unit Weight & Basic Soil Property Relationships**

### 🎯 The Must-Memorize Master Equations

- ✅ $\gamma = G(1+w)\gamma_w/(1+e)$ — **The general bulk unit weight formula**
- ✅ $\gamma_d = G\gamma_w/(1+e) = \gamma/(1+w)$ — **Two forms, both critical**
- ✅ $\gamma_{sat} = (G+e)\gamma_w/(1+e)$ — **Saturated condition, S = 1**
- ✅ $\gamma' = (G-1)\gamma_w/(1+e) = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ — **Submerged condition**
- ✅ $Se = wG$ — **The master identity linking four variables**
- ✅ $e = n/(1-n)$ and $n = e/(1+e)$ — **Void ratio ↔ Porosity**

### 🎯 The Ordering Principle

$$\gamma' \leq \gamma_d \leq \gamma \leq \gamma_{sat}$$

This ordering holds for all natural soils. It is the basis of **2-mark conceptual questions** every year.

### 🎯 Standard Values to Remember

- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (theoretical) or $10$ kN/m³ (KTU exam simplification)
- $G$ for quartz = 2.65; for typical mineral soils = 2.65–2.75
- For organic soils, $G$ may be as low as 1.5–2.0

### 🎯 Problem-Solving Strategy

1. **List the given data** with symbols
2. **Identify the unknown** and select the matching formula
3. **Convert all percentages to decimals** before substitution
4. **Verify dimensional consistency** (γ in kN/m³)
5. **Cross-check using the ordering principle**

### 🎯 Connection to Future Modules

- Module 2 (Permeability) uses $\gamma_w$ in Darcy's law
- Module 3 (Compaction) uses $\gamma_d$ vs. $w$ relationships
- Module 4 (Consolidation) uses $\gamma'$ for effective stress
- Module 5 (Shear Strength) uses unit weights in Mohr-Coulomb analysis
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Density - Numerical Problems. Determination of Water Content by Oven Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relative Density - Numerical Problems. Determination of Water Content by Oven Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Relative Density of Cohesionless Soils

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Relative Density (also called Density Index, $D_r$, or $I_D$) is a measure of the degree of denseness of a cohesionless (granular) soil, expressed as the ratio of the difference between the void ratio of the soil in its loosest state and the natural void ratio, to the difference between the void ratios in the loosest and densest states. It is expressed as a percentage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Relative density is exclusively applicable to **cohesionless soils** (sands and gravels). For cohesive soils (silts and clays), consistency indices (Liquid Limit, Plastic Limit, Shrinkage Limit) are used instead because void ratio alone does not define the engineering behavior of clays.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Crowded Train" Concept

Imagine three train compartments:
- **Compartment A (Loosest State):** People are spread out — lots of empty space between them. Maximum void ratio ($e_{max}$).
- **Compartment B (Densest State):** People are shoulder-to-shoulder, no gaps. Minimum void ratio ($e_{min}$).
- **Compartment C (Natural State):** The actual crowd in your train — somewhere in between.

**Relative density simply tells you: "How close is your natural crowd to the densest possible packing, as a percentage of the total range from loosest to densest?"**

- A value of **0%** means your soil is as loose as it can possibly be.
- A value of **100%** means your soil is as dense as it can possibly be.
- A value of **50%** means it lies exactly in the middle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants and Standard Metrics used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Drying temperature for oven drying method: **$105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$**
> - Drying duration as per IS 2720 (Part 2): **24 hours** (until constant mass is achieved)
> - Specific gravity of soil solids ($G_s$) for typical quartz sand: **2.65 to 2.67**
> - Unit weight of water ($\gamma_w$): **$9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$** or **$1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**

### 1.3 Water Content by Oven Drying Method

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Water content ($w$) is defined as the ratio of the mass of water present in the voids of a soil mass to the mass of the oven-dried soil solids, expressed as a percentage. The **Oven Drying Method** (IS 2720: Part 2 – 1973, Reaffirmed 2010) is the standard laboratory method to determine water content, where a wet soil sample is dried in a thermostatically controlled oven at **$105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$** until a constant mass is achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$?**
> At this temperature range, all the **free water** and most **adsorbed water** are driven off, but the **structural water** within clay mineral lattices is **NOT** lost. This ensures we measure only the "free" water that affects soil behavior.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge in the Sun" Concept

Think of a wet sponge (the soil). The sponge itself is the **solids (soil grains)**, and the water trapped inside is the **pore water**.
- If you place the sponge in the sun (the oven at $105^\circ C$–$110^\circ C$), the water evaporates.
- The water that drips out / evaporates = **Mass of water ($M_w$)**.
- The dry, shriveled sponge left behind = **Mass of solids ($M_s$)**.
- Water content = **"How heavy was the water compared to the dry sponge?"**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Relative Density — Why and How

**Why Relative Density?**
For cohesionless soils (sands), the engineering properties such as:
- Shear strength
- Compressibility
- Bearing capacity
- Liquefaction potential
- Permeability

...are all **strongly dependent on the packing density** of the grains, NOT on the consistency (as in clays). Two sands with the same void ratio but different grain shapes can behave very differently. Relative density normalizes this and gives a single index value.

**The Core Logic in Bulleted Steps:**

- **Step 1:** Find the natural void ratio ($e$) of the soil in-situ using undisturbed sampling or correlations with SPT N-values.
- **Step 2:** Determine the loosest state void ratio ($e_{max}$) by pouring the dry soil gently into a mold (loose state).
- **Step 3:** Determine the densest state void ratio ($e_{min}$) by vibrating or tamping the soil densely in layers in a mold.
- **Step 4:** Apply the formula:

$$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \quad (\text{in } \%)$$

- **Step 5:** Classify the packing using the standard table.

**Classification of Packing Based on Relative Density (KTU Board Standard):**

| Relative Density $D_r$ (%) | State of Packing |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0 - 15$ | Very Loose |
| $15 - 35$ | Loose |
| $35 - 65$ | Medium Dense |
| $65 - 85$ | Dense |
| $85 - 100$ | Very Dense |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In the design of foundations on sandy soils, liquefaction analysis (especially in Kerala's seismic Zone III areas), and the design of earth dams with sand cores, **Relative Density is a direct input parameter**. A relative density below 50% on a foundation sand indicates a potentially problematic deposit requiring ground improvement (compaction, vibroflotation, stone columns, etc.).

### 2.2 Water Content by Oven Drying — Why and How

**Why Oven Drying?**
It is the most **accurate** and **universally accepted** method. Other methods (sand bath, alcohol burning, calcium carbide, TDR, microwave) are quicker but less precise. KTU board questions and IS code both default to oven drying for the official value.

**The Core Logic in Bulleted Steps:**

- **Step 1:** Take a representative wet soil sample. Clean and weigh a non-corrodible container (mass $M_1$).
- **Step 2:** Place the wet soil in the container. Weigh it (mass $M_2$).
- **Step 3:** Place the open container in the oven at **$105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$** for **24 hours** (or until consecutive weighings differ by less than 0.1% of the original mass).
- **Step 4:** Remove the container, cool it in a **desiccator** (to prevent moisture re-absorption), and weigh it (mass $M_3$).
- **Step 5:** Apply the water content formula.

$$w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100 \quad (\text{in } \%)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU STUDENTS:** The following table uses `\vert` instead of `\` for absolute values to preserve markdown table syntax. Always use the vertical bar operator with spaces, like $\vert x \vert$, in your written exams too.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | Boundary / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100$ | Relative Density (by void ratio) | Dimensionless (%) | $0 \le D_r \le 100$; cohesionless soils only |
| 2 | $D_r = \dfrac{\gamma_{d(max)} (\gamma_d - \gamma_{d(min)})}{\gamma_d (\gamma_{d(max)} - \gamma_{d(min)})} \times 100$ | Relative Density (by dry unit weight) | Dimensionless (%) | Used when dry unit weights are measured directly |
| 3 | $D_r = \dfrac{1/\gamma_{d(min)} - 1/\gamma_d}{1/\gamma_{d(min)} - 1/\gamma_{d(max)}}$ | Relative Density (alternate form using reciprocal of unit weights) | Dimensionless (%) | Algebraic rearrangement of Eq. 1 |
| 4 | $e = \dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | Void ratio from dry unit weight | Dimensionless | Valid for any soil |
| 5 | $w = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100$ | Water content (Oven Drying Method) | % | Mass in grams (g); IS 2720 Part 2 |
| 6 | $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ | Phase relation: $S$ = degree of saturation | Dimensionless | Fundamental relation; $S \le 1$ |
| 7 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Dry unit weight from void ratio | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | Used to convert between $e$ and $\gamma_d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Correlation (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> Relative density of sands is correlated to the corrected Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value ($N_{corr}$) using the empirical relation:
>
> $$D_r \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{corr}}{C}}$$
>
> where $C \approx 0.3$ for normally consolidated sands (Terzaghi & Peck). This is **NOT** in the KTU 2024 PCCET402 syllabus for Module 1 numericals but is excellent context for viva.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Density Formula

**Statement to prove:** $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100$

**Derivation:**

Consider a cohesionless soil mass (a sand) with natural void ratio $e$.

- Let $e_{max}$ be the void ratio in the **loosest** state (achieved by pouring dry sand gently into a calibrated mold).
- Let $e_{min}$ be the void ratio in the **densest** state (achieved by tamping/vibrating the same sand in layers in the same mold).
- The natural state lies between these two extremes, so the **available range** of void ratios is: $(e_{max} - e_{min})$.
- The **amount of "looseness" remaining** in the natural state (how far it is from the densest packing) is: $(e - e_{min})$.
- Equivalently, the **amount of "looseness removed"** (how far it is from the loosest state) is: $(e_{max} - e)$.

By definition, relative density is the ratio of "looseness removed" to the "total available looseness":

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{\text{Looseness removed}}{\text{Total available looseness}} \\
&= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
D_r \, (\%) = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100
$$

**Proved.** ∎

**Alternate Derivation Using Dry Unit Weights:**

Starting from the void ratio form, and using $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \\
&= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d(max)}} - 1\right) - \left(\dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1\right)}{\left(\dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d(max)}} - 1\right) - \left(\dfrac{G_s \gamma_w}{\gamma_{d(min)}} - 1\right)} \\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d(max)}} - \dfrac{1}{\gamma_d}}{\dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d(max)}} - \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{d(min)}}} \\
&= \dfrac{\gamma_{d(max)} \cdot \left(\gamma_d - \gamma_{d(min)}\right)}{\gamma_d \cdot \left(\gamma_{d(max)} - \gamma_{d(min)}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by 100 gives the **percentage form** used in the formula sheet (Eq. 2). ∎

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Relative Density (Direct Application)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A sand deposit in Kerala has a natural void ratio of $0.65$. The maximum void ratio (loosest state) of the sand is $0.85$ and the minimum void ratio (densest state) is $0.40$. Calculate the relative density of the sand and comment on its state of packing.

**Given Data:**
- Natural void ratio, $e = 0.65$
- Maximum void ratio, $e_{max} = 0.85$
- Minimum void ratio, $e_{min} = 0.40$

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Write the relative density formula.

$$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \quad (\%) $$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values in the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{0.85 - 0.65}{0.85 - 0.40} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.20}{0.45} \times 100 \\
&= 0.4444 \times 100 \\
&= 44.44 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result using the standard classification table.

| $D_r$ Range | State of Packing |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 15 | Very Loose |
| 15 – 35 | Loose |
| **35 – 65** | **Medium Dense** ← 44.44% lies here |
| 65 – 85 | Dense |
| 85 – 100 | Very Dense |

**Conclusion:** The relative density of the sand is **$44.44\%$**, which classifies the sand as **"Medium Dense"**. The deposit has medium load-bearing capacity and moderate compressibility. **No ground improvement is generally required for light to moderate loads.**

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Relative Density (Using Saturated Unit Weight)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A saturated sand sample has a natural moisture content of $30\%$. The specific gravity of the soil solids is $2.68$. The maximum void ratio and minimum void ratio of the sand are $0.78$ and $0.42$ respectively. Determine the relative density of the sand.

**Given Data:**
- Natural water content, $w = 30\% = 0.30$
- Specific gravity of solids, $G_s = 2.68$
- Maximum void ratio, $e_{max} = 0.78$
- Minimum void ratio, $e_{min} = 0.42$

**Step 1:** For a **saturated** soil, $S = 1$ (degree of saturation). Use the phase relation $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ to find the natural void ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot e &= w \cdot G_s \\
1 \times e &= 0.30 \times 2.68 \\
e &= 0.804
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Check whether the natural void ratio lies within the valid range $[e_{min}, e_{max}]$.

- $e_{min} = 0.42$, $e_{max} = 0.78$
- Calculated $e = 0.804$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $e = 0.804$ is **slightly greater than $e_{max} = 0.78$**! This indicates the soil in its natural state is **looser than the loosest state measured in the lab** — an unusual situation. The natural deposit is in an extremely loose state, possibly metastable, and could be susceptible to **liquefaction** under cyclic loading. We still apply the formula mathematically.

**Step 3:** Apply the relative density formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.78 - 0.804}{0.78 - 0.42} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{-0.024}{0.36} \times 100 \\
&= -6.67 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The relative density is **$-6.67\%$**, which is physically impossible (negative). This means the in-situ void ratio is so high that the sand is essentially in a "loose" metastable structure, suggesting possible sampling disturbance or a freshly deposited alluvial sand that has not yet settled. For all practical purposes, **assume $D_r \approx 0\%$ (Very Loose state)** and recommend ground improvement.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Water Content by Oven Drying

> **Problem Statement:**
> A wet soil sample was taken in a clean, dry, non-corrodible container of mass $250 \text{ g}$. The mass of the container with the wet soil was $1050 \text{ g}$. After oven drying at $105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$ for 24 hours and cooling in a desiccator, the mass of the container with the dry soil was $920 \text{ g}$. Calculate the water content of the soil.

**Given Data:**
- Mass of empty container, $M_1 = 250 \text{ g}$
- Mass of container + wet soil, $M_2 = 1050 \text{ g}$
- Mass of container + oven-dried soil, $M_3 = 920 \text{ g}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the mass of water ($M_w$).

$$M_w = M_2 - M_3 = 1050 - 920 = 130 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of dry soil solids ($M_s$).

$$M_s = M_3 - M_1 = 920 - 250 = 670 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the water content formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{130}{670} \times 100 \\
&= 0.19403 \times 100 \\
&= 19.40 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The water content of the soil is **$w = 19.40\%$**.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 4 — Water Content with Multiple Containers (KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Three wet soil samples were taken in containers and subjected to oven drying at $105^\circ C$ – $110^\circ C$ for 24 hours. The following data were obtained:
>
> | Container No. | Mass of empty container (g) | Mass of container + wet soil (g) | Mass of container + dry soil (g) |
> | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | 1 | 125 | 480 | 425 |
> | 2 | 130 | 510 | 450 |
> | 3 | 120 | 525 | 460 |
>
> Determine the water content of the soil. Comment on the reliability of the test.

**Step 1:** Calculate $M_w$ and $M_s$ for each container.

**Container 1:**
$M_w = 480 - 425 = 55 \text{ g}$ ; $M_s = 425 - 125 = 300 \text{ g}$

**Container 2:**
$M_w = 510 - 450 = 60 \text{ g}$ ; $M_s = 450 - 130 = 320 \text{ g}$

**Container 3:**
$M_w = 525 - 460 = 65 \text{ g}$ ; $M_s = 460 - 120 = 340 \text{ g}$

**Step 2:** Calculate $w$ for each container.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \dfrac{55}{300} \times 100 = 18.33 \text{ \%} \\
w_2 &= \dfrac{60}{320} \times 100 = 18.75 \text{ \%} \\
w_3 &= \dfrac{65}{340} \times 100 = 19.12 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the average water content.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{avg} &= \dfrac{w_1 + w_2 + w_3}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{18.33 + 18.75 + 19.12}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{56.20}{3} \\
&= 18.73 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Check dispersion (KTU expects this).

- Range: $w_{max} - w_{min} = 19.12 - 18.33 = 0.79\%$
- The dispersion is small (within 1%), so the test is **reliable** and the average value $w = 18.73\%$ can be accepted as the representative water content of the soil mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** When the difference between the maximum and minimum water content values exceeds 2%, IS 2720 Part 2 recommends repeating the test with fresh samples. In this problem, the difference is acceptable.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Procedure for Water Content by Oven Drying (IS 2720 Part 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Representative Wet Soil Sample] --> B[Step 1: Clean and Weigh Empty Container as M1]
    B --> C[Step 2: Place Wet Soil in Container and Weigh as M2]
    C --> D[Step 3: Place Open Container in Oven at 105 to 110 degrees Celsius]
    D --> E[Step 4: Dry for 24 Hours Until Constant Mass]
    E --> F[Step 5: Remove Container and Cool in Desiccator]
    F --> G[Step 6: Weigh Container with Dry Soil as M3]
    G --> H[Step 7: Calculate Mw equals M2 minus M3]
    H --> I[Step 8: Calculate Ms equals M3 minus M1]
    I --> J[Step 9: Apply Formula w equals Mw by Ms times 100]
    J --> K[End: Report Water Content w in Percentage]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Cohesionless Soil Sample Available] --> B{Type of Test Performed?}
    B -->|In-Situ Void Ratio e| C[Use Direct Void Ratio Method]
    B -->|In-Situ Dry Unit Weight gamma_d| D[Use Unit Weight Method]
    B -->|Saturation Condition w and Gs| E[Compute e from Phase Relation S dot e equals w dot Gs]
    C --> F[Step 1: Determine emax and emin from Lab Tests]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 2: Apply Relative Density Formula]
    G --> H[Step 3: Compute Dr in Percentage]
    H --> I{Classify the State of Packing}
    I -->|0 to 15 percent| J[Very Loose]
    I -->|15 to 35 percent| K[Loose]
    I -->|35 to 65 percent| L[Medium Dense]
    I -->|65 to 85 percent| M[Dense]
    I -->|85 to 100 percent| N[Very Dense]
    J --> O[End: Recommendation for Ground Improvement]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```
