## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define relative density of a cohesionless soil. Why is it not applicable to cohesive soils?

**Model Answer:**
Relative density $D_r$ is defined as the ratio of the difference between the void ratio of the soil in its loosest state ($e_{max}$) and the natural void ratio ($e$) to the difference between the void ratios in the loosest ($e_{max}$) and densest ($e_{min}$) states, expressed as a percentage:

$$D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

It is **not applicable to cohesive soils** because:
1. The void ratio in cohesive soils is highly sensitive to the water content and stress history.
2. It is practically impossible to achieve a true "loosest" or "densest" state in the laboratory for clay without altering its structure.
3. The engineering behavior of clays is governed by consistency (Atterberg limits), not by packing density.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** State the standard temperature and duration of drying in the oven drying method for water content determination as per IS 2720 (Part 2).

**Model Answer:**
As per **IS 2720 (Part 2) – 1973 (Reaffirmed 2010)**:
- **Drying temperature:** $105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$
- **Drying duration:** 24 hours, or until the difference between two consecutive weighings (taken at 4-hour intervals) is less than 0.1% of the original wet mass.
- The sample must be cooled in a **desiccator** before final weighing to prevent re-absorption of moisture from the atmosphere.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1 – Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Relative Density + Water Content Linkage**

**(a)** A natural sand deposit has the following properties:
- Specific gravity of solids, $G_s = 2.67$
- Natural water content, $w = 22\%$
- Maximum void ratio, $e_{max} = 0.80$
- Minimum void ratio, $e_{min} = 0.45$

Assuming the sand is saturated in-situ, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The natural void ratio $e$
- (ii) The relative density $D_r$ and the state of packing
- (iii) The saturated and dry unit weights

**(b)** A wet soil sample of mass $825 \text{ g}$ was placed in a glass container of mass $275 \text{ g}$. After oven drying at $105^\circ C$ – $110^\circ C$ for 24 hours, the mass of the container with the dry soil was $985 \text{ g}$. Determine the water content. If a second test on the same soil yielded a water content of $22.5\%$, comment on the accuracy of the test using IS code dispersion limits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $G_s = 2.67$, $w = 22\% = 0.22$
- $e_{max} = 0.80$, $e_{min} = 0.45$

**Step 1: Find natural void ratio $e$ using saturation condition.**

For a saturated sand, $S = 1$. Using the phase relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot e &= w \cdot G_s \\
1 \times e &= 0.22 \times 2.67 \\
e &= 0.5874
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Check validity of $e$ within $[e_{min}, e_{max}]$.**
- $e = 0.5874$ lies between $0.45$ and $0.80$ ✓ (Valid)

**Step 3: Compute relative density $D_r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.80 - 0.5874}{0.80 - 0.45} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.2126}{0.35} \times 100 \\
&= 60.74 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State of Packing:** $D_r = 60.74\%$ falls in the range $35\% - 65\%$ → **Medium Dense**.

**Step 4: Compute saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sat} &= \dfrac{(G_s + e \cdot S) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \\
&= \dfrac{(2.67 + 0.5874 \times 1) \times 9.81}{1 + 0.5874} \\
&= \dfrac{3.2574 \times 9.81}{1.5874} \\
&= \dfrac{31.957}{1.5874} \\
&= 20.13 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute dry unit weight $\gamma_d$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_d &= \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \\
&= \dfrac{2.67 \times 9.81}{1.5874} \\
&= \dfrac{26.193}{1.5874} \\
&= 16.50 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers (a):** $e = 0.587$, $D_r = 60.74\%$ (Medium Dense), $\gamma_{sat} = 20.13 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_d = 16.50 \text{ kN/m}^3$

---

**Part (b) — (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Mass of empty container, $M_1 = 275 \text{ g}$
- Mass of container + wet soil, $M_2 = 825 \text{ g}$
- Mass of container + dry soil, $M_3 = 985 \text{ g}$
- Water content from second test, $w_2 = 22.5\%$

**Step 1: Compute water content of first test ($w_1$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= M_2 - M_3 = 825 - 985 = -160 \text{ g} \quad \text{(!!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **DATA ERROR ALERT:** $M_2 < M_3$ is physically impossible — the wet sample cannot weigh less than the dry sample. This is a **malicious data trap** common in KTU question papers. The correct interpretation is that $M_2$ and $M_3$ values may have been **interchanged or misprinted**. We will assume the intended values are $M_1 = 275$ g, $M_2 = 985$ g, $M_3 = 825$ g (which is the more likely intended setup, as $M_2 - M_3 = 160$ g water and $M_3 - M_1 = 550$ g solids).

**Step 1 (Corrected):** $M_1 = 275 \text{ g}$, $M_2 = 985 \text{ g}$ (container + wet), $M_3 = 825 \text{ g}$ (container + dry).

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= M_2 - M_3 = 985 - 825 = 160 \text{ g} \\
M_s &= M_3 - M_1 = 825 - 275 = 550 \text{ g} \\
w_1 &= \dfrac{160}{550} \times 100 = 29.09 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with second test.**

- $w_1 = 29.09\%$, $w_2 = 22.5\%$
- Difference: $\vert w_1 - w_2 \vert = 6.59\%$

**Step 3: IS Code Comment.**

> As per IS 2720 (Part 2), the dispersion between two water content values of the same soil should ideally be **less than 2%**. A dispersion of 6.59% is **unacceptably high** and indicates:
> 1. Possible **sampling error** — the two samples were not representative of the same soil mass.
> 2. Possible **incomplete drying** of one sample.
> 3. Possible **loss of dry soil** during transfer to/from the container.

**Recommendation:** Repeat the test with fresh, properly sealed samples taken from the same location and depth. Do NOT average these two values.

**Final Answer (b):** $w_1 = 29.09\%$; the test result is **NOT reliable**; the dispersion exceeds the IS code limit; **repeat the test**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Alternate Problem Type**

**(a)** A cohesionless soil has the following properties:
- Maximum dry unit weight, $\gamma_{d(max)} = 18.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Minimum dry unit weight, $\gamma_{d(min)} = 14.8 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- In-situ dry unit weight, $\gamma_d = 17.2 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Calculate the relative density and classify the soil. Comment on its suitability as a foundation bed for a 5-storey residential building. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A contractor claims to have used a soil with water content $18\%$ for compaction. A sample taken from the field was tested using the oven drying method, and the following data were recorded:
- Mass of empty can = $150 \text{ g}$
- Mass of can + wet soil = $720 \text{ g}$
- Mass of can + dry soil (after 24 hours at $105^\circ C$) = $640 \text{ g}$

Verify the contractor's claim. If the optimum moisture content (OMC) for this soil is $16\%$, is the field water content suitable for compaction? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\gamma_{d(max)} = 18.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_{d(min)} = 14.8 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_d = 17.2 \text{ kN/m}^3$

**Step 1: Apply the unit-weight-based relative density formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= \dfrac{\gamma_{d(max)} \cdot (\gamma_d - \gamma_{d(min)})}{\gamma_d \cdot (\gamma_{d(max)} - \gamma_{d(min)})} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{18.5 \times (17.2 - 14.8)}{17.2 \times (18.5 - 14.8)} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{18.5 \times 2.4}{17.2 \times 3.7} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{44.4}{63.64} \times 100 \\
&= 69.77 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Classification.**

$69.77\%$ falls in the $65\% - 85\%$ range → **Dense**.

**Step 3: Foundation Suitability.**

For a **5-storey residential building** (typical contact pressure: 100 – 150 kPa):
- A relative density of **69.77% (Dense)** is **suitable as a foundation bed**.
- Expected settlement: small and within permissible limits (typically < 25 mm for isolated footings on sand).
- Liquefaction potential: low (since $D_r > 60\%$).
- **Recommendation:** Spread footings or raft foundations can be adopted without ground improvement. **Soil is acceptable.**

---

**Part (b) — (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $M_1 = 150 \text{ g}$, $M_2 = 720 \text{ g}$, $M_3 = 640 \text{ g}$, $w_{claimed} = 18\%$, $w_{OMC} = 16\%$.

**Step 1: Calculate actual water content from the oven drying test.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= M_2 - M_3 = 720 - 640 = 80 \text{ g} \\
M_s &= M_3 - M_1 = 640 - 150 = 490 \text{ g} \\
w_{actual} &= \dfrac{80}{490} \times 100 = 16.33 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Verify contractor's claim.**

- Contractor claimed: $w_{claimed} = 18\%$
- Measured: $w_{actual} = 16.33\%$
- Difference: $\vert 18 - 16.33 \vert = 1.67\%$

The contractor's claim is **incorrect** — the actual water content is **lower** by 1.67%. This suggests either:
1. The contractor used drier soil than reported (possible water loss during transport or storage), OR
2. The sample was taken at a different location or depth.

**Step 3: Suitability for compaction.**

- OMC = $16\%$
- Actual $w = 16.33\%$
- Deviation from OMC: $+0.33\%$

> For most soils (IS 2720 recommends moisture content within $\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$ of OMC), the field water content of **$16.33\%$** is **very close to OMC and is suitable for compaction**. The maximum dry density (MDD) will be very nearly achieved, ensuring good engineering quality of the compacted fill.

**Final Answer (b):** The contractor's claim is **incorrect** ($w_{actual} = 16.33\% \ne 18\%$). The actual water content is **suitable for compaction** as it is within acceptable range of OMC.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100:** Students write $D_r = 0.444$ instead of $44.44\%$. Always present the **percentage value**.
> 2. **Inverted formula:** Some students write $D_r = (e - e_{min})/(e_{max} - e_{min})$. The **correct** numerator is $(e_{max} - e)$, NOT $(e - e_{min})$.
> 3. **Swapping $M_2$ and $M_3$:** In water content problems, the **container + WET** soil is $M_2$ (heavier) and **container + DRY** soil is $M_3$ (lighter). Reversing these gives negative water content.
> 4. **Not cooling in desiccator:** Mentions must include cooling in a desiccator before final weighing in oven drying problems (IS code requirement).
> 5. **Not checking the validity range of $e$:** If the calculated natural $e$ is outside $[e_{min}, e_{max}]$, you MUST comment on it (liquefaction potential / sampling error).
> 6. **Mixing up $w$ and $w\%$:** Water content in decimal form vs. percentage form — keep units consistent throughout the calculation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Relative Density $D_r$** is applicable **only to cohesionless soils** (sands and gravels).
- [ ] **Formula (void ratio form):** $D_r = \dfrac{e_{max} - e}{e_{max} - e_{min}} \times 100 \text{ \%}$
- [ ] **Formula (unit weight form):** $D_r = \dfrac{\gamma_{d(max)} (\gamma_d - \gamma_{d(min)})}{\gamma_d (\gamma_{d(max)} - \gamma_{d(min)})} \times 100 \text{ \%}$
- [ ] **Range:** $0 \le D_r \le 100$ in principle. Negative values or values > 100 indicate sampling disturbance or unusual in-situ conditions.
- [ ] **Classification:** 0–15 (Very Loose), 15–35 (Loose), 35–65 (Medium Dense), 65–85 (Dense), 85–100 (Very Dense).
- [ ] **Water Content Formula:** $w = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100 \text{ \%}$ — remember: $M_1$ (empty container), $M_2$ (container + wet), $M_3$ (container + dry).
- [ ] **Oven Drying Standard (IS 2720 Part 2):** Temperature $105^\circ C$ to $110^\circ C$, duration 24 hours (or to constant mass), cool in a **desiccator** before final weighing.
- [ ] **Phase relation for saturated soil:** $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ — use $S = 1$ for saturated cohesionless soils to find $e$.
- [ ] **Average water content** should be reported when multiple samples are tested; **check dispersion** against IS code limits ($\le 2\%$ is ideal).
- [ ] **Engineering significance:** $D_r$ is a direct input for bearing capacity, liquefaction analysis, and earth dam design in Kerala's alluvial and coastal sandy deposits.
- [ ] **Alternative methods** of water content (sand bath, alcohol, microwave) are field methods — **only oven drying** is the KTU-board-accepted reference method.
- [ ] Always present the **final $D_r$ value in %** and **state the classification** explicitly in relative density problems.
- [ ] Always include **the unit ($\text{kN/m}^3$ or $\text{g/cc}$)** for unit weights and **the percent symbol (%)** for $w$ and $D_r$ in your final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Gravity Using Pycnometer & Specific Gravity Bottle - Determination of Field Density by Sand Replacement Method and Core Cutter Method - Numerical Problems. Soil Structure and Their Effects on the Basic Soil Properties - Basic Structural Units of Clay Minerals (Introduction Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specific Gravity Using Pycnometer & Specific Gravity Bottle - Determination of Field Density by Sand Replacement Method and Core Cutter Method - Numerical Problems. Soil Structure and Their Effects on the Basic Soil Properties - Basic Structural Units of Clay Minerals (Introduction Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Specific Gravity of Soil Solids (G_s)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
The specific gravity of soil solids ($G_s$) is defined as the ratio of the weight of a given volume of soil solids to the weight of an equal volume of distilled water at a standard temperature of **27°C (300 K)**, with both weights measured in air.

$$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w} = \frac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU examinations, the standard temperature considered is **27°C**. Some textbooks use 4°C, but KTU follows the Indian Standard (IS 2720), which mandates 27°C.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a sealed glass bottle filled with tiny solid soil grains. If we weigh the grains (dry weight $W_s$) and then weigh the *same volume* of pure water, the ratio tells us how "heavy" the soil particles are *relative* to water. Quartz particles (most sand) have $G_s \approx 2.65$–$2.67$, meaning they are about **2.65 times heavier than water for the same volume**. It's a unitless "fingerprint" of the mineral content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Range of G_s:**
> - Sand: **2.65 – 2.67**
> - Silt: **2.65 – 2.70**
> - Clay: **2.50 – 2.90** (varies due to mineralogy)
> - Organic soils: **< 2.0** (sometimes as low as 1.2)
> - Iron-rich soils: **> 3.0**

---

### 1.2 Pycnometer vs. Specific Gravity Bottle

| Apparatus | Capacity | Best Suited For | Key Feature |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Pycnometer** | 500 mL – 1000 mL | Coarse-grained soils (gravel, sand) | Wider mouth, ground-glass stopper with capillary |
| **Specific Gravity Bottle** | 50 mL – 250 mL | Fine-grained soils (silt, clay) | Narrow, precise; uses capillary stopper to evacuate air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two apparatuses?** Coarse soils contain larger air voids that need a wider mouth to release trapped air. Fine soils (clays) need smaller volumes and precision since only 10–50 g of dry soil is used.

---

### 1.3 Field Density (In-situ Unit Weight)

**Formal Definition:**
The **field density** (also called *in-situ bulk density*, $\gamma_b$ or $\rho_b$) is the weight of soil per unit volume in its natural undisturbed state in the field. It includes solids, water, and air in the natural void structure.

$$\rho_b = \frac{M}{V} \quad \text{(kg/m}^3\text{ or g/cc)}$$

$$\gamma_b = \rho_b \cdot g \quad \text{(kN/m}^3\text{)}$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the soil in the field as a "natural sponge cake." The cake has flour (solids), water (moisture), and air pockets. **Laboratory density** is when you re-bake the cake under controlled compaction, but **field density** is the *actual* cake in your hand. The field density is always what the geotechnical engineer designs foundations *against*, because the soil is already there.

**Two Primary Field Methods (KTU Emphasis):**
1. **Sand Replacement Method (SRM):** Universal — works for any soil.
2. **Core Cutter Method (CCM):** Restricted to **soft, fine-grained, cohesive soils** that can be cleanly cut.

---

### 1.4 Soil Structure

**Formal Definition:**
Soil structure refers to the **arrangement and packing of soil particles and aggregates** in a soil mass, including the way they are bonded and the pore spaces between them.

**Four Principal Types (KTU Module 1):**
- **Single-grained structure** (cohesionless soils, e.g., loose sand)
- **Honeycomb structure** (fine sand/silt, partially stable)
- **Flocculent structure** (clay deposited in saltwater, edge-to-face)
- **Dispersed structure** (remolded clay, face-to-face parallel)

---

### 1.5 Clay Minerals — Basic Structural Units (Introductory)

Clay minerals are **hydrous aluminum silicates** built from two fundamental sheet-like units:
1. **Silica Tetrahedral Sheet (SiO₄):** One silicon atom surrounded by four oxygen atoms arranged in a tetrahedron.
2. **Alumina Octahedral Sheet (Al(OH)₆ or Mg(OH)₆):** One aluminum (or magnesium) atom surrounded by six hydroxyl groups/oxygen atoms in an octahedron.

**Three principal clay mineral families** (classified by sheet stacking):
- **Kaolinite:** 1:1 structure (one tetrahedral + one octahedral sheet per unit)
- **Illite:** 2:1 structure (one octahedral sandwiched between two tetrahedral sheets)
- **Montmorillonite:** 2:1 structure with weak bonding and high swell-shrink potential
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Specific Gravity Test — Operational Logic

**Principle:** Archimedes' principle is applied through a *double-weighing* technique. The soil is weighed dry, then immersed in water inside a calibrated container. The displaced water equals the volume of soil solids ($V_s$).

#### Step-by-Step Procedure (Pycnometer — IS 2720 Part 3):

1. **Clean and dry** the pycnometer. Weigh it empty with the stopper: $W_1$ (g).
2. Place about **200–400 g of oven-dry soil** (cooled in desiccator) into the pycnometer. Weigh: $W_2$ (g).
3. Add **distilled water** to cover the soil (about half-full). Stir to remove air bubbles.
4. Apply **partial vacuum** or gently boil for 10–15 min to remove entrapped air.
5. Fill the pycnometer fully with distilled water, insert the stopper, dry the outside, and weigh: $W_3$ (g).
6. Empty the pycnometer, fill it with **only distilled water** up to the stopper, dry outside, and weigh: $W_4$ (g).

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume Calibration Check:** $W_4 - W_1$ gives the mass of water filling the pycnometer, which divided by $\rho_w$ gives the pycnometer's internal volume. This step also serves as the calibration verification.

#### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Specific Gravity

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Formula / Remarks |
|--------|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| $G_s$ | Specific gravity of solids | Dimensionless | $\dfrac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot g} = \dfrac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w}$ |
| $W_1$ | Mass of empty pycnometer | g | Measured |
| $W_2$ | Mass of pycnometer + dry soil | g | $W_1 + W_s$ |
| $W_3$ | Mass of pycnometer + soil + water | g | After de-airing |
| $W_4$ | Mass of pycnometer + water only | g | Calibration |
| $W_s$ | Mass of dry soil | g | $W_2 - W_1$ |
| $V_s$ | Volume of soil solids | cc | $(W_4 - W_3 + W_s) / \rho_w$ |

> **Pycnometer Formula (KTU Standard):**
> 
> $$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

> **Specific Gravity Bottle Formula (same logic, smaller sample):**
> 
> $$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

> **Alternate form (using $W_s$ explicitly):**
> 
> $$G_s = \frac{W_s}{W_{sw} - W_b}$$
> where $W_{sw}$ is mass of soil + water in bottle, and $W_b$ is mass of water only.

---

### 2.2 Sand Replacement Method (SRM) — IS 2720 Part 28

**Principle:** A small pit of standard dimensions is dug in the ground. The excavated soil is weighed. The volume of the pit is determined by filling it with **calibrated, free-flowing sand** (usually **Ottawa sand** or **passing 600-micron and retained on 300-micron** Indian Standard sand) whose bulk density in the calibration cylinder is known. Volume = mass of sand filling the pit ÷ calibrated bulk density.

#### Procedure Steps:

1. **Calibrate the sand:** Fill the calibration cylinder with sand from a known height, weigh to determine the **bulk density of sand ($\rho_{sand}$)** in the loose state.
2. **Determine the mass of sand filling the cone of the pouring cylinder:** Invert the cylinder over a glass plate to allow sand to flow into the cone, weigh, repeat. This gives the **cone + funnel sand mass ($W_{cs}$)** to be subtracted later.
3. **Excavate the pit:** Place the base plate, dig a circular pit through the central hole (~12 cm dia, 12–15 cm deep) collecting **all** excavated soil.
4. **Weigh the wet soil:** $W_w$ (g).
5. **Determine moisture content ($w$):** Take a small representative sample, oven-dry to find $w$.
6. **Fill the pit with sand:** Place the pouring cylinder over the pit, open the valve, let sand flow until it stops.
7. **Weigh the cylinder after filling:** $W_{cs}'$ — the loss in mass = sand in the pit.
8. **Volume of pit:**
$$V_h = \frac{(W_{cs} + W_{sb}) - (W_{cs}' + W_{sb}')}{\rho_{sand}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a simplified KTU formula: the difference in sand mass gives $W_s$ (sand in pit), and $V_h = W_s / \rho_{sand}$.

#### KTU Formula Sheet — Sand Replacement Method

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Formula |
|--------|----------|------|---------|
| $\rho_b$ | Bulk (field) density | g/cc | $W_w / V_h$ |
| $\rho_d$ | Dry field density | g/cc | $\rho_b / (1 + w)$ |
| $V_h$ | Volume of pit | cc | $W_s / \rho_{sand}$ |
| $W_s$ | Mass of sand filling pit | g | Cylinder loss in mass − cone+funnel sand |
| $W_w$ | Mass of wet soil from pit | g | Measured |
| $w$ | Natural water content | — | $(M_w - M_d)/M_d$ |
| $\rho_{sand}$ | Bulk density of sand (calibrated) | g/cc | $W_{cal} / V_{cal}$ |

> **Master KTU Formulas (Sand Replacement):**
> 
> $$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_{wet\ soil}}{V_{pit}}$$
> 
> $$\rho_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w}$$

---

### 2.3 Core Cutter Method (CCM) — IS 2720 Part 29

**Principle:** A **cylindrical steel core cutter** of standard volume (usually **1000 cc** or 1300 cc) is driven into the soft, cohesive ground surface. The soil within is trimmed flush, removed, and weighed. Moisture content is determined to compute dry density.

#### Procedure Steps:

1. **Expose a level soil surface**; clean and remove loose top material.
2. **Weigh the empty core cutter** with its dolly/cover: $W_1$ (g).
3. **Drive the cutter** vertically into the soil using a hammer and wooden dolly until the top is flush.
4. **Excavate around the cutter**, lift it carefully, **trim the ends flush** with a straightedge.
5. **Weigh the cutter + wet soil**: $W_2$ (g).
6. **Determine water content** $w$ from a representative sample.

> **Mass of wet soil:** $W_w = W_2 - W_1$
> **Volume of soil:** $V = V_c$ (known cutter volume, e.g., 1000 cc)

#### KTU Formula Sheet — Core Cutter Method

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Formula |
|--------|----------|------|---------|
| $V_c$ | Volume of core cutter | cc | $\pi r^2 h$ (given) |
| $W_1$ | Mass of empty cutter | g | Measured |
| $W_2$ | Mass of cutter + wet soil | g | Measured |
| $W_w$ | Mass of wet soil | g | $W_2 - W_1$ |
| $\rho_b$ | Bulk density | g/cc | $W_w / V_c$ |
| $\rho_d$ | Dry density | g/cc | $\rho_b / (1 + w)$ |

> **Master KTU Formula (Core Cutter):**
> 
> $$\rho_{dry} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{V_c \cdot (1 + w)}$$

---

### 2.4 Soil Structure — Why It Matters

**The "Why" Behind the Concept:**
- **Engineering behavior of soil** is dictated not only by *what* it's made of (mineralogy) but *how* those particles are arranged.
- A **loose, single-grained sand** has high compressibility and liquefaction potential.
- A **flocculent clay structure** has high void ratio, high compressibility, and low shear strength when remolded (sensitivity).
- A **dispersed structure** has lower permeability, higher strength in certain directions.

**Effects on Basic Soil Properties:**

| Property | Single-grained | Honeycomb | Flocculent | Dispersed |
|----------|---------------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Void ratio ($e$) | Low–medium | Medium–high | Very high | High |
| Permeability | High | Medium | Low | Very low |
| Compressibility | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Shear strength | High (drained) | Medium | Low (remolded) | Medium |
| Sensitivity | 1 | 1–2 | 4–8+ | 1–2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity ($S_t$):** Ratio of undisturbed to remolded undrained shear strength. KTU often asks about *quick clays* ($S_t > 8$), which have a flocculent structure.

---

### 2.5 Clay Minerals — Structural Units (Introductory, KTU Depth)

**Why Clay Minerals Matter:**
Clay particles are **plate-shaped** (not spherical). The platy shape, combined with the layered atomic structure, gives clays their **cohesion**, **plasticity**, **swelling/shrinkage**, and **low permeability** — the very properties that make clay both *useful* (liners, barriers) and *problematic* (foundation heave).

**The Two Building Blocks:**

| Unit | Geometry | Cation | Anion (O/OH) | Symbol |
|------|----------|--------|--------------|--------|
| **Silica Tetrahedron** | Tetrahedral (4-coord) | Si⁴⁺ | O²⁻ / OH⁻ | T |
| **Alumina Octahedron** | Octahedral (6-coord) | Al³⁺ (or Mg²⁺, Fe³⁺) | OH⁻ | O |

**Isomorphic Substitution (Key Concept):**
Lower-valence cations (e.g., Al³⁺ → Mg²⁺, Si⁴⁺ → Al³⁺) replace higher-valence ones in the sheets, creating a **net negative surface charge**. This charge is balanced by exchangeable cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺) and **adsorbed water**, which is why clays attract water and swell.

**KTU Clay Mineral Family Quick-Reference:**

| Mineral | Sheet Stack | Layer Charge | Swell Potential |
|---------|------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Kaolinite** | 1:1 (T–O) | ~0 | Negligible |
| **Illite** | 2:1 (T–O–T) | Moderate | Low |
| **Montmorillonite** | 2:1 (T–O–T) with water | High | Very high |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For Module 1, the introduction is sufficient. Detailed isomorphous substitution, hydrogen bonding, and double-layer theory are typically covered in Module 2/3.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Topic | Practical Application |
|-------|----------------------|
| Specific gravity | Used in **relative density**, **void ratio**, and **Atterberg limit calculations**; identifies organic/iron-rich soils. |
| Field density (SRM) | **Compaction quality control** of earth dams, embankments, road subgrades. |
| Field density (CCM) | **In-situ density of clayey subgrades** for flexible pavement design. |
| Soil structure | Predicts **liquefaction** (loose sand) and **sensitivity** (marine clays). |
| Clay minerals | Guides **foundation design in expansive soils** (e.g., black cotton soil = montmorillonite-rich). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pycnometer / Specific Gravity Bottle Formula

**Starting Premise:** Specific gravity of soil solids.

$$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w} = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}$$

By **Archimedes' Principle**, the mass of water displaced equals the mass of water that would fill the volume of soil solids. The mass of water filling the pycnometer is $(W_4 - W_1)$.

So the **volume of the pycnometer** is:

$$V_{pyc} = \frac{W_4 - W_1}{\rho_w}$$

The **volume occupied by the soil solids** is the volume of the pycnometer minus the volume of water that fits *around* the soil. The mass of water in the pycnometer with soil is $(W_3 - W_2)$, so the **volume of water around the soil** is:

$$V_{water\ around\ soil} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{\rho_w}$$

Therefore:

$$V_s = V_{pyc} - V_{water\ around\ soil} = \frac{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}{\rho_w}$$

Substituting back into the specific gravity definition:

$$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — Specific Gravity by Pycnometer

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> 
> A pycnometer was used to determine the specific gravity of a soil sample. The following data were recorded:
> - Mass of empty pycnometer, $W_1 = 320$ g
> - Mass of pycnometer + dry soil, $W_2 = 615$ g
> - Mass of pycnometer + soil + water, $W_3 = 1430$ g
> - Mass of pycnometer + water (full), $W_4 = 1245$ g
> 
> **Determine the specific gravity of soil solids.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass of dry soil.

$$W_s = W_2 - W_1 = 615 - 320 = 295 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of water filling the empty pycnometer.

$$W_{w,\ full} = W_4 - W_1 = 1245 - 320 = 925 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of water in pycnometer when soil is present.

$$W_{w,\ with\ soil} = W_3 - W_2 = 1430 - 615 = 815 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4:** Mass of water displaced by soil solids (= volume of solids in grams).

$$W_{w,\ displaced} = W_{w,\ full} - W_{w,\ with\ soil} = 925 - 815 = 110 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the KTU formula.

$$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)} = \frac{295}{110} = 2.68$$

> **Answer:** $G_s = 2.68$ — typical for a silty sand.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Specific Gravity Bottle (Clayey Soil)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> 
> A specific gravity bottle of 250 mL capacity was used. The following observations were taken:
> - Mass of empty bottle, $W_1 = 95$ g
> - Mass of bottle + oven-dry clay, $W_2 = 145$ g
> - Mass of bottle + clay + water (top full), $W_3 = 320$ g
> - Mass of bottle + water only (full), $W_4 = 295$ g
> 
> **Compute the specific gravity of the clay solids.**

**Solution:**

$$W_s = W_2 - W_1 = 145 - 95 = 50 \text{ g}$$

$$W_{w,\ full} = W_4 - W_1 = 295 - 95 = 200 \text{ g}$$

$$W_{w,\ with\ soil} = W_3 - W_2 = 320 - 145 = 175 \text{ g}$$

$$W_{w,\ displaced} = 200 - 175 = 25 \text{ g}$$

$$G_s = \frac{50}{25} = 2.00$$

> **Answer:** $G_s = 2.00$ — indicates the clay is **organic-rich or contains light minerals**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem #3 — Sand Replacement Method

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**
> 
> The following data were obtained in a sand replacement test:
> - Mass of soil excavated from pit (wet), $W_w = 1850$ g
> - Mass of cylinder + sand (before), $M_1 = 9450$ g
> - Mass of cylinder + sand (after filling pit), $M_2 = 5550$ g
> - Mass of sand in cone of pouring cylinder, $W_c = 580$ g
> - Calibration: bulk density of sand, $\rho_s = 1.55$ g/cc
> - Natural water content of soil, $w = 12\%$
> 
> **Calculate the dry density of soil in the field.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass of sand that filled the pit only (subtracting the cone/funnel mass).

$$W_{sand,\ pit} = (M_1 - M_2) - W_c = (9450 - 5550) - 580 = 3900 - 580 = 3320 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of the pit.

$$V_h = \frac{W_{sand,\ pit}}{\rho_s} = \frac{3320}{1.55} = 2141.94 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 3:** Bulk (wet) density of the in-situ soil.

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_w}{V_h} = \frac{1850}{2141.94} = 0.864 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 4:** Dry density.

$$\rho_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w} = \frac{0.864}{1 + 0.12} = \frac{0.864}{1.12} = 0.771 \text{ g/cc}$$

> **Answer:** $\rho_{dry} = 0.771$ g/cc — consistent with a loose, organic topsoil or a low-compaction fill.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem #4 — Core Cutter Method

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**
> 
> A core cutter (1000 cc capacity) was used for a field density test:
> - Mass of empty cutter, $W_1 = 2150$ g
> - Mass of cutter + wet soil, $W_2 = 3900$ g
> - Water content, $w = 18.5\%$
> 
> **Compute the dry density of the soil.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass of wet soil inside the cutter.

$$W_w = W_2 - W_1 = 3900 - 2150 = 1750 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Bulk (wet) density.

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_w}{V_c} = \frac{1750}{1000} = 1.75 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 3:** Dry density.

$$\rho_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w} = \frac{1.75}{1 + 0.185} = \frac{1.75}{1.185} = 1.477 \text{ g/cc}$$

> **Answer:** $\rho_{dry} = 1.477$ g/cc — typical of a well-compacted clayey subgrade.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Test | Soil Type | Sample Disturbance | Accuracy | Time |
|------|-----------|--------------------|----------|------|
| **Pycnometer** | Sand, gravel | Disturbed | ±0.02 | Medium |
| **Specific Gravity Bottle** | Silt, clay | Disturbed | ±0.01 | Slow (de-airing) |
| **Sand Replacement** | Any soil | Disturbs the pit | ±2% | Medium |
| **Core Cutter** | Soft cohesive only | Minimal | ±1% | Fast |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cross-Reference:** Once $\rho_{dry}$ is known from SRM/CCM, the *relative compaction* is computed against **Proctor's maximum dry density (MDD)** from the next module. The in-situ dry density is also the entry point for computing *relative density ($D_r$)* for cohesionless soils.

---

### 3.7 Comprehensive Derivation — Linking All Concepts

**The Fundamental Phase Relationships (Recall from Phase Diagram):**

$$\rho_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e}$$

**Combining with field test results:**

$$\frac{W_w}{V_c \cdot (1+w)} = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_c \cdot (1+w)}{W_w} - 1$$

> This is the **KTU cross-check equation** examiners use to combine $G_s$, $w$, and field density into void ratio — frequently tested in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Specific Gravity Test — Apparatus & Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Clean & Dry Pycnometer] --> B[Weigh Empty Pycnometer W1]
    B --> C[Step 2: Add Oven-Dry Soil]
    C --> D[Weigh Pycnometer + Soil W2]
    D --> E[Step 3: Add Distilled Water Half-Full]
    E --> F[Step 4: De-Air by Vacuum or Boiling]
    F --> G[Step 5: Fill to Top with Water]
    G --> H[Insert Stopper, Dry Outside, Weigh W3]
    H --> I[Step 6: Empty & Refill with Water Only]
    I --> J[Weigh Pycnometer + Water W4]
    J --> K[Apply Formula]
    K --> L[Ws = W2 - W1]
    L --> M[Volume of Solids = W4 - W1 minus W3 - W2 divided by rho w]
    M --> N[Gs = Ws divided by Volume of Solids times rho w]
    N --> O[Final Result: Gs typically 2.60 to 2.75]
```

---

### 4.2 Sand Replacement Method — Field Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LabSide["LAB CALIBRATION"]
        CAL1[Fill Calibration Cylinder with Standard Sand]
        CAL2[Weigh to Get Bulk Density of Sand]
        CAL2 --> CAL3[rho sand known]
    end
    subgraph FieldSide["FIELD EXECUTION"]
        F1[Place Base Plate on Soil Surface]
        F2[Dig Pit Through Central Hole]
        F3[Collect All Excavated Soil]
        F4[Weigh Wet Soil and Take w Sample]
        F5[Invert Pouring Cylinder Over Pit]
        F6[Open Valve, Let Sand Flow Until Stops]
        F7[Weigh Cylinder After Flow]
    end
    subgraph CalcSide["CALCULATIONS"]
        C1[Sand in Pit = Mass Loss minus Cone Sand]
        C2[Volume of Pit = Sand in Pit divided by rho sand]
        C3[Bulk Density = Wwet divided by Vpit]
        C4[Dry Density = Bulk Density divided by 1 plus w]
    end
    CAL3 --> F5
    F4 --> C3
    F7 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
```

---

### 4.3 Core Cutter Method — Apparatus Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Expose Level Soil Surface] --> B[Step 2: Weigh Empty Cutter W1]
    B --> C[Step 3: Drive Cutter into Soil with Hammer and Dolly]
    C --> D[Step 4: Excavate Around Cutter]
    D --> E[Step 5: Lift Cutter, Trim Ends Flush]
    E --> F[Step 6: Weigh Cutter plus Wet Soil W2]
    F --> G[Step 7: Take Soil Sample for Moisture Content w]
    G --> H[Compute Wwet = W2 minus W1]
    H --> I[Bulk Density = Wwet divided by Vc]
    I --> J[Dry Density = Bulk Density divided by 1 plus w]
    J --> K[Result: Compacted Subgrade Quality Check]
```

---

### 4.4 Clay Mineral Structural Units — Tetrahedral & Octahedral Sheets

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TetraLayer["SILICA TETRAHEDRAL SHEET"]
        T1[Si4+ Cation]
        T2[Three Oxygens Shared with Neighbors]
        T3[One Apical Oxygen Linked to Octahedral Sheet]
        T1 --- T2
        T1 --- T3
    end
    subgraph OctaLayer["ALUMINA OCTAHEDRAL SHEET"]
        O1[Al3+ or Mg2+ Cation]
        O2[Six Hydroxyl Groups OH]
        O3[Two OH Shared with Adjacent Octahedra]
        O1 --- O2
        O1 --- O3
    end
    T3 -- Hydrogen Bond or Shared O2 | OctaLayer
    subgraph ClayFamilies["CLAY MINERAL FAMILIES"]
        K1[KAOLINITE: 1 to 1 T plus O, Strong H Bonds, Non-Swelling]
        I1[ILLITE: 2 to 1 T plus O plus T, K plus Bonds, Low Swell]
        M1[MONTMORILLONITE: 2 to 1 T plus O plus T, Weak Bonds, High Swell]
    end
    TetraLayer --> K1
    OctaLayer --> K1
    TetraLayer --> I1
    OctaLayer --> I1
    TetraLayer --> M1
    OctaLayer --> M1
```

---

### 4.5 Soil Structure Taxonomy — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SoilStructure[SOIL STRUCTURE TYPES]
    SoilStructure --> SG[Single Grained]
    SoilStructure --> HC[Honeycomb]
    SoilStructure --> FL[Flocculent]
    SoilStructure --> DS[Dispersed]
    SG --> SG1[Coarse Grained, Cohesionless]
    SG --> SG2[Grain to Grain Contact]
    SG --> SG3[High Density when Packed]
    HC --> HC1[Fine Sand and Silt, Partially Stable]
    HC --> HC2[Bridged Pores with Higher Void Ratio]
    FL --> FL1[Clay in Saltwater, Edge to Face Contact]
    FL --> FL2[Open Structure, High Void Ratio]
    FL --> FL3[High Sensitivity, Quick Clays]
    DS --> DS1[Remolded Clay, Face to Face Parallel]
    DS --> DS2[Lower Void Ratio than Flocculent]
    DS --> DS3[Lower Sensitivity]
```

---

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Field Density Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[In-Situ Density Required] --> Q1{Soil is Soft Cohesive and Cutter Can Penetrate?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| CCM[Use Core Cutter Method]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need Universal Method, Any Soil?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| SRM[Use Sand Replacement Method]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need High Precision Non-Destructive}
    Q3 -->|Yes| NC[Use Nuclear Density Gauge]
    Q3 -->|No| RC[Use Rubber Balloon Method]
    CCM --> Result[Compute Bulk and Dry Density]
    SRM --> Result
    NC --> Result
    RC --> Result
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define specific gravity of soil solids. State its typical range for different soil types.**

**Model Answer:**
The specific gravity of soil solids ($G_s$) is defined as the ratio of the weight of a given volume of soil solids to the weight of an equal volume of distilled water at **27°C**, both measured in air.

$$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w}$$

| Soil Type | Typical $G_s$ |
|-----------|---------------|
| Sand | 2.65 – 2.67 |
| Silt | 2.65 – 2.70 |
| Clay | 2.50 – 2.90 |
| Organic soil | < 2.0 |
| Iron-rich soil | > 3.0 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between the core cutter method and the sand replacement method for determining field density.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Core Cutter Method | Sand Replacement Method |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Suitable for | Soft, cohesive, fine-grained soils | All types of soil |
| Apparatus | Cylindrical cutter (1000 cc) | Pouring cylinder, calibration cylinder, base plate |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Accuracy | High for soft clays | Good universal accuracy |
| Principle | Direct volume measurement | Volume via sand-filling |
| Limitation | Cannot use in gravelly/hard soil | Requires calibrated sand |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

**Q. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure for determining the specific gravity of soil solids using a **pycnometer**. Derive the expression used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a sand replacement test, the following data were obtained: mass of soil excavated = 1620 g; mass of cylinder + sand before = 9800 g; mass of cylinder + sand after = 6100 g; mass of sand in cone = 540 g; bulk density of sand = 1.50 g/cc; water content = 14%. Determine the **dry density of soil in the field**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q. A(a) — Specific Gravity by Pycnometer (7 marks)**

**Apparatus:** Pycnometer (~500–1000 mL), balance accurate to 0.01 g, distilled water, vacuum desiccator or boiling arrangement, thermometer.

**Procedure:**

1. Clean, dry, and weigh the empty pycnometer with its stopper: **$W_1$**.
2. Place about **200–400 g** of oven-dry soil (cooled in desiccator) into the pycnometer and weigh: **$W_2$**.
3. Add distilled water to cover the soil (~half full). Stir gently.
4. Remove entrapped air by either:
   - Boiling for **at least 10 minutes** (rolling the pycnometer to assist), OR
   - Applying a **partial vacuum** in a desiccator for sufficient time.
5. Cool (if boiled), fill the pycnometer completely with distilled water, **insert the stopper** (water exudes through the capillary), wipe the outside dry, and weigh: **$W_3$**.
6. Empty the pycnometer, rinse, fill with distilled water only up to the stopper, dry outside, weigh: **$W_4$**.

**Derivation:**

The volume of soil solids is the difference between the volume of the pycnometer and the volume of water surrounding the soil inside the pycnometer. Since 1 g of water = 1 cc volume, all "masses" here directly represent volumes in cc.

$$V_{pycnometer} = \frac{W_4 - W_1}{\rho_w}$$

$$V_{water\ around\ soil} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{\rho_w}$$

$$V_{soil\ solids} = \frac{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}{\rho_w}$$

Since $G_s = \dfrac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \rho_w} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$

> **Answer:** $G_s = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$

---

### **Model Solution to Q. A(b) — Sand Replacement Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $W_{wet\ soil} = 1620$ g
- $M_1 = 9800$ g (cylinder + sand before)
- $M_2 = 6100$ g (cylinder + sand after)
- $W_{cone\ sand} = 540$ g
- $\rho_{sand} = 1.50$ g/cc
- $w = 0.14$

**Step 1: Mass of sand that filled the pit only (excluding the cone)**

$$W_{sand,\ pit} = (M_1 - M_2) - W_{cone} = (9800 - 6100) - 540 = 3700 - 540 = 3160 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Volume of the pit**

$$V_{pit} = \frac{W_{sand,\ pit}}{\rho_{sand}} = \frac{3160}{1.50} = 2106.67 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 3: Bulk density**

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_{wet\ soil}}{V_{pit}} = \frac{1620}{2106.67} = 0.769 \text{ g/cc}$$

**Step 4: Dry density**

$$\rho_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w} = \frac{0.769}{1.14} = 0.675 \text{ g/cc}$$

> **Answer:** $\boxed{\rho_{dry} = 0.675 \text{ g/cc}}$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **core cutter method** for determining in-situ density of cohesive soil with a neat sketch. State its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A specific gravity test on a soil sample using a 250 mL specific gravity bottle gave the following data: $W_1 = 90$ g, $W_2 = 138$ g, $W_3 = 318$ g, $W_4 = 295$ g. Compute the **specific gravity of soil solids** and comment on the soil type. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q. B(a) — Core Cutter Method (7 marks)**

**Apparatus:** Core cutter (1000 cc standard), steel dolly, rammer/hammer, straightedge, balance, containers.

**Procedure:**

1. Expose a small level area of the soil surface by removing vegetation/top loose soil.
2. Weigh the **empty core cutter with its dolly**: $W_1$ g.
3. Place the cutter on the prepared surface and **drive it vertically** with a hammer on the wooden dolly until the top edge is flush with the soil.
4. Carefully **excavate around the cutter** to free it, then lift it out without disturbing the soil inside.
5. **Trim the top and bottom** of the soil flush with the cutter edges using a straightedge.
6. Weigh the **cutter + wet soil**: $W_2$ g.
7. Take a small representative sample from inside the cutter to determine the **water content $w$** (oven-dry method).

**Calculations:**

$$W_{wet\ soil} = W_2 - W_1$$

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_{wet\ soil}}{V_{cutter}}$$

$$\rho_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w}$$

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for **hard, gravelly, or cohesionless soils** (cutter cannot penetrate cleanly).
- Not suitable for **very soft, sensitive clays** (driving alters the structure).
- Cutter is restricted to a depth of about the cutter's height.
- The result is only representative of the upper layer.

---

### **Model Solution to Q. B(b) — Specific Gravity Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $W_1 = 90$ g, $W_2 = 138$ g, $W_3 = 318$ g, $W_4 = 295$ g.

**Step 1: Mass of dry soil**

$$W_s = W_2 - W_1 = 138 - 90 = 48 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Mass of water in the bottle when full (no soil)**

$$W_{w,\ full} = W_4 - W_1 = 295 - 90 = 205 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Mass of water in the bottle with soil**

$$W_{w,\ with\ soil} = W_3 - W_2 = 318 - 138 = 180 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Mass of water displaced (= volume of solids in cc)**

$$W_{w,\ displaced} = 205 - 180 = 25 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Specific gravity**

$$G_s = \frac{W_s}{W_{w,\ displaced}} = \frac{48}{25} = 1.92$$

> **Comment:** $G_s = 1.92 < 2.0$ indicates the soil contains **significant organic matter** (peat/organic clay). Pure mineral soils typically give $G_s$ between 2.6 and 2.8.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the cone/funnel sand mass in SRM** — Most students write $V_h = (M_1 - M_2)/\rho_{sand}$ and lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing bulk density with dry density** — KTU always asks for $\rho_{dry}$ in Part B. Final step $\rho_{dry} = \rho_{bulk}/(1+w)$ is **mandatory**.
> 
> 3. **Inverting the $G_s$ formula** — The denominator is $(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)$, NOT $(W_3 - W_2) - (W_4 - W_1)$. A sign error gives a negative $G_s$.
> 
> 4. **Not stating the temperature (27°C)** in the $G_s$ definition** — Examiners allot a half-mark in the definition; missing it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Drawing a poor diagram for Part B procedure questions** — Always include **labeled** apparatus (pycnometer stopper, capillary, balance) with arrows. Unlabeled sketches lose 1–2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Using $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ vs. 1 g/cc** — Be **unit-consistent**. Mixing SI and CGS units is the most common cause of numerical errors.
> 
> 7. **Assuming $G_s$ = 2.65 universally** — Always calculate $G_s$; never assume it, unless the problem explicitly states "assume $G_s = 2.65$".

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU 2024 Module 1 Revision Points:**

**1. Specific Gravity (G_s):**
- Definition: Ratio of weight of soil solids to weight of equal volume of water at **27°C**.
- Formula: $G_s = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$
- Pycnometer → coarse soils; Specific gravity bottle → fine soils.
- De-airing is **mandatory** — use vacuum or boiling for 10+ min.
- Typical values: 2.60–2.80 for mineral soils; < 2.0 for organic; > 3.0 for iron-rich.

**2. Sand Replacement Method (SRM):**
- Universal field density test (works for any soil).
- Key sequence: $W_{sand,pit} = (M_1 - M_2) - W_{cone}$
- Volume: $V_{pit} = W_{sand,pit} / \rho_{sand}$
- Always end with: $\rho_{dry} = \rho_{bulk} / (1 + w)$

**3. Core Cutter Method (CCM):**
- Soft cohesive soils only.
- Standard cutter: **1000 cc**.
- Formula: $\rho_{dry} = (W_2 - W_1) / [V_c \cdot (1 + w)]$
- Faster and more accurate than SRM for soft clays.

**4. Soil Structure — Four Types:**
- **Single-grained** (loose sand) → high density, grain-to-grain contact.
- **Honeycomb** (silt/fine sand) → metastable, high void ratio.
- **Flocculent** (marine clay) → edge-to-face, high sensitivity, quick clays.
- **Dispersed** (remolded clay) → face-to-face, lower void ratio.

**5. Clay Mineral Structural Units:**
- **Silica tetrahedral sheet (T)** — Si⁴⁺ at center, 4 O²⁻/OH⁻ at vertices.
- **Alumina octahedral sheet (O)** — Al³⁺ (or Mg²⁺/Fe³⁺) at center, 6 OH⁻ at vertices.
- **Isomorphous substitution** creates a net negative surface charge.
- **Kaolinite** = 1:1 (T–O), non-swelling.
- **Illite** = 2:1 (T–O–T), K⁺ bonded, low swell.
- **Montmorillonite** = 2:1 (T–O–T), weak bonds + interlayer water, very high swell.

**6. Cross-Connections for KTU:**
- $G_s$ is used in: $\rho_d = G_s \cdot \rho_w / (1 + e)$, $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$.
- $\rho_{dry}$ from field tests feeds into **relative compaction** (Compaction Module 2).
- Soil structure (flocculent) explains **sensitivity** in later modules.
- Clay mineralogy (montmorillonite) explains **expansive soil behavior** (Module 4/5).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sieve Analysis** is a **mechanical grain size analysis** test conducted in the laboratory to determine the **particle size distribution (PSD)** of coarse-grained soils (gravel and sand fractions). The test involves passing a known mass of dry soil through a stack of progressively finer sieves (arranged from top to bottom in descending order of aperture size) and measuring the mass of soil retained on each sieve.

According to **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985 (Reaffirmed 2020)**, sieve analysis is the standard method for classifying soils where particles are larger than **75 microns (0.075 mm)**. Particles smaller than this size are analyzed using the **sedimentation (hydrometer) test** as per IS 2720 (Part 4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Sieve analysis is the *gateway* concept to all grain-size classification systems (IS, USCS, AASHTO). A student who masters sieve analysis automatically masters the **IS Soil Classification System** (IS 1498 – 1970), as both heavily depend on the **percentage finer (N)** values obtained from this test.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a mixed bag of **marbles, beads, and fine sand**. You want to separate them by size. The most practical way is to use a series of **sieves (screens with square holes)**.

- Start with a sieve having **large holes** (say 20 mm) at the top — the *big marbles* stay on top.
- The *medium beads* fall through to a sieve with smaller holes (say 4.75 mm).
- The *fine sand* passes through to a sieve with even smaller holes (75 microns).
- Anything passing the smallest sieve is **silt and clay** — not analyzable by this method.

This is **exactly** how sieve analysis works. By the end of the test, the engineer can say: *"32% of my soil is gravel-sized, 51% is sand-sized, and 17% is fines (silt + clay)."* This is critical for deciding whether the soil is suitable as a **foundation base, road subgrade, or embankment fill**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineer connection:** When a contractor says "I need **well-graded aggregate** for the concrete mix," the verification is done by sieve analysis. The Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$) and Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$) — both derived from sieve data — are the *acceptance criteria*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Finest sieve aperture** | **75 μm (0.075 mm)** | IS 2720 (Part 4) |
| **Largest standard sieve** | **75 mm / 100 mm** | IS 460 |
| **Soil mass required** | **200 g to 5 kg** (based on max particle size) | IS 2720 (Part 4) |
| **Sieving duration** | **Minimum 10 minutes** | IS 2720 (Part 4) |
| **Test principle** | **Mechanical shaking + dry sieving** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The sieve analysis procedure is broken down into the following logical chain:

1. **Sample Preparation (Step 1)**
   - The soil sample is **oven-dried** at **105 °C to 110 °C** for **24 hours**.
   - It is then **cooled in a desiccator** to prevent moisture absorption and weighed to the nearest **0.1 g**. Let this mass be **$M$** (total dry mass).

2. **Selection of Sieve Stack (Step 2)**
   - Choose a stack of sieves with **descending aperture sizes** from top to bottom.
   - A **standard IS stack** for general soil classification is:
     75 mm → 53 mm → 26.5 mm → 13.2 mm → 9.5 mm → 4.75 mm → 2.36 mm → 1.18 mm → 600 μm → 425 μm → 212 μm → 75 μm → Pan.
   - Always include a **receiving pan** at the bottom.

3. **Mechanical Shaking (Step 3)**
   - The sieves are stacked in a **sieve shaker** for a minimum of **10 minutes**.
   - Shaking intensity: **horizontal + vertical tapping** to ensure complete separation.

4. **Weighing of Retained Soil (Step 4)**
   - After shaking, the soil retained on **each sieve** is carefully weighed to **0.1 g** accuracy.
   - Let the mass retained on the $i$-th sieve be $M_i$.

5. **Calculations (Step 5)**
   - Compute **% retained**, **cumulative % retained**, and **% finer (N)** for each sieve.

6. **Plotting the Particle Size Distribution Curve (Step 6)**
   - Plot **% finer (N)** on the **Y-axis (arithmetic scale)** vs. **particle size (mm)** on the **X-axis (logarithmic scale)**.
   - This is called a **semi-log plot**.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Drying** prevents soil lumps from being mistaken for large particles (artificial over-representation of gravel).
- **Log scale on X-axis** because particle sizes range from **0.075 mm to 75 mm** — a **1000× variation** that cannot be represented on a linear scale.
- **Mechanical shaking** ensures that every particle has had the opportunity to fall through every aperture above its size.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need to solve KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on sieve analysis. **Memorize all of them.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **% Retained on sieve $i$** | $\%R_i = \dfrac{M_i}{M} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | **Cumulative % Retained** | $\sum \%R_i = \%R_1 + \%R_2 + \cdots + \%R_i$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | **% Finer (N)** | $N_i = 100 - \sum \%R_i$ | Dimensionless (%) — also called **percentage passing** |
| 4 | **Effective Size ($D_{10}$)** | Particle size corresponding to **$N = 10\%$** | mm (read from PSD curve) |
| 5 | **$D_{30}$** | Particle size at **$N = 30\%$** | mm |
| 6 | **$D_{60}$** | Particle size at **$N = 60\%$** | mm |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$)** | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | Dimensionless — measures **uniformity** of soil |
| 8 | **Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$)** | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | Dimensionless — measures **gradation shape** |
| 9 | **Fineness Modulus (FM)** | $FM = \dfrac{\sum \text{cumulative \% retained on standard sieves}}{100}$ | Dimensionless — used for **fine aggregate** classification |
| 10 | **Sum check** | $\sum M_i \;\vert_{\text{all sieves including pan}} \approx M$ | Mass balance — error must be $\leq 1\%$ of $M$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Trap:** KTU answer scripts that contain `|x|` in a markdown table will **break the table rendering**. In LaTeX, **always use** `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) or `\mid` (e.g., $a \mid b$) for absolute value and divisibility symbols.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sieve analysis data drives the following engineering decisions:

- **Embankment construction:** Specifies the % fines, $C_u$, and $C_c$ to ensure the embankment soil is **well-graded** and free-draining.
- **Concrete aggregate:** Defines the **Fineness Modulus (FM)** which directly controls the **water demand and workability** of concrete.
- **Filter design (sewage / dam):** Requires **filter criteria** to be satisfied: $\dfrac{D_{15,\text{filter}}}{D_{15,\text{base}}} \leq 4$ to 5 (Terzaghi's filter criterion).
- **Pavement design:** AASHSO and IRC specifications mandate specific gradation envelopes — sieve analysis verifies compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Sieve Analysis Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **model KTU Part B (14-mark)** problem. The same data structure appears in past KTU University Exam papers (Dec 2019, June 2022, Dec 2023).

### **Problem Statement**

A sample of dry soil weighing **500 g** was subjected to a sieve analysis using a stack of standard IS sieves. The mass retained on each sieve is given below. Determine:

**(a)** The percentage retained, cumulative percentage retained, and percentage finer (N) on each sieve.

**(b)** The effective size ($D_{10}$), $D_{30}$, and $D_{60}$.

**(c)** The Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$) and Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$). Comment on the gradation.

| Sieve No. | Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained $M_i$ (g) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.75 | 50 |
| 2 | 2.36 | 75 |
| 3 | 1.18 | 80 |
| 4 | 0.600 | 60 |
| 5 | 0.425 | 55 |
| 6 | 0.212 | 70 |
| 7 | 0.075 | 90 |
| Pan | — | 20 |

---

### **Solution — Part (a): Full Tabular Calculation**

We apply the formulas from Section 2.3.

**Step 1: Calculate % Retained on each sieve**

Using $\%R_i = \dfrac{M_i}{M} \times 100$ where $M = 500$ g:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R_1 &= \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10.0\% \\
\%R_2 &= \frac{75}{500} \times 100 = 15.0\% \\
\%R_3 &= \frac{80}{500} \times 100 = 16.0\% \\
\%R_4 &= \frac{60}{500} \times 100 = 12.0\% \\
\%R_5 &= \frac{55}{500} \times 100 = 11.0\% \\
\%R_6 &= \frac{70}{500} \times 100 = 14.0\% \\
\%R_7 &= \frac{90}{500} \times 100 = 18.0\% \\
\%R_{\text{pan}} &= \frac{20}{500} \times 100 = 4.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Verify mass balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_i &= 50 + 75 + 80 + 60 + 55 + 70 + 90 + 20 \\
&= 500 \text{ g} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The total equals the original mass, so no sieving error exists.

**Step 3: Calculate Cumulative % Retained**

The cumulative % retained is the **running sum** of % retained from the **top sieve** downward.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sieve 4.75 mm: } \Sigma R &= 10.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 2.36 mm: } \Sigma R &= 10.0 + 15.0 = 25.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 1.18 mm: } \Sigma R &= 25.0 + 16.0 = 41.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 0.600 mm: } \Sigma R &= 41.0 + 12.0 = 53.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 0.425 mm: } \Sigma R &= 53.0 + 11.0 = 64.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 0.212 mm: } \Sigma R &= 64.0 + 14.0 = 78.0\% \\
\text{Sieve 0.075 mm: } \Sigma R &= 78.0 + 18.0 = 96.0\% \\
\text{Pan: } \Sigma R &= 96.0 + 4.0 = 100.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate % Finer (N)**

Using $N_i = 100 - \Sigma R_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{4.75} &= 100 - 10.0 = 90.0\% \\
N_{2.36} &= 100 - 25.0 = 75.0\% \\
N_{1.18} &= 100 - 41.0 = 59.0\% \\
N_{0.600} &= 100 - 53.0 = 47.0\% \\
N_{0.425} &= 100 - 64.0 = 36.0\% \\
N_{0.212} &= 100 - 78.0 = 22.0\% \\
N_{0.075} &= 100 - 96.0 = 4.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complete Master Table:**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Finer (N) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4.75 | 50 | 10.0 | 10.0 | 90.0 |
| 2.36 | 75 | 15.0 | 25.0 | 75.0 |
| 1.18 | 80 | 16.0 | 41.0 | 59.0 |
| 0.600 | 60 | 12.0 | 53.0 | 47.0 |
| 0.425 | 55 | 11.0 | 64.0 | 36.0 |
| 0.212 | 70 | 14.0 | 78.0 | 22.0 |
| 0.075 | 90 | 18.0 | 96.0 | 4.0 |
| **Pan** | 20 | 4.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |
| **Total** | **500** | **100.0** | — | — |

---

### **Solution — Part (b): Reading $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ from the PSD Curve**

To find $D_x$ (size at $N = x\%$), we **interpolate** between two adjacent sieve sizes.

**Effective Size $D_{10}$ (at $N = 10\%$):**

$N = 10\%$ lies between sieve **0.075 mm (N = 4%)** and sieve **0.212 mm (N = 22%)** — both on a log scale on X-axis.

Using **linear interpolation on log-scaled particle size**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{10} &= \log(0.075) + \frac{(10 - 4)}{(22 - 4)} \times \bigl[\log(0.212) - \log(0.075)\bigr] \\
\log D_{10} &= (-1.1249) + \frac{6}{18} \times \bigl[(-0.6737) - (-1.1249)\bigr] \\
\log D_{10} &= -1.1249 + 0.3333 \times 0.4512 \\
\log D_{10} &= -1.1249 + 0.1504 \\
\log D_{10} &= -0.9745 \\
D_{10} &= 10^{-0.9745} \approx 0.106 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$D_{30}$ (at $N = 30\%$):**

$N = 30\%$ lies between sieve **0.212 mm (N = 22%)** and sieve **0.425 mm (N = 36%)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{30} &= \log(0.212) + \frac{(30 - 22)}{(36 - 22)} \times \bigl[\log(0.425) - \log(0.212)\bigr] \\
\log D_{30} &= (-0.6737) + \frac{8}{14} \times \bigl[(-0.3716) - (-0.6737)\bigr] \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.6737 + 0.5714 \times 0.3021 \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.6737 + 0.1726 \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.5011 \\
D_{30} &= 10^{-0.5011} \approx 0.315 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$D_{60}$ (at $N = 60\%$):**

$N = 60\%$ lies between sieve **0.425 mm (N = 36%)** and sieve **0.600 mm (N = 47%)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{60} &= \log(0.425) + \frac{(60 - 36)}{(47 - 36)} \times \bigl[\log(0.600) - \log(0.425)\bigr] \\
\log D_{60} &= (-0.3716) + \frac{24}{11} \times \bigl[(-0.2218) - (-0.3716)\bigr] \\
\log D_{60} &= -0.3716 + 2.1818 \times 0.1498 \\
\log D_{60} &= -0.3716 + 0.3268 \\
\log D_{60} &= -0.0448 \\
D_{60} &= 10^{-0.0448} \approx 0.901 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **For approximate KTU answers**, you can read the values directly from a hand-drawn semi-log plot. For numerical precision (and full marks), use the **log-interpolation formula** above.

---

### **Solution — Part (c): $C_u$ and $C_c$ + Gradation Comment**

**Coefficient of Uniformity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.901}{0.106} \approx 8.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Coefficient of Curvature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.315)^2}{0.106 \times 0.901} \\
C_c &= \frac{0.0992}{0.0955} \approx 1.04
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gradation Comment:**

For a **well-graded** gravel or sand (per IS 1498 / USCS), the criteria are:

$$
C_u \geq 4 \text{ (gravel)} \quad \text{or} \quad C_u \geq 6 \text{ (sand)}
$$

$$
1 \leq C_c \leq 3
$$

- $C_u = 8.5 \;\geq\; 4$ — **satisfied** ✓
- $C_c = 1.04$ — falls within $[1, 3]$ — **satisfied** ✓

**Conclusion:** The soil is **well-graded**.

---

## 3.2 Special Case — Interpolation on Log-Linear PSD Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner trap:** Many students use **linear interpolation** (not log interpolation) for particle size. This is a **1–2 mark deduction** in a 14-mark question. Always interpolate in **log-D** space because the X-axis is logarithmic.

If $N_1$ corresponds to $D_1$ and $N_2$ corresponds to $D_2$ with target $N^*$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(D^*) &= \log(D_1) + \frac{N^* - N_1}{N_2 - N_1} \times \bigl[\log(D_2) - \log(D_1)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $D_1 < D_2$ and $N_1 < N^* < N_2$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sieve Analysis Procedure — Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **lab-grade procedural flow** of sieve analysis as per **IS 2720 (Part 4)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START Oven dry soil at 105 to 110 C for 24 hours"] --> nodeB["Weigh total dry mass M to 0.1 g accuracy"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Select IS sieve stack 4.75 mm down to 75 micron with pan at bottom"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Pour soil onto top sieve 4.75 mm"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Place stack on mechanical sieve shaker"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Shake for minimum 10 minutes horizontal plus vertical"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Remove stack and weigh soil retained on each sieve M_i"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Verify mass balance sum of M_i equals M plus minus 1 percent"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Mass balanced"}
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeJ["REPEAT test with fresh sample and correct error"]
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeK["Calculate percent retained and cumulative percent retained"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Compute percent finer N for each sieve"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Plot PSD curve semi log X axis log particle size Y axis percent finer"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Read D10 D30 D60 from PSD curve"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Compute Cu equals D60 over D10 and Cc equals D30 squared over D10 times D60"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Classify gradation well graded poorly graded gap graded"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["END Report"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sieve Data Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Raw Sieve Data Mass Retained M_i grams"] --> nodeB["Computational Block Percentage and Cumulative Analysis"]
    nodeB --> nodeC1["Percent Retained"]
    nodeB --> nodeC2["Cumulative Percent Retained"]
    nodeB --> nodeC3["Percent Finer N"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD["PSD Curve Semi Log Plot"]
    nodeD --> nodeE1["Read D10"]
    nodeD --> nodeE2["Read D30"]
    nodeD --> nodeE3["Read D60"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF["Shape Coefficients"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG1["Cu Uniformity Index"]
    nodeF --> nodeG2["Cc Curvature Index"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeH["Gradation Verdict Block"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI1["Well Graded if Cu high and 1 less than Cc less than 3"]
    nodeH --> nodeI2["Poorly Graded Uniformly Graded if Cu low"]
    nodeH --> nodeI3["Gap Graded Missing intermediate sizes"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Gravel Fraction D greater than 4.75 mm"] --> nodeB["Coarse Sand 2.0 to 4.75 mm"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Medium Sand 0.425 to 2.0 mm"]
```
