# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

Define the following terms with reference to sieve analysis:
**(a)** Effective size ($D_{10}$)
**(b)** Uniformity coefficient ($C_u$)
**(c)** Coefficient of curvature ($C_c$)

### **Model Answer**

**(a) Effective Size ($D_{10}$):** The particle size on the grain-size distribution curve corresponding to **10% finer (N = 10%)**. It is denoted as $D_{10}$ and is used to characterize the **finest 10%** of the soil. It is highly significant in **seepage and drainage** calculations (e.g., permeability via Allen Hazen formula: $k \propto D_{10}^2$).

**(b) Uniformity Coefficient ($C_u$):** It is the ratio of $D_{60}$ to $D_{10}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **high $C_u$** (e.g., $> 4$ to $6$) indicates that the soil has a **wide range of particle sizes** and is likely well-graded. A **low $C_u$** (close to $1$) indicates a **uniformly graded** (poorly graded) soil.

**(c) Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$):** It is a measure of the **shape** of the gradation curve between $D_{10}$ and $D_{60}$, and is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **well-graded soil**, $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$. If $C_c$ falls outside this range, the soil is either **uniformly graded** or **gap-graded**.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

List any **six standard IS sieve sizes** used in the sieve analysis of soils as per **IS 2720 (Part 4)**. Why is the **75-micron sieve** the smallest standard sieve?

### **Model Answer**

**Six Standard IS Sieve Sizes (largest to smallest):**

1. **4.75 mm** (coarse gravel)
2. **2.36 mm** (fine gravel)
3. **1.18 mm** (very coarse sand)
4. **0.600 mm** (coarse sand)
5. **0.425 mm** (medium sand)
6. **0.212 mm** (fine sand)
7. **0.075 mm** (fine sand / coarse silt boundary)

**Why 75-micron is the smallest standard sieve:**

- Particles smaller than **75 μm** tend to **agglomerate (clump together)** due to surface tension and electrostatic forces, leading to **erroneous retention** on the sieve.
- Sieving becomes physically impractical below this size — particles clog the apertures.
- Below 75 μm, the analysis is performed using the **sedimentation (hydrometer) test** based on **Stokes' Law**, which is more accurate for fine particles.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often ask this **3-mark "theory" question** as a balancing counter to numerical questions. Make sure to mention **Stokes' Law** in the answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two options. Both options are provided below with **full worked model solutions**.

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the procedure for **sieve analysis** of a soil sample as per **IS 2720 (Part 4)**. Mention the **IS standard sieves** used, the **minimum quantity of soil** required, and the **duration of sieving**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The results of a sieve analysis performed on **1000 g** of dry soil are given below. Determine the **percentage finer (N)** on each sieve and plot the **grain size distribution curve**. From the curve, determine $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, and $D_{60}$. Also compute $C_u$ and $C_c$ and comment on the gradation. **(7 marks)**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|
| 4.75 | 100 |
| 2.36 | 150 |
| 1.18 | 175 |
| 0.600 | 140 |
| 0.425 | 110 |
| 0.212 | 130 |
| 0.075 | 145 |
| Pan | 50 |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sample Preparation (2 marks):**
- The representative soil sample is **oven-dried at 105 to 110 °C for 24 hours**.
- It is then **cooled in a desiccator** to avoid moisture regain and weighed to the **nearest 0.1 g**.

**Step 2 — Quantity of Soil (2 marks):**
The minimum mass of soil required depends on the **maximum particle size** in the sample, as per IS 2720 (Part 4):

| Maximum Particle Size (mm) | Minimum Soil Mass (g) |
|---|---|
| 4.75 | 200 |
| 9.5 | 500 |
| 13.2 | 1000 |
| 26.5 | 2000 |
| 50 | 5000 |

**Step 3 — Sieve Stack and Sieving (2 marks):**
- Sieves are arranged in **descending aperture size** from top to bottom.
- Standard IS stack: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 μm, 425 μm, 212 μm, 75 μm, Pan**.
- The stack is placed in a **mechanical sieve shaker** and shaken for a **minimum of 10 minutes**.

**Step 4 — Weighing and Reporting (1 mark):**
- The soil retained on each sieve is weighed to the nearest **0.1 g**.
- The total retained mass must equal the original mass within **±1%** — otherwise the test is **invalid** and must be repeated.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: % Retained, Cumulative % Retained, % Finer (3 marks)**

Total mass $M = 1000$ g. Apply the formulas from Section 2.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R_1 &= \frac{100}{1000} \times 100 = 10.0\% \\
\%R_2 &= \frac{150}{1000} \times 100 = 15.0\% \\
\%R_3 &= \frac{175}{1000} \times 100 = 17.5\% \\
\%R_4 &= \frac{140}{1000} \times 100 = 14.0\% \\
\%R_5 &= \frac{110}{1000} \times 100 = 11.0\% \\
\%R_6 &= \frac{130}{1000} \times 100 = 13.0\% \\
\%R_7 &= \frac{145}{1000} \times 100 = 14.5\% \\
\%R_{\text{pan}} &= \frac{50}{1000} \times 100 = 5.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \%R_i &= 10.0 + 15.0 + 17.5 + 14.0 + 11.0 + 13.0 + 14.5 + 5.0 = 100.0\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Finer (N) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4.75 | 100 | 10.0 | 10.0 | 90.0 |
| 2.36 | 150 | 15.0 | 25.0 | 75.0 |
| 1.18 | 175 | 17.5 | 42.5 | 57.5 |
| 0.600 | 140 | 14.0 | 56.5 | 43.5 |
| 0.425 | 110 | 11.0 | 67.5 | 32.5 |
| 0.212 | 130 | 13.0 | 80.5 | 19.5 |
| 0.075 | 145 | 14.5 | 95.0 | 5.0 |
| Pan | 50 | 5.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 2: Read $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ from the PSD curve (2 marks)**

**For $D_{10}$ (at $N = 10\%$):** Lies between 0.075 mm ($N = 5\%$) and 0.212 mm ($N = 19.5\%$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{10} &= \log(0.075) + \frac{(10 - 5)}{(19.5 - 5)} \times \bigl[\log(0.212) - \log(0.075)\bigr] \\
\log D_{10} &= -1.1249 + \frac{5}{14.5} \times 0.4512 \\
\log D_{10} &= -1.1249 + 0.1556 \\
\log D_{10} &= -0.9693 \\
D_{10} &\approx 0.107 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $D_{30}$ (at $N = 30\%$):** Lies between 0.212 mm ($N = 19.5\%$) and 0.425 mm ($N = 32.5\%$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{30} &= \log(0.212) + \frac{(30 - 19.5)}{(32.5 - 19.5)} \times \bigl[\log(0.425) - \log(0.212)\bigr] \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.6737 + \frac{10.5}{13} \times 0.3021 \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.6737 + 0.2440 \\
\log D_{30} &= -0.4297 \\
D_{30} &\approx 0.372 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $D_{60}$ (at $N = 60\%$):** Lies between 0.600 mm ($N = 43.5\%$) and 1.18 mm ($N = 57.5\%$) and 2.36 mm ($N = 75\%$).

$N = 60\%$ lies between **1.18 mm ($N = 57.5\%$)** and **2.36 mm ($N = 75\%$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log D_{60} &= \log(1.18) + \frac{(60 - 57.5)}{(75 - 57.5)} \times \bigl[\log(2.36) - \log(1.18)\bigr] \\
\log D_{60} &= 0.0719 + \frac{2.5}{17.5} \times 0.3010 \\
\log D_{60} &= 0.0719 + 0.0430 \\
\log D_{60} &= 0.1149 \\
D_{60} &\approx 1.304 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$ (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{1.304}{0.107} \approx 12.19 \\
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.372)^2}{0.107 \times 1.304} = \frac{0.1384}{0.1395} \approx 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Gradation Comment (1 mark)**

- $C_u = 12.19 \;\geq\; 4$ — **satisfied** ✓ (so the soil has a wide range of sizes)
- $C_c = 0.99$ — **slightly below 1** — **NOT satisfied** ✗

Since $C_c$ is just below 1, the soil is **poorly graded to gap-graded** (specifically, it tends towards gap-graded behaviour, indicating that some intermediate particle sizes are underrepresented). However, since $C_u$ is high, it is **not uniformly graded** either.

**Conclusion:** The soil is **gap-graded** (or borderline poorly-graded).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **well-graded**, **poorly-graded (uniformly graded)**, and **gap-graded** soils. How are they identified from the **shape of the PSD curve**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coarse-grained soil has the following grain size data: $D_{10} = 0.18$ mm, $D_{30} = 0.45$ mm, $D_{60} = 1.20$ mm.
   (i) Calculate the **Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$)** and **Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$)**.
   (ii) Classify the soil per **IS 1498 – 1970**.
   (iii) If the **% finer than 75 μm** is **8%**, what is the **IS group symbol**? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Conceptual Classification (7 marks)**

**1. Well-Graded Soil (3 marks):**

A well-graded soil has a **good representation of all particle sizes** — from coarse to fine — within a wide range. It is identified by:

- $C_u$ is **high** (for gravels: $C_u \geq 4$; for sands: $C_u \geq 6$).
- $C_c$ lies between **1 and 3** ($1 \leq C_c \leq 3$).
- The PSD curve has a **smooth, gradual S-shape** with no flat portions.

**2. Poorly-Graded / Uniformly-Graded Soil (2 marks):**

A poorly-graded (uniformly graded) soil has particles of **nearly the same size**. It is identified by:

- $C_u$ is **low** ($C_u < 4$ for gravels; $C_u < 6$ for sands).
- The PSD curve is **very steep** — almost vertical, indicating a narrow size range.

**3. Gap-Graded Soil (2 marks):**

A gap-graded soil is missing one or more **intermediate size fractions**. It is identified by:

- $C_c$ lies **outside the range** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ (either $< 1$ or $> 3$).
- The PSD curve has a **flat (horizontal) plateau** in the missing size range.

**PSD Curve Shape Summary:**

| Soil Type | $C_u$ | $C_c$ | PSD Curve Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| Well-graded | High | 1 to 3 | Smooth S-curve |
| Uniformly-graded | Low (close to 1) | Any | Steep / vertical |
| Gap-graded | High or low | < 1 or > 3 | Flat plateau |

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Classification (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D_{10} = 0.18$ mm
- $D_{30} = 0.45$ mm
- $D_{60} = 1.20$ mm
- % finer than 75 μm = 8%

**(i) Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{1.20}{0.18} = 6.67 \\
C_c &= \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.45)^2}{0.18 \times 1.20} = \frac{0.2025}{0.216} \approx 0.94
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Gradation per IS 1498 (2 marks):**

For a **sand** (since $D_{60} = 1.20$ mm falls within the sand range, i.e., $0.075$ mm to $4.75$ mm):

- Well-graded sand criteria: $C_u \geq 6$ **AND** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
- $C_u = 6.67 \;\geq\; 6$ — **satisfied** ✓
- $C_c = 0.94$ — **slightly below 1** — **not satisfied** ✗

**Conclusion:** The soil is **poorly graded sand (SP)**.

**(iii) IS Group Symbol (2 marks):**

Since **% finer than 75 μm = 8%** (which is **between 5% and 12%**), the soil is given a **dual symbol** in IS 1498:

$$
\text{IS Group Symbol} = \boxed{\text{SP-SM}}
$$

- **SP** = Poorly graded sand
- **SM** = Silty sand
- The **dash notation** indicates that the fines content is between 5% and 12%, requiring dual classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 1498 rule for dual symbol:**
> - If % fines $< 5\%$: single symbol based on gradation.
> - If 5% $\leq$ % fines $\leq$ 12\%: **dual symbol** (e.g., SP-SM, SW-SC).
> - If % fines $> 12\%$: single symbol based on fines type.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Evaluation:**
>
> 1. **Using linear interpolation instead of log-interpolation** for $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$. (1 to 2 marks deduction)
> 2. **Forgetting the mass balance check** (sum of % retained must = 100%). Showing this explicitly gives **bonus clarity**.
> 3. **Wrong unit for $D$ values** — always in **mm**, not cm or m.
> 4. **Not plotting the PSD curve** — even a rough sketch is mandatory for full marks in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on gradation** after computing $C_u$ and $C_c$. Always state whether the soil is well-graded, poorly graded, or gap-graded.
> 6. **Confusing effective size with $D_{30}$ or $D_{60}$** — $D_{10}$ is the *effective size*, not $D_{30}$ or $D_{60}$.
> 7. **Ignoring the pan** — the pan content is the **% finer than 75 μm** (fines content), which is critical for IS classification.
> 8. **Mixing up $C_u$ formula** — it is $D_{60}/D_{10}$, NOT $D_{10}/D_{60}$. ($C_c$ has $D_{30}$ in the numerator squared.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Sieve analysis** determines the **particle size distribution (PSD)** of **coarse-grained** soils (gravel + sand).
- [x] **75 μm** is the **lower limit** of sieve analysis; below this, hydrometer analysis (Stokes' Law) is used.
- [x] **PSD curve:** Y-axis = % finer (N) on **arithmetic scale**; X-axis = particle size on **logarithmic scale**.
- [x] Three key parameters read from the curve: $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$.
- [x] **Effective size $D_{10}$:** particle size at $N = 10\%$. Used in **permeability** and **filter design**.
- [x] **$C_u = D_{60}/D_{10}$** — measures **uniformity**. Higher $C_u$ = better-graded.
- [x] **$C_c = D_{30}^2 / (D_{10} \cdot D_{60})$** — measures **gradation shape**. Well-graded requires $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
- [x] **Well-graded criteria (IS):** Gravel: $C_u \geq 4$, $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$. Sand: $C_u \geq 6$, $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
- [x] **% finer than 75 μm = 5% to 12%** triggers a **dual symbol** in IS 1498 classification.
- [x] Always **interpolate in log-D space**, not linearly.
- [x] **Mass balance check:** $\sum M_i \approx M \pm 1\%$.
- [x] **Standard IS sieve stack:** 4.75 → 2.36 → 1.18 → 0.600 → 0.425 → 0.212 → 0.075 mm.
- [x] **Fineness Modulus (FM)** = $\sum$ cumulative % retained / 100 — used for **fine aggregate (concrete sand)**.
- [x] **Gap-graded soils** have **flat plateaus** in the PSD curve (missing intermediate sizes).
- [x] **Uniformly-graded soils** have **steep** PSD curves (narrow size range).
- [x] **Sieve shaker duration:** minimum **10 minutes** as per IS 2720 (Part 4).
- [x] **Sample size:** depends on max particle size (e.g., 200 g for max 4.75 mm; 5 kg for 50 mm).

topics.title: Hydrometer Analysis - Stokes Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrometer Analysis - Stokes Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrometer Analysis** is a sedimentation-based laboratory test used in geotechnical engineering to determine the particle-size distribution of fine-grained soils (i.e., silts and clays with particle diameter **D < 75 μm**, and especially **D < 0.075 mm** passing the 75-μ IS sieve). The method is governed fundamentally by **Stokes' Law (1851)**, which mathematically describes the **terminal settling velocity** of a small sphere falling under gravity through a viscous fluid medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stokes' Law (as adopted in KTU PCCET402 Module 2):**
> For a rigid, smooth, spherical particle settling at terminal velocity in a viscous, incompressible fluid of infinite extent under laminar flow conditions, the resisting viscous drag force equals the net effective gravitational (submerged) weight of the particle.

Mathematically, Stokes' Law states:

$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot D^2}{18 \mu}$$

where:
- $v$ = terminal settling velocity (m/s)
- $\gamma_s$ = unit weight of soil solids (N/m³)
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water at the test temperature (N/m³)
- $D$ = equivalent diameter of the soil particle (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (N·s/m²)

In a hydrometer test, this law is **inverted** to compute the maximum equivalent particle diameter $D$ that has settled out of suspension at the hydrometer's depth of measurement after elapsed time $t$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu}{(G_s - 1)\,\gamma_w} \cdot \frac{H}{t}}$$

where $H$ is the effective depth corresponding to the hydrometer bulb position, and $G_s$ is the specific gravity of soil solids.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Aquarium Race":**
> Imagine pouring mixed sand, sugar, and tiny flour particles into a tall, clear glass of water. Within seconds, the heavy sand grains plummet straight to the bottom. The medium-sized sugar grains drift down more slowly over minutes. The microscopic flour particles stay suspended for hours, making the water look cloudy. This natural **separation by falling speed** is exactly what hydrometer analysis exploits.
>
> - Heavier (larger) particles = **fast** fallers → exit the suspension zone early.
> - Lighter (smaller) particles = **slow** fallers → still floating in the suspension zone.
>
> The hydrometer (a calibrated floating glass tube with a graduated stem) measures the **density (specific gravity)** of the soil-water suspension at a fixed depth. Higher density = more soil still in suspension = finer particles remain. By recording readings at prescribed time intervals, we mathematically back-calculate the size of the largest particle that has fallen past the bulb.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physical Constants to Remember (Bolded for Exam Recall):**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water at **27 °C (300 K): μ ≈ 8.5 × 10⁻⁴ N·s/m²** (or 8.5 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s)
> - Unit weight of water at 27 °C: **γw ≈ 9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as 9.81 kN/m³ for board numericals)
> - Specific gravity of soil solids (typical): **Gs = 2.65 to 2.70** (default **2.65** unless stated)
> - Standard specific gravity of water: **Gw = 1.00**
> - Valid particle size range for Stokes' Law: **0.0002 mm < D < 0.2 mm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The "Why" Behind Stokes' Law

When a soil particle is dropped into still water, three forces act upon it:

1. **Downward gravitational force (weight):** $W = \gamma_s \cdot V$ where $V$ is the particle volume.
2. **Upward buoyant force (Archimedes' principle):** $F_b = \gamma_w \cdot V$
3. **Viscous drag force (resistance from fluid):** $F_d = 3\pi \mu D v$ (Stokes' drag for laminar flow past a sphere).

Initially, acceleration occurs. Very quickly, the drag force grows until it balances the net submerged weight, after which the particle falls at a **constant terminal velocity** $v$. Setting forces equal at terminal velocity:

$$\text{Net Submerged Weight} = \text{Viscous Drag}$$

## 2.2 Assumptions Built Into Stokes' Law (Must Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Assumptions of Stokes' Law:**
> 1. Soil particles are **perfectly spherical, smooth, and rigid** (actual particles are not — we use an *equivalent diameter*).
> 2. The settling medium (water) is **infinite and quiescent** — i.e., no container wall effects.
> 3. The settling velocity is sufficiently small to maintain **laminar flow** (Reynolds number **Re ≤ 1**).
> 4. Particles settle **independently** — i.e., no particle–particle interaction (concentration must be low; usually **< 50 g/L** suspension).
> 5. The fluid is **Newtonian** (water at ordinary temperatures qualifies).
> 6. There is **no flocculation** — particles settle as individuals (a deflocculating agent such as sodium hexametaphosphate is added during test preparation).

## 2.3 Limitations of Stokes' Law

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Stokes' Law Fails at Extremes (Frequent 3-Mark Question):**
> - **D > 0.2 mm** → Settling velocity is too high → **turbulent flow** (Re > 1) develops → drag is no longer purely viscous. (Sieve analysis is used instead.)
> - **D < 0.0002 mm (0.2 μm)** → **Brownian motion** of water molecules agitates these ultrafine particles, preventing gravity settling. (Other methods like the pipette analysis or laser diffraction are needed.)
> - **Non-spherical particles (platy clays):** Drag is over-estimated; we get a *nominal* equivalent diameter only.

## 2.4 The Hydrometer Test — Operating Principle

The hydrometer is a thin, calibrated glass float with:
- A **bulb** (constant volume, weighted at the bottom to float upright).
- A **graduated stem** at the top, marked in g/L of suspension density (type 152H) or specific gravity (type 151H).

When the hydrometer is gently inserted into a 1000-mL soil-water suspension:

- The **depth of the bulb's center** below the water surface corresponds to a particular density reading on the stem.
- The reading at time $t$ (corrected for meniscus, temperature, and deflocculant) gives the **mass of soil still in suspension** above the bulb.
- A particle with settling velocity $v = H/t$ is the **largest** one that has just reached the bulb at time $t$.
- All finer particles are still suspended above the bulb.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18\mu}$ | Stokes' Law — terminal velocity of a sphere | m/s |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{H}{t}$ | Hydrometer settling velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu}{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w)} \cdot \dfrac{H}{t}}$ | Equivalent particle diameter from hydrometer | m |
| 4 | $D (\text{mm}) = M \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{t}}$ | Compact form, $H$ in cm, $t$ in min | mm |
| 5 | $M = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu}{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot 10^6}}$ | Hydrometer constant (combines viscosity & Gs) | — |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \dfrac{V}{M_d} \cdot \gamma_d \cdot (R - R_w)$ | Percent finer than D (for ASTM 152H) | % |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{G_s - 1} \cdot \dfrac{V}{M_s} \cdot (R - R_w)$ | Percent finer than D (for ASTM 151H) | % |
| 8 | $R_c = R + C_m \pm C_t$ | Corrected hydrometer reading | g/L |
| 9 | $H = L_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(L_2 - \dfrac{V_b}{A_c}\right)$ | Effective depth (general) | cm |
| 10 | $H = 16.29 - 0.164 R$ | ASTM 152H effective depth (R in g/L) | cm |
| 11 | $L_e = L_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}(L_2 - \dfrac{V_b}{A_c})$ | Length of hydrometer bulb effect | cm |

Where:
- $R$ = observed hydrometer reading
- $R_w$ = reading in pure water (or meniscus correction $C_m$)
- $C_m$ = meniscus correction (positive constant, typically **+0.5 to +1.0 g/L**)
- $C_t$ = temperature correction (positive if $T > 20°C$, negative if $T < 20°C$)
- $V_b$ = volume of hydrometer bulb (≈ 67 cm³ for 152H)
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of the sedimentation cylinder (≈ 27.8 cm² for 1000 mL jar)
- $M_d$ = mass of oven-dry soil used in the test (g)
- $\gamma_d$ = unit weight of dry soil (g/cm³)
- $\rho_w$ = density of water (g/cm³)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in practice:**
> - **Foundation design:** Clay/silt content controls **compressibility, swelling, and shrinkage** behavior.
> - **Earth dam construction:** Filter design depends on the **15% finer size ($D_{15}$)** of base and filter materials.
> - **Soil classification (IS 1498, USCS):** Soils with > 50% passing 75 μ are classified as fine-grained — their behavior is **plasticity-based** (Atterberg limits), but hydrometer is essential to confirm the silt–clay split.
> - **Shrinkage & frost-heave assessment:** Frost-susceptible soils have specific particle size criteria (e.g., 3% finer than 20 μ).
> - **Contaminant transport:** Fine particle fraction dictates adsorption and migration of pollutants in groundwater.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Force-Balance Method)

### Step 1 — Identify the forces on a single sphere

Let the spherical particle have:
- Diameter = $D$
- Radius = $r = D/2$
- Volume $V_s = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$
- Cross-sectional area $A = \pi r^2 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$

The three forces in the vertical direction are:

**(a) Downward weight of particle:**
$$W = V_s \cdot \gamma_s = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \gamma_s$$

**(b) Upward buoyant force (Archimedes):**
$$F_b = V_s \cdot \gamma_w = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \gamma_w$$

**(c) Viscous drag force (Stokes' empirical law for laminar flow past a sphere):**
$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu D v$$

### Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium condition at terminal velocity

At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero, so:

$$W = F_b + F_d$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\frac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \gamma_s = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \gamma_w + 3 \pi \mu D v$$

### Step 3 — Simplify algebraically

Move the buoyant term to the left:

$$\frac{\pi D^3}{6} (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) = 3 \pi \mu D v$$

Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\frac{D^2}{6} (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) = 3 \mu v$$

Multiply both sides by 6:

$$D^2 (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) = 18 \mu v$$

### Step 4 — Solve for terminal velocity $v$

$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18 \mu}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, derived purely from a static force balance with the empirically observed Stokes drag formula. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful alternative form** (using $G_s = \gamma_s / \gamma_w$):
> $$v = \frac{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w D^2}{18 \mu}$$
> This form is more convenient for numerical problems because $G_s$ and $\gamma_w$ are direct inputs.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hydrometer Diameter Equation

### Step 1 — Velocity from sedimentation geometry

In the hydrometer test, the particle travels from the water surface to the hydrometer's measuring depth $H$ in time $t$. At terminal velocity:

$$v = \frac{H}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Equate with Stokes' Law velocity

$$\frac{H}{t} = \frac{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w D^2}{18 \mu}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $D^2$

$$D^2 = \frac{18 \mu H}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w \, t}$$

### Step 4 — Take the square root

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w} \cdot \frac{H}{t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental hydrometer diameter equation**. All variations used in IS, ASTM, and KTU board exam numericals reduce to this single relation.

## 3.3 Conversion to the Compact Form $D = M \sqrt{H/t}$

Let $D$ be in **mm**, $H$ in **cm**, $t$ in **minutes**, and $\gamma_w$ in **g/cm³**.

Convert units carefully:
- $H/t$ in cm/min → m/s requires multiplication by $\dfrac{10^{-2}}{60} = \dfrac{1}{6000}$
- $D$ in m → mm requires multiplication by $10^3$

So the equation in SI becomes:

$$D \, (\text{m}) = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}}$$

For $D$ in mm and $H/t$ in cm/min, we introduce the constant $M$:

$$D \, (\text{mm}) = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w \cdot 10^6}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}}$$

Define:
$$M = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w \cdot 10^6}}$$

Thus:
$$D \, (\text{mm}) = M \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Table of $M$ for $G_s = 2.65$ (extract commonly asked in KTU 3-markers):**

| Temperature (°C) | $\mu$ (N·s/m²) | $M$ |
|---|---|---|
| 17 | 1.082 × 10⁻³ | 0.01491 |
| 20 | 1.002 × 10⁻³ | 0.01434 |
| 23 | 0.932 × 10⁻³ | 0.01377 |
| 25 | 0.891 × 10⁻³ | 0.01344 |
| 27 | 0.854 × 10⁻³ | 0.01314 |
| 30 | 0.798 × 10⁻³ | 0.01275 |

For different $G_s$ values, $M$ must be recomputed as $M_{2.65} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2.65 - 1}{G_s - 1}}$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Single-Particle Terminal Velocity (Foundation Concept)

> **Question:** A soil grain with $D = 0.05$ mm and $G_s = 2.70$ settles in water at 27 °C. Compute the terminal settling velocity. Take $\mu = 8.5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m² and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

**Step 1 — Unit conversions:**
$$D = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute unit weight of soil solids:**
$$\gamma_s = G_s \cdot \gamma_w = 2.70 \times 9.81 = 26.487 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' Law:**
$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18 \mu} = \frac{(26487 - 9810) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Compute numerator:
$$(26487 - 9810) = 16677 \text{ N/m}^3$$
$$(5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$
$$16677 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 4.169 \times 10^{-5}$$

Compute denominator:
$$18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.53 \times 10^{-2}$$

Therefore:
$$v = \frac{4.169 \times 10^{-5}}{1.53 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 2.72 \text{ mm/s}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Finding Equivalent Particle Diameter from a Hydrometer Reading

> **Question:** In a hydrometer test, at $t = 4$ minutes the corrected reading $R = 28$ g/L. Take $G_s = 2.65$, $T = 27°$ C. Compute the equivalent particle diameter $D$ and the percent finer $N$. Use $H = 16.29 - 0.164 R$ cm and assume 50 g of dry soil in 1000 mL of suspension.

**Step 1 — Compute effective depth $H$:**
$$H = 16.29 - 0.164 \times 28 = 16.29 - 4.592 = 11.70 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Pick $M$ at 27 °C for $G_s = 2.65$:**
$$M = 0.01314$$

**Step 3 — Compute $D$ in mm:**
$$D = M \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}} = 0.01314 \times \sqrt{\frac{11.70}{4}}$$
$$\sqrt{2.925} = 1.7103$$
$$D = 0.01314 \times 1.7103 = 0.02248 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0225 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute percent finer $N$ (ASTM 152H form):**
$$N = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{V}{M_d} \cdot \gamma_d \cdot (R - R_w)$$

For a 1000-mL jar with $M_d = 50$ g of dry soil (assuming $\gamma_d \approx 1.0$ g/cm³ as a slurry equivalent; technically $M_d/V$ gives the concentration), and $R_w = 0$ (pure water reading):

$$N = \frac{2.65}{2.65 - 1} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{50 \text{ g}} \times (28 - 0) \times \text{conversion factor}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Board Formula (when 50 g soil in 1000 mL is used):**
> $$N(\%) = \frac{R \cdot a}{50} \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \times 1000 = \frac{2.65}{1.65} \times 1000 = 1606$$
> $$N = \frac{28 \times 1606}{50} = \frac{44968}{50} = 899.4 \text{ (this is in g/L ratio; convert to \%)}$$

> Simplified working formula (KTU boards accept):
> $$N = \frac{1.65 \times R}{50} \text{ is incorrect; use the correct:} \quad N(\%) = \frac{R \cdot G_s \times 100}{M_d (G_s - 1)} \times 1000 \text{ (in correct form)}$$

**Correct simplified form for $G_s = 2.65$ and $M_d = 50$ g:**
$$N(\%) = \frac{1606 \times R}{50} \times \frac{1}{100} = \frac{1606 \times 28}{5000} = \frac{44968}{5000} = 8.99 \approx 9.0\%$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.0225 \text{ mm}, \quad N = 9.0\%}$$

This means **9% of the soil is finer than 0.0225 mm** (i.e., in the medium-silt to fine-silt range).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls and Their Fixes

| Common Mistake (Why Marks Are Lost) | Correct Approach |
|---|---|
| Using $D$ in mm directly in Stokes' SI formula | Convert $D$ to **metres** before substituting |
| Forgetting the meniscus correction $C_m$ | Always apply $R_c = R_{\text{observed}} + C_m$ (it is added, not subtracted) |
| Confusing temperature correction sign | $C_t$ is **+ve** if $T > 20°C$ (water is less dense, hydrometer sinks more) |
| Mixing up sieve vs hydrometer ranges | Sieves handle $D \geq 75\,\mu$m; hydrometer handles $D < 75\,\mu$m |
| Treating 151H and 152H formulas as identical | 152H reads in g/L; 151H reads in specific gravity. The percent-finer formula differs |
| Forgetting deflocculant correction $C_d$ | Subtract $C_d$ from $R$ (it accounts for salt added to disperse clay) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydrometer Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step A: Soil Sampling and Preparation"] --> B["Step B: Oven-dry Soil and Weigh 50 g Mass"]
    B --> C["Step C: Add 125 mL of 4 Percent Sodium Hexametaphosphate Solution"]
    C --> D["Step D: Soak for 16 to 24 Hours for Full Dispersion"]
    D --> E["Step E: Agitate in Mechanical Stirrer for 1 to 2 Minutes"]
    E --> F["Step F: Transfer Suspension to 1000 mL Graduated Cylinder"]
    F --> G["Step G: Add Distilled Water to Reach 1000 mL Mark"]
    G --> H["Step H: Insert Hydrometer and Start Stopwatch t = 0"]
    H --> I["Step I: Record Hydrometer Reading R at Times 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, 1440 min"]
    I --> J["Step J: Read Temperature at Each Interval"]
    J --> K["Step K: Apply Corrections R_c = R + C_m + C_t - C_d"]
    K --> L["Step L: Compute Effective Depth H = 16.29 - 0.164 R_c"]
    L --> M["Step M: Compute Equivalent Diameter D = M sqrt H over t"]
    M --> N["Step N: Compute Percent Finer N from Corrected R_c"]
    N --> O["Step O: Plot Semi-log Grain Size Curve: D on log axis vs Percent Finer"]
    O --> P["Step P: Read D10, D30, D60 for Classification"]
    P --> Q["Step Q: Compute Cu and Cc for Soil Grading Evaluation"]
    Q --> R["Step R: Combine with Sieve Analysis Curve to Form Full PSD"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Force Balance on a Single Settling Sphere

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Forces Acting on Soil Particle"] --> F2["Downward Weight W = gamma_s times Volume"]
    F1 --> F3["Upward Buoyancy F_b = gamma_w times Volume"]
    F1 --> F4["Viscous Drag F_d = 3 pi mu D v"]
    F2 --> F5["Terminal Velocity Condition: W = F_b + F_d"]
    F3 --> F5
    F4 --> F5
    F5 --> F6["Stokes Equation: v = (gamma_s - gamma_w) D squared over 18 mu"]
    F6 --> F7["Hydrometer Form: D = sqrt 18 mu H over G_s minus 1 gamma_w t"]
    F7 --> F8["Compact Form: D in mm = M sqrt H_cm over t_min"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Validity Range Map of Stokes' Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1["Particle Diameter D in mm"] --> V2{"D greater than 0.2 mm?"}
    V2 -- Yes --> V3["Use Sieve Analysis. Stokes Law Invalid because Re greater than 1"]
    V2 -- No --> V4{"D less than 0.0002 mm?"}
    V4 -- Yes --> V5["Stokes Law Invalid because Brownian Motion Dominates"]
    V4 -- No --> V6["Stokes Law Valid Range: 0.0002 mm less than D less than 0.2 mm"]
    V6 --> V7["Apply Hydrometer Test with Laminar Flow Assumption"]
    V3 --> V8["End of Validity Check"]
    V5 --> V8
    V7 --> V8
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Hydrometer Itself (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Hydrometer Float Assembly"] --> A2["Top: Graduated Stem (Reads 0 to 60 g/L for 152H)"]
    A1 --> A3["Middle: Constant Volume Bulb (V_b approx 67 cm cubed)"]
    A1 --> A4["Bottom: Ballast Weight (Mercury or Lead Shot for Upright Float)"]
    A2 --> B1["Reading at Waterline: R cm above bulb center"]
    A3 --> B2["Bulb Center sits at effective depth H below suspension surface"]
    A4 --> B3["Ballast keeps stem vertical and bulb submerged"]
    B1 --> C1["Reading Interpretation: Higher R means Denser Suspension"]
    B2 --> C2["Depth Interpretation: H equals 16.29 minus 0.164 R cm"]
    B3 --> C3["Stability: Reduces tilting error during measurement"]
```
