# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State Stokes' Law. List any three assumptions involved in its application to soil sedimentation analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stokes' Law:** The terminal settling velocity of a small spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid at low Reynolds numbers is given by:

$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18 \mu}$$

where $v$ is the terminal velocity, $\gamma_s$ and $\gamma_w$ are the unit weights of the particle and water respectively, $D$ is the particle diameter, and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of water.

**Three Assumptions:**
1. Soil particles are **smooth, rigid, and spherical** (we use the *equivalent* diameter for non-spherical particles).
2. The fluid medium is **infinite in extent and the flow is laminar** (Reynolds number Re ≤ 1).
3. Each particle settles **independently** with no particle-to-particle interaction (valid only in dilute suspensions, typically < 50 g/L).
*(Any three of the six assumptions from Section 2.2 are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the range of validity of Stokes' Law in hydrometer analysis? Why does it fail outside this range?**

**Model Answer:**

**Valid Range:** $0.0002 \text{ mm} < D < 0.2 \text{ mm}$, i.e., $0.2 \text{ μm} < D < 200 \text{ μm}$.

**Reasons for Failure:**

1. **Upper Limit (D > 0.2 mm):** The settling velocity becomes too high; flow around the particle becomes **turbulent** (Re > 1). Stokes' Law, derived for laminar viscous drag, is no longer valid. (Sieve analysis is used instead.)

2. **Lower Limit (D < 0.0002 mm):** **Brownian motion** of the water molecules agitates these ultra-fine particles randomly, preventing orderly gravity settling. The Stokes drag formula assumes the particle is large compared to the mean free path of water molecules, which fails here.

3. **Consequence:** For particles outside this range, alternative methods such as pipette analysis, laser diffraction, or microscopy are employed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive Stokes' Law from first principles by drawing a free-body diagram of a settling spherical particle and writing the equilibrium equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a hydrometer test, the following readings were observed for a soil suspension prepared with 50 g of oven-dry soil ($G_s = 2.65$) in 1000 mL of water at 27 °C. Using $H = 16.29 - 0.164 R$ cm and $M = 0.01314$ (for $G_s = 2.65$, 27 °C), compute the equivalent particle diameter and the percent finer at $t = 4$ min, given the corrected hydrometer reading $R_c = 28$ g/L. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Stokes' Law (7 Marks)

**Free-Body Diagram Description:**

A single spherical soil particle of diameter $D$ settles vertically through still water. The forces acting on it are:
- $W$ = downward weight = $\gamma_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$
- $F_b$ = upward buoyancy = $\gamma_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$
- $F_d$ = upward viscous drag = $3\pi\mu D v$

**Equilibrium at terminal velocity:**

$$\gamma_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = \gamma_w \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{6} + 3\pi\mu D v$$

**Rearranging:**

$$\frac{\pi D^3}{6}(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) = 3\pi\mu D v$$

**Dividing by $\pi D$:**

$$\frac{D^2}{6}(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) = 3\mu v$$

**Final expression:**

$$v = \frac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18\mu}$$

> **Mark Allocation Note (7 marks):** Free-body diagram (2) + Force expressions (2) + Equilibrium (1) + Algebraic simplification (1) + Statement of assumptions (1).

**Assumptions (write all 6 as discussed in Section 2.2):** sphericity, infinite medium, laminar flow (Re ≤ 1), no inter-particle interaction, Newtonian fluid, no flocculation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective Depth:**
$$H = 16.29 - 0.164 \times 28 = 16.29 - 4.592 = 11.70 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Particle Diameter:**
$$D = M \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}} = 0.01314 \times \sqrt{\frac{11.70}{4}}$$
$$D = 0.01314 \times \sqrt{2.925} = 0.01314 \times 1.7103$$
$$\boxed{D = 0.02248 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0225 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Percent Finer (ASTM 152H Formula):**

For 50 g of dry soil in 1000 mL:
$$N(\%) = \frac{R_c \cdot G_s \cdot 1000}{M_d (G_s - 1)} \times \frac{1}{100}$$
$$N = \frac{28 \times 2.65 \times 1000}{50 \times 1.65 \times 100} = \frac{74200}{8250}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 8.99\% \approx 9.0\%}$$

**Conclusion:** At $t = 4$ min, particles larger than **0.0225 mm** have settled out; only **9%** of the soil is finer than this size — confirming the soil is predominantly a **sandy silt** rather than clay.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a hydrometer. What is the function of a deflocculating agent? Why is sodium hexametaphosphate preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrometer reading of $R = 40$ g/L is taken at $t = 2$ min and $T = 27°C$. The effective depth relation is $H = 16.29 - 0.164R$ cm. For $G_s = 2.70$, compute the equivalent particle diameter $D$ and the percent finer $N$. Take $M = 0.01304$ (for $G_s = 2.70$, 27 °C) and 60 g of dry soil in 1000 mL. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Hydrometer Working Principle (7 Marks)

A hydrometer is a thin, calibrated, weighted glass float consisting of:
- A **graduated stem** at the top.
- A **constant-volume bulb** in the middle.
- A **ballast chamber** at the bottom (mercury or lead shot).

**Working Principle:**
When the hydrometer is gently lowered into the soil-water suspension, it floats at a depth determined by the suspension's **density** at the bulb. The denser the suspension, the higher the hydrometer floats, and a higher reading is obtained on the graduated stem. The reading $R$ (in g/L for ASTM 152H type) is proportional to the **mass of soil particles still in suspension** above the bulb's measuring depth $H$.

> **Mark Allocation Note:** Sketch (2) + Principle (2) + Deflocculant role (1.5) + Why Na-hexametaphosphate (1.5).

**Function of Deflocculating Agent:**
Clay particles in water tend to **flocculate** (form loose aggregates) due to electrical surface charges. This causes them to settle as larger clumps, leading to erroneous (over-estimated) particle sizes. A deflocculant like **sodium hexametaphosphate (Calgon)** neutralizes the edge charges of clay platelets, causing individual particles to settle as discrete units — thereby ensuring Stokes' Law assumptions (independent settling, spherical behavior) are satisfied.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective Depth:**
$$H = 16.29 - 0.164 \times 40 = 16.29 - 6.56 = 9.73 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Particle Diameter:**
$$D = M \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{t}} = 0.01304 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.73}{2}}$$
$$D = 0.01304 \times \sqrt{4.865} = 0.01304 \times 2.2057$$
$$\boxed{D = 0.02876 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0288 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Percent Finer:**

For 60 g of dry soil in 1000 mL:
$$N(\%) = \frac{R_c \cdot G_s \cdot 1000}{M_d (G_s - 1)} \times \frac{1}{100}$$
$$N = \frac{40 \times 2.70 \times 1000}{60 \times 1.70 \times 100} = \frac{108000}{10200}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 10.59\%}$$

**Conclusion:** At $t = 2$ min, **10.59%** of the soil is finer than **0.0288 mm** (coarse-silt boundary).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting meniscus correction ($C_m$):** All hydrometer readings must be **increased** by $C_m$ (typically +0.5 to +1 g/L). Reading the hydrometer from below the meniscus level introduces a constant positive error; failure to correct loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up units in Stokes' Law:** $D$ must be in **metres**, $\gamma$ in **N/m³**, $\mu$ in **N·s/m²**, and $v$ in **m/s**. A frequent error is plugging $D$ in mm and getting a velocity 10⁶ times too small.
> 
> 3. **Using the wrong $M$ value:** $M$ depends on both **temperature** and **$G_s$**. If the problem gives a $G_s$ different from 2.65, you must either recompute $M$ or scale: $M_{\text{new}} = M_{2.65} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.65}{G_s - 1}}$.
> 
> 4. **Temperature correction sign error:** If $T > 20°C$, the water is less dense, the hydrometer sinks **deeper**, so $C_t$ is **+ve** (added to $R$). If $T < 20°C$, $C_t$ is **-ve**. Getting this sign wrong propagates through all subsequent calculations.
> 
> 5. **Confusing ASTM 151H and 152H formulas:** The percent-finer formula is **different** for the two hydrometer types. 152H reads g/L; 151H reads specific gravity. Examiners often test this distinction.
> 
> 6. **Omitting assumptions in derivations:** In the 7-mark derivation question, students who skip the assumption list lose up to 1.5 marks even if the math is perfect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrometer Analysis — Stokes' Law: One-Page Rapid Recap**
> 
> ✅ **Stokes' Law formula:** $v = \dfrac{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) D^2}{18\mu}$ — relates settling velocity to particle diameter for **laminar, viscous, independent** settling of **spherical** particles.
> 
> ✅ **Inverse (hydrometer) form:** $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\mu}{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w} \cdot \dfrac{H}{t}}$ — used to compute the equivalent particle diameter from a hydrometer reading at time $t$.
> 
> ✅ **Compact form:** $D(\text{mm}) = M \sqrt{H/t}$, where $H$ in cm, $t$ in min, $M \approx 0.0131$ at 27 °C for $G_s = 2.65$.
> 
> ✅ **Valid range:** $0.0002 \text{ mm} < D < 0.2 \text{ mm}$.
> 
> ✅ **Why it fails below 0.0002 mm:** Brownian motion dominates. **Why it fails above 0.2 mm:** Turbulent flow (Re > 1).
> 
> ✅ **Six core assumptions:** Sphericity · infinite medium · laminar flow · no inter-particle interaction · Newtonian fluid · no flocculation.
> 
> ✅ **Deflocculant:** Sodium hexametaphosphate (Calgon) — used to prevent clay flocculation.
> 
> ✅ **Effective depth for 152H:** $H = 16.29 - 0.164 R$ cm.
> 
> ✅ **Corrections to $R$:** $R_c = R + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$ (meniscus +ve always, temperature sign depends on $T \gtrless 20°C$).
> 
> ✅ **Percent finer (152H, 50 g in 1000 mL, $G_s = 2.65$):** $N(\%) = \dfrac{1606 \times R}{50} \times \dfrac{1}{100} \approx 32.1 \times R/100$.
> 
> ✅ **Real-world application:** Foundation design, filter criteria, soil classification (silt vs clay boundary at 2 μ), frost-susceptibility assessment, contaminant transport modeling.
> 
> ✅ **Hydrometer vs Sieve:** Sieve = $D \geq 75\,\mu$m; Hydrometer = $D < 75\,\mu$m. **Combined** they yield the full Particle Size Distribution (PSD) curve.
> 
> ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 9810 N/m³); $\mu = 8.5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m² at 27 °C; $G_s = 2.65$ (default).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Hydrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Hydrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrometer Calibration** is the systematic process of applying standardized corrections to the raw hydrometer reading to account for **meniscus error**, **temperature deviation**, and **dispersing agent volume**, so that the reading accurately represents the **specific gravity of the soil suspension** at the centre of buoyancy of the hydrometer bulb.

In the **sedimentation (hydrometer) test**, a calibrated hydrometer (typically **ASTM 152H** or **ASTM 151H**) is immersed in a soil-water suspension. The depth of immersion is governed by the density of the suspension, which in turn depends on the mass and particle size distribution of soil still in suspension at time *t*. Since the raw reading is influenced by **instrumental errors** and **test conditions**, calibration converts the *observed reading* $R_H$ into a *corrected reading* $R$, which alone can be used in **Stokes' Law** calculations for particle size $D$ and in mass-balance equations for the **percentage finer** $N$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **floating marker** dropped in a **swimming pool** where someone has dissolved different amounts of sugar at different times:

- The marker sinks deeper where the water is **denser** (more sugar still floating) and rises where it is **clearer** (sugar already settled down).
- Now, suppose the **water temperature rises** — the water itself expands and becomes naturally less dense. The marker would sink *a little* even if no sugar was present.
- Also, the marker is read at the **top of the curved water surface (meniscus)**, but it should ideally be read at the **flat water level**. The gap between these two is a fixed **personal/instrumental error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping:** The floating marker = **Hydrometer bulb**, Sugar settling = **Soil particles settling under gravity**, Temperature rise = **Temperature correction**, Curved surface gap = **Meniscus correction**. The calibration is simply the "tuning" of the marker to ignore all such *unwanted* influences and report **only the real density of the suspension**.

### 1.3 The Three Calibration Corrections

| Correction | Symbol | Reason | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Meniscus correction** | $C_m$ | Readings are taken at the top of the meniscus; the true water level is at its bottom | **+0.5 to +1.5** divisions |
| **Temperature correction** | $C_T$ | Suspension is rarely at the calibration temperature (typically **20 °C** or **27 °C** in India) | **−2 to +4** divisions |
| **Dispersing agent correction** | $C_D$ | Sodium hexametaphosphate (calgon) added to deflocculate clays adds its own density | **+0.5 to +1.5** divisions |

The **composite correction** is:

$$C = C_m + C_T - C_D$$

> [!TIP]
> **$C_D$ is subtracted** (not added) because the dispersing agent artificially *increases* the hydrometer reading; we must remove this artificial rise to recover the true soil contribution. Always cross-check the sign convention in your laboratory manual.

### 1.4 Reference Temperature and Hydrometer Type

- **ASTM 152H** is calibrated to read **specific gravity of suspension** directly (0.995 – 1.030 g/cc range).
- **ASTM 151H** reads in **grams per litre** of soil in suspension (0 – 60 g/L range).
- The **standard reference temperature** is **20 °C** (ASTM) or **27 °C** (IS 2720, used widely in Indian labs and KTU).
- The hydrometer is **standardized in a clear liquid at 20 °C / 27 °C** so that the reading at the meniscus bottom = 0 (i.e., specific gravity of pure water at that temperature).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Calibration is Mathematically Necessary

The hydrometer reading $R_H$ is *not* a direct measurement of soil in suspension. It is a measurement of the **specific gravity of the soil–water–dispersant mixture** at the centre of the bulb. Stokes' Law, however, requires the **effective depth $H_e$** corresponding to particles *just settling past the bulb's centre of volume*, and this is related to the *actual* density of the suspension — free from meniscus, temperature, and dispersant artefacts.

Therefore, the order of operations is:

1. Observe the raw hydrometer reading $R_H$ at time $t$.
2. Apply composite correction: $R = R_H + C$
3. Compute effective depth: $H_e = f(R)$
4. Compute particle diameter using Stokes' Law: $D = f(H_e, t, G_s)$
5. Compute percentage finer: $N\% = f(R, W_s, G_s)$

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU Board exams, "calibration" questions almost always test Steps 1–3. A common error is to use the *raw* $R_H$ in the Stokes equation, which yields an incorrect $D$ (off by 1–3 % — not negligible in lab grading).**

### 2.2 The Composite Correction in Detail

#### 2.2.1 Meniscus Correction $C_m$
The meniscus correction is the **height (in hydrometer divisions) between the flat water surface and the bottom of the meniscus** as seen against the hydrometer stem. Since the hydrometer is calibrated for the bottom of the meniscus (a *concave-down* reading), but the operator reads the *top* of the meniscus, the observed reading is *lower* than the true reading. Hence, we **add** $C_m$.

$$C_m = R_{\text{bottom}} - R_{\text{top}} \quad \text{(always positive)}$$

#### 2.2.2 Temperature Correction $C_T$
Water viscosity decreases and density also changes with temperature. The hydrometer is calibrated for **pure water reading = 0** at 20 °C. At any other temperature, the pure water itself gives a non-zero reading. The **temperature correction** is the reading that the hydrometer would give in **pure water (no soil)** at the test temperature, taken at the meniscus bottom.

- If $T > 20\,^\circ\text{C}$ (or $27\,^\circ\text{C}$): $C_T$ is **positive** (water less dense → hydrometer sinks more → higher reading).
- If $T < 20\,^\circ\text{C}$: $C_T$ is **negative**.

> [!WARNING]
> Many KTU students mistakenly treat $C_T$ as always positive. **Sign depends on whether the test temperature is above or below the calibration temperature** of the hydrometer. Check the calibration temperature printed on the hydrometer stem.

#### 2.2.3 Dispersing Agent Correction $C_D$
A deflocculating agent (typically **sodium hexametaphosphate**, ~ 33 g/L stock solution, ~ 100 mL added per 1000 mL suspension) is added to prevent flocculation. This solution itself has a non-zero specific gravity. $C_D$ is the hydrometer reading observed in **distilled water + dispersing agent** (no soil) at the calibration temperature.

$$C_D = R_{\text{water + dispersant}} \quad \text{(taken at the meniscus bottom)}$$

Since this agent artificially raises the density, we **subtract** $C_D$ in the composite formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you need for a 14-mark KTU problem on hydrometer calibration. Memorize the units alongside each variable.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composite correction | $C = C_m + C_T - C_D$ | divisions (unitless) | Apply to **every** reading |
| 2 | Corrected hydrometer reading | $R = R_H + C$ | divisions | Use in all downstream calcs |
| 3 | Effective depth (152H) | $H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R$ | cm | $R$ is the *corrected* reading |
| 4 | Effective depth (151H) | $H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R$ | cm | Same numerical formula in ASTM |
| 5 | Particle diameter (Stokes) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18\,\mu\,H_e}{(G_s - 1)\,\rho_w\,g\,t}}$ | m | $\mu$ in Pa·s, $t$ in s, $H_e$ in m |
| 6 | Diameter (combined constant) | $D = M\sqrt{\dfrac{H_e}{t}}$ | mm | $M$ is computed from $G_s$ and $T$ |
| 7 | Percentage finer | $N = \dfrac{100\,G_s}{W_s/G_s \times \rho_w V_s}\cdot R$ | % | Simplified forms below |
| 8 | % Finer (typical form) | $N = \dfrac{100\,R}{W_s/(G_s - 1)}$ | % | For 1000 mL of suspension |
| 9 | Coefficient $a$ | $a = \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$ | — | $G_s = 2.65 \Rightarrow a = 1.606$ |
| 10 | Shortcut % finer | $N = \dfrac{100\,a\,R}{W_s}$ | % | Common in KTU numericals |
| 11 | Viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | Pa·s | Standard value |
| 12 | Density of water at 20 °C | $\rho_w = 998.2$ | kg/m³ | Standard value |

> [!NOTE]
> **M (Stokes Coefficient) for $G_s = 2.65$ at 27 °C ≈ 0.00905 mm·min$^{1/2}$**, and for 20 °C ≈ 0.00531 mm·min$^{1/2}$. KTU labs mostly use 27 °C. Always confirm from the question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation design** in fine-grained soils: Hydrometer-derived particle size controls **frost susceptibility**, **swelling potential**, and **consolidation behaviour**.
- **Embankment and earth-dam design**: Filter criteria (Terzaghi's filter rule) require **$D_{15}$, $D_{85}$** of base and filter soils, which come from hydrometer + sieve combined.
- **Liquefaction assessment**: Fine content (>15 % passing 75 µm from hydrometer) is a key input to the **"Chinese criteria"** by Seed & Idriss.
- **Soil classification** (IS 1498, USCS): The **silt–clay boundary (0.002 mm)** is **only** obtainable from the hydrometer test — the sieve cannot go below 75 µm.

> [!TIP]
> In **KTU Part B 14-mark questions**, a frequent pattern is: *"A hydrometer test is conducted with 50 g of oven-dried soil (Gs = 2.65) dispersed in 1000 mL of water. The composite correction $C = 2$, observed reading $R_H = 30$ at $t = 60$ min. Compute $D$ and $N\%$."* — this is a **must-do template**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Depth Equation $H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R$

The hydrometer bulb has a fixed volume $V_B$ and cross-sectional area $A$. When immersed to a depth such that the corrected reading is $R$, the **centre of the bulb** is below the water surface by a distance that depends on $R$ and the geometry of the stem.

Let:

- $L_1$ = length of hydrometer bulb (typically **14.0 cm** for ASTM 152H)
- $L_2$ = distance from the **top of the bulb to the lowest graduation mark** (typically **2.3 cm**)
- $V_B$ = volume of the bulb (typically **67.0 cm³**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the stem (typically **0.78 cm²**)

> **Why do we need the centre of the bulb?**
> The hydrometer integrates the suspension density over the entire bulb. By **Archimedes' principle**, the reading corresponds to the **average** density along the bulb. Stokes' law, however, computes the **velocity of a single particle**, which is meaningful only for the depth of the **centre of the bulb** — the centroid of the volume where settling velocity is being "sampled".

**Step 1.** When the hydrometer is in a suspension of density $\rho$ higher than water, it floats higher than in pure water. If $R$ is the corrected reading (in divisions, each = 0.001 specific gravity = 0.001 g/cc), then the additional buoyancy equals a rise of stem equal to $(R \times \text{unit length per division})$.

**Step 2.** The depth from the suspension surface to the **centre of the bulb** is the original depth minus half the bulb length, plus the rise due to density:

$$H_e = L_2 + \frac{1}{2}L_1 - \frac{V_B}{A} + \text{(rise correction)}$$

**Step 3.** Substituting standard ASTM 152H values and noting that **1 division ≈ 0.1645 cm of stem length** because the stem's circumference is calibrated such that a 0.001 change in specific gravity corresponds to ~ 0.1645 cm rise:

$$H_e = \underbrace{2.3}_{L_2} + \underbrace{7.0}_{L_1/2} + \underbrace{7.0}_{V_B/A} - 0.1645\,R$$

Wait — let me re-derive the sign correctly. The rise of the hydrometer in denser fluid means the **centre moves up**, hence **less** depth below the surface. So we **subtract** a positive multiple of $R$:

$$H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R \quad \text{(cm)}$$

where 16.29 = $L_2 + L_1/2 + V_B/A$ combines all geometric constants.

**Numerical check (for $R = 30$):**

$$H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645 \times 30 = 16.29 - 4.935 = 11.355 \text{ cm}$$

This means at the moment the hydrometer reads 30, the centre of its bulb is 11.36 cm below the suspension surface — and only particles **smaller than $D$ computed from $H_e = 11.36$ cm and the elapsed time $t$** are still in suspension at that depth.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Stokes' Law for Particle Diameter $D$

A spherical soil particle settling under gravity in a viscous fluid experiences a balance between **gravity (downward)**, **buoyancy (upward)**, and **viscous drag (upward)**.

At **terminal velocity** $v$:

$$\text{Gravity} = \text{Buoyancy} + \text{Drag}$$

$$W_s = F_b + F_d$$

**Step 1.** Weight of the particle in air (volume $V_p$):

$$W_s = V_p \cdot G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6}\,D^3 \cdot G_s \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**Step 2.** Buoyancy force (weight of displaced water):

$$F_b = V_p \cdot \rho_w \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6}\,D^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**Step 3.** Viscous drag (Stokes' drag for a sphere, valid for Reynolds number $Re < 1$):

$$F_d = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,D\,v$$

**Step 4.** Net driving force (effective weight):

$$W_s - F_b = \frac{\pi}{6}\,D^3 \cdot (G_s - 1)\cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**Step 5.** Setting net force = drag at terminal velocity:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}\,D^3 \cdot (G_s - 1)\cdot \rho_w \cdot g = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,D\,v$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $v$:

$$v = \frac{D^2 \cdot (G_s - 1)\cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{18\,\mu}$$

**Step 7.** Velocity is also $v = H_e / t$ (a particle starting at the surface settles to depth $H_e$ in time $t$):

$$\frac{H_e}{t} = \frac{D^2 \cdot (G_s - 1)\cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{18\,\mu}$$

**Step 8.** Solving for $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu\,H_e}{(G_s - 1)\,\rho_w\,g\,t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional check:** Numerator: $[\text{Pa·s}]\,[\text{m}] = [\text{kg/(m·s)}]\,[\text{m}] = [\text{kg/s}]$. Denominator: $[\text{kg/m³}]\,[\text{m/s²}]\,[\text{s}] = [\text{kg/(m²·s)}]$. Ratio: $[\text{kg/s} \div \text{kg/(m²·s)}] = [\text{m²}]$. Square root: $[\text{m}]$. ✅

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Percentage Finer $N$

Consider a 1000 mL (=$10^{-3}$ m³) suspension jar with $W_s$ grams of soil. At any time $t$, all particles larger than $D$ have settled *below* the bulb's centre. Therefore, the **mass of soil in suspension at depth $H_e$** is proportional to the **excess specific gravity** of the suspension above pure water.

Let $G$ = specific gravity of suspension, $G_w$ = specific gravity of water (≈ 1), $G_s$ = specific gravity of soil grains.

**Step 1.** Mass of soil in suspension in the volume $V_s$ sampled (assume whole 1000 mL well-mixed, the bulb samples a uniform slice):

$$M_{\text{susp}} = (G - G_w)\,\rho_w\,V_s = (G - 1)\,\rho_w\,V_s$$

**Step 2.** The hydrometer is calibrated such that $R$ divisions ≈ $(G - 1) \times 1000$ (since each division = 0.001 g/cc). So:

$$G - 1 = \frac{R}{1000}$$

**Step 3.** Substituting:

$$M_{\text{susp}} = \frac{R}{1000}\,\rho_w\,V_s = R\,\rho_w \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} = R \times 10^{-6}\,\rho_w\,(\text{kg})$$

For $V_s$ in mL and $R$ in divisions, the simple working form is:

$$M_{\text{susp}} = \frac{a \cdot R \cdot W_s}{100} \quad \text{where } a = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

**Step 4.** Percentage finer:

$$N = \frac{M_{\text{susp}}}{W_s} \times 100 = a \cdot R \times \frac{100}{W_s}$$

$$\boxed{N\% = \frac{100\,a\,R}{W_s} = \frac{100\,G_s\,R}{W_s\,(G_s - 1)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical shortcut for $G_s = 2.65$, $W_s = 50$ g:** $a = 1.606$, so $N = (100 \times 1.606 \times R)/50 = 3.21\,R$. If $R = 30$, then $N = 96.3\,\%$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Correction on Final Answer

| Step | What changes? | How is it computed? | Effect on $D$ | Effect on $N$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meniscus correction $C_m$ | $R \uparrow$ by $C_m$ | Reading water + hydrometer at top & bottom of meniscus | $D$ increases (larger $H_e$) | $N$ increases (proportional) |
| Temperature correction $C_T$ | $R \uparrow$ if $T > 20\,^\circ$C | Reading hydrometer in pure water at test $T$ | $D$ changes via $H_e$ | $N$ changes linearly |
| Dispersing agent correction $C_D$ | $R \downarrow$ by $C_D$ | Reading hydrometer in water + dispersant (no soil) | $D$ decreases | $N$ decreases |
| Composite $C$ | $R = R_H + C$ | Sum of above | Combined effect | Combined effect |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrometer Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sedimentation Test in Progress] --> B[Observe Raw Hydrometer Reading RH at time t]
    B --> C{Determine Composite Correction C}
    C --> D[Meniscus Correction Cm: Read at top minus bottom in pure water]
    C --> E[Temperature Correction CT: Read in pure water at test temperature]
    C --> F[Dispersing Agent Correction CD: Read in water + dispersant, no soil]
    D --> G[Compute C = Cm + CT - CD]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Corrected Reading R = RH + C]
    H --> I[Compute Effective Depth He = 16.29 minus 0.1645R in cm]
    I --> J[Apply Stokes Law: D = sqrt of 18 mu He divided by Gs minus 1 times rho w times g times t]
    J --> K[Compute Percentage Finer: N = 100 times Gs times R divided by Ws times Gs minus 1]
    K --> L[Plot Particle Size Distribution: D on log x axis, N on y axis]
    L --> M[End: Calibrated D and N obtained]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Components of the Calibrated Reading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OBS[OBSERVED INPUTS]
        RH[Raw Hydrometer Reading RH]
        T[Test Temperature]
        DA[Dispersing Agent Added]
    end

    subgraph CAL[CALIBRATION BLOCK]
        CM[Meniscus Cm]
        CT[Temp CT]
        CD[Dispersant CD]
        SUM[Composite C = Cm + CT - CD]
    end

    subgraph COR[CORRECTED OUTPUTS]
        R[Corrected R = RH + C]
        HE[Effective Depth He]
    end

    subgraph ENG[ENGINEERING USE]
        D[Particle Diameter D]
        N[Percent Finer N]
        PSD[Particle Size Distribution Curve]
    end

    RH --> SUM
    T --> CT
    DA --> CD
    CM --> SUM
    CT --> SUM
    CD --> SUM
    SUM --> R
    R --> HE
    HE --> D
    R --> N
    D --> PSD
    N --> PSD
```

### 4.3 Sedimentation Column Schematic (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JAR[1000 mL Sedimentation Jar]
        TOP[Top of Suspension: Coarsest particles have settled below bulb centre]
        MID[Centre of Hydrometer Bulb: Depth He below surface - this is the sampling plane]
        BOT[Bottom of Jar: All particles larger than D have accumulated]
    end

    subgraph HYD[Hydrometer Assembly]
        STEM[Stem with graduation R]
        BULB[Bulb of volume VB]
        BALL[Balling chamber at bottom for stability]
    end

    STEM --> BULB
    BULB --> BALL
    BALL -. floats at depth .-> MID
    MID ---|particles smaller than D remain| TOP
    BOT ---|particles larger than D settled| MID
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **functional block schematic**, not a physical drawing. It captures the relationship between the **sampling plane** (centre of bulb at depth $H_e$) and the **settled mass** at the bottom of the jar — the two key conceptual anchors of the test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "composite correction" in hydrometer analysis. List the three individual corrections and state with reasons whether each is added or subtracted in the composite formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Composite correction $C$ is the algebraic sum of the three individual corrections applied to the raw hydrometer reading $R_H$ to obtain the corrected reading $R$ that truly represents the specific gravity of the soil suspension.
>
> 1. **Meniscus correction $C_m$** — *added* (+). The hydrometer stem is read at the top of the meniscus, but is calibrated for the bottom; the difference must be added to recover the calibrated reading. (1 mark)
> 2. **Temperature correction $C_T$** — *added* (+) if $T > 20\,^\circ\text{C}$, subtracted (−) if $T < 20\,^\circ\text{C}$. Pure water at temperatures other than the calibration temperature (typically 20 °C) gives a non-zero reading, which must be added to correct the soil reading. (1 mark)
> 3. **Dispersing agent correction $C_D$** — *subtracted* (−). Sodium hexametaphosphate added to deflocculate the clay artificially increases the suspension density; this artificial increase must be removed. (1 mark)
>
> **Composite formula:** $C = C_m + C_T - C_D$ and **Corrected reading** $R = R_H + C$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Stokes' law as applied in the hydrometer test and mention the two key assumptions made in its application to soil particles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Stokes' Law for a sphere settling at terminal velocity $v$ in a viscous fluid:
>
> $$v = \frac{D^2\,(G_s - 1)\,\rho_w\,g}{18\,\mu}$$
>
> Solving for particle diameter $D$:
>
> $$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu\,H_e}{(G_s - 1)\,\rho_w\,g\,t}}$$
>
> where $H_e$ is the effective depth (in metres), $t$ is the elapsed settling time (in seconds), $\mu$ is the viscosity of water, $G_s$ is the specific gravity of soil grains, $\rho_w$ is the density of water, and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Two key assumptions:**
> 1. Soil particles are **spherical** (real particles are platy/angular — this leads to overestimation of $D$). (0.75 marks)
> 2. **No inter-particle interference / each particle settles independently** (achieved by using a low concentration, e.g., 50 g per 1000 mL, and a dispersing agent to prevent flocculation). (0.75 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In hydrometer calibration problems, students frequently (i) forget to convert $H_e$ from cm to m before substituting in Stokes' law, (ii) use $R_H$ (raw) instead of $R = R_H + C$, (iii) confuse the sign of $C_T$, and (iv) forget that $W_s$ in the percentage-finer formula is the **oven-dry mass**, not the wet mass. **Each of these typically costs 1–2 marks** out of 14. Read the question twice and box the values of $C$, $R$, $G_s$, $W_s$, $T$ before computing.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Modelled on KTU Dec 2022 / May 2023 pattern*

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A hydrometer test was performed on a soil sample with the following data:
- Mass of oven-dry soil used: $W_s = 50$ g
- Specific gravity of soil grains: $G_s = 2.65$
- Volume of suspension: $V_s = 1000$ mL
- Calibration temperature: $T_c = 20\,^\circ$C
- Meniscus correction: $C_m = +1.0$ division
- Temperature correction at test temperature (28 °C): $C_T = +1.5$ division
- Dispersing agent correction: $C_D = +0.8$ division
- Observed hydrometer reading at $t = 60$ min: $R_H = 28$ divisions

**(a)** Compute the corrected hydrometer reading and the effective depth. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the corresponding particle diameter $D$ and the percentage finer $N$ at this time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Corrected reading and effective depth (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Composite correction** (1 mark)

$$C = C_m + C_T - C_D = 1.0 + 1.5 - 0.8 = 1.7 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step 2: Corrected hydrometer reading** (1 mark)

$$R = R_H + C = 28 + 1.7 = 29.7 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step 3: Effective depth** using ASTM 152H formula (2 marks)

$$H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R = 16.29 - 0.1645 \times 29.7$$

$$H_e = 16.29 - 4.88565 = 11.404 \text{ cm} = 0.11404 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Convert units and present result** (1 mark)

$$H_e = 11.40 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(or } 0.1140 \text{ m)}$$

**Step 5: Tabulated summary** (2 marks)

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Composite correction $C$ | 1.7 | divisions |
| Corrected reading $R$ | 29.7 | divisions |
| Effective depth $H_e$ | 11.40 | cm |

---

**(b) Particle diameter and percentage finer (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Stokes' Law constant $M$** (1 mark)

For $G_s = 2.65$, $T = 20\,^\circ$C, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_w = 998$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu\,H_e}{(G_s - 1)\,\rho_w\,g\,t}}$$

**Step 2: Convert $H_e$ to metres and $t$ to seconds** (1 mark)

$$H_e = 0.1140 \text{ m}, \quad t = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Substitute** (2 marks)

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1140}{(2.65 - 1) \times 998 \times 9.81 \times 3600}}$$

**Step 4: Numerator and denominator** (1 mark)

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.0560 \times 10^{-3}}{1.65 \times 998 \times 9.81 \times 3600}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0560 \times 10^{-3}}{5.8156 \times 10^{7}}}$$

**Step 5: Final value** (1 mark)

$$D = \sqrt{3.5354 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.946 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00595 \text{ mm} \approx 5.95\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 6: Percentage finer** (1 mark)

$$N = \frac{100\,G_s\,R}{W_s\,(G_s - 1)} = \frac{100 \times 2.65 \times 29.7}{50 \times 1.65} = \frac{7870.5}{82.5} = 95.4\,\%$$

**Final Answer:** $D \approx 0.00595$ mm (silt-sized particle), $N \approx 95.4\,\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice: Alternative numerical set*

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

For a hydrometer test on a fine-grained soil:
- Oven-dry mass: $W_s = 60$ g
- Specific gravity: $G_s = 2.70$
- Suspension volume: 1000 mL
- Calibration temperature: 27 °C
- Corrections at test temperature: $C_m = +0.8$, $C_T = -0.5$, $C_D = +1.2$ (all in divisions)
- At $t = 2$ hours, observed reading $R_H = 22$ divisions

**(a)** Calculate the composite correction and the corrected hydrometer reading. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the effective depth, the particle diameter using Stokes' law, and the percentage finer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Composite correction and corrected reading (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Composite correction** (2 marks)

$$C = C_m + C_T - C_D = 0.8 + (-0.5) - 1.2 = 0.8 - 0.5 - 1.2 = -0.9 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step 2: Corrected reading** (2 marks)

$$R = R_H + C = 22 + (-0.9) = 21.1 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation** (3 marks — a slightly negative $C$ arises when the test temperature is below the calibration temperature, giving a negative $C_T$ that outweighs $C_m$).

**Tabulated answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Composite correction $C$ | −0.9 divisions |
| Corrected reading $R$ | 21.1 divisions |

---

**(b) Effective depth, particle diameter, and percentage finer (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Effective depth** (2 marks)

$$H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645 \times 21.1 = 16.29 - 3.4710 = 12.819 \text{ cm} = 0.12819 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Convert $t$ to seconds** — $t = 2 \times 3600 = 7200$ s (1 mark)

**Step 3: Stokes' Law computation** (2 marks)

For 27 °C, $\mu = 0.00085$ Pa·s, $\rho_w = 996$ kg/m³, $G_s = 2.70$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.00085 \times 0.12819}{(2.70 - 1) \times 996 \times 9.81 \times 7200}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{1.9617 \times 10^{-3}}{1.70 \times 996 \times 9.81 \times 7200}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.9617 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1950 \times 10^{8}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.6416 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.052 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 0.00405 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Percentage finer** (2 marks)

$$N = \frac{100 \times 2.70 \times 21.1}{60 \times (2.70 - 1)} = \frac{5697.0}{102.0} = 55.85\,\%$$

**Final Answer:** $H_e \approx 12.82$ cm, $D \approx 0.00405$ mm, $N \approx 55.9\,\%$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Calibration of Hydrometer"**

- ✅ **Composite correction formula:** $C = C_m + C_T - C_D$ — **memorize the signs**.
- ✅ **Corrected reading:** $R = R_H + C$ — *always use this in Stokes' Law and % finer*.
- ✅ **Effective depth (ASTM 152H / 151H):** $H_e = 16.29 - 0.1645\,R$ in **cm**.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $D = \sqrt{18\mu H_e / ((G_s - 1)\rho_w g t)}$ — verify units: $H_e$ in m, $t$ in s.
- ✅ **% Finer shortcut (1000 mL jar):** $N = 100\,G_s\,R / (W_s\,(G_s - 1))$. For $G_s = 2.65$, $W_s = 50$ g: $N = 3.21\,R$.
- ✅ **Sign of $C_T$:** Positive if $T > T_{\text{calibration}}$, negative if $T < T_{\text{calibration}}$.
- ✅ **Sign of $C_D$:** Always **subtracted** (dispersant artificially raises density).
- ✅ **Why $C_m$ added?** Hydrometer stem read at the meniscus *top* (operator convention) but calibrated for the *bottom*.
- ✅ **Two key Stokes' assumptions:** (1) Spherical particles, (2) Independent settling (no flocculation — hence the dispersing agent).
- ✅ **Test temperature in KTU labs:** Usually **27 °C** (IS 2720 convention), not 20 °C (ASTM). Read the question carefully.
- ✅ **Hydrometer types:** **152H** reads specific gravity; **151H** reads g/L. Same $H_e$ formula.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law validity:** Reynolds number $Re < 1$ (otherwise drag coefficient is wrong). Particles must be **< 0.2 mm** for Stokes to apply.
- ✅ **Units audit before final answer:** $D$ in mm (or µm), $H_e$ in cm, $t$ in min (for short-form) or s (for SI form). Mismatch = **mark loss**.
- ✅ **PSD curve plotting:** Use **semi-log** plot, $\log D$ on $x$-axis, $N$ on $y$-axis. Sieve + hydrometer combined gives full curve from gravel to clay.
- ✅ **Engineering impact:** $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ from PSD control **permeability, frost susceptibility, filter design, and USCS classification**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mnemonic for the three corrections:** **"My Teacher Disperses"** → **M**eniscus added, **T**emperature signed (with $T$), **D**ispersant subtracted. The composite formula reads **"My Teacher Disperses Negatively"** because $C_D$ is the only subtraction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrections to Hydrometer Readings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrections to Hydrometer Readings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrometer analysis** is a sedimentation test used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **particle size distribution** of fine-grained soils (silt and clay fractions, i.e., particles smaller than **75 microns / 0.075 mm**). It is based on **Stokes' Law**, which relates the settling velocity of a spherical particle in a viscous fluid to its diameter.

A **hydrometer** is a calibrated glass instrument that measures the **specific gravity (density)** of a soil-water suspension at any given depth and time. The raw hydrometer reading, however, does **not directly represent the true density** of the suspension because of three unavoidable experimental and physical interferences. Therefore, **corrections** must be applied to the raw reading before any valid computation of particle size or percentage finer can be made.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> *Corrections to hydrometer readings are empirical numerical adjustments applied to the directly observed hydrometer scale reading to account for (i) the curvature of the meniscus at the air-liquid interface, (ii) the deviation of the suspension temperature from the calibration temperature (usually 20°C), and (iii) the presence of the chemical dispersing agent added to the suspension to prevent flocculation.*

The three standard corrections are:

| Symbol | Correction Name | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $C_m$ | Meniscus Correction | Always **Positive (+)** |
| $C_t$ | Temperature Correction | Positive or Negative (±) |
| $C_d$ | Dispersing Agent Correction | Always **Positive (+)** |

The **corrected hydrometer reading** is:

$$R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$$

where $R_h$ is the raw (observed) hydrometer reading, and $R_c$ is the corrected reading used in all subsequent particle-size calculations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef making a smoothie in a tall, narrow glass, and you want to measure how much "stuff" is suspended at different depths. You lower a long, floating weighted tube (analogous to the hydrometer) into the liquid, and the depth it sinks tells you how dense the liquid is. But here is the catch:

1. **Surface Tension Problem (Meniscus Correction):** When you look at the glass from the side, the liquid curves upward at the edges where it touches the glass. This curve (the meniscus) tricks your eye — you read the level of the curved water surface, not the true flat level. It is like trying to read the fuel level in a car on a bumpy road; you must add a "bumpiness correction." This correction $C_m$ is **always added** because you always read from the bottom of the meniscus (lower than the true level), making the actual density lower than what the reading suggests.

2. **Temperature Problem (Temperature Correction):** Liquids expand when warm and contract when cool. Your smoothie will sink to different depths depending on whether the kitchen is hot or cold. The hydrometer was originally calibrated at **20 °C (68 °F)**. If the test runs at a different temperature, the fluid density — and therefore the reading — will be off. So we add or subtract a temperature correction $C_t$.

3. **Chemical Soup Problem (Dispersing Agent Correction):** Before the test, you add a small amount of "anti-clumping" chemical (typically **sodium hexametaphosphate / Calgon**) to the soil-water mixture. This chemical itself has weight, and it makes the suspension denser than pure water would be. The hydrometer doesn't know that — it just reads "denser than water." We must subtract the extra reading $C_d$ caused by the chemical alone (measured by a blank test).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** These corrections are not academic luxuries. Even an uncorrected reading can mislead a geotechnical engineer into misclassifying a soil, misjudging its permeability, or underestimating its compressibility. Mis-sizing a particle by even 0.001 mm can change a clay from "low plasticity" to "high plasticity" in classification systems like **IS 1498 / ASTM D2487**. The **3 corrections** are the very reason hydrometer analysis produces a reliable, code-compliant particle size distribution curve for foundation design, dam construction, and earthwork projects.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Conditions

- **Calibration temperature of hydrometer:** $\mathbf{T_{cal} = 20^\circ C}$
- **Density of water at 20 °C:** $\rho_w = \mathbf{998.2 \ kg/m^3}$ (effectively **1.0 g/cm³** for practical calculations)
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu = \mathbf{1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ N \cdot s/m^2}$
- **ASTM 152H hydrometer** (Bouyoucos): scale calibrated in g/L of suspension (range 0 to 60)
- **ASTM 151H hydrometer** (long form): scale calibrated in specific gravity (range 0.995 to 1.038)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In numerical problems, students are expected to know that the **meniscus correction is always added**, the **dispersing agent correction is always subtracted**, and the **temperature correction is signed based on whether the test temperature is above or below 20 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Three Corrections?

A hydrometer is a **buoyancy-based instrument**. According to **Archimedes' Principle**, a floating body displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own weight. The depth to which the hydrometer sinks depends on the **density of the surrounding suspension**. The bulb displaces a fixed volume $V_b$, and the stem carries a graduated scale.

The governing equilibrium is:

$$\gamma_w \cdot V_{displaced} = W_{hydrometer}$$

where $\gamma_w$ is the unit weight of the suspension and $W_{hydrometer}$ is the (constant) weight of the hydrometer.

Because the suspension density at the location of the bulb is a function of **time, temperature, and chemistry**, the reading must be corrected for all three influences. Each correction isolates one source of error.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Correction

### 2.2.1 Meniscus Correction ($C_m$)

**Physical cause:** Surface tension pulls the liquid up along the hydrometer stem, creating a concave meniscus. The observer's eye naturally reads the **bottom of the meniscus** (since the liquid is transparent), but the hydrometer is calibrated for the **top of the meniscus** (the flat surface). This means the **observed reading is lower** than the true reading. To compensate, we **add** a positive value.

**How to determine $C_m$:**
1. Place the hydrometer in a clear cylinder of distilled water at calibration temperature (20 °C).
2. Observe the scale at the bottom of the meniscus.
3. The reading should ideally be **0** (since pure water has no soil).
4. The deviation from zero is the meniscus correction $C_m$.

$$C_m = R_{observed \ in \ water} - 0 = R_{observed \ in \ water}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Meniscus:** "**M**eniscus = **M**ust be **M**ade-up" → always added, because the true reading is always higher than what you see.

**Typical magnitude:** $C_m \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ (for 152H scale) and $C_m \approx 0.0005$ to $0.001$ (for 151H scale).

---

### 2.2.2 Temperature Correction ($C_t$)

**Physical cause:** The volume of liquids changes with temperature. The hydrometer stem and the suspension both expand or contract. If the test temperature $T_{test} \neq 20^\circ C$, the suspension density — and hence the hydrometer reading — deviates from the calibrated value.

- If $T_{test} > 20^\circ C$: water is **less dense** → hydrometer sinks **deeper** → reading is **higher** → we must **subtract** $C_t$ (i.e., apply a **negative** correction).
- If $T_{test} < 20^\circ C$: water is **more dense** → hydrometer floats **higher** → reading is **lower** → we must **add** $C_t$ (i.e., apply a **positive** correction).

**How to determine $C_t$:**
- It is **read from a standard table** provided in textbooks (e.g., Punmia, Murthy, Arora) — see Table 2.1 below.
- Alternatively, $C_t$ is computed from the linear thermal expansion of the hydrometer stem and the change in water density.

**Standard Table for 152H Hydrometer (ASTM D422):**

| Temperature (°C) | $C_t$ (152H) | $C_t$ (151H) |
|---|---|---|
| 15 | +0.75 | +0.0011 |
| 20 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 25 | -1.00 | -0.0013 |
| 30 | -2.10 | -0.0027 |
| 35 | -3.30 | -0.0042 |

---

### 2.2.3 Dispersing Agent Correction ($C_d$)

**Physical cause:** A chemical dispersing agent (usually **sodium hexametaphosphate, NaPO₃**, also called "Calgon") is added to the soil-water suspension to **deflocculate** the clay particles. Without it, fine particles clump together (flocculate) and settle faster, giving false coarse-grained results. However, the dispersing agent itself has mass and slightly increases the density of the suspension, producing a small but non-zero hydrometer reading even when **no soil is present**.

**How to determine $C_d$:**
1. Prepare a **blank** cylinder: same volume of distilled water + same quantity of dispersing agent, but **no soil**.
2. Insert the hydrometer at the calibration temperature (20 °C).
3. Read the scale at the top of the meniscus (this is important — for $C_d$, we use the **top** of the meniscus, since we want the actual reading including the dispersing agent's contribution).
4. The reading of this blank test is the dispersing agent correction $C_d$.

$$C_d = R_{blank \ test}$$

**Typical magnitude:** $C_d \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ (for 152H scale).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtleties worth noting:**
> 1. In **Indian/IS practice** (most KTU problems), $C_d$ is **subtracted** from the observed reading.
> 2. In some **ASTM conventions**, the meniscus correction is **already accounted for** in the $C_d$ reading (because the blank is read at the top of the meniscus), and the correction formula becomes slightly different. KTU problems typically follow the **IS convention** where the formula is $R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$.

---

## 2.3 Composite Correction

For convenience, some textbooks combine $C_m$, $C_t$, and $C_d$ into a single **composite correction** $C$:

$$C = C_m + C_t \pm C_d \quad \text{(some texts)}$$ 

$$C = C_m \pm C_t - C_d \quad \text{(IS / KTU convention)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The most common KTU-board convention is: $R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$, where $C_m$ and $C_d$ are positive constants and $C_t$ is signed.

---

## 2.4 Post-Correction Computations (Brief)

Once $R_c$ is obtained, the percent finer is:

$$N = \frac{R_c}{W_s} \times 100 \times \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \times \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_{w,20}} \quad \text{(152H form)}$$

For **151H hydrometer** (specific gravity scale):

$$N = \frac{1606}{W_s} (R_c - 1) \cdot G_s \quad \text{(approximate)}$$ 

or more precisely:

$$N = \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1} \cdot \frac{R_c - 1}{W_s/W_{suspension}} \times 100$$

The effective depth $H_e$ is:

$$H_e = H - \frac{R_c}{2} - \frac{V_b}{2A} \quad \text{(or tabulated as a function of } R_c)$$

Stokes' diameter:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(G_s - 1) \rho_w \cdot g \cdot t}} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w \cdot t}}$$

Often simplified using a constant $K$:

$$D (\text{mm}) = K \sqrt{\frac{H_e}{t}}$$

where for $G_s = 2.65$, $\mu$ at 20 °C:

$$K \approx 0.0127 \text{ (for 152H) or } 0.0139 \text{ (for 151H)}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrected hydrometer reading | $R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$ | dimensionless (152H) or in g/L |
| 2 | Composite correction | $C = C_m + C_t - C_d$ | dimensionless (152H) |
| 3 | Effective depth (152H, IS) | $H_e = 16.3 - 0.164 \cdot R_c$ | in **cm**; valid for $R_c < 50$ |
| 4 | Effective depth (151H) | $H_e = 16.3 - 0.164 \cdot (R_c - 1) \times 1000$ | cm |
| 5 | Stokes' particle diameter | $D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{18 \cdot \mu}{(G_s - 1) \cdot \rho_w \cdot g} \cdot \dfrac{H_e}{t} }$ | in **metres** |
| 6 | Compact form (152H) | $D (\text{mm}) = K \sqrt{\dfrac{H_e}{t}}$ | $K$ depends on $G_s$, hydrometer, temperature |
| 7 | Percent finer (152H) | $N = \dfrac{R_c \cdot 100}{W_s} \cdot \dfrac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$ | in % |
| 8 | Percent finer (151H) | $N = \dfrac{1606}{W_s} (R_c - 1) \cdot G_s$ | in % |
| 9 | Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ at 20 °C | N·s/m² |
| 10 | Density of water | $\rho_w = 998.2 \ kg/m^3$ at 20 °C | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 151H hydrometer**: The percent-finer formula is $N = \dfrac{1606 \cdot G_s}{W_s \cdot (G_s - 1)} \cdot (R_c - 1)$ where $R_c$ is the **specific gravity** reading (between 0.995 and 1.038).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The corrected hydrometer readings drive the construction of the **particle size distribution (PSD) curve**, a foundational input for:

1. **Soil classification** (IS 1498, USCS, AASHTO).
2. **Hydraulic conductivity estimation** (Kozeny-Carman, Chapman's equation).
3. **Frost susceptibility** evaluation.
4. **Filter design** in earth dams (Terzaghi's filter criteria).
5. **Compaction and stabilization** design.
6. **Settlement analysis** of fine-grained soils.

> [!NOTE]
> Without the corrections, every downstream parameter would be biased, potentially leading to **over-estimation of fines content** and hence **over-conservative (and expensive) foundation design**.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Foundational)

Stokes' Law gives the terminal (steady) settling velocity $v$ of a small sphere in a viscous fluid under gravity. The forces acting on the sphere are:

1. **Weight (downward):** $W = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot V_s = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_s = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3$
3. **Viscous drag (upward, Stokes' regime):** $F_d = 3 \pi \mu \cdot D \cdot v$

At terminal velocity, **net force = 0**:

$$\rho_s g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = \rho_w g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 + 3 \pi \mu D v$$

Step-by-step rearrangement:

$$(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = 3 \pi \mu D v$$

Cancel $D$ from both sides (since $D \neq 0$):

$$(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^2 = 3 \pi \mu v$$

Solve for $v$:

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot D^2}{18 \mu}$$

Now, the distance $H_e$ fallen in time $t$ is $v \cdot t = H_e$, so:

$$H_e = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot D^2}{18 \mu} \cdot t$$

Rearranging for $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}}$$

Introducing $G_s = \rho_s / \rho_w$ and $\rho_w = \gamma_w / g$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w \cdot t}}$$

This is the **fundamental Stokes' diameter formula** used in hydrometer analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Depth Formula $H_e = 16.3 - 0.164 R_c$

The hydrometer bulb is at depth $H$ from the suspension surface. As the bulb sinks deeper (because suspension is denser), the effective depth — i.e., the depth at which the density is being measured — decreases. Empirically (for ASTM 152H hydrometer), the relationship is:

$$H_e = H_0 - k \cdot R_c$$

where $H_0$ and $k$ are calibrated constants.

For the standard 152H hydrometer (Bouyoucos), the calibration is:

- Bulb volume: $V_b \approx 67 \ cm^3$
- Stem cross-sectional area: $A \approx 2.84 \ cm^2$ (so $V_b / (2A) \approx 11.8 \ cm$)
- Reading at top of bulb: 0 (suspension density = water)
- Reading at bottom of bulb: 60 g/L

The full calibration is:

$$H_e = 16.3 - 0.164 \cdot R_c \quad \text{(in cm)}$$

Derivation outline (from geometric calibration of bulb and stem):

The depth from the suspension surface to the centre of the bulb is:

$$H = L_{stem} - \frac{R_c \cdot A_{stem}}{2A_{stem}} - \frac{V_b}{2A_{stem}}$$

With numerical substitution:

$$H = 16.3 - 0.164 R_c$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **16.3 cm** represents the depth to the bulb centre when $R_c = 0$ (i.e., in pure water). The constant **0.164 cm/unit** is the ratio of stem cross-sectional area to the cylinder cross-section.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Percent-Finer Formula (152H)

The mass of soil in suspension per unit volume at any time is proportional to the corrected reading $R_c$ (for 152H hydrometer, $R_c$ is in g/L). The total mass of soil used in the test is $W_s$ (oven-dry). The total suspension volume is $V_{susp}$ (typically 1000 mL or 1 litre).

The mass of soil in suspension at any instant (above the bulb depth) is:

$$M_{susp} = R_c \cdot V_{susp} \quad \text{(in grams, for 152H)}$$

The **percentage finer** $N$ is:

$$N = \frac{M_{susp}}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{R_c \cdot V_{susp}}{W_s} \times 100$$

For $V_{susp} = 1000$ mL = 1 L and the density correction for $G_s$ (since the 152H hydrometer assumes the soil is 2.65 times denser than water, but actual soil may have a different $G_s$):

$$N = \frac{R_c \cdot 100}{W_s} \cdot \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

where $G_s / (G_s - 1)$ is the **specific gravity correction factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor $G_s / (G_s - 1)$?** The 152H hydrometer is calibrated assuming the soil particles have specific gravity 2.65. If the actual $G_s$ is different, the same mass of soil displaces a different volume of water, and a correction is needed. The factor $G_s / (G_s - 1) > 1$ for all real soils, so the actual percentage finer is slightly higher than the naïve reading suggests.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Percent-Finer Formula (151H)

The 151H hydrometer reads **specific gravity** of the suspension at the bulb depth. For a soil of specific gravity $G_s$ dispersed in water, the apparent specific gravity of the suspension is:

$$G_{susp} = 1 + \frac{R_c - 1}{1 - 1/G_s} = 1 + \frac{(R_c - 1) G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

Solving for the mass of soil per unit volume:

$$M_{susp} = \rho_w \cdot V_{susp} \cdot (G_{susp} - 1) = \rho_w \cdot V_{susp} \cdot \frac{(R_c - 1) G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

For $V_{susp} = 1000$ mL = 1 L and $\rho_w = 1$ g/cm³:

$$M_{susp} = 1000 \cdot \frac{(R_c - 1) G_s}{G_s - 1} \quad \text{(grams)}$$

The percentage finer:

$$N = \frac{M_{susp}}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{100000 \cdot (R_c - 1) G_s}{(G_s - 1) W_s}$$

In the simplified form commonly used in KTU problems:

$$N = \frac{1606 \cdot (R_c - 1) G_s}{W_s (G_s - 1)}$$

(The factor 1606 = 1000 × 1.606 is sometimes introduced to absorb a units conversion; different textbooks present it slightly differently.)

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hydrometer analysis is conducted on a soil sample. The following data are recorded at $t = 120$ minutes and $T = 25^\circ C$:

- Raw hydrometer reading (152H): $R_h = 22$
- Meniscus correction: $C_m = 0.8$
- Dispersing agent correction: $C_d = 0.5$
- Temperature correction (from table, at 25 °C): $C_t = -1.00$
- $G_s = 2.70$
- $W_s = 50$ g (oven-dry mass of soil)
- Effective depth: $H_e = 12.6$ cm

**Step 1: Apply the corrections.**

$$R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$$

$$R_c = 22 + 0.8 + (-1.00) - 0.5$$

$$R_c = 22 + 0.8 - 1.0 - 0.5$$

$$R_c = 21.3$$

**Step 2: Compute the percent finer.**

$$N = \frac{R_c \times 100}{W_s} \times \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

$$N = \frac{21.3 \times 100}{50} \times \frac{2.70}{2.70 - 1}$$

$$N = \frac{2130}{50} \times \frac{2.70}{1.70}$$

$$N = 42.6 \times 1.5882$$

$$N = 67.66\%$$

**Step 3: Compute the particle diameter.**

Using Stokes' law (in SI units):

- $\mu = 0.001$ N·s/m² (water at 20 °C; using 25 °C value gives ~0.00089, but KTU problems use 20 °C value)
- $H_e = 12.6$ cm $= 0.126$ m
- $t = 120$ min $= 7200$ s
- $G_s = 2.70$
- $\gamma_w = 9810$ N/m³

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.001 \times 0.126}{(2.70 - 1) \times 9810 \times 7200}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 0.001 \times 0.126 = 0.002268$

Denominator: $1.70 \times 9810 \times 7200 = 1.70 \times 7.0632 \times 10^7 = 1.2007 \times 10^8$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{0.002268}{1.2007 \times 10^8}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.889 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$D = 4.347 \times 10^{-6} \ m = 0.004347 \ mm \approx 4.35 \ \mu m$$

**Step 4: Express the result.**

The soil has **67.66% finer than 4.35 μm (i.e., finer than medium silt)** at this reading.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2 (151H Hydrometer)

**Problem:** A 151H hydrometer analysis gives:

- $R_h = 1.0125$ (raw specific gravity reading)
- $C_m = 0.0008$
- $C_t = +0.0005$ (test at 18 °C, so positive)
- $C_d = 0.0012$
- $W_s = 50$ g, $G_s = 2.70$
- $H_e = 12.4$ cm at $t = 60$ min

**Step 1: Apply corrections.**

$$R_c = 1.0125 + 0.0008 + 0.0005 - 0.0012$$

$$R_c = 1.0126$$

**Step 2: Percent finer.**

$$N = \frac{1606 \times (R_c - 1) \times G_s}{W_s \times (G_s - 1)}$$

$$N = \frac{1606 \times 0.0126 \times 2.70}{50 \times 1.70}$$

$$N = \frac{1606 \times 0.03402}{85}$$

$$N = \frac{54.63}{85}$$

$$N = 0.6427 = 64.27\%$$

**Step 3: Particle diameter.**

$$\mu = 0.001 \ N \cdot s / m^2, \quad H_e = 0.124 \ m, \quad t = 3600 \ s$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.001 \times 0.124}{1.70 \times 9810 \times 3600}}$$

Numerator: $0.002232$

Denominator: $1.70 \times 3.5316 \times 10^7 = 6.0037 \times 10^7$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{0.002232}{6.0037 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{3.717 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$D = 6.097 \times 10^{-6} \ m = 0.0061 \ mm \approx 6.1 \ \mu m$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Add or Subtract

| Correction | Sign | Reasoning | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Meniscus ($C_m$) | **Always +** | Reading is artificially low due to curved surface | "**M** = **M**ake **u**p" |
| Temperature ($C_t$) | **+** if $T < 20^\circ C$, **−** if $T > 20^\circ C$ | Cooler water is denser; reading is low → add. Warmer water is less dense; reading is high → subtract. | "**C**old = **C**ompensate positively" |
| Dispersing agent ($C_d$) | **Always −** | Blank reading adds false density; remove it | "**D** = **D**rop it" |

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How Each Correction Is Determined

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Begin Hydrometer Test]) --> Raw["Record Raw Reading R_h<br/>at time t and temperature T"]

    Raw --> M1["Set up blank cylinder<br/>Distilled water only<br/>No soil, no dispersing agent"]
    M1 --> M2["Insert hydrometer<br/>at 20 deg C"]
    M2 --> M3["Read at bottom of meniscus"]
    M3 --> M4["C_m = R observed minus 0<br/>Always Positive"]

    Raw --> T1["Check test temperature T"]
    T1 --> T2{"T vs 20 deg C"}
    T2 -->|T greater than 20| T3["C_t is negative<br/>Subtract from R_h"]
    T2 -->|T less than 20| T4["C_t is positive<br/>Add to R_h"]
    T2 -->|T equals 20| T5["C_t equals 0<br/>Ignore"]

    Raw --> D1["Set up blank cylinder 2<br/>Distilled water plus<br/>dispersing agent only"]
    D1 --> D2["Insert hydrometer<br/>at 20 deg C"]
    D2 --> D3["Read at top of meniscus"]
    D3 --> D4["C_d = R blank<br/>Always positive to subtract"]

    M4 --> Combine
    T3 --> Combine
    T4 --> Combine
    T5 --> Combine
    D4 --> Combine["R_c = R_h + C_m plus or minus C_t minus C_d"]
    Combine --> Done([Proceed to<br/>Diameter and<br/>Percent Finer Calculation])
```
