# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: List and explain the three corrections applied to hydrometer readings in a sedimentation analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a hydrometer (sedimentation) test, the raw (directly observed) reading $R_h$ is influenced by three experimental and physical effects, and must be corrected before use:

1. **Meniscus Correction ($C_m$):** Due to surface tension, the liquid surface curves upward along the hydrometer stem (concave meniscus). The observer reads the bottom of the meniscus, which is **lower** than the calibrated reference level. Therefore, the true reading is higher than observed, and a positive correction $C_m$ is added. It is determined by placing the hydrometer in clear distilled water and noting the deviation from zero. (1 mark)

2. **Temperature Correction ($C_t$):** The hydrometer is calibrated at **20 °C**. If the test temperature differs, the suspension density changes (water is denser when cold, less dense when hot), altering the reading. If $T < 20^\circ C$, the reading is low → $C_t$ is positive (added). If $T > 20^\circ C$, the reading is high → $C_t$ is negative (subtracted). The value is read from a standard temperature-correction table. (1 mark)

3. **Dispersing Agent Correction ($C_d$):** A chemical deflocculant (e.g., sodium hexametaphosphate) is added to disperse the soil. This chemical itself contributes to the suspension's density, giving a non-zero reading even in a soil-free blank test. $C_d$ is determined from a blank test with water + dispersing agent, and is **always subtracted** from the observed reading. (1 mark)

The corrected reading is computed as:

$$R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is the meniscus correction always added? Justify with reference to hydrometer calibration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The hydrometer is calibrated assuming the suspension level is a flat (planar) surface intersecting the stem at the calibration mark. The bulb floats such that the volume displaced equals its weight.

In practice, due to **surface tension**, the liquid climbs the glass stem and forms a **concave meniscus** (curving upward at the contact line). The observer's eye, looking horizontally, reads the **bottom of the meniscus** (because the surface is transparent above and reflective below).

Since the bottom of the meniscus is **lower** than the true flat level, the observed reading $R_h$ is **always smaller** than the true reading corresponding to the actual suspension density.

To recover the true reading, we must **add a positive correction** $C_m$, the magnitude of which equals the difference between the bottom-of-meniscus reading and the true flat-level reading.

$$C_m > 0 \quad \text{(always)}$$

$$R_{true} = R_h + C_m$$

Hence, the meniscus correction is **universally positive** for any hydrometer, regardless of soil type, temperature, or chemistry. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** For each 14-mark Part B question, the examiner provides internal choice between two alternative questions (A and B). Both are at the same cognitive level and cover the same module content. Students answer ONE.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the three corrections applied to hydrometer readings in a sedimentation test. Mention the physical reason for each correction, the sign convention, and how each correction is experimentally determined. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a hydrometer analysis using a **152H hydrometer**, the following data were recorded:

- Mass of dry soil used: $W_s = 50$ g
- Specific gravity of soil solids: $G_s = 2.70$
- Temperature during test: $T = 28^\circ C$
- Meniscus correction: $C_m = 0.6$
- Dispersing agent correction: $C_d = 0.5$
- Raw hydrometer reading at $t = 60$ min: $R_h = 28$
- Effective depth at this reading: $H_e = 11.7$ cm

**Compute:**
(i) The corrected hydrometer reading $R_c$.
(ii) The percent finer $N$ corresponding to this reading.
(iii) The equivalent particle diameter $D$ at this time, using Stokes' law. (Take $\mu = 0.001$ N·s/m², $\gamma_w = 9810$ N/m³.)

(7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A

**Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of the Three Corrections (7 marks):**

**1. Meniscus Correction ($C_m$):**
- **Physical Reason:** Surface tension between the water and the glass stem causes the liquid to climb the stem and form a concave meniscus. The observer reads the bottom of this curved surface, which is **lower** than the calibrated flat reference level. (1 mark)
- **Sign Convention:** Always **positive (+)** because observed reading < true reading. (0.5 mark)
- **Determination:** Place the hydrometer in a clear cylinder of distilled water at 20 °C. Read the scale at the bottom of the meniscus. The deviation from the expected reading of 0 is $C_m$. (0.5 mark)

**2. Temperature Correction ($C_t$):**
- **Physical Reason:** Water density decreases as temperature increases (above 4 °C). The hydrometer is calibrated at 20 °C, so a different test temperature gives a different buoyancy and hence a different reading. (1 mark)
- **Sign Convention:**
  - If $T > 20^\circ C$: water is less dense, hydrometer sinks deeper, reading is **higher** → **subtract** $C_t$ (negative).
  - If $T < 20^\circ C$: water is denser, hydrometer floats higher, reading is **lower** → **add** $C_t$ (positive). (1 mark)
- **Determination:** Read directly from a standard temperature-correction table (e.g., ASTM D422 Table 2) corresponding to the test temperature. (0.5 mark)

**3. Dispersing Agent Correction ($C_d$):**
- **Physical Reason:** Sodium hexametaphosphate (or equivalent) is added to deflocculate clay particles. This chemical has mass and slightly increases the suspension density, producing a non-zero hydrometer reading even when **no soil is present**. (1 mark)
- **Sign Convention:** Always **subtracted (−)** from the raw reading, since the agent's contribution must be removed to obtain the soil-only density. (0.5 mark)
- **Determination:** Prepare a blank cylinder of distilled water + the same quantity of dispersing agent (no soil). Insert the hydrometer at 20 °C. Read at the **top of the meniscus** (so $C_m$ is not double-counted). This reading is $C_d$. (0.5 mark)

**Composite corrected reading:**

$$R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Corrected hydrometer reading $R_c$ (2 marks):**

At $T = 28^\circ C$, the temperature correction is negative (warmer than 20 °C). From the standard 152H table:

$$C_t = -1.4 \quad \text{(interpolated, 152H hydrometer)}$$

> Note: For $T = 25^\circ C$, $C_t = -1.00$; for $T = 30^\circ C$, $C_t = -2.10$. By linear interpolation at $T = 28^\circ C$:

$$C_t = -1.00 + \frac{28 - 25}{30 - 25}(-2.10 - (-1.00)) = -1.00 + 0.6 \times (-1.10) = -1.66$$

Applying all corrections:

$$R_c = R_h + C_m + C_t - C_d$$

$$R_c = 28 + 0.6 + (-1.66) - 0.5$$

$$R_c = 28 + 0.6 - 1.66 - 0.5$$

$$R_c = 26.44$$

**(ii) Percent finer $N$ (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{R_c \times 100}{W_s} \times \frac{G_s}{G_s - 1}$$

$$N = \frac{26.44 \times 100}{50} \times \frac{2.70}{2.70 - 1}$$

$$N = 52.88 \times \frac{2.70}{1.70}$$

$$N = 52.88 \times 1.5882$$

$$N = 83.99\% \approx 84.0\%$$

**(iii) Particle diameter $D$ (3 marks):**

Convert units: $H_e = 11.7$ cm $= 0.117$ m; $t = 60$ min $= 3600$ s.

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(G_s - 1) \gamma_w \cdot t}}$$

Numerator:

$$18 \times 0.001 \times 0.117 = 0.002106$$

Denominator:

$$(2.70 - 1) \times 9810 \times 3600 = 1.70 \times 9810 \times 3600$$

$$= 1.70 \times 35,316,000 = 60,037,200 \approx 6.0037 \times 10^7$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{0.002106}{6.0037 \times 10^7} = 3.508 \times 10^{-11}$$

Square root:

$$D = \sqrt{3.508 \times 10^{-11}} = 5.923 \times 10^{-6} \ m$$

$$D = 0.00592 \ mm \approx 5.92 \ \mu m$$

**Final Answer:** At $t = 60$ min, the soil has **84% finer than 5.92 μm (i.e., finer than coarse silt)**.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Describe the principle of hydrometer analysis. What is Stokes' Law? State the assumptions and limitations of Stokes' Law as applied to soil sedimentation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sedimentation test was conducted using a **151H hydrometer** on 50 g of oven-dry soil ($G_s = 2.68$). The following observations were made at $t = 30$ min and $T = 22^\circ C$:

- Raw hydrometer reading (specific gravity): $R_h = 1.0185$
- Meniscus correction: $C_m = 0.0008$
- Dispersing agent correction: $C_d = 0.0010$
- Effective depth: $H_e = 13.2$ cm
- Calibration temperature: 20 °C

**Compute:**
(i) The corrected hydrometer reading.
(ii) The percent finer corresponding to this reading.
(iii) The Stokes' diameter of the particles corresponding to this reading.

(7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B

**Part (a) — Principle and Stokes' Law (7 marks):**

**Principle of Hydrometer Analysis (2 marks):**
Hydrometer analysis is used to determine the **grain-size distribution of fine-grained soils** (particles smaller than 75 μm). A known mass of soil is dispersed in a known volume of water (typically 1000 mL) using a chemical dispersing agent, and allowed to settle in a graduated cylinder. A hydrometer is inserted at specified time intervals, and the density of the suspension at the depth of the hydrometer bulb is measured. By combining this density with the elapsed time and Stokes' Law, the **diameter** of the largest particles still in suspension at that depth is calculated, and the **percentage finer** is determined from the density reading.

**Stokes' Law (2 marks):**
Stokes' Law describes the terminal settling velocity $v$ of a small sphere in a viscous fluid:

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot D^2}{18 \mu}$$

where $\rho_s, \rho_w$ are the densities of the particle and fluid, $D$ is the diameter, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. Rearranged for particle diameter:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot t}}$$

where $H_e$ is the effective depth and $t$ is the elapsed settling time.

**Assumptions of Stokes' Law (1.5 marks):**
1. Soil particles are **spherical** in shape.
2. The suspension is **dilute** (no particle-particle interaction).
3. Flow around each particle is **laminar** (low Reynolds number, $Re < 1$).
4. Each particle settles **independently** of others.
5. The soil particles are **rigid** and **smooth**.
6. The fluid is **homogeneous, viscous, and unbounded**.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Real soil particles are **not spherical** — they are plate-like (clay) or angular (silt). Stokes' Law gives an "equivalent" or "Stokes' diameter," not the true geometric diameter.
2. The lower size limit is about **0.0002 mm (0.2 μm)**; below this, Brownian motion dominates and Stokes' Law fails.
3. The upper size limit is about **0.2 mm (200 μm)**; above this, flow becomes turbulent and Stokes' Law is invalid.
4. For dense suspensions, settling is hindered by other particles, and the suspension's effective viscosity increases.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Corrected hydrometer reading (2 marks):**

At $T = 22^\circ C$, the temperature correction is small and negative (slightly above 20 °C). From the 151H correction table:

$$C_t \approx -0.0003$$

Applying corrections:

$$R_c = R_h + C_m + C_t - C_d$$

$$R_c = 1.0185 + 0.0008 + (-0.0003) - 0.0010$$

$$R_c = 1.0185 + 0.0008 - 0.0003 - 0.0010$$

$$R_c = 1.0180$$

**(ii) Percent finer $N$ (2 marks):**

For 151H hydrometer:

$$N = \frac{1606 \cdot (R_c - 1) \cdot G_s}{W_s \cdot (G_s - 1)}$$

$$N = \frac{1606 \times (1.0180 - 1) \times 2.68}{50 \times (2.68 - 1)}$$

$$N = \frac{1606 \times 0.0180 \times 2.68}{50 \times 1.68}$$

$$N = \frac{1606 \times 0.04824}{84}$$

$$N = \frac{77.47}{84}$$

$$N = 0.9223 = 92.23\%$$

**(iii) Stokes' particle diameter (3 marks):**

Convert units: $H_e = 13.2$ cm $= 0.132$ m; $t = 30$ min $= 1800$ s.

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu \cdot H_e}{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w \cdot t}}$$

Numerator:

$$18 \times 0.001 \times 0.132 = 0.002376$$

Denominator:

$$(2.68 - 1) \times 9810 \times 1800 = 1.68 \times 9810 \times 1800$$

$$= 1.68 \times 17,658,000 = 2.9665 \times 10^7$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{0.002376}{2.9665 \times 10^7} = 8.010 \times 10^{-11}$$

Square root:

$$D = \sqrt{8.010 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.950 \times 10^{-6} \ m$$

$$D = 0.00895 \ mm \approx 8.95 \ \mu m$$

**Final Answer:** At $t = 30$ min, the soil has **92.23% finer than 8.95 μm (i.e., finer than fine silt)**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in corrections-to-hydrometer-questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on Temperature Correction** — Most common error. If the test is at 25 °C (warmer than 20 °C), the suspension is **less dense**, the hydrometer **sinks deeper**, the reading is **higher**, so the correction must be **subtracted (negative)**. KTU examiners specifically check this sign.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $G_s / (G_s - 1)$ Factor in Percent Finer** — Students compute $N = R_c \times 100 / W_s$ and forget the specific-gravity correction factor. This is a 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency in Stokes' Formula** — The formula $D = \sqrt{18 \mu H_e / [(G_s - 1) \gamma_w t]}$ must use **consistent SI units** (m, s, N/m³, N·s/m²). Using cm or minutes without conversion gives a 10⁴ to 10⁶ error, which is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Confusing 151H and 152H Hydrometer Formulas** — 152H reads in g/L; 151H reads in specific gravity. The percent-finer formulas are different. Using the wrong formula yields numerical answers off by 1000×.
>
> 5. **Meniscus Correction Sign Reversal** — Some students think the meniscus correction is subtracted. It is **always added** because the observed reading is always less than the true reading.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Determination Procedure** — In 7-mark theory questions, merely stating the corrections is worth only 3 marks. KTU examiners award 2 additional marks for explaining **how** each correction is determined, and 1 mark for the sign convention.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Stokes' Law Assumptions** — In "limitations" sub-questions, students often just say "particles are not spherical" but forget to mention the **laminar flow** assumption, the **dilute suspension** assumption, and the **size range validity (0.2 μm to 200 μm)**. KTU board examiners expect at least 4 assumptions + 2 limitations for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Corrections to Hydrometer Readings**
>
> - [ ] The **raw hydrometer reading** $R_h$ is **never used directly** in calculations; it must always be corrected.
> - [ ] The **three corrections** are: meniscus ($C_m$), temperature ($C_t$), and dispersing agent ($C_d$).
> - [ ] **$C_m$ is ALWAYS positive** (added).
> - [ ] **$C_d$ is ALWAYS positive** to subtract (i.e., subtracted from $R_h$).
> - [ ] **$C_t$ is signed**: positive if $T < 20^\circ C$ (added), negative if $T > 20^\circ C$ (subtracted).
> - [ ] The **composite corrected reading** is: $R_c = R_h + C_m \pm C_t - C_d$.
> - [ ] The hydrometer is **calibrated at 20 °C**; any deviation requires a temperature correction.
> - [ ] The **meniscus correction** is determined by placing the hydrometer in clear distilled water at 20 °C and reading the deviation from zero at the **bottom of the meniscus**.
> - [ ] The **dispersing agent correction** is determined using a **blank test** (water + dispersing agent, no soil) and read at the **top of the meniscus** (so $C_m$ is not double-counted).
> - [ ] The **effective depth** $H_e$ for a 152H hydrometer is $H_e = 16.3 - 0.164 R_c$ (in cm), valid for $R_c < 50$.
> - [ ] The **Stokes' diameter** is $D = \sqrt{18 \mu H_e / [(G_s - 1) \gamma_w t]}$ with **all quantities in SI units**.
> - [ ] The **percent finer** (152H) is $N = (R_c \times 100 / W_s) \times [G_s / (G_s - 1)]$.
> - [ ] The **percent finer** (151H) is $N = 1606 (R_c - 1) G_s / [W_s (G_s - 1)]$.
> - [ ] Stokes' Law is valid only for **laminar flow** ($Re < 1$), **dilute suspensions**, and particle sizes between **0.2 μm and 200 μm**.
> - [ ] Stokes' Law assumes **spherical, smooth, rigid particles** settling **independently** in a **homogeneous, unbounded** fluid.
> - [ ] **Mnemonic:** "**M**ake **u**p for the meniscus, **C**ompensate for the **T**emperature, **D**rop the **D**ispersing agent." → Add $C_m$, sign $C_t$, subtract $C_d$.
> - [ ] The **K-value shortcut** for Stokes' diameter: $D (\text{mm}) = K \sqrt{H_e / t}$ where $K \approx 0.0127$ (152H, $G_s = 2.65$, 20 °C).
> - [ ] These corrections ultimately drive the **particle size distribution (PSD) curve**, which is used in **soil classification (IS 1498)**, **hydraulic conductivity** estimation, and **filter design** for earth dams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradation of Soil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gradation of Soil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradation of Soil** is defined as the distribution and relative proportions of different grain sizes constituting a soil mass. It is a fundamental property of coarse-grained soils that governs their engineering behavior, including shear strength, permeability, compressibility, and density.

In Soil Mechanics (per the KTU 2024 PCCET402 syllabus), **Gradation** (also called **Grain Size Distribution**) refers to the quantitative assessment of particle sizes present in a soil. The analysis is performed using two principal laboratory methods:

- **Sieve Analysis (Mechanical Analysis)** — Applicable for **coarse-grained soils** (particles > 75 µm, i.e., retained on the 75 µm IS Sieve).
- **Sedimentation Analysis (Hydrometer Analysis)** — Applicable for **fine-grained soils** (particles < 75 µm, i.e., silt and clay fractions).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Mixed Fruit Bowl**
> Imagine a transparent bowl filled with mixed fruits — large mangoes, medium apples, small grapes, and tiny blueberries. If the bowl contains *all* sizes in *balanced proportions*, it packs well (just like a **well-graded soil**). If it contains *only* mangoes of the same size, there are huge air gaps between them (like a **uniformly-graded soil**). If it has mangoes and blueberries but no apples or grapes, it is **gap-graded** — the small particles cannot fill the voids between the larger ones because the intermediate sizes are missing.

This is precisely why gradation matters in civil engineering: **a well-graded soil is denser, stronger, and less permeable** than a poorly-graded one, because smaller particles fill the voids left by larger ones.

### 1.3 Key Standard Reference Metrics

The following **standard IS Sieve sizes** (per **IS 2720 Part 4**) are used for sieve analysis:

| Sieve Size (mm) | Sieve Size (µm) | Designation |
|---|---|---|
| 4.75 | 4750 | Coarse aggregate boundary |
| 2.00 | 2000 | Gravel–Sand boundary |
| 0.425 | 425 | — |
| 0.075 | 75 | Sand–Silt boundary |
| 0.002 | 2 | Silt–Clay boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **75 micron (0.075 mm)** sieve is the dividing line between **coarse-grained and fine-grained** soils. Particles retained on this sieve are analyzed by sieving; particles passing through it require hydrometer (sedimentation) analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Particle Size Classification (IS Standard)

Based on the KTU 2024 PCCET402 syllabus, soils are classified by particle size as follows:

| Soil Fraction | Size Range |
|---|---|
| **Boulder / Cobble** | > 300 mm |
| **Gravel (G)** | 4.75 mm – 80 mm |
| **Sand (S)** | 75 µm – 4.75 mm |
| **Silt (M)** | 2 µm – 75 µm |
| **Clay (C)** | < 2 µm |

### 2.2 Sieve Analysis — Stepwise Logic

Sieve analysis is performed by passing a known mass of dry soil through a stack of progressively finer sieves arranged vertically.

- **Step 1:** Take oven-dried soil (typically **500 g to 1000 g** for coarse soils).
- **Step 2:** Stack sieves in descending order of aperture (largest at top, pan at bottom).
- **Step 3:** Place the soil on the top sieve and shake on a **mechanical sieve shaker** for **10–15 minutes**.
- **Step 4:** Weigh the soil retained on each sieve carefully.
- **Step 5:** Calculate the percentage retained, cumulative percentage retained, and **percentage finer (N)**.

> **Why Each Step Matters:** The mechanical shaking ensures all fine particles find their way down to the correct sieve. Weighing to **0.1 g accuracy** is critical because small errors compound across 8–10 sieves.

### 2.3 Sedimentation Analysis (Hydrometer Method)

For particles finer than 75 µm, sieving is impractical. Instead, the **Stokes' Law** principle is used, which states that the terminal velocity of a sphere settling in a viscous fluid is:

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Where the variables are defined in the formula sheet below. From Stokes' law, the diameter of the smallest particle still in suspension at depth $H$ after time $t$ is:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \cdot \mu \cdot H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 board examinations on this topic.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Percentage Retained | $\frac{M_{sieve}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | $M_{sieve}$ = mass retained (g); $M_{total}$ = total mass (g) | Sieve analysis |
| Cumulative % Retained | $\sum (\text{Individual \% Retained})$ | — | Plotting grain size curve |
| Percentage Finer (N) | $100 - \text{Cumulative \% Retained}$ | Expressed in % | Y-axis of grain size curve |
| Effective Size ($D_{10}$) | Particle diameter at 10% finer | mm | Found from grain size curve |
| Constriction Size ($D_{30}$) | Particle diameter at 30% finer | mm | Found from grain size curve |
| $D_{60}$ | Particle diameter at 60% finer | mm | Found from grain size curve |
| Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$) | $\frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates soil uniformity |
| Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$) | $\frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates soil gradation shape |
| Stokes' Velocity | $v = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g D^2}{18 \mu}$ | $v$ in cm/s; $D$ in cm | Sedimentation analysis |
| Hydrometer Diameter | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g t}}$ | $D$ in mm; $H$ in cm; $t$ in min | Hydrometer analysis |
| Stoke's Correction Factor | $D' = D \cdot C$ where $C \approx 0.85$ | — | Accounts for non-spherical particles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder (Table Safety):** All vertical bars in the formulas above are rendered as $\frac{a}{b}$ (fractions) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. When writing manually on the answer sheet, use the standard fraction bar.

### 2.5 Engineering Significance of Gradation

- **Well-graded soils** are preferred for **embankments, road subgrades, and backfill** because they offer higher density, higher shear strength, and reduced compressibility.
- **Uniformly-graded soils** are used as **filters in dams** because they have predictable and consistent permeability.
- **Gap-graded soils** are avoided in critical load-bearing applications due to **high void ratio** and **potential for collapse settlement**.
- Gradation directly affects **frost susceptibility, drainage capacity, and liquefaction potential** under cyclic loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law Applied to Soil Sedimentation

Stokes' law was originally derived for a smooth, rigid sphere falling through a viscous fluid under laminar flow conditions. We adapt it to a soil particle settling in water.

**Starting Assumption:** A spherical particle of diameter $D$ and density $\rho_s$ falls under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_w$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$. The particle accelerates until the **buoyant weight equals the viscous drag force**.

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the falling particle**

- Weight of the particle (downward): $W = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot V = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3$
- Buoyancy force (upward): $F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3$
- Viscous drag force (upward, by Stokes): $F_d = 3 \pi \mu v D$

**Step 2: Apply the equilibrium condition (terminal velocity)**

At terminal velocity, the net downward force equals the viscous drag:

$$\rho_s \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = 3 \pi \mu v D$$

**Step 3: Simplify the equation algebraically**

The common factor $\frac{\pi}{6} D^3$ on the left can be factored out:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D^3 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu v D$$

Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\frac{1}{6} D^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \mu v$$

**Step 4: Solve for the terminal velocity $v$**

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

This is the fundamental **Stokes' settling velocity** for a soil particle.

**Step 5: Convert to the hydrometer diameter formula**

The particle falls a distance $H$ in time $t$, so $v = H/t$. Substituting into Stokes' equation:

$$\frac{H}{t} = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot D^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \cdot \mu \cdot H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoke's Law Validity:** This law holds strictly for **Reynolds number $R_e < 1$** (laminar flow). For soil particles larger than approximately 0.2 mm, the law is no longer valid because turbulence sets in. This is why hydrometer analysis is restricted to **silt and clay fractions (< 75 µm)**.

### 3.2 Derivation of $C_u$ and $C_c$ — Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

The **coefficient of uniformity** is a measure of the *range* of particle sizes in a soil. A higher $C_u$ means a wider spread of particle sizes (i.e., the soil contains both very small and very large particles).

**Step 1: Define effective size**
The **effective size $D_{10}$** is the particle diameter corresponding to 10% finer on the grain size curve. It is called "effective" because, by the **Allen Hazen formula (1892)**, the permeability of clean sand is approximated as $k \propto (D_{10})^2$.

**Step 2: Define $D_{60}$**
The diameter corresponding to 60% finer represents the **median size of the coarser fraction** of the soil.

**Step 3: Formulate the Coefficient of Uniformity**

$$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$$

A **high $C_u$** indicates a **well-graded** soil. A **low $C_u$ (close to 1)** indicates a **uniformly-graded** soil.

**Step 4: Formulate the Coefficient of Curvature**

$C_u$ alone does not fully describe the shape of the grain size curve. A soil could have a high $C_u$ but still be **gap-graded**. To capture this, **coefficient of curvature $C_c$** is introduced:

$$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the values**

- $C_u > 4$ (for gravels) or $C_u > 6$ (for sands) AND $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ ⇒ **Well-graded soil**
- $C_u$ small (close to 1) ⇒ **Uniformly-graded soil**
- $C_c < 1$ or $C_c > 3$ ⇒ **Gap-graded soil**

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Computing $C_u$ and $C_c$

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** From a sieve analysis of a sandy soil, the following grain sizes are observed: $D_{10} = 0.18$ mm, $D_{30} = 0.45$ mm, $D_{60} = 1.20$ mm. Classify the soil based on gradation.

**Solution:**

$$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{1.20}{0.18} = 6.67$$

$$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.45)^2}{0.18 \times 1.20} = \frac{0.2025}{0.216} = 0.9375$$

**Conclusion:**

- For sand, well-graded criteria: $C_u > 6$ ✓ (6.67 > 6) AND $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ ✗ (0.9375 < 1)
- Since $C_c < 1$, the soil is classified as **Poorly-Graded Sand (SP)**. The intermediate particles are deficient.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combined Grain Size + Atterberg Limits Classification (IS 1498)

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to use gradation alongside Atterberg limits for full classification. The combined criteria table:

| Soil Type | Gradation Criteria | Plasticity Criteria | IS Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Well-Graded Gravel | $C_u \geq 4$ and $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ | — | GW |
| Poorly-Graded Gravel | Fails gradation criteria | — | GP |
| Silty Gravel | Fails gradation criteria | Below A-line | GM |
| Clayey Gravel | Fails gradation criteria | Above A-line | GC |
| Well-Graded Sand | $C_u \geq 6$ and $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ | — | SW |
| Poorly-Graded Sand | Fails gradation criteria | — | SP |
| Silty Sand | Fails gradation criteria | Below A-line | SM |
| Clayey Sand | Fails gradation criteria | Above A-line | SC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gradation Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete gradation analysis process, including decision branches based on particle size and plasticity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Soil Sample Received] --> B[Weigh Oven-Dried Soil Sample]
    B --> C{Particle Size > 75 micron?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Stack of IS Sieves: 4.75mm to 75um]
    D --> E[Mechanical Sieve Shaking 10 to 15 min]
    E --> F[Weigh Retained Mass on Each Sieve]
    F --> G[Compute Percentage Finer N]
    C -- No --> H[Hydrometer Analysis: Stokes Law]
    H --> I[Record Readings at 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, 1440 min]
    I --> J[Compute D and N for Each Reading]
    G --> K[Plot Grain Size Curve: log D vs N]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Read D10, D30, D60 from Curve]
    L --> M[Compute Cu and Cc]
    M --> N{Cu and Cc within Range?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Classify as Well Graded]
    N -- No --> P[Check Atterberg Limits]
    P --> Q{Above or Below A Line?}
    Q -- Above A Line --> R[Clay Dominant: SC or GC]
    Q -- Below A Line --> S[Silt Dominant: SM or GM]
    O --> T[Final IS Classification]
    R --> T
    S --> T
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Sieve Analysis Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Sample 500g] --> B[Top Sieve 4.75mm]
    B --> C[Sieve 2.00mm]
    C --> D[Sieve 0.425mm]
    D --> E[Sieve 0.075mm]
    E --> F[Pan]
    F --> G[Mechanical Shaker]
    G --> H[Mass Retained per Sieve]
    H --> I[Percent Finer N]
    I --> J[Grain Size Curve]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix Diagram: Well-Graded vs Poorly-Graded vs Gap-Graded

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A1[Well Graded Soil]
    A1a[Wide Range of Particle Sizes]
    A1b[Smaller Particles Fill Voids of Larger]
    A1c[High Density and Strength]
    A1d[Cu Greater Than 4 or 6]
    A1e[Cc Between 1 and 3]
    end

    subgraph B1[Poorly Graded Soil]
    B1a[Uniformly Graded: Cu Near 1]
    B1b[Gap Graded: Cc Outside 1 to 3]
    B1c[High Voids, Low Density]
    B1d[Poor Packing]
    B1e[Unsuitable for Foundations]
    end

    subgraph C1[Uniformly Graded Soil]
    C1a[All Particles Same Size]
    C1b[High Permeability]
    C1c[Used as Filter Material]
    C1d[Symbol: SP or GP]
    C1e[Cu Less Than 4 or 6]
    end
```

### 4.4 Sedimentation Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Soil Suspension in Water] --> P2[Larger Particles Settle Fast]
    P2 --> P3[Smaller Particles Settle Slow]
    P3 --> P4[Hydrometer Measures Density at Depth H]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Stokes Law: D equals sqrt of 18 mu H over rho s minus rho w times g times t]
    P5 --> P6[Compute N from Hydrometer Reading]
    P6 --> P7[Plot on Grain Size Curve]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms: (a) Effective size $D_{10}$, (b) Coefficient of uniformity $C_u$, and (c) Coefficient of curvature $C_c$.

> **Model Answer:**
> (a) **Effective Size $D_{10}$:** The particle diameter (in mm) on the grain size curve corresponding to 10% finer. It is called "effective" because the permeability of clean sand is approximately proportional to $(D_{10})^2$ (Hazen's formula).
>
> (b) **Coefficient of Uniformity $C_u$:** Defined as $C_u = D_{60} / D_{10}$. It is a numerical measure of the range of particle sizes in a soil. A higher $C_u$ means a wider range and a more well-graded soil.
>
> (c) **Coefficient of Curvature $C_c$:** Defined as $C_c = (D_{30})^2 / (D_{10} \cdot D_{60})$. It measures the shape of the grain size curve between $D_{10}$ and $D_{60}$ and identifies gap-graded soils.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between well-graded, poorly-graded, and gap-graded soils with reference to $C_u$ and $C_c$.

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Soil Type | $C_u$ Criteria | $C_c$ Criteria | Distinguishing Feature |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Well-Graded | $C_u > 4$ (gravel) or $C_u > 6$ (sand) | $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ | Wide range of particle sizes; smaller particles fill voids of larger ones |
> | Poorly-Graded (Uniform) | $C_u$ is small (close to 1) | Not relevant | Most particles are of nearly the same size; high void ratio |
> | Gap-Graded | $C_u$ may be high | $C_c < 1$ or $C_c > 3$ | Missing intermediate particle sizes; cannot achieve full density |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure for sieve analysis of a coarse-grained soil as per IS 2720 Part 4. Mention any two precautions to be taken during the test.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Apparatus Required:** Set of IS sieves (4.75 mm, 2.00 mm, 1.00 mm, 0.600 mm, 0.425 mm, 0.300 mm, 0.150 mm, 0.075 mm), sieve lid, sieve pan, mechanical sieve shaker, weighing balance (accuracy 0.1 g), oven, brush.
>
> **Procedure:**
>
> 1. **Sample Preparation:** Take a representative soil sample, oven-dry it at 105°C to 110°C for 24 hours, and allow it to cool. Weigh approximately **500 g to 1000 g** of the dry soil (for gravelly soils, take 5 kg).
> 2. **Sieve Stack Assembly:** Arrange the sieves in **descending order of aperture size** — the largest sieve (4.75 mm) on top and the smallest (0.075 mm) at the bottom. Place a clean pan at the very bottom.
> 3. **Loading:** Pour the weighed soil sample onto the top sieve carefully. Fit a **lid on the top** to prevent loss of material.
> 4. **Shaking:** Place the entire assembly on the mechanical sieve shaker and operate for **10 to 15 minutes** continuously.
> 5. **Weighing:** Remove the sieves one by one from the top. Weigh the soil retained on each sieve accurately to **0.1 g**. Also weigh any soil collected in the pan.
> 6. **Verification:** The total mass retained plus the mass in the pan should equal the original mass (within 1% tolerance). If not, the test must be repeated.
> 7. **Calculations:** Compute the **percentage retained** on each sieve as: $\% \text{ Retained} = (M_{sieve}/M_{total}) \times 100$. Compute the **cumulative percentage retained**. Compute the **percentage finer N** as $N = 100 - \text{Cumulative \% Retained}$.
> 8. **Plotting:** Draw the grain size curve with **logarithm of particle size (mm)** on the X-axis and **percentage finer (N)** on the Y-axis (semi-log plot).
>
> **Precautions:**
> - Sieves must be **clean, dry, and free of damage**. Check sieves visually before use.
> - The sample must be **thoroughly oven-dried**; moisture causes clumping and erroneous results.
> - The sieving time should not be excessive (more than 15 minutes may cause sieve damage) and not too short (less than 10 minutes may give incomplete separation).
> - Sieves must be **tapped and brushed gently** to dislodge any stuck particles into the next sieve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The results of a sieve analysis on a soil sample are as follows. Draw the grain size curve and determine the effective size, $C_u$, and $C_c$. Classify the soil.

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|
| 4.75 | 0 |
| 2.00 | 80 |
| 0.425 | 220 |
| 0.150 | 250 |
| 0.075 | 150 |
| Pan | 50 |

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1: Compute percentage retained, cumulative % retained, and % finer N**
>
> | Sieve (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Finer (N) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 4.75 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 100 |
> | 2.00 | 80 | 10.67 | 10.67 | 89.33 |
> | 0.425 | 220 | 29.33 | 40.00 | 60.00 |
> | 0.150 | 250 | 33.33 | 73.33 | 26.67 |
> | 0.075 | 150 | 20.00 | 93.33 | 6.67 |
> | Pan | 50 | 6.67 | 100.00 | 0.00 |
> | **Total** | **750** | | | |
>
> **Step 2: Read $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$ from the grain size curve (semi-log plot)**
>
> - $D_{10}$ (at 10% finer): read from the curve → approximately **0.08 mm**
> - $D_{30}$ (at 30% finer): read from the curve → approximately **0.16 mm**
> - $D_{60}$ (at 60% finer): read from the curve → approximately **0.42 mm**
>
> **Step 3: Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$**
>
> $$C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.42}{0.08} = 5.25$$
>
> $$C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.16)^2}{0.08 \times 0.42} = \frac{0.0256}{0.0336} = 0.76$$
>
> **Step 4: Classification**
>
> Since the soil has more than 50% passing 0.075 mm — wait, 6.67% passing only, so it is a **sand**. For well-graded sand: $C_u \geq 6$ and $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$. Here $C_u = 5.25 < 6$ and $C_c = 0.76 < 1$. **Therefore, the soil is classified as Poorly-Graded Sand (SP).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of hydrometer analysis of soils. Derive Stokes' law and the expression used to compute the diameter of soil particles in suspension.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Principle:**
> Hydrometer analysis is used to determine the grain size distribution of fine-grained soils (silt and clay, particles < 75 µm). It is based on **Stokes' law**, which states that the terminal velocity of a sphere settling in a viscous fluid is proportional to the square of its diameter. By measuring the density of the soil-water suspension at a known depth and time, the diameter of the largest particle still in suspension at that depth can be computed.
>
> **Apparatus:** Hydrometer (calibrated to read specific gravity of suspension), 1000 mL glass measuring cylinder, dispersing agent (sodium hexametaphosphate, 4 g/L), mechanical stirrer, thermometer, stopwatch.
>
> **Procedure Outline:**
> 1. Take **50 g of oven-dried soil** (passing 75 µm) and mix with 125 mL of dispersing agent solution.
> 2. Stir the suspension for 10 minutes and transfer to the 1000 mL cylinder. Top up with distilled water to the 1000 mL mark.
> 3. Insert the hydrometer and take readings at **0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, and 1440 minutes** from the start of sedimentation.
> 4. Record the temperature at each reading.
> 5. Apply **Stokes' law** to compute the particle diameter $D$ corresponding to each reading.
>
> **Derivation of Stokes' Law** (full derivation as in Section 3.1):
>
> - At terminal velocity, **Buoyant weight = Viscous drag**.
> - $\rho_s g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 - \rho_w g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = 3 \pi \mu v D$
> - Solving: $v = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g D^2}{18 \mu}$
> - Since $v = H/t$: $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g t}}$
>
> **Limitations of Stokes' Law:**
> - Holds only for laminar flow (Reynolds number < 1). Soil particles > 0.2 mm do not satisfy this.
> - Assumes particles are perfect spheres (soil particles are not).
> - Requires a **stoke's correction factor (≈ 0.85)** to account for the non-spherical shape.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrometer test was conducted on a fine soil sample. At a depth of 10 cm below the suspension surface, the temperature of the suspension was 27°C. The hydrometer reading was 1.020. Compute the equivalent particle diameter. Given: $G_s = 2.70$, viscosity of water $\mu = 0.0009$ Pa·s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:**
> - Depth $H = 10$ cm = 0.10 m
> - Time $t$ — assumed for the question = **1 hour** = 3600 s (standard reading time after 1 hr)
> - Specific gravity of soil $G_s = 2.70$
> - Viscosity $\mu = 0.0009$ Pa·s
> - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - Density of water $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
> - Density of soil $\rho_s = 2.70 \times 1000 = 2700$ kg/m³
>
> **Step 1: Apply Stokes' formula**
>
> $$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu H}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g t}}$$
>
> **Step 2: Substitute values**
>
> $$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.0009 \times 0.10}{(2700 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 3600}}$$
>
> **Step 3: Compute the numerator**
>
> $$\text{Numerator} = 18 \times 0.0009 \times 0.10 = 0.00162$$
>
> **Step 4: Compute the denominator**
>
> $$\text{Denominator} = 1700 \times 9.81 \times 3600 = 1700 \times 9.81 = 16677; \quad 16677 \times 3600 = 60,037,200$$
>
> **Step 5: Compute $D$**
>
> $$D = \sqrt{\frac{0.00162}{60,037,200}} = \sqrt{2.698 \times 10^{-11}}$$
>
> $$D = 5.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00519 \text{ mm} = 5.19 \text{ µm}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The particles still in suspension after 1 hour at depth 10 cm have a maximum diameter of approximately **5.19 µm**, which falls in the **clay fraction (< 2 µm is clay, 2 µm–75 µm is silt; but 5.19 µm is silt, not clay)** — *correction: 5.19 µm lies in the silt range (2 µm to 75 µm)*. This shows that after 1 hour, all particles larger than 5.19 µm have settled below the hydrometer bulb.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take logarithms on the X-axis:** The grain size curve is a **semi-log plot**. Students often draw a linear plot, leading to incorrect readings of $D_{10}$, $D_{30}$, $D_{60}$, and consequently wrong $C_u$ and $C_c$. This typically costs **3 to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Not reconciling total mass:** After sieve analysis, the sum of masses retained on all sieves plus the pan should equal the original mass. If there is a discrepancy > 1%, the test must be repeated. Examiners often deduct marks if this check is not mentioned.
> 3. **Confusion between $C_u$ and $C_c$ criteria for gravel vs. sand:** $C_u > 4$ applies to **gravels**, while $C_u > 6$ applies to **sands**. Using the wrong threshold will misclassify the soil.
> 4. **Mixing up units in Stokes' law:** Students commonly fail to convert $H$ in cm and $t$ in minutes into consistent SI units (m and s). The diameter $D$ must be in metres first, then converted to mm. Unit errors alone can deduct **2 marks**.
> 5. **Neglecting the meniscus correction** in hydrometer readings: The hydrometer reading should be taken at the **top of the meniscus**, and a meniscus correction (typically +0.5 to +1.0) added. Skipping this leads to systematic error in computed $D$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gradation** is the distribution of particle sizes in a soil, expressed as the percentage finer ($N$) versus particle diameter ($D$).
- **Sieve analysis** is used for particles **> 75 µm** (coarse-grained soils); **Hydrometer analysis** is used for particles **< 75 µm** (fine-grained soils).
- The **grain size curve** is a **semi-log plot**: log of particle size on X-axis, percentage finer on Y-axis.
- **Effective size $D_{10}$** is read at 10% finer. It is correlated with permeability by Hazen's formula $k \approx C \cdot (D_{10})^2$.
- **Coefficient of Uniformity $C_u = D_{60} / D_{10}$** measures the range of particle sizes.
- **Coefficient of Curvature $C_c = (D_{30})^2 / (D_{10} \cdot D_{60})$** measures the shape of the gradation curve.
- **Well-graded criteria:** $C_u \geq 4$ (gravels) or $C_u \geq 6$ (sands), AND $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
- **Gap-graded soils** have $C_c < 1$ or $C_c > 3$, even if $C_u$ is large.
- **Stokes' law** derivation hinges on the balance of **buoyant weight** and **viscous drag** at terminal velocity.
- The hydrometer diameter formula: $D = \sqrt{18 \mu H / [(\rho_s - \rho_w) g t]}$.
- **Stoke's law validity:** Reynolds number < 1 (laminar flow), applicable only for fine particles < 0.2 mm.
- **IS 2720 Part 4** is the governing code for sieve analysis in India.
- **Final IS classification** uses gradation criteria *combined with* Atterberg limits (plasticity chart).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Sieve and Hydrometer Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Combined Sieve and Hydrometer Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: COMBINED SIEVE AND HYDROMETER ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Sieve and Hydrometer Analysis** is the standard laboratory procedure used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **complete particle-size distribution (gradation curve)** of a soil mass, integrating two complementary tests: the **sieve analysis** (for the coarse fraction, particles coarser than **75 µm** which is the **IS Standard Sieve No. 200** / ASTM No. 200) and the **hydrometer (sedimentation) analysis** (for the fine fraction, particles finer than **75 µm** down to the **clay-size threshold of about 0.2 µm**).

The result is a single **grain-size distribution curve**, plotted on a **semi-logarithmic graph** with the **ordinate** as the **percentage finer by weight (N, %)** and the **abscissa** as the **particle diameter D (mm) on a logarithmic scale**. This combined curve is the fundamental input to the **IS 1498–1970 (Reaffirmed 2020)** soil classification system and to the computation of **coefficients of uniformity (Cᵤ)** and **coefficients of curvature (C꜀)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef trying to describe a spice mix. The mix contains large chunks (peppercorns), medium grains (mustard seeds), fine dust (turmeric powder), and ultra-fine powder (cinnamon). You cannot use a kitchen strainer to separate the dust from the cinnamon — they both pass through. You need two tools:

- **A stack of sieves with progressively smaller holes** to separate the coarse spices (this is the **sieve analysis**, useful for sizes down to **75 µm = 0.075 mm**).
- **A long glass cylinder filled with water** to let the fine particles settle at different speeds, because the smaller (lighter) particles settle slower and the larger (heavier) particles settle faster (this is the **hydrometer analysis**, governed by **Stokes' Law**).

**Combining both gives you the complete "recipe" of your soil — from gravel-sized to clay-sized particles on a single chart.** This is what the KTU syllabus calls the **Combined Grain-Size Distribution Curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why combine?** No single test covers the entire range. Sieves cannot practically handle particles finer than 75 µm (clogging + static charge). Hydrometers cannot reliably measure particles coarser than 75 µm (they settle too fast to be timed accurately at the start). Hence, the two tests are **complementary and overlapping at the 75 µm boundary**.

### 1.3 The Size Boundaries (KTU Classification Bands)

| Soil Fraction | Diameter Range | Test Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Boulder / Cobble** | > 300 mm | Field / calliper measurement |
| **Gravel (G)** | 4.75 mm – 80 mm | Sieve Analysis |
| **Sand (S)** | 0.075 mm – 4.75 mm | Sieve Analysis |
| **Silt (M)** | 0.002 mm – 0.075 mm | Hydrometer Analysis |
| **Clay (C)** | < 0.002 mm (< 2 µm) | Hydrometer Analysis |

### 1.4 The 75 µm Boundary Sieve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IS Sieve 75 µm (No. 200)** is the **bridge sieve** — every combined analysis pivots on the mass retained on it. The soil passing this sieve is the **"fines fraction"** that is transferred quantitatively to the sedimentation cylinder. The mass retained on it is the **"coarse fraction"** that is sieved through the full stack of coarse sieves.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Part Philosophy

### Part A — Sieve Analysis (Coarse Fraction)
The dry soil is passed through a stack of standard IS sieves arranged in **descending order of aperture** (largest at top, smallest at bottom, **pan at the very bottom**). The stack is mechanically shaken for **10–15 minutes** on a **sieve shaker**. The mass retained on each sieve is measured, and the following are computed:

- **Mass retained** on sieve $i$: $M_i$ (grams)
- **Cumulative mass retained** up to sieve $i$: $\sum M_i$
- **Cumulative percentage retained**: $P_i = \dfrac{\sum M_i}{M_{total}} \times 100$
- **Percentage finer than sieve $i$**: $N_i = 100 - P_i$

### Part B — Hydrometer Analysis (Fine Fraction)
The fines passing the **75 µm sieve** are mixed with **distilled water + 125 mL of dispersing agent (sodium hexametaphosphate / Calgon at 40 g/L concentration)** in a **1000 mL sedimentation cylinder**. The suspension is agitated, and a **Bouyoucos hydrometer** (calibrated to read in g/L of suspension density) is inserted. Readings are taken at **standard elapsed times**: **0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, and 1440 minutes**.

## 2.2 Stokes' Law — The Heart of the Hydrometer Test

A spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid reaches a constant **terminal velocity** when the **gravitational force** is balanced by the **buoyant force** and the **viscous drag** (Stokes, 1851). The terminal velocity $v$ is:

$$v = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \, \mu} \, g \, D^2$$

where:
- $v$ = terminal (settling) velocity, m/s
- $\rho_s$ = mass density of soil particle, kg/m³
- $\rho_w$ = mass density of water at test temperature, kg/m³
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water, N·s/m² (Pa·s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, **9.81 m/s²**
- $D$ = diameter of the equivalent sphere (the soil particle), m

> [!NOTE]
> Stokes' Law is valid only when flow is **laminar** (Reynolds number Re < 1) and particles are **smaller than ~75 µm**. This is precisely why the test is restricted to the **fines fraction**.

Since the particle travels a distance $H$ (effective depth) in time $t$, we have $v = H/t$. Substituting and solving for $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{g \, t \, (\rho_s - \rho_w)} }$$

In soil mechanics, the term $(\rho_s - \rho_w)$ is often written using **unit weights** $\gamma_s$ and $\gamma_w$ as $(G_s - 1)\gamma_w$:

$$D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t \, (G_s - 1) \, \gamma_w} } \quad \text{(in consistent SI units)}$$

For hydrometer readings using the **standard 1000 mL cylinder**, the **effective depth $H$ (cm)** corresponding to a hydrometer reading $R_h$ is given by the standard calibration relation (IS 2720 Part 4):

$$H = -0.5 \, R_h + 16.3 \quad \text{(for the standard IS Bouyoucos hydrometer, in cm)}$$

(Other versions exist; always check the calibration chart supplied with the instrument.)

## 2.3 The Percentage Finer Equation (Hydrometer)

The percentage of soil particles finer than the calculated diameter $D$ is:

$$N \, (\%) = \frac{R_h^{corr}}{M_d} \times 100$$

where $R_h^{corr}$ is the **fully corrected hydrometer reading** (after meniscus, temperature, and dispersing agent corrections) and $M_d$ is the **dry mass of soil actually used in the 1000 mL cylinder** (typically **50 g** or **100 g**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for fast revision. Note the use of `\mid` (instead of vertical pipes) to prevent markdown breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Percentage finer (sieve) | $N = \dfrac{M_{passing}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Cumulative % retained | $P_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} M_j}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Stokes' terminal velocity | $v = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w)\,g\,D^2}{18\,\mu}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Particle diameter from Stokes | $D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{18\,\mu\,H}{t\,(\rho_s - \rho_w)\,g} }$ | m |
| 5 | Equivalent using $G_s$ | $D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{18\,\mu\,H}{t\,(G_s - 1)\,\gamma_w} }$ | m |
| 6 | Effective depth (Bouyoucos) | $H = -0.5\,R_h + 16.3$ | cm |
| 7 | Percentage finer (hydrometer) | $N = \dfrac{R_h^{corr}}{M_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Meniscus correction | $R_{true} = R_{obs} + C_m$ | additive, typically +0.5 to +1.0 |
| 9 | Temperature correction | $R_{corr} = R_{true} + C_T$ | varies with $T$ (ref. 27 °C) |
| 10 | Dispersing agent correction | $R_{corr} = R_{true} - C_d$ | subtract blank reading |
| 11 | Coefficient of uniformity | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Coefficient of curvature | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation design**: Granular soils with $C_u > 4$ (gravel) or $C_u > 6$ (sand) and $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ are **well-graded** and pack densely, giving high bearing capacity. Poorly graded soils ($C_u < 4$) need stabilization.
- **Filter design in earth dams**: The grain-size distribution ensures the filter material is fine enough to retain the base soil but coarse enough not to clog (Terzaghi's filter criteria: $\frac{D_{15,filter}}{D_{85,base}} \leq 4$).
- **Frost susceptibility classification**: Soils with more than **3% finer than 20 µm** are frost-susceptible.
- **Compaction control**: Fine-grained content directly affects the **OMC (Optimum Moisture Content)** and **MDD (Maximum Dry Density)**.
- **Pavement subgrade**: The grain-size curve determines whether the soil qualifies as **G1, G2, ... G12** (IRC / MORTH classification).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Diameter Equation (Stokes' Law → Sedimentation Formula)

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Falling Spherical Particle

Three forces act on a spherical soil particle falling through water:
1. **Weight (downward)**: $W = \gamma_s \cdot V = \gamma_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$
2. **Buoyancy (upward)**: $F_b = \gamma_w \cdot V = \gamma_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$
3. **Viscous drag (upward)**: $F_d = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,D\,v$ (Stokes' drag for laminar flow)

### Step 2: Equilibrium at Terminal Velocity

At terminal velocity, the net force is zero:

$$W - F_b - F_d = 0$$

### Step 3: Algebraic Substitution

$$\gamma_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{6} - \gamma_w \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{6} - 3\,\pi\,\mu\,D\,v = 0$$

Factor out $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$:

$$\frac{\pi D^3}{6} \left( \gamma_s - \gamma_w \right) = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,D\,v$$

Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\frac{D^2}{6} \left( \gamma_s - \gamma_w \right) = 3\,\mu\,v$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v$

$$v = \frac{D^2 \left( \gamma_s - \gamma_w \right)}{18 \, \mu}$$

This is Stokes' velocity in terms of **unit weights** ($\gamma$). To convert to **mass densities** (for SI), divide each $\gamma$ by $g$:

$$v = \frac{g \, D^2 \left( \rho_s - \rho_w \right)}{18 \, \mu}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Particle Diameter $D$

Since $v = H / t$ (distance over time), substitute:

$$\frac{H}{t} = \frac{g \, D^2 \left( \rho_s - \rho_w \right)}{18 \, \mu}$$

Solve for $D$:

$$D^2 = \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t \, g \, \left( \rho_s - \rho_w \right)}$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t \, g \, \left( \rho_s - \rho_w \right)} } }$$

In the alternative form using the **specific gravity $G_s$** and the **unit weight of water $\gamma_w$**:

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t \, (G_s - 1) \, \gamma_w} } }$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **governing equation** for every hydrometer problem. The KTU board will usually give you a value of $\mu$ at the test temperature, $G_s$ of the soil, and the hydrometer calibration. You then compute $D$ at each elapsed time and pair it with the percentage finer to plot the curve.

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problem

### **Problem Statement**

> A combined sieve and hydrometer analysis was conducted on a soil sample. The total dry mass used was **500 g** for the sieve test and **50 g** (of the fraction finer than 75 µm) for the hydrometer test. The specific gravity of soil solids is **$G_s = 2.70$**. The test temperature was **27 °C**, for which $\mu = 8.5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m² and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. The blank (meniscus + dispersing agent) correction is **$C_{blank} = +1.2$** and the temperature correction at 27 °C is **0** (reference). Use $H = -0.5 R_h + 16.3$ cm.
>
> **Sieve data** (partial):

| Sieve (mm) | Mass retained (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 4.75 | 25 |
| 2.00 | 60 |
| 0.425 | 95 |
| 0.075 | 120 |
| Pan | 200 |

> **Hydrometer data** (partial):

| Elapsed time $t$ (min) | Hydrometer reading $R_h$ (uncorrected, g/L) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.5 | 38 |
| 1 | 34 |
| 4 | 30 |
| 30 | 24 |
| 120 | 18 |
| 1440 | 8 |

**Required:**
1. Compute the percentage finer from the sieve analysis.
2. Compute the percentage finer and the equivalent particle diameter $D$ at each hydrometer reading time.
3. Find $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$ and compute $C_u$ and $C_c$.

---

### **Solution**

#### Step 1: Sieve Analysis Computation

Total mass = 500 g. Compute cumulative mass retained, cumulative % retained, and % finer for each sieve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4.75 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{25}{500} \times 100 = 5.0\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 95.0\% \\
\text{2.00 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{25+60}{500} \times 100 = 17.0\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 83.0\% \\
\text{0.425 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{25+60+95}{500} \times 100 = 36.0\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 64.0\% \\
\text{0.075 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{25+60+95+120}{500} \times 100 = 60.0\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 40.0\% \\
\text{Pan:} \quad & P = \frac{500}{500} \times 100 = 100\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that **40% of the soil is finer than 75 µm** and was transferred to the hydrometer. The 200 g in the pan is the "fines" used in the hydrometer test on a mass-percentage basis. However, the **actual dry mass in the hydrometer cylinder is 50 g**, so the percentage-finer formula uses that 50 g.

#### Step 2: Hydrometer Corrections

The **corrected hydrometer reading** $R_h^{corr}$ = (uncorrected reading) + $C_{blank}$ (since the blank correction includes both meniscus and dispersing agent):

$$
R_h^{corr} = R_h + 1.2
$$

#### Step 3: Effective Depth (in metres, for SI units)

Convert $H$ from cm to m: $H(m) = \dfrac{H(cm)}{100}$.

$$H = \frac{-0.5 \, R_h + 16.3}{100} \;\;\text{[m]}$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Particle Diameter $D$ at Each Time

The working formula (convert time to seconds: $t_s = 60 \times t_{min}$):

$$D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t_s \, (G_s - 1) \, \gamma_w} }$$

Substitute constants: $\mu = 8.5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m², $(G_s - 1) = 1.70$, $\gamma_w = 9810$ N/m³.

For $t = 0.5$ min, $R_h = 38$:
- $R_h^{corr} = 38 + 1.2 = 39.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(39.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{-3.3}{100}$ — **negative**, which is **unphysical**!

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative $H$ means the hydrometer is physically above the suspension surface, so this reading is invalid (the hydrometer is essentially "floating" near the top).** In KTU board problems, this happens when the uncorrected reading is very high. The standard practice is to **cap the maximum valid reading at the value that makes $H \geq 0$**, which is $R_h \leq 32.6$ for this calibration. Therefore, in a real test, the data would be re-recorded or the first reading dropped.

Let us **re-cast the problem assuming valid readings** (i.e., cap $R_h$ at 32 max for safety — for KTU numericals, we will use the data as given but note that any negative $H$ makes the row "unusable" for that time instant). For the **first valid row** at $t = 1$ min, $R_h = 34$:

- $R_h^{corr} = 34 + 1.2 = 35.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(35.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{-1.3}{100}$ — still negative!

We must use $R_h = 30$ (valid):

- $R_h^{corr} = 30 + 1.2 = 31.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(31.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{0.7}{100} = 0.007$ m

For $t = 4$ min:
- $R_h^{corr} = 30 + 1.2 = 31.2$ → wait, problem says $R_h = 30$ at $t = 4$
- $R_h^{corr} = 31.2$, $H = 0.007$ m

For $t = 30$ min, $R_h = 24$:
- $R_h^{corr} = 25.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(25.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{3.7}{100} = 0.037$ m

For $t = 120$ min, $R_h = 18$:
- $R_h^{corr} = 19.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(19.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{6.7}{100} = 0.067$ m

For $t = 1440$ min, $R_h = 8$:
- $R_h^{corr} = 9.2$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(9.2) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{11.7}{100} = 0.117$ m

Compute $D$ for each valid time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 4 \text{ min} \;(t_s = 240 \text{ s}): \quad D &= \sqrt{ \frac{18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.007}{240 \times 1.70 \times 9810} } \\
&= \sqrt{ \frac{1.071 \times 10^{-4}}{4.003 \times 10^{6}} } = \sqrt{ 2.676 \times 10^{-11} } \\
&= 5.17 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00517 \text{ mm} \approx 5.17 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 30 \text{ min} \;(t_s = 1800 \text{ s}): \quad D &= \sqrt{ \frac{18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.037}{1800 \times 1.70 \times 9810} } \\
&= \sqrt{ \frac{5.661 \times 10^{-4}}{3.002 \times 10^{7}} } = \sqrt{ 1.886 \times 10^{-11} } \\
&= 4.34 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00434 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 120 \text{ min} \;(t_s = 7200 \text{ s}): \quad D &= \sqrt{ \frac{18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.067}{7200 \times 1.70 \times 9810} } \\
&= \sqrt{ \frac{1.025 \times 10^{-3}}{1.201 \times 10^{8}} } = \sqrt{ 8.535 \times 10^{-12} } \\
&= 2.92 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00292 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 1440 \text{ min} \;(t_s = 86400 \text{ s}): \quad D &= \sqrt{ \frac{18 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.117}{86400 \times 1.70 \times 9810} } \\
&= \sqrt{ \frac{1.790 \times 10^{-3}}{1.441 \times 10^{9}} } = \sqrt{ 1.243 \times 10^{-12} } \\
&= 1.115 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.00112 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute the Percentage Finer $N$

$$N = \frac{R_h^{corr}}{M_d} \times 100 = \frac{R_h^{corr}}{50} \times 100 = 2 \times R_h^{corr}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 4 \text{ min:} \quad N &= 2 \times 31.2 = 62.4\% \\
\text{At } t = 30 \text{ min:} \quad N &= 2 \times 25.2 = 50.4\% \\
\text{At } t = 120 \text{ min:} \quad N &= 2 \times 19.2 = 38.4\% \\
\text{At } t = 1440 \text{ min:} \quad N &= 2 \times 9.2 = 18.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The percentages above are the **percentage of the hydrometer sample (50 g of fines) that is finer than $D$**. To express them as a **percentage of the total original soil (500 g)**, multiply by the proportion of fines in the original soil: $\dfrac{200}{500} = 0.40$. For example, the 62.4% of fines finer than 5.17 µm corresponds to **62.4 × 0.40 = 24.96% of the total soil**. This **scaling step is critical** in combined analysis and is the most common error students make in the KTU exam.

#### Step 6: Combined Analysis Table (Stitching the Curves)

| Sieve / Time | $D$ (mm) | % Finer (of total soil) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 4.75 mm | 4.75 | 95.0 |
| 2.00 mm | 2.00 | 83.0 |
| 0.425 mm | 0.425 | 64.0 |
| 0.075 mm | 0.075 | 40.0 |
| $t = 4$ min | 0.00517 | 24.96 |
| $t = 30$ min | 0.00434 | 20.16 |
| $t = 120$ min | 0.00292 | 15.36 |
| $t = 1440$ min | 0.00112 | 7.36 |

#### Step 7: Read $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$ from the Smoothed Curve

From the semi-log plot (constructed by the student):
- $D_{10} \approx 0.0015$ mm
- $D_{30} \approx 0.0040$ mm
- $D_{60} \approx 0.080$ mm (slightly past 0.075; reads from the curve at 60% finer)

#### Step 8: Compute Gradation Coefficients

$$
C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.080}{0.0015} = 53.33
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.0040)^2}{0.0015 \times 0.080} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.133
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $C_u > 4$ (sand criterion) but $C_c = 0.133 < 1$, the sand fraction is **poorly graded (gap-graded)**. This indicates a deficiency of intermediate-sized particles, often a sign of an **alluvial or fill material** with a bimodal distribution.

---

## 3.3 Three Mandatory Corrections in Hydrometer Readings

| Correction | Reason | How to apply |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Meniscus correction $C_m$** | Hydrometer is read at the **top of the meniscus** (lower reading), but the scale is calibrated at the **bottom of the meniscus** (true reading). The difference is added. | $R_{true} = R_{obs} + C_m$ (always **+**ve) |
| **Temperature correction $C_T$** | Standard temperature is **27 °C** (or 20 °C in some standards). If actual $T$ is higher, water is less dense → particles settle faster → reading is lower than true. | Add $C_T$ if $T > 27$ °C; subtract if $T < 27$ °C |
| **Dispersing agent correction $C_d$** | Calgon solution itself adds density. A "blank" cylinder with only water + Calgon is read separately. | Subtract $C_d$ from the observed reading |

The **composite correction** is:

$$R_h^{corr} = R_{obs} + C_m + C_T - C_d$$

---
