# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Why is the **75 µm sieve** (IS Sieve No. 200) considered the **boundary sieve** in a combined grain-size analysis? What happens to the soil passing this sieve?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **75 µm IS sieve** is the boundary sieve because it is the **smallest practical aperture** for mechanical sieving — finer sieves tend to clog, and particles below this size carry static charge. The soil passing this sieve (the **fines fraction**, consisting of silt and clay-sized particles) cannot be graded by sieving and is therefore transferred to a **1000 mL sedimentation cylinder** for **hydrometer analysis** based on **Stokes' Law of settling velocity**. The soil retained on the 75 µm sieve (coarse fraction: gravel + sand) is graded by the standard **sieve stack** from 4.75 mm down to 75 µm. The two curves are then **stitched** at the 75 µm boundary to obtain the **complete combined grain-size distribution curve**.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State **Stokes' Law** as applied to the hydrometer test. Why must the test be limited to particles finer than **75 µm**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Stokes' Law** states that the **terminal settling velocity** $v$ of a small sphere falling under gravity in a viscous fluid is given by $v = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \, g \, D^2}{18 \, \mu}$, where $D$ is the particle diameter. The test is restricted to particles finer than **75 µm** because (a) **laminar flow** (Reynolds number Re < 1) is required for the law to hold, which is true only for very small particles; (b) coarser particles settle too quickly (in seconds) for the hydrometer to be inserted and read accurately; and (c) the **hydrometer reading** is calibrated on the assumption that the suspension behaves as a dilute colloidal system, which breaks down for coarse particles.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A combined sieve and hydrometer analysis was performed on a soil sample. The dry mass of soil used in the sieve test was **600 g** and the dry mass used in the hydrometer cylinder was **50 g** (fines fraction). The specific gravity of solids was **$G_s = 2.65$**, the test temperature was **30 °C** ($\mu = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m², $\gamma_w = 9.79$ kN/m³), and the hydrometer calibration was $H = -0.5 R_h + 16.3$ cm. The blank correction was **+1.5** and the temperature correction at 30 °C was **+1.0**.

Sieve data:

| Sieve (mm) | Mass retained (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 4.75 | 30 |
| 2.00 | 75 |
| 0.425 | 90 |
| 0.075 | 165 |
| Pan | 240 |

Hydrometer data:

| $t$ (min) | $R_h$ (g/L) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 30 |
| 15 | 26 |
| 60 | 20 |
| 240 | 14 |

Compute the **percentage finer** from the sieve analysis and verify the percentage of fines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the hydrometer readings, compute the **corrected hydrometer reading, effective depth, particle diameter $D$ at each time, and the percentage finer of the total soil** (remembering the mass scaling). Plot the combined curve description and estimate **$D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$**, then compute **$C_u$ and $C_c$**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for performing a **combined sieve and hydrometer analysis** in the laboratory, including the apparatus required, the **dispersing agent** used and its purpose, the standard elapsed times for hydrometer readings, and the three **corrections** that must be applied to the raw hydrometer readings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrometer test is conducted at **20 °C** on a soil with **$G_s = 2.70$** using **50 g** of dry fines. The hydrometer reading at **$t = 60$ min** is **$R_h = 22$ g/L** (uncorrected). The meniscus correction is **+0.8**, the temperature correction at 20 °C (ref 27 °C) is **-1.2**, and the dispersing agent correction is **-0.5**. Using $H = -0.5 R_h + 16.3$ cm, $\mu_{20°C} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², and $\gamma_w = 9.79$ kN/m³, compute the **equivalent particle diameter $D$** and the **percentage finer** of the fines. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Worked Model Solutions (Complete Step-by-Step)

### **Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute % finer for each sieve:**

Total sieve mass = 30 + 75 + 90 + 165 + 240 = 600 g ✓

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4.75 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{30}{600} \times 100 = 5\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 95\% \\
\text{2.00 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{105}{600} \times 100 = 17.5\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 82.5\% \\
\text{0.425 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{195}{600} \times 100 = 32.5\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 67.5\% \\
\text{0.075 mm:} \quad & P = \frac{360}{600} \times 100 = 60.0\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 40.0\% \\
\text{Pan (fines):} \quad & P = \frac{600}{600} \times 100 = 100\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify fines proportion:**
The pan contains 240 g of fines, which is $\dfrac{240}{600} = 40\%$ of the total soil. This matches the % finer at 0.075 mm.

**Step 3 — Mark allocation (3 marks for tabulated calculation, 2 marks for the principle explanation, 2 marks for verification):**
- Correct cumulative % retained: 3 marks
- Correct % finer: 2 marks
- Verification of fines: 2 marks

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Corrected hydrometer reading:**
$R_h^{corr} = R_h + C_m + C_T - C_d = R_h + 1.5 + 1.0 - 0 = R_h + 2.5$

(Note: dispersing agent is already included in the blank correction of +1.5, so we don't subtract separately.)

**Step 2 — Effective depth $H$ (in metres):**

$$
H = \frac{-0.5 \, R_h^{corr} + 16.3}{100} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Particle diameter $D$:**

$$
D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \, \mu \, H}{t_s \, (G_s - 1) \, \gamma_w} }
$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate for each time instant:**

| $t$ (min) | $t_s$ (s) | $R_h$ | $R_h^{corr}$ | $H$ (m) | $D$ (m) | $D$ (mm) | $N_{fines}$ (%) | $N_{total}$ (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 120 | 30 | 32.5 | 0.00125 | 7.67e-6 | 0.00767 | 65.0 | 26.0 |
| 15 | 900 | 26 | 28.5 | 0.0205 | 1.92e-5 | 0.0192 | 57.0 | 22.8 |
| 60 | 3600 | 20 | 22.5 | 0.0505 | 1.51e-5 | 0.0151 | 45.0 | 18.0 |
| 240 | 14400 | 14 | 16.5 | 0.0805 | 9.55e-6 | 0.00955 | 33.0 | 13.2 |

**Detailed computation for $t = 2$ min:**
- $R_h^{corr} = 30 + 2.5 = 32.5$
- $H = \dfrac{-0.5(32.5) + 16.3}{100} = \dfrac{0.05}{100} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{18 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{120 \times 1.65 \times 9790} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.939 \times 10^{6}} } = \sqrt{ 3.713 \times 10^{-12} } = 1.927 \times 10^{-6}$ m ≈ 0.00193 mm

**Step 5 — % finer of total soil:** $N_{total} = N_{fines} \times \dfrac{M_{fines}}{M_{total}} = N_{fines} \times \dfrac{240}{600} = 0.40 \times N_{fines}$

**Step 6 — Estimate $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$:**
From the curve: $D_{10} \approx 0.0025$ mm, $D_{30} \approx 0.012$ mm, $D_{60} \approx 0.18$ mm

**Step 7 — Gradation coefficients:**

$$
C_u = \frac{0.18}{0.0025} = 72
$$

$$
C_c = \frac{(0.012)^2}{0.0025 \times 0.18} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.32
$$

Since $C_u > 6$ (sand criterion) and $C_c < 1$, the soil is **poorly graded (gap-graded sand)**.

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- Correction + $H$ calculation: 2 marks
- $D$ computation: 2 marks
- $N_{fines}$ and scaling to $N_{total}$: 2 marks
- $C_u, C_c$ and conclusion: 1 mark

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)**

**Complete Laboratory Procedure:**

1. **Sample preparation:** Take ~500 g of representative soil, oven-dry at 105–110 °C for 24 hours, then weigh $M_{total}$.
2. **Wet sieving through 75 µm sieve:** Wash the soil gently through the 75 µm sieve, collecting the **fines** in a large bowl. Dry the fines in the oven.
3. **Coarse fraction sieving:** Place the oven-dried coarse fraction on the top sieve of a stack arranged as: **4.75 mm, 2.00 mm, 0.425 mm, 0.212 mm, 0.075 mm, pan**. Shake for 10–15 min. Record mass retained on each sieve.
4. **Hydrometer setup:** Weigh **50 g** of the oven-dried fines. Transfer to a **1000 mL graduated sedimentation cylinder**. Add **125 mL of 4% sodium hexametaphosphate (Calgon) solution** as a dispersing agent to deflocculate clay particles. Top up with **distilled water to 1000 mL**.
5. **Agitation:** Stopper the cylinder, shake vigorously for **1 minute** (end-over-end), then place on a level surface and start the stopwatch immediately.
6. **Hydrometer readings:** Insert the Bouyoucos hydrometer and take readings at **0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, and 1440 minutes**. Remove and rinse the hydrometer between readings.
7. **Three corrections applied to each reading:**
   - **Meniscus correction ($C_m$):** added to observed reading.
   - **Temperature correction ($C_T$):** added if $T > 27$ °C, subtracted if $T < 27$ °C.
   - **Dispersing agent correction ($C_d$):** subtracted (from a blank cylinder containing only water + Calgon).
8. **Compute $D$ and % finer** using the formulas in this note, and combine with the sieve curve.

(7 marks: 2 for procedure, 2 for apparatus/dispersing agent, 1 for timing, 2 for corrections)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T = 20$ °C, $G_s = 2.70$, $M_d = 50$ g, $R_h = 22$, $C_m = +0.8$, $C_T = -1.2$, $C_d = -0.5$, $t = 60$ min = 3600 s, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², $\gamma_w = 9790$ N/m³.

**Step 1 — Apply corrections:**

$$
R_h^{corr} = 22 + 0.8 - 1.2 - 0.5 = 21.1
$$

(Note: the question presents the temperature correction as -1.2 directly added to the reading, and dispersing agent as -0.5. We follow the convention: $R_h^{corr} = R_{obs} + C_m + C_T - C_d$.)

**Step 2 — Effective depth:**

$$
H = \frac{-0.5(21.1) + 16.3}{100} = \frac{5.75}{100} = 0.0575 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Particle diameter:**

$$
D = \sqrt{ \frac{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0575}{3600 \times (2.70 - 1) \times 9790} }
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{ \frac{1.0371 \times 10^{-3}}{5.979 \times 10^{7}} } = \sqrt{ 1.735 \times 10^{-11} } = 4.165 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D \approx 0.00417 \text{ mm} = 4.17 \, \mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage finer of the fines:**

$$
N_{fines} = \frac{21.1}{50} \times 100 = 42.2\%
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- Correct composite correction: 1 mark
- Correct $H$: 1 mark
- Correct substitution and $D$: 3 marks
- Correct $N$: 1 mark
- Units and final answer: 1 mark

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons KTU students lose marks on Combined Analysis problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the mass scaling step.** The hydrometer percentage is **of the fines only**, not of the total soil. You MUST multiply by $\dfrac{M_{fines}}{M_{total}}$ to get the % finer for the combined curve. Failure to do this gives an inflated curve.
> 2. **Sign errors in the three corrections.** Meniscus is **always added**. Temperature correction is **added if $T > 27$ °C** and **subtracted if $T < 27$ °C**. Dispersing agent is **subtracted** (it adds to the density of the blank).
> 3. **Using the wrong $H$ calibration equation.** The IS Bouyoucos hydrometer has a specific $H$–$R_h$ relation (e.g., $H = -0.5 R_h + 16.3$). Other hydrometers (ASTM 152H) have different constants. Always check the calibration chart.
> 4. **Not converting $H$ from cm to m** before substituting into the SI form of Stokes' Law. Unit inconsistency is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
> 5. **Confusing $C_u > 4$ (gravel) and $C_u > 6$ (sand) criteria.** The well-graded criterion for gravel is $C_u > 4$; for sand it is $C_u > 6$. Both need $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$ to be well-graded.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Combined Sieve and Hydrometer Analysis**
>
> - [ ] **75 µm IS Sieve (No. 200)** is the **bridge** between the two tests. Soil passing it → hydrometer; soil retained → sieve stack.
> - [ ] **Sieve analysis** uses **% finer** = $\frac{M_{passing}}{M_{total}} \times 100$.
> - [ ] **Stokes' Law** is the **physical basis** of the hydrometer test: $v = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g D^2}{18 \mu}$.
> - [ ] The **equivalent particle diameter** is $D = \sqrt{ \dfrac{18 \mu H}{t (G_s - 1) \gamma_w} }$.
> - [ ] **Three corrections** are mandatory: **Meniscus (+), Temperature (+ if T > 27 °C), Dispersing agent (-)**.
> - [ ] **Effective depth $H$** is computed from the hydrometer calibration equation: $H = -0.5 R_h + 16.3$ cm (IS Bouyoucos).
> - [ ] **% finer (hydrometer)** is **of the fines only**; remember to **scale by $\frac{M_{fines}}{M_{total}}$** for the combined curve.
> - [ ] **$C_u$** = $\frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$; **$C_c$** = $\frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$.
> - [ ] **Well-graded sand:** $C_u > 6$ **and** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
> - [ ] **Well-graded gravel:** $C_u > 4$ **and** $1 \leq C_c \leq 3$.
> - [ ] Standard elapsed times: **0.5, 1, 2, 4, 15, 30, 60, 250, 1440 minutes**.
> - [ ] The **combined curve** is plotted on **semi-log paper** with **% finer (linear)** on Y-axis and **D in mm (log)** on X-axis.
> - [ ] Hydrometer test is **only valid** for particles finer than **75 µm** (Stokes' Law laminar flow limit).
> - [ ] Dispersing agent is **sodium hexametaphosphate (Calgon)**, used at **40 g/L concentration**, **125 mL per cylinder**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU PCCET402 – Soil Mechanics, Module 2**, the topic "**Limitations**" primarily refers to the **Limitations of Darcy's Law** in soil permeability analysis. 

**Darcy's Law** states that for laminar flow of water through a saturated soil mass, the discharge velocity $v$ is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient $i$:

$$v = k \cdot i$$

where $k$ is the coefficient of permeability (hydraulic conductivity) with units of **m/s** or **cm/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCET402 – Module 2):**
> "Darcy's law is a **linear, empirical relationship** valid only under specific flow regimes. Its validity breaks down when flow becomes turbulent, when soils exhibit non-Darcian behavior (e.g., coarse gravels, clays at low gradients), or when dealing with non-Newtonian fluids."

The **limitations of Darcy's Law** define the boundary conditions — *both physical and mathematical* — under which this fundamental equation produces reliable, engineering-accurate results for the calculation of seepage quantity, seepage pressure, and effective stress in soils.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Straw vs. Funnel" Experiment:**
> 
> Imagine sipping water through a **thin cocktail straw** (representing fine sand/silt where flow is slow and smooth). The water comes out in a thin, steady stream — this is **LAMINAR flow** and Darcy's Law works perfectly.
> 
> Now try sipping water through a **wide plastic funnel** (representing coarse gravel or a rock fill). The water rushes out in a turbulent, swirling, chaotic burst — this is **TURBULENT flow** and Darcy's Law **FAILS** because the simple linear proportionality breaks down.
> 
> Additionally, imagine trying to suck **cold honey** through the straw. Even with high effort (high gradient), very little honey moves because the viscous resistance is enormous. This represents **clay at low hydraulic gradients**, where flow may not even initiate — another limitation of Darcy's Law.
> 
> **Therefore**, Darcy's Law is like a "specialized tool" that only works for: **clean, slow water flow through medium-to-fine soils (sands and silts).**

## 1.3 Categories of Limitations — The Master Framework

The limitations of Darcy's Law in soil mechanics are grouped into **four major categories**, which the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to identify and explain:

| # | Category | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Flow Regime Limitation** | Valid only for laminar flow (Re < 1–10) |
| 2 | **Soil-Type Limitation** | Fails for coarse gravels, rock fills, and fissured rocks |
| 3 | **Hydraulic Gradient Limitation** | Fails at very low gradients (clays) and very high gradients |
| 4 | **Fluid & Temperature Limitation** | Assumes Newtonian, single-phase, isothermal water flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently test these four categories as short-answer questions. Memorize the category names and one example for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — What Makes Darcy's Law "Limited"?

Darcy's Law is **empirical** (derived experimentally by Henry Darcy in 1856 on sand filters in Dijon, France). Because it is empirical and not a derivation from first principles of fluid dynamics, it has *inherent boundaries of applicability*.

### 2.1.1 Assumption #1: Laminar Flow
Darcy's Law assumes **streamline (viscous) flow** where each fluid particle moves along a defined, non-intersecting path.

- **Laminar flow** is characterized by a low **Reynolds Number** (Re), typically $\text{Re} < 1$ for most soils, with an upper limit of approximately **1 to 10** before turbulent eddies appear.
- When Re exceeds this critical value, energy losses increase **disproportionately** with velocity, violating the linear $v = k i$ relationship.

### 2.1.2 Assumption #2: Saturated Soil
The soil voids must be **fully filled with water** (degree of saturation $S = 100\%$). For partially saturated soils:
- Air pockets block flow paths
- The effective cross-section for flow reduces
- The $k$ value becomes a function of $S$, making the constant-$k$ assumption invalid.

### 2.1.3 Assumption #3: Homogeneous, Inert Soil Matrix
- Soil grains must be **rigid** and **chemically inactive** (no swelling, no dissolution, no piping).
- Soils with high organic content, swelling clays (montmorillonite), or soluble minerals violate this assumption.

### 2.1.4 Assumption #4: Steady-State, Single-Phase Flow
- The flow rate must be **constant with time** (steady state).
- Only **water** is flowing (no air bubbles, no chemical transport, no multiphase flow).

### 2.1.5 Assumption #5: No Soil-Fluid Interaction
- The fluid must be **Newtonian** (viscosity independent of flow rate).
- For non-augmented soils at typical groundwater temperatures, this holds.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Explanation of Each Limitation

### Limitation 1: **Failure at High Velocities (Turbulent Flow)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this happen?**
> 
> In **coarse-grained soils** (gravel, rockfill, boulder beds), the void spaces are large. At high hydraulic gradients (e.g., during heavy pumping or in dam filters), the water velocity becomes high enough that flow transitions from **laminar → turbulent**.
> 
> In turbulent flow, energy losses are proportional to $v^2$ (not $v$), so the linear $v = k i$ relationship breaks down. A non-linear, Forchheimer-type equation must be used.

The **Forchheimer Equation** (replacement for Darcy's Law at high Re):

$$i = a v + b v^2$$

where $a$ and $b$ are empirical coefficients. The first term dominates at low velocities (Darcy regime), the second term dominates at high velocities (non-Darcy regime).

### Limitation 2: **Failure at Very Low Gradients (Clays)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Threshold Gradient in Clays:**
> 
> In **fine-grained cohesive soils** (clays of low plasticity, silts), the adsorbed water layer around clay particles is strongly held by electrochemical forces. Below a certain **threshold hydraulic gradient** $i_0$, there is *essentially no flow* because the viscous driving force is less than the soil's resistance.
> 
> Modified Darcy's equation for clays:
> 
> $$v = k (i - i_0)$$
> 
> where $i_0$ is the **initial or threshold gradient** (typically 4 to 10+ for clays).

### Limitation 3: **Failure in Highly Dispersive / Piping-Prone Soils**

In **dispersive clays** or loose fine sands subjected to upward seepage (e.g., beneath a cofferdam), Darcy's Law predicts a uniform discharge velocity. In reality:
- Flow becomes **channelized** (piping)
- Erosion creates preferred paths
- The soil structure **collapses** progressively
- Darcy's assumption of a uniform porous matrix is violated.

### Limitation 4: **Temperature & Fluid Viscosity Dependence**

Darcy's coefficient $k$ is defined at a standard temperature (usually **20 °C** or **27 °C**). If the permeant fluid is:
- Hot (geothermal applications)
- Cold (permafrost)
- Contaminated (oil, chemicals)

…then the viscosity $\mu$ changes, and the constant-$k$ assumption is invalid. The general form of Darcy's Law includes viscosity:

$$v = \frac{k \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{\mu} \cdot i = K \cdot i$$

where $K$ (hydraulic conductivity) depends on $\mu$, and $k$ (intrinsic permeability) depends only on the soil skeleton.

---

## 2.3 The Reynolds Number Criterion for Soils

The **Reynolds Number** for flow through a porous medium is:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$$

where:
- $\rho_w$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $v$ = discharge velocity (m/s)
- $d_{10}$ = effective grain size (m) — the sieve size through which 10% of soil passes
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water ≈ **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** at 20 °C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Values to Memorize:**
> 
> - $\text{Re} < 1$ → Darcy's Law is **valid** (laminar flow)
> - $1 < \text{Re} < 10$ → **transition zone** (limit of validity)
> - $\text{Re} > 10$ → **non-Darcian** (turbulent flow)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Validity Range / Units | KTU Significance |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Darcy's Law (basic) | $v = k \cdot i$ | Laminar flow, saturated soil | ⭐⭐⭐ Core equation |
| 2 | Darcy's Law (discharge form) | $Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m² | ⭐⭐⭐ Most-used form |
| 3 | Hydraulic gradient | $i = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | $h$ = head loss (m), $L$ = length (m) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Forchheimer Equation (non-Darcy) | $i = a v + b v^2$ | Turbulent flow regime | ⭐⭐ High-yield |
| 5 | Modified Darcy for clays | $v = k(i - i_0)$ | $i_0$ = threshold gradient | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Reynolds Number (soil) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$ | Critical Re = 1 to 10 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Intrinsic permeability | $k = \dfrac{K \cdot \mu}{\rho_w \cdot g}$ | $k$ in m² or darcy (1D ≈ 9.87×10⁻¹³ m²) | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Seepage velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{v}{n}$ | $n$ = porosity | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Critical hydraulic gradient (quick condition) | $i_{cr} = \dfrac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}$ | $G_s$ = specific gravity, $e$ = void ratio | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Permeability range (table) | Clay: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-7}$ m/s; Sand: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ m/s; Gravel: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ m/s | — | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Conversion Note:** $1 \text{ darcy} \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2$ is the petroleum-engineering unit. KTU Civil Engineering uses **m/s** for $k$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the limitations of Darcy's Law is **critical in**:

1. **Earthen Dam Design** — Seepage through dam body must be quantified; if the dam core uses dispersive clay, non-Darcian flow must be considered.
2. **Sheet Pile Cofferdams** — Piping failure at the downstream toe occurs when $i \to i_{cr}$; ignoring threshold gradient $i_0$ in clays leads to unsafe designs.
3. **Landfill Liners** — Synthetic + compacted clay liners must be tested at gradients *similar to field conditions*; Darcy's Law may over-predict flow at low $i$.
4. **Geothermal Reservoirs** — High temperature alters viscosity; intrinsic permeability $k$ (not $K$) must be used.
5. **Dewatering of Excavations** — Coarse gravel layers require non-Darcian correction for accurate drawdown predictions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Critical Hydraulic Gradient and the Quick Condition

The **critical hydraulic gradient** $i_{cr}$ is the gradient at which effective stress becomes zero and the soil "quickens" (liquefies) due to upward seepage. This derivation is fundamental to understanding limitations of Darcy-related seepage analysis.

### Step 1: Consider a Soil Element Under Upward Seepage

Let:
- Total stress at depth $z$: $\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$
- Pore water pressure at depth $z$ under seepage: $u = \gamma_w \cdot (z + h)$
where $h$ is the **excess head** due to upward seepage (h is positive when seepage is upward).

### Step 2: Apply Effective Stress Principle

By Terzaghi's principle:

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u$$

Substitute:

$$\sigma' = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z - \gamma_w \cdot (z + h)$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Submerged Unit Weight

Recall that $\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_w (G_s + e)/(1+e)$ and the submerged (buoyant) unit weight is:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$$

Rearranging the effective stress equation:

$$\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z - \gamma_w \cdot h$$

### Step 4: Express Head Loss in Terms of Hydraulic Gradient

The hydraulic gradient is $i = h/L$ where $L$ is the flow path length. For a soil element of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$:

$$h = i \cdot z$$

Substitute into effective stress equation:

$$\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z - \gamma_w \cdot i \cdot z = (\gamma' - \gamma_w \cdot i) \cdot z$$

### Step 5: Set Effective Stress to Zero (Quick Condition)

At the **quick condition** (sand becomes a heavy liquid, soil loses all shear strength):

$$\sigma' = 0 \implies \gamma' = \gamma_w \cdot i_{cr}$$

Solve for $i_{cr}$:

$$i_{cr} = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w}$$

### Step 6: Substitute the Expression for $\gamma'$

$$i_{cr} = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot \gamma_w / (1+e)}{\gamma_w}$$

$$\boxed{i_{cr} = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}}$$

This is the famous **critical hydraulic gradient**. For typical soils, $G_s \approx 2.65$ and $e \approx 0.65$, giving:

$$i_{cr} = \frac{2.65 - 1}{1 + 0.65} = \frac{1.65}{1.65} \approx 1.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $i_{cr} \approx 1$ for most saturated sands. When upward seepage gradient approaches 1.0, the sand "boils" and loses bearing capacity. This is why Darcy's Law alone — without checking $i_{cr}$ — is insufficient for design.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Validating Darcy's Law Using Reynolds Number

**Problem:** Water at 20 °C percolates through a sand filter. The effective grain size is $d_{10} = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, and the measured discharge velocity is $v = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$. Check whether Darcy's Law is valid.

### Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_w &= 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
\mu &= 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s} \\
d_{10} &= 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
v &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Substitute into Reynolds Number Formula

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$$

### Step 2: Plug in Numerical Values

$$\text{Re} = \frac{(1000) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 3: Compute Numerator

$$(1000) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 4: Divide by Denominator

$$\text{Re} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.996 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.02$$

### Step 5: Compare with Critical Reynolds Number

$$\text{Re} = 0.02 \ll 1 \quad \therefore \text{ Laminar flow}$$

### Conclusion

Darcy's Law is **valid** for this case. The flow regime is well within the laminar range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-Example:** If the same sand were replaced with a **gravel** having $d_{10} = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ and $v = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$ (high pumping rate):
> $$\text{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 0.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 249.5$$
> This is **far above 10**, and Darcy's Law **cannot** be used. The Forchheimer equation or empirical corrections are required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Darcy vs. Non-Darcy Regimes

| Parameter | Darcy Regime (Valid) | Non-Darcy Regime (Limited) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} < 1$ | $\text{Re} > 10$ |
| Flow Type | Laminar, streamline | Turbulent, eddies |
| $v$–$i$ Relation | Linear ($v = k i$) | Non-linear ($i = a v + b v^2$) |
| Typical Soil | Clay, silt, fine–medium sand | Coarse gravel, rockfill, boulder beds |
| Energy Loss | Viscous ($ \propto v$) | Inertial + Viscous ($ \propto v + v^2$) |
| Head Loss Equation | $h_f = \dfrac{v \cdot L}{k}$ | $h_f = a v L + b v^2 L$ |
| KTU Validity | Full marks awarded | Use Forchheimer; partial credit may apply |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture — Validity Decision Tree for Darcy's Law

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** a KTU student or practicing engineer must follow to determine whether Darcy's Law is applicable to a given soil–flow scenario.

<!-- Mermaid code block - tested and safe -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Soil Permeability Problem] --> B{Is the soil saturated?}
    B -- No --> B1[Use unsaturated flow theory - van Genuchten]
    B -- Yes --> C{Is flow steady-state?}
    C -- No --> C1[Use transient flow theory - consolidation]
    C -- Yes --> D{What is the grain size d10?}
    D -- Clay, Silt --> E[Clay Silt Path]
    D -- Fine to Medium Sand --> F[Sand Path]
    D -- Coarse Sand or Gravel --> G[Gravel Path]
    D -- Rockfill or Boulder --> H[Rockfill Path]
    E --> E1{Is gradient i less than threshold i0?}
    E1 -- Yes --> E2[Effectively zero flow - use modified Darcy v equals k times i minus i0]
    E1 -- No --> E3[Apply modified Darcy with threshold gradient]
    F --> F1{Compute Reynolds number Re}
    F1 --> F2{Re less than 1?}
    F2 -- Yes --> F3[Darcy Law is VALID - v equals k times i]
    F2 -- No --> F4[Re between 1 and 10 - transition zone - use with caution]
    G --> G1{Compute Reynolds number Re}
    G1 --> G2{Re between 1 and 100?}
    G2 -- Yes --> G3[Use Forchheimer equation - i equals a v plus b v squared]
    H --> H1[Re greater than 100 - fully turbulent]
    H1 --> H2[Use empirical non-Darcian equations - Izbash or Ergun]
    F3 --> Z[Final Design Permeability]
    E3 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    H2 --> Z
    C1 --> Z
    B1 --> Z
    F4 --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Begin at the top with "Start", then answer each yes/no question in sequence. Each terminal node gives the appropriate governing equation. For KTU exam answers, always state *which path* you took through the diagram.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Soil Permeability Regimes

This second diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how the four limitations interact with the four soil categories to produce different governing equations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Soil Type]
        P2[Hydraulic Gradient i]
        P3[Grain Size d10]
        P4[Velocity v]
    end
    subgraph REGIME[FLOW REGIME CLASSIFIER]
        R1{Laminar?}
        R2{Turbulent?}
    end
    subgraph LIMIT[FOUR LIMITATIONS]
        L1[Limitation 1: High Re Failure]
        L2[Limitation 2: Low Gradient Clay]
        L3[Limitation 3: Dispersive Piping]
        L4[Limitation 4: Non-Newtonian Fluid]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[GOVERNING EQUATION]
        O1[v equals k times i - Darcy Original]
        O2[i equals a v plus b v squared - Forchheimer]
        O3[v equals k times i minus i0 - Modified Clay]
        O4[Empirical Multi-Factor Correction]
    end
    P1 --> R1
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R1
    P4 --> R2
    R1 -- Yes --> O1
    R1 -- No --> L1
    R1 -- Partial --> L2
    R2 -- Yes --> O2
    L3 --> O4
    L4 --> O4
    L1 --> O2
    L2 --> O3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Aid Tip:** Drawing a small flowchart in your answer sheet (even hand-drawn) earns additional clarity marks. Examiners reward structured, well-organized answers.

## 4.3 Schematic Table — Permeability Range vs. Darcy Validity

| Soil Category | $d_{10}$ Range | Typical $k$ (m/s) | Darcy Law Validity |
|---------------|----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Clay** | $< 0.002$ mm | $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-7}$ | Valid only above $i_0$ |
| **Silt** | 0.002 to 0.075 mm | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Valid, with care |
| **Fine Sand** | 0.075 to 0.425 mm | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | **Fully valid** (sweet spot) |
| **Medium Sand** | 0.425 to 2.0 mm | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Valid at low $i$ |
| **Coarse Sand** | 2.0 to 4.75 mm | $10^{-3}$ | Valid only at very low $i$ |
| **Gravel** | 4.75 to 80 mm | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | **Often invalid — use Forchheimer** |
| **Rockfill** | $> 80$ mm | $> 10^{-1}$ | **Almost always invalid** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Decision Rule:** Darcy's Law is most reliable for **fine and medium sands at low to moderate hydraulic gradients** (typical field seepage scenarios). It is least reliable for **rockfill and gravel under high gradients** (e.g., dam toe filters, well screens).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"State and briefly explain any three limitations of Darcy's Law."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Darcy's Law ($v = k i$) is an empirical relationship that is valid only under specific conditions. The major limitations are:

1. **Limitation of Laminar Flow Assumption:** Darcy's Law is strictly valid only for laminar flow, where the Reynolds number $\text{Re} < 1$. In coarse-grained soils (gravel, rockfill) at high gradients, the flow becomes turbulent and the linear relationship between velocity and hydraulic gradient fails. A non-linear equation such as the Forchheimer equation $i = av + bv^2$ must then be used. **(1 mark)**

2. **Limitation in Cohesive Soils (Clays):** In clays, flow does not initiate until the applied hydraulic gradient exceeds a **threshold (initial) gradient** $i_0$ due to the strong electrochemical bonding of adsorbed water. Darcy's Law overestimates flow in clays; a modified equation $v = k(i - i_0)$ is more appropriate. **(1 mark)**

3. **Limitation Due to Soil Heterogeneity and Piping:** Darcy's Law assumes a homogeneous, stable porous matrix. In dispersive clays or loose fine sands, seepage causes internal erosion (piping), creating preferred flow channels and altering the soil structure. This violates the assumption of a uniform flow medium, rendering the simple $v = ki$ equation inaccurate. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"The Reynolds number for flow through a sandy soil is calculated as 15. Is Darcy's Law valid? Justify your answer with a suitable replacement equation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Answer:** No, Darcy's Law is **not valid** for $\text{Re} = 15$.

**Justification (2 marks):**
The critical Reynolds number for the upper limit of Darcy's Law validity in soils is approximately **1 to 10**. A value of $\text{Re} = 15$ falls in the **non-Darcy / turbulent flow regime**, where the linear relationship $v = k i$ breaks down due to inertial energy losses that scale with $v^2$ rather than $v$.

**Replacement Equation (1 mark):**
The **Forchheimer equation** is used:

$$i = a v + b v^2$$

where $a$ and $b$ are empirical coefficients determined experimentally. The first term accounts for viscous losses (Darcy contribution), and the second term accounts for inertial losses (non-Darcy contribution). When $v$ is small, the first term dominates (Darcy regime); when $v$ is large, the second term dominates (turbulent regime).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** simply list limitations — for full 14 marks, you must **derive**, **numerically validate**, or **apply** the limitation to a real engineering problem.
> - Always cite the **Reynolds number threshold** when discussing flow regime limitations.
> - When asked about "limitations in clays", mention the **threshold gradient $i_0$** specifically; failing to do so costs 2–3 marks.
> - Include **units** in every numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** State Darcy's Law and discuss its major limitations with reference to soil type, flow regime, and hydraulic gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A constant-head permeability test is conducted on a medium sand sample. Given:
- Length of sample, $L = 200$ mm
- Cross-sectional area, $A = 60$ cm²
- Constant head difference, $h = 300$ mm
- Discharge collected in 5 minutes, $Q = 250$ cm³
- Effective grain size, $d_{10} = 0.18$ mm
- Water temperature: 20 °C

Compute the coefficient of permeability $k$ and verify whether Darcy's Law is valid for this test. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Statement of Darcy's Law (2 marks):**
Darcy's Law states that for laminar flow of water through a saturated soil, the discharge velocity $v$ is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient $i$:

$$v = k \cdot i \quad \text{or} \quad Q = k \cdot i \cdot A$$

**Discussion of Limitations (5 marks — 5 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Limitation due to soil type (1 mark):** Darcy's Law is strictly valid for **fine and medium sands** in which the void channels are small enough to maintain laminar flow. In **gravels and rockfills** with large void sizes, the law fails because flow becomes turbulent. In **clays**, the law over-predicts flow below the threshold gradient $i_0$ because adsorbed water resists flow.

2. **Limitation due to flow regime (1 mark):** Darcy's Law requires **laminar flow**, which corresponds to a Reynolds number $\text{Re} < 1$. The Reynolds number for soils is $\text{Re} = \rho_w v d_{10}/\mu$. When $\text{Re} > 10$, flow becomes turbulent and the $v$–$i$ relationship is no longer linear.

3. **Limitation due to hydraulic gradient (1 mark):** In clays, flow does not begin until the gradient exceeds a minimum threshold $i_0$. In coarse soils, very high gradients induce turbulence. Thus Darcy's Law is valid only within a **finite range of gradients** specific to the soil type.

4. **Limitation due to soil heterogeneity and piping (1 mark):** Darcy's Law assumes a uniform, stable porous matrix. In real soils with layers, cracks, or dispersive behavior, flow becomes **non-uniform and channelized** (piping), violating this assumption.

5. **Limitation due to fluid properties (1 mark):** Darcy's Law assumes a Newtonian, isothermal fluid (water at ~20 °C). For non-Newtonian fluids (slurry, contaminated water) or at elevated temperatures (geothermal), the viscosity varies, and the constant-$k$ assumption is invalid. The **intrinsic permeability** $k$ (not hydraulic conductivity $K$) should be used.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the hydraulic gradient (1 mark)**

$$i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{200 \text{ mm}} = 1.5$$

**Step 2: Compute the discharge velocity (1 mark)**

$$A = 60 \text{ cm}^2 = 60 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
$$Q = 250 \text{ cm}^3 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$
$$t = 5 \text{ min} = 300 \text{ s}$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A \cdot t} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{60 \times 10^{-4} \times 300} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8} = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the coefficient of permeability (2 marks)**

$$k = \frac{v}{i} = \frac{1.389 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5} = 9.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Units Check:** $k = 9.26 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s ≈ $9.26 \times 10^{-3}$ cm/s. This lies in the typical range for **fine to medium sand** ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ m/s). The result is dimensionally and physically consistent.

**Step 4: Compute the Reynolds number (2 marks)**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$$

Given: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $d_{10} = 0.18 \text{ mm} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{2.500 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02495 \approx 0.025$$

**Step 5: Verify Darcy's Law validity (1 mark)**

Since $\text{Re} \approx 0.025 \ll 1$, the flow is **well within the laminar regime**, and **Darcy's Law is valid** for this test.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical hydraulic gradient** $i_{cr}$ at which a saturated sand becomes quick (loses all effective stress). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the limitations of Darcy's Law, explain the **Forchheimer equation** for non-Darcian flow. A coarse gravel has been tested and the following data were obtained:

| Gradient $i$ (m/m) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity $v$ (m/s) | 0.02 | 0.035 | 0.060 | 0.095 |

Determine the empirical Forchheimer coefficients $a$ and $b$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(See Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. Summarized here:)*

1. **Total stress at depth $z$:** $\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$ — **(1 mark)**
2. **Pore water pressure with seepage:** $u = \gamma_w (z + h)$ — **(1 mark)**
3. **Apply effective stress principle:** $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ — **(1 mark)**
4. **Substitute $h = i z$:** $\sigma' = \gamma' z - \gamma_w i z = (\gamma' - \gamma_w i) z$ — **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Set $\sigma' = 0$ for the quick condition:** $\gamma' = \gamma_w i_{cr}$ — **(1 mark)**
6. **Substitute $\gamma' = (G_s - 1)\gamma_w / (1 + e)$ and simplify:** $i_{cr} = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$ — **(1.5 marks)**

$$\boxed{i_{cr} = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Theoretical Explanation of Forchheimer Equation (3 marks):**

When the flow regime in coarse soils (gravel, rockfill) is turbulent, Darcy's Law fails because it accounts only for viscous losses (linear in $v$). The **Forchheimer equation** adds an **inertial loss term** (quadratic in $v$) to capture turbulence effects:

$$i = a v + b v^2$$

where:
- $a$ = viscous-flow coefficient (related to permeability)
- $b$ = inertial-flow coefficient (related to turbulence)

At low $v$: the first term dominates → Darcy regime.
At high $v$: the second term dominates → non-Darcy regime.

**Determination of $a$ and $b$ — Method of Two Points (4 marks):**

Using the data, pick two representative points: $(i_1, v_1) = (1.0, 0.035)$ and $(i_2, v_2) = (4.0, 0.095)$.

**Step 1: Set up two simultaneous equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.0 &= a (0.035) + b (0.035)^2 = 0.035 a + 0.001225 b \quad \text{...(1)} \\
4.0 &= a (0.095) + b (0.095)^2 = 0.095 a + 0.009025 b \quad \text{...(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve the system**

Multiply (1) by $\frac{0.095}{0.035} = 2.7143$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.7143 &= 0.095 a + 0.003325 b \quad \text{...(1')}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtract (1') from (2):

$$4.0 - 2.7143 = (0.009025 - 0.003325) b$$
$$1.2857 = 0.0057 b$$
$$b = \frac{1.2857}{0.0057} = 225.6 \text{ s/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for $a$ from equation (1)**

$$1.0 = 0.035 a + 0.001225 \times 225.6$$
$$1.0 = 0.035 a + 0.2763$$
$$0.035 a = 0.7237$$
$$a = 20.68 \text{ s/m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{a \approx 20.68 \text{ s/m}, \quad b \approx 225.6 \text{ s/m}^2}$$

The Forchheimer equation for this gravel is:

$$i = 20.68\,v + 225.6\,v^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students compute $a$ and $b$ with wrong units. Always verify: $[a] = \text{s/m}$ (so that $a v$ is dimensionless in $i$) and $[b] = \text{s}^2/\text{m}^2$. Marks are deducted for missing or incorrect units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Limitations of Darcy's Law (Module 2, PCCET402)**

- ✅ **Darcy's Law formula:** $v = k i$ or $Q = k i A$ — *write it first, always.*
- ✅ **Four limitation categories:** Flow regime, Soil type, Hydraulic gradient, Fluid properties.
- ✅ **Critical Reynolds Number:** $\text{Re}_{cr} = 1$ to $10$ — *memorize this range.*
- ✅ **Reynolds Number formula:** $\text{Re} = \rho_w v d_{10} / \mu$ — *uses effective grain size $d_{10}$, not $D_{50}$.*
- ✅ **Threshold gradient in clays:** $v = k(i - i_0)$ — *the modified Darcy's equation for cohesive soils.*
- ✅ **Forchheimer equation (non-Darcy):** $i = a v + b v^2$ — *replaces Darcy's Law in gravel/rockfill at high $v$.*
- ✅ **Critical hydraulic gradient (quick condition):** $i_{cr} = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$ — *typical value $\approx 1.0$ for sands.*
- ✅ **Intrinsic vs. Hydraulic Conductivity:** $k$ depends only on soil; $K$ depends on fluid too.
- ✅ **Permeability range order:** Clay < Silt < Sand < Gravel < Rockfill (ascending $k$).
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ darcy} \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2$; $1 \text{ cm/s} = 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$.
- ✅ **When in doubt, check Re:** If $\text{Re} < 1$, use Darcy; if $\text{Re} > 10$, use Forchheimer.
- ✅ **Piping phenomenon:** Repeatedly asked — always link it to the *dispersive soil limitation* of Darcy's Law.
- ✅ **Standard temperature for $k$:** Always 20 °C unless stated otherwise; viscosity $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- ✅ **Sweet spot of Darcy's Law:** Fine-to-medium sands at low-to-moderate gradients — *always mention this in answers.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** No Derivation Required for Percentage Finer and Diameter. Consistency - Atterberg Limits and Indices - Plasticity Charts - Activity of Soil - Laboratory Tests for Liquid Limit (Casagrande's Apparatus and Cone Penetrometer)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Percentage Finer and Diameter (No Derivation)

### Formal Definition
**Percentage Finer (N)** is the cumulative percentage of soil particles by weight that are smaller than a given particle diameter **D**. It is the fundamental y-axis parameter of the **Grain Size Distribution Curve** (also called the **Particle Size Distribution Curve** or **PSD Curve**). The x-axis is the **Particle Diameter (D)** plotted on a **logarithmic scale**, while the y-axis is the **Percentage Finer (N)** plotted on a regular arithmetic scale (as per **IS 2720 Part 4**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The derivations for relationships between hydrometer readings, percentage finer (N), and equivalent particle diameter (D) — based on **Stokes' Law** — are **NOT required** for the exam. Students must understand the **concept, formula structure, and how to use these values** to plot the grain size curve.

### Key Equations (Conceptual Understanding Only)

For **Sieve Analysis** (coarse-grained particles, D > 75 µm):

$$N = \frac{\text{Cumulative Mass of Soil Retained Above Sieve}}{\text{Total Mass of Soil}} \times 100$$

For **Hydrometer Analysis** (fine-grained particles, D < 75 µm), using **Stokes' Law**:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \cdot \eta \cdot H_r}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot t}}$$

$$N = \frac{100 \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot (R - R_w)}{M_s}$$

where:
- $H_r$ = effective depth (cm)
- $\eta$ = viscosity of water (poise)
- $R$ = hydrometer reading corrected for meniscus
- $R_w$ = hydrometer reading in plain water
- $V_s$ = specific gravity correction factor
- $M_s$ = mass of dry soil (g)
- $\rho_s$ = density of soil particles
- $\rho_w$ = density of water
- $t$ = elapsed time (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you have a mixed bag of marbles of different sizes. The "percentage finer" tells you what fraction of marbles (by weight) are smaller than a particular size. If you plot this against size, you get a clear picture of the particle distribution — like a "fingerprint" of your soil.

---

## 1.2 Consistency of Soils

### Formal Definition
**Consistency** is defined as the **relative ease with which a soil can be deformed** or its **resistance to deformation and rupture** at various moisture contents. For **fine-grained soils (cohesive soils)**, consistency describes the physical state of the soil as it transitions with water content changes.

### States of Consistency
A fine-grained soil can exist in **four states**:

1. **Solid State** — Soil behaves like a solid, no volume change on drying
2. **Semi-Solid State** — Soil undergoes volume reduction on drying
3. **Plastic State** — Soil can be moulded/remoulded without cracking
4. **Liquid State** — Soil flows like a liquid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of chocolate — a frozen chocolate bar is **solid**, a slightly warm one is **plastic** (you can shape it), melted chocolate is **liquid**. Adding/removing water in soil does exactly the same thing! The boundaries between these states are called **Atterberg Limits**.

### Visual Representation of Consistency States

```
        LIQUID                         ←→  Water content DECREASES
         |                                  
    |---------LL---------|              ← Liquid Limit (LL)
    |                   |
    |     PLASTIC       |              ←→  Water content DECREASES  
    |                   |
    |---------PL---------|              ← Plastic Limit (PL)
    |                   |
    |    SEMI-SOLID     |              ←→  Water content DECREASES
    |                   |
    |---------SL---------|              ← Shrinkage Limit (SL)
    |                   |
    |      SOLID        |              
```

---

## 1.3 Atterberg Limits

### Formal Definition
**Atterberg Limits** are the **water content boundaries** that define the transitions between the consistency states of fine-grained soils. They were originally developed by **Swedish scientist Albert Atterberg (1911)** and later refined by **Arthur Casagrande (1932)** for engineering applications.

### The Three Primary Limits

| Limit | Symbol | Definition | Physical Significance |
|-------|--------|------------|----------------------|
| **Liquid Limit** | $W_L$ or $LL$ | Minimum water content at which soil flows like a liquid | Boundary between liquid and plastic states |
| **Plastic Limit** | $W_P$ or $PL$ | Minimum water content at which soil can be rolled into 3mm threads without cracking | Boundary between plastic and semi-solid states |
| **Shrinkage Limit** | $W_S$ or $SL$ | Maximum water content at which reduction in water content does not cause volume reduction | Boundary between semi-solid and solid states |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** IS Codes use different notations: $W_L, W_P, W_S$ (with subscripts) or $LL, PL, SL$ (without subscripts). Both are acceptable in KTU exams.

### Conceptual Analogy
- **Liquid Limit (LL):** The soil is at the brink of becoming soup — add a little more water, and it starts to flow on its own.
- **Plastic Limit (PL):** The soil is like **damp clay** — you can roll it into a snake, but if you roll it thinner, it cracks.
- **Shrinkage Limit (SL):** The soil is at its **minimum volume** — any drying below this point causes no further shrinkage.

---

## 1.4 Plasticity Charts — Formal Definition

A **Plasticity Chart** is a graph plotting **Plasticity Index (PI)** on the y-axis versus **Liquid Limit (LL)** on the x-axis, used to **classify fine-grained soils** as per **IS 1498 (1970)** and the **ISCS (Indian Soil Classification System)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casagrande's Chart (1932):** The original A-line equation: $PI = 0.73(LL - 20)$ — used to distinguish **clays (above A-line)** from **silts (below A-line)**.

---

## 1.5 Activity of Soil — Formal Definition

**Activity (A)** of a soil, as defined by **Skempton (1953)**, is the **ratio of the Plasticity Index (PI) to the percentage of clay-sized particles** (finer than **2 µm**):

$$A = \frac{PI}{\% \text{ Clay Fraction}}$$

**Classification of Activity:**

| Activity Value | Classification | Behaviour |
|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| $A < 0.75$ | **Inactive** | Low swelling potential |
| $0.75 \leq A \leq 1.25$ | **Normal** | Moderate behaviour |
| $A > 1.25$ | **Active** | High swelling potential (e.g., Montmorillonite) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** "Activity" tells us how "reactive" the clay minerals are. Think of a **sponge (active clay)** vs. a **piece of wood (inactive silt)**. Both can be moulded when wet, but the sponge swells dramatically while wood barely changes.

---

## 1.6 Laboratory Tests for Liquid Limit

Two standard laboratory tests for determining the **Liquid Limit (LL)**:

1. **Casagrande's Apparatus Test** (Percussion/Cup Test) — Traditional method
2. **Cone Penetrometer Test** (Swedish Fall Cone / BS Cone) — More reliable modern method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **IS 2720 (Part 5) - 1985**, the **Casagrande method** is standard in India. The **Cone Penetrometer method** is covered under **IS 2720 (Part 5/Sec 2)** and **BS 1377**. Both yield the LL but with different operational procedures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Atterberg Indices (Derived Parameters)

The **Atterberg Indices** are **derived quantities** from the Atterberg Limits and natural water content, used to characterize soil behaviour.

### 2.1.1 Plasticity Index (PI)

$$PI = LL - PL$$

**Physical Meaning:** Represents the **range of water content** over which the soil remains in a **plastic state**. Higher PI = more plastic soil = more compressible clay.

| PI Range | Soil Type |
|----------|-----------|
| $PI = 0$ | Non-plastic (sand) |
| $PI < 7$ | Low plasticity (silt) |
| $7 \leq PI \leq 17$ | Medium plasticity |
| $PI > 17$ | High plasticity (clay) |

---

### 2.1.2 Liquidity Index (LI)

$$LI = \frac{w - PL}{PI} = \frac{w - PL}{LL - PL}$$

where $w$ = natural water content

**Physical Meaning:** Indicates the **natural state** of the soil relative to its plastic range.
- $LI < 0$: Soil is in **semi-solid/solid** state
- $0 \leq LI \leq 1$: Soil is in **plastic** state
- $LI > 1$: Soil is in **liquid** state (very weak)

> [!NOTE]
> LI = 0 means soil is at PL (just about to crack when rolled). LI = 1 means soil is at LL (about to flow).

---

### 2.1.3 Consistency Index (CI)

$$CI = \frac{LL - w}{PI} = \frac{LL - w}{LL - PL}$$

**Physical Meaning:** **Inverse of LI**; gives the **consistency** (firmness) of the soil.
- $CI < 0$: Liquid state
- $0 \leq CI \leq 1$: Plastic state
- $CI > 1$: Semi-solid/solid state

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** $LI + CI = 1$

---

### 2.1.4 Shrinkage Index (SI)

$$SI = PL - SL$$

**Physical Meaning:** Represents the range of water content over which soil is in the **semi-solid state** (i.e., the soil shrinks as water content decreases).

---

### 2.1.5 Shrinkage Ratio (SR)

$$SR = \frac{\frac{V_1 - V_2}{M_2 - M_1}}{\rho_w}$$

where:
- $V_1, V_2$ = initial and final volumes of soil mass
- $M_1, M_2$ = initial (wet) and final (oven-dry) masses
- $\rho_w$ = density of water

**Physical Meaning:** Represents the **specific volume change** per unit change in water content below SL.

---

## 2.2 Plasticity Chart (IS 1498 — Indian Soil Classification)

The Indian Standard **Plasticity Chart** has a **U-line** and **A-line** with different equations from the original Casagrande chart.

### Key Lines on the Chart

| Line | Equation | Significance |
|------|----------|--------------|
| **A-line** | $PI = 0.73(LL - 20)$ | Boundary: Clays (above) vs Silts (below) |
| **U-line** | $PI = 0.9(LL - 8)$ | Upper bound (most plastic possible soil) |

### Classification as per IS 1498

| Zone | Location | Classification |
|------|----------|----------------|
| Zone 1 | LL < 35, above A-line | CL (Lean Clay) |
| Zone 2 | LL < 35, below A-line | ML (Silt) |
| Zone 3 | 35 ≤ LL ≤ 50, above A-line | CI (Medium Clay) |
| Zone 4 | 35 ≤ LL ≤ 50, below A-line | MI (Silt of Medium Compressibility) |
| Zone 5 | LL > 50, above A-line | CH (Fat Clay) |
| Zone 6 | LL > 50, below A-line | MH (Elastic Silt) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Highlight:** Students MUST memorize the A-line equation: $PI_A = 0.73(LL - 20)$ and use it to check whether a soil plots above or below.

---

## 2.3 Activity of Soil — Skempton's Classification (Detailed)

### Formal Formula Recap

$$A = \frac{PI}{\% \text{ Clay Fraction (finer than 2 µm)}}$$

### Mineralogy and Activity

| Clay Mineral | Typical Activity | Behaviour |
|--------------|------------------|-----------|
| **Kaolinite** | $0.3 - 0.5$ | Inactive |
| **Illite** | $0.5 - 1.0$ | Normal |
| **Montmorillonite** | $1.0 - 7.0$ | Highly Active (swelling) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Active soils are problematic because they **swell when wet** and **shrink when dry**, causing severe damage to foundations and pavements. Identifying activity is critical for **black cotton soils** of central India.

---

## 2.4 Laboratory Tests for Liquid Limit — Detailed Procedure

### Method 1: Casagrande's Apparatus (IS 2720 Part 5)

**Apparatus Components:**
- Brass cup mounted on a hard rubber base
- Rotating cam (1 cm drop height)
- Grooving tool (standard or ASTM)

**Procedure:**
1. Take ~120 g of soil passing **425 µm IS sieve**
2. Mix with distilled water to a paste
3. Place soil paste in the cup (max depth ~1 cm)
4. Cut a **groove** through the middle of the soil pat using the grooving tool
5. Rotate the cam at **2 revolutions/second** (120 rpm)
6. **Count the number of blows (N)** required to close the groove over a length of **12 mm (1.25 cm)** at the bottom
7. Repeat for **4 trials** with different water contents (N varying from 15-35)
8. Plot **Water Content (y-axis) vs. log₁₀ N (x-axis)** — this is the **Flow Curve**
9. **LL = water content at N = 25 blows** (read from the flow curve)

**One-Point Method (Quick):** $LL = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{\tan \beta}$

For the standard Casagrande cup: $\tan \beta = 0.121$

$$LL = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{0.121}$$

---

### Method 2: Cone Penetrometer Test (IS 2720 Part 5/Sec 2, BS 1377)

**Apparatus Components:**
- Stainless steel cone with **30° apex angle**, **35 mm long**, mass of **80 g** (BS) or **100 g** (Swedish)
- Penetration gauge (dial gauge, accuracy 0.1 mm)
- Sample cup (55 mm diameter, 40 mm deep)

**Procedure:**
1. Mix soil with water to a uniform paste
2. Fill the cup with soil paste, removing air bubbles
3. Place cone tip just touching soil surface (zero the dial gauge)
4. Release the cone — allow it to penetrate for **5 seconds**
5. Read penetration depth (d) in mm
6. **LL = water content at cone penetration of 20 mm**

**Multi-Point Method:** Take 4-5 readings, plot **penetration vs. water content**, read LL at **20 mm penetration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cone Penetrometer Advantages:** More objective (not operator-dependent), no vibration errors, suitable for soft soils where Casagrande cup gives variable results.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Range** |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Plasticity Index | $PI = LL - PL$ | % (0 to ∞) |
| Liquidity Index | $LI = (w - PL) / PI$ | dimensionless |
| Consistency Index | $CI = (LL - w) / PI$ | dimensionless |
| Shrinkage Index | $SI = PL - SL$ | % |
| Shrinkage Ratio | $SR = \left[\frac{V_1 - V_2}{M_2 - M_1}\right] \div \rho_w$ | dimensionless |
| Activity | $A = PI / (\% \text{ Clay})$ | dimensionless |
| A-line equation | $PI_A = 0.73(LL - 20)$ | IS 1498 |
| U-line equation | $PI_U = 0.9(LL - 8)$ | Upper bound |
| Casagrande LL (flow curve) | $w = w_N \cdot (N/25)^{\tan \beta}$ | $\tan \beta = 0.121$ |
| Cone Penetrometer LL | $w$ at 20 mm penetration | mm |
| Toughness Index | $TI = PI / I_F$ | dimensionless (where $I_F$ = flow index) |
| Flow Index | $I_F$ | slope of flow curve on semi-log plot |
| **Casagrande Classification** | Above A-line → Clay; Below → Silt | IS 1498 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These indices are the **first step in geotechnical design** — they determine soil classification, predict settlement, swelling potential, and suitability for embankment/foundation construction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of A-line Equation (Conceptual)

The **A-line equation** $PI = 0.73(LL - 20)$ is **empirically derived** by Arthur Casagrande from experimental data on natural soils. KTU students are **NOT required to derive** this equation, but understanding its **application** is critical.

### How to Use the A-line

For a given soil with known LL and PI:
1. **Calculate $PI_A$**: Substitute LL into A-line equation
2. **Compare $PI_A$ with actual PI**:
   - If $PI > PI_A$: Soil plots **ABOVE** A-line → **Clay (C)**
   - If $PI < PI_A$: Soil plots **BELOW** A-line → **Silt (M)**
3. **Compare LL with 35 and 50**:
   - LL < 35: L (low compressibility)
   - 35 ≤ LL ≤ 50: I (medium compressibility)
   - LL > 50: H (high compressibility)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:** Soil A: LL = 40, PI = 22. Then $PI_A = 0.73(40 - 20) = 14.6$. Since 22 > 14.6, soil plots above A-line. Since 35 ≤ 40 ≤ 50, classify as **CI (Medium Clay)**.

---

## 3.2 Casagrande's Flow Curve Derivation (Conceptual)

The flow curve relates water content to the number of blows (N) for the Casagrande cup test. Empirical studies have shown this relationship to be **linear on a semi-log plot**.

### One-Point Method Derivation

Starting from the **linear semi-log relationship**:

$$w = a \cdot \log N + b$$

where $a$ = slope (negative, because higher N corresponds to lower water content), $b$ = y-intercept.

Define **Flow Index ($I_F$)** = slope of flow curve = magnitude of slope of w vs log N plot:
$$I_F = \frac{w_1 - w_2}{\log(N_2 / N_1)}$$

For two points on the flow curve: $(N_1, w_1)$ and $(N_2, w_2)$:

$$w_1 = w_2 + I_F \cdot \log(N_1 / N_2)$$

Setting $N_1 = N$ (test point) and $N_2 = 25$ (LL definition):

$$w_N = w_{LL} + I_F \cdot \log(N / 25)$$

Solving for $w_{LL}$ (which is LL):

$$LL = w_{LL} = w_N - I_F \cdot \log(N / 25) = w_N - I_F \cdot \log N + I_F \cdot \log 25$$

For standard Casagrande cup: $I_F = 0.121$ (empirically determined from $w_N - LL = I_F \log(N/25)$)

$$LL = w_N - 0.121 \cdot \log(N / 25) = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{-0.121}$$

Or in conventional form (using $\tan \beta$):

$$LL = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{\tan \beta}, \quad \tan \beta = 0.121$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For the one-point method, $LL = w_N \cdot (N/25)^{0.121}$ is the **exact formula** students must use. Standard cup gives $\tan \beta = 0.121$. Some Chinese cups use $\tan \beta = 0.090$.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Liquid Limit by Multi-Point Method

**Problem:** The following data was obtained in a Casagrande's Liquid Limit test:

| Trial | Number of Blows (N) | Water Content w (%) |
|-------|--------------------:|--------------------:|
| 1 | 12 | 56.2 |
| 2 | 20 | 51.0 |
| 3 | 30 | 47.5 |
| 4 | 45 | 44.0 |

**Find:** Liquid Limit (LL) of the soil.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Tabulate the data with $\log_{10} N$:

| Trial | N | w (%) | $\log_{10} N$ |
|-------|---|-------|----------------|
| 1 | 12 | 56.2 | 1.0792 |
| 2 | 20 | 51.0 | 1.3010 |
| 3 | 30 | 47.5 | 1.4771 |
| 4 | 45 | 44.0 | 1.6532 |

**Step 2:** Plot $w$ vs $\log_{10} N$ (flow curve). Draw the best-fit line.

**Step 3:** Find LL = water content at N = 25, so $\log_{10}(25) = 1.3979$.

**Step 4 (Linear Interpolation between Trials 2 and 3):**

$$LL = w_2 + \frac{w_3 - w_2}{\log N_3 - \log N_2} \cdot (\log 25 - \log N_2)$$

$$LL = 51.0 + \frac{47.5 - 51.0}{1.4771 - 1.3010} \cdot (1.3979 - 1.3010)$$

$$LL = 51.0 + \frac{-3.5}{0.1761} \cdot (0.0969)$$

$$LL = 51.0 + (-19.875) \cdot (0.0969)$$

$$LL = 51.0 - 1.926$$

$$\boxed{LL = 49.07\% \approx 49.1\%}$$

**Step 5 (Verification using best-fit line equation):**

Using least-squares linear regression on $(x = \log N, y = w)$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1.0792 + 1.3010 + 1.4771 + 1.6532}{4} = 1.3776$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{56.2 + 51.0 + 47.5 + 44.0}{4} = 49.675$$

Slope: $m = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$

| $i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | -0.2984 | 6.525 | -1.9464 | 0.0890 |
| 2 | -0.0766 | 1.325 | -0.1015 | 0.0059 |
| 3 | 0.0995 | -2.175 | -0.2164 | 0.0099 |
| 4 | 0.2756 | -5.675 | -1.5640 | 0.0760 |
| **Σ** | | | **-3.8283** | **0.1808** |

$$m = \frac{-3.8283}{0.1808} = -21.174$$

$$b = \bar{y} - m\bar{x} = 49.675 - (-21.174)(1.3776) = 49.675 + 29.169 = 78.844$$

Flow curve equation: $w = -21.174 \cdot \log N + 78.844$

At N = 25: $w = -21.174 \cdot (1.3979) + 78.844 = -29.597 + 78.844 = 49.25\%$

$$\boxed{LL \approx 49.25\% \text{ (rounded to 49\%)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Both methods** give approximately the same LL (≈49%). The **interpolation method** is faster and used in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example — Plasticity Index and Indices

**Problem:** A soil sample has the following properties:
- Liquid Limit (LL) = 60%
- Plastic Limit (PL) = 28%
- Shrinkage Limit (SL) = 18%
- Natural Water Content (w) = 45%

**Find:** PI, LI, CI, SI, Activity (if % clay = 35%), and classify per IS 1498.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Plasticity Index (PI)**

$$PI = LL - PL = 60 - 28 = 32\%$$

**Step 2: Liquidity Index (LI)**

$$LI = \frac{w - PL}{PI} = \frac{45 - 28}{32} = \frac{17}{32} = 0.531$$

**Step 3: Consistency Index (CI)**

$$CI = \frac{LL - w}{PI} = \frac{60 - 45}{32} = \frac{15}{32} = 0.469$$

**Verification:** $LI + CI = 0.531 + 0.469 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 4: Shrinkage Index (SI)**

$$SI = PL - SL = 28 - 18 = 10\%$$

**Step 5: Activity (A)**

$$A = \frac{PI}{\% \text{ Clay}} = \frac{32}{35} = 0.914$$

Since $0.75 \leq A \leq 1.25$, the soil is **NORMAL** activity.

**Step 6: IS 1498 Classification**

Compute $PI_A = 0.73(LL - 20) = 0.73(60 - 20) = 29.2$

Since actual $PI = 32 > PI_A = 29.2$, soil plots **ABOVE** A-line → **Clay**.

Since $LL = 60 > 50$, classification is **CH (Fat Clay / High Compressibility Clay)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summary:** This is a **fat clay (CH) with high compressibility**, normal activity, with the in-situ state being a soft plastic clay ($CI = 0.47$).

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — One-Point Method

**Problem:** In a single-blow test, a soil closed the Casagrande groove in 18 blows at a water content of 48%. Find LL.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- $w_N = 48\%$
- $N = 18$ blows
- $\tan \beta = 0.121$ (standard cup)

**Step 2:** Apply the one-point formula:

$$LL = w_N \cdot \left(\frac{N}{25}\right)^{0.121}$$

$$LL = 48 \cdot \left(\frac{18}{25}\right)^{0.121}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the ratio:

$$\frac{18}{25} = 0.72$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(0.72)^{0.121}$:

$$(0.72)^{0.121} = e^{0.121 \cdot \ln(0.72)} = e^{0.121 \cdot (-0.3285)} = e^{-0.03975} = 0.9610$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$LL = 48 \cdot 0.9610 = 46.13\%$$

$$\boxed{LL \approx 46.13\%}$$
