# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: Define Pressure Bulb. What is its significance in foundation design? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **pressure bulb** is defined as the zone in the soil mass bounded by an isobar representing a specific fraction (commonly 0.2q or 20%) of the applied surface pressure $q$. It represents the region within which the vertical stress induced by the foundation is significant for engineering purposes (settlement, bearing capacity).

**Significance in Foundation Design:**

1. **Depth of Exploration:** Guides the depth of boreholes for subsoil investigation — soil within the pressure bulb must be tested for compressibility.
2. **Settlement Analysis:** Only soil layers within the pressure bulb contribute significantly to settlement; layers below can often be ignored.
3. **Foundation Type Selection:** A deep pressure bulb may indicate the need for deep foundations (piles) rather than shallow footings.
4. **Influence Zone Identification:** Adjacent foundations or underground utilities within the pressure bulb of one footing may be affected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **0.2q isobar** with the 0.1q or 0.5q isobars. The **0.2q isobar** is the convention — remember it as the "twenty-percent rule."

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Standard Proctor Test and Modified Proctor Test. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standard Proctor Test | Modified Proctor Test |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| IS Code | IS 2720 (Part 7) | IS 2720 (Part 8) |
| Hammer Weight | 2.6 kg (5.5 lb) | 4.9 kg (10 lb) |
| Drop Height | 310 mm | 450 mm |
| Number of Layers | 3 | 5 |
| Number of Blows/Layer | 25 | 25 |
| Compaction Energy | ~595 kJ/m³ | ~2700 kJ/m³ |
| MDD Achieved | Lower | Higher |
| OMC Achieved | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Light structures, fills | Heavy structures, highway bases |

**Key Insight:** Modified Proctor applies **4.5 times more compaction energy**, resulting in higher MDD and lower OMC. This better simulates modern heavy-roller field compaction.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A rectangular footing **2 m × 3 m** carries a uniform load of **150 kN/m²**. Compute the vertical stress at depth **4 m** below the center of the footing using:
   (i) Boussinesq's theory (Equivalent Point Load Method)
   (ii) 2:1 Distribution Method **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Standard Proctor compaction test gave the following results. Determine **OMC** and **MDD**, and plot the **compaction curve** along with the **ZAV line** (assume $G = 2.65$). **[7 Marks]**

| Water Content $w$ (%) | Bulk Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 8 | 1.78 |
| 11 | 1.96 |
| 14 | 2.08 |
| 17 | 2.10 |
| 20 | 2.05 |
| 23 | 1.95 |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Vertical Stress Computation

**Given:** $B = 2$ m, $L = 3$ m, $q = 150$ kN/m², $z = 4$ m, point is below center.

**Method (i) — Equivalent Point Load:**

**Step 1: Compute total load $Q$**

$$Q = q \times A = 150 \times (2 \times 3) = 150 \times 6 = 900 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Apply Boussinesq's equation at $r = 0$ (directly below)**

$$\sigma_z = \frac{3Q}{2\pi z^2} = \frac{3 \times 900}{2 \pi \times 4^2}$$

$$\sigma_z = \frac{2700}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 16} = \frac{2700}{100.53} = 26.86 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_z \approx 26.86 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Method (ii) — 2:1 Distribution Method:**

**Step 1: Compute spread dimensions at $z = 4$ m**

$$B_z = B + z = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ m}$$

$$L_z = L + z = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute spread area**

$$A_z = 6 \times 7 = 42 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply 2:1 formula**

$$\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)} = \frac{150 \times 2 \times 3}{6 \times 7} = \frac{900}{42}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_z = 21.43 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | $\sigma_z$ (kN/m²) | % of $q$ |
|--------|-------------------|----------|
| Equivalent Point Load | 26.86 | 17.9% |
| 2:1 Distribution | 21.43 | 14.3% |

---

#### Part (b) — Compaction Test Analysis

**Step 1: Calculate dry density $\rho_d = \rho / (1+w)$**

| $w$ | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $1+w$ | $\rho_d$ (g/cm³) |
|-----|----------------|-------|------------------|
| 0.08 | 1.78 | 1.08 | **1.648** |
| 0.11 | 1.96 | 1.11 | **1.766** |
| 0.14 | 2.08 | 1.14 | **1.825** |
| 0.17 | 2.10 | 1.17 | **1.795** |
| 0.20 | 2.05 | 1.20 | **1.708** |
| 0.23 | 1.95 | 1.23 | **1.585** |

**Step 2: Identify OMC and MDD**

From the table, peak $\rho_d = 1.825$ g/cm³ occurs at $w = 14\%$.

$$\boxed{\text{OMC} = 14\%, \quad \text{MDD} = 1.825 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1825 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3: Compute ZAV line using $G = 2.65$**

$$\rho_{d,zav} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + wG}$$

| $w$ | $wG$ | $1 + wG$ | $\rho_{d,zav}$ (g/cm³) |
|-----|------|----------|------------------------|
| 0.08 | 0.212 | 1.212 | 2.187 |
| 0.11 | 0.292 | 1.292 | 2.052 |
| 0.14 | 0.371 | 1.371 | 1.933 |
| 0.17 | 0.451 | 1.451 | 1.827 |
| 0.20 | 0.530 | 1.530 | 1.732 |
| 0.23 | 0.610 | 1.610 | 1.646 |

**Step 4: Verify ZAV > Compaction curve**

At $w = 0.14$: $\rho_{d,zav} = 1.933$ > $\rho_d = 1.825$ ✓ (compaction curve lies below ZAV)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal** in dry density formula (e.g., using $w = 14$ instead of $0.14$). This gives wrong $\rho_d$.
> 2. **Confusing bulk density with dry density** when reading values from the graph. MDD is the **dry** density at the peak, not bulk density.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the ZAV line** — examiners often deduct 1–2 marks for missing the ZAV curve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A circular footing of **diameter 4 m** carries a uniform pressure of **200 kN/m²**. Calculate the vertical stress at depths of **2 m, 4 m, 6 m, and 8 m** below the center using Boussinesq's equation. Tabulate the results and plot the **influence factor** $I_c$ vs $z/a$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the **distribution of contact pressure** beneath (i) a rigid footing on sandy soil, (ii) a rigid footing on clayey soil, and (iii) a flexible footing. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Vertical Stress Under Circular Footing

**Given:** Diameter = 4 m → Radius $a = 2$ m; $q = 200$ kN/m²

**Formula:**

$$I_c = 1 - \left\{ \frac{1}{1 + (a/z)^2} \right\}^{3/2}$$

**Step-by-Step Calculations:**

**Case 1: $z = 2$ m**

$$a/z = 2/2 = 1, \quad (a/z)^2 = 1$$

$$1 + (a/z)^2 = 2, \quad 2^{3/2} = 2.828$$

$$I_c = 1 - (1/2.828) = 1 - 0.3536 = 0.646$$

$$\sigma_z = 200 \times 0.646 = 129.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

**Case 2: $z = 4$ m**

$$a/z = 2/4 = 0.5, \quad (a/z)^2 = 0.25$$

$$1 + (a/z)^2 = 1.25, \quad (1.25)^{3/2} = 1.25 \times 1.118 = 1.3975$$

$$I_c = 1 - (1/1.3975) = 1 - 0.7155 = 0.2845$$

$$\sigma_z = 200 \times 0.2845 = 56.9 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

**Case 3: $z = 6$ m**

$$a/z = 2/6 = 0.333, \quad (a/z)^2 = 0.111$$

$$1 + (a/z)^2 = 1.111, \quad (1.111)^{3/2} = 1.111 \times 1.0541 = 1.1711$$

$$I_c = 1 - (1/1.1711) = 1 - 0.854 = 0.146$$

$$\sigma_z = 200 \times 0.146 = 29.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

**Case 4: $z = 8$ m**

$$a/z = 2/8 = 0.25, \quad (a/z)^2 = 0.0625$$

$$1 + (a/z)^2 = 1.0625, \quad (1.0625)^{3/2} = 1.0625 \times 1.0308 = 1.0952$$

$$I_c = 1 - (1/1.0952) = 1 - 0.9131 = 0.0869$$

$$\sigma_z = 200 \times 0.0869 = 17.38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

**Summary Table:**

| $z$ (m) | $z/a$ | $I_c$ | $\sigma_z$ (kN/m²) | % of $q$ |
|---------|-------|-------|---------------------|----------|
| 2 | 1.00 | 0.646 | 129.2 | 64.6% |
| 4 | 2.00 | 0.284 | 56.9 | 28.4% |
| 6 | 3.00 | 0.146 | 29.2 | 14.6% |
| 8 | 4.00 | 0.087 | 17.4 | 8.7% |

**Graph of $I_c$ vs $z/a$:**

```
I_c
1.0 |*
0.8 |  *
0.6 |     *
0.4 |         *
0.2 |              *
0.0 |___________________*_______ z/a
    0   1   2   3   4   5
```

The curve is **non-linear**, with rapid decay initially, then asymptotic towards zero as $z \to \infty$.

---

#### Part (b) — Contact Pressure Distribution

**(i) Rigid Footing on Sandy (Cohesionless) Soil:**

The contact pressure distribution is **parabolic**, with **maximum pressure at the edges** and **minimum at the center**. This is because sandy soil has low resistance to plastic flow; the edges of a rigid footing tend to "dig" into the soil, concentrating pressure at the edges.

**Why this happens:**
- Sand cannot resist tension
- The soil at the center undergoes more strain than at the edges
- Equilibrium requires the pressure to concentrate at the edges

---

**(ii) Rigid Footing on Clayey (Cohesive) Soil:**

The contact pressure distribution is **saddle-shaped**: high at the edges, low near the center, but more uniform than sand. This is because clay has some cohesive strength that redistributes stresses.

**Why this happens:**
- Clay has both cohesion and some tensile resistance
- The soil-structure interaction is more complex
- The pressure at edges is theoretically infinite in the elastic case, but in practice, soil yields and limits the maximum pressure

---

**(iii) Flexible Footing:**

A flexible footing deflects to match the settlement profile of the soil. The contact pressure is **uniform** and equals the applied pressure $q$. This is the most common design assumption.

**Why this happens:**
- The footing is flexible enough to conform to soil deformation
- No redistribution of pressure occurs
- The applied pressure $q$ is directly transmitted as contact pressure

---

**Schematic Diagrams (Mermaid Flow):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Rigid Footing on Sand] --> B[Parabolic Curve]
    A1[Rigid Footing on Clay] --> C[Saddle Shaped Curve]
    A2[Flexible Footing on any Soil] --> D[Uniform Pressure q]
    B --> E[Max at Edges]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Constant q across base]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls — Part (b):**
> 1. **Reversing sand and clay pressure profiles** — Rigid footing on sand has max at edges, but students often write it as uniform.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention why** — Just drawing the curve without explaining the soil-structure interaction loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing rigid and flexible** — A flexible footing is assumed to have **uniform** contact pressure in design, NOT parabolic.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Using $r = 0$ incorrectly** in Boussinesq's equation — remember $r$ is the horizontal distance from the load to the point of interest, not the footing radius.
>
> 2. **Confusing Boussinesq with Westergaard** — Boussinesq is for **isotropic** soil; Westergaard is for layered/sedimentary deposits (not in KTU syllabus typically, but students mix them up).
>
> 3. **Forgetting superposition principle** — For non-corner points under rectangular loads, students forget to divide the rectangle into 4 sub-rectangles.
>
> 4. **2:1 method units mismatch** — The 2:1 formula gives stress in kN/m² only if $q$ is in kN/m² and dimensions are in meters. Mixing units (e.g., cm) gives wrong answers.
>
> 5. **OMC identification error** — In compaction, OMC is at the **peak of dry density curve**, not at the peak of bulk density curve. The peak shifts!

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### **Quick Revision Checklist:**

✅ **Vertical Stress Concepts:**
- Boussinesq's theory assumes **homogeneous, isotropic, elastic** half-space
- Vertical stress decreases with depth and spreads laterally
- Influence factor $I = \sigma_z / q$ is dimensionless

✅ **Circular Area (No Derivation Required):**
- Formula: $\sigma_z = q[1 - \{1/(1+(a/z)^2)\}^{3/2}]$
- At $z = 2a$, $\sigma_z \approx 0.284q$ (28.4%)
- At $z = 0$, $\sigma_z = 0$; at $z \to \infty$, $\sigma_z \to q$

✅ **Rectangular Area (Fadum's Chart):**
- $m = B/z$, $n = L/z$
- $\sigma_z = q \cdot I$ (corner of rectangle)
- For non-corner points: use **superposition** (4 sub-rectangles)

✅ **Equivalent Point Load Method:**
- $Q = qA$ at centroid
- Boussinesq equation: $\sigma_z = 3Q/(2\pi z^2 [1+(r/z)^2]^{5/2})$
- Accurate for $z > 1.5B$

✅ **2:1 Distribution Method:**
- $\sigma_z = qBL/[(B+z)(L+z)]$
- Quick, simple, widely used in pile group design
- Conservative for shallow depths

✅ **Pressure Isobars & Bulbs:**
- Isobar: contour of equal $\sigma_z$
- Pressure bulb: zone within 0.2q isobar
- Used for **depth of exploration** and **settlement analysis**

✅ **Contact Pressure Distribution:**
- Rigid on sand: **parabolic** (max at edges)
- Rigid on clay: **saddle-shaped**
- Flexible: **uniform** ($= q$)
- Design assumption: **uniform** for rigid footings on clay (IS 456)

✅ **Compaction (OMC & MDD):**
- OMC: water content at peak $\rho_d$
- MDD: maximum dry density at OMC
- Standard Proctor: 2.6 kg hammer, 310 mm drop, 3 layers
- Modified Proctor: 4.9 kg hammer, 450 mm drop, 5 layers
- Modified Proctor → higher MDD, lower OMC
- ZAV line: theoretical limit (100% saturation)
- Compaction curve always **below** ZAV line
- Field control: ≥ 95% MDD (typical)

### **Key Formulas at a Glance:**

$$\sigma_z \text{ (Boussinesq Point)} = \frac{3Q}{2\pi z^2 [1+(r/z)^2]^{5/2}}$$

$$\sigma_z \text{ (Circular)} = q\left[1 - \left\{\frac{1}{1+(a/z)^2}\right\}^{3/2}\right]$$

$$\sigma_z \text{ (2:1 Method)} = \frac{qBL}{(B+z)(L+z)}$$

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1+w}$$

$$\rho_{d,zav} = \frac{G \rho_w}{1+wG}$$

### **Final Exam Tip:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid — "BOUSS 2:1 FADUM"**
> - **BOUSS**inesq = exact, complex
> - **2:1** = approximate, simple
> - **FADUM** = graphical, for rectangles
> - Always read the question carefully: "Boussinesq" or "approximate method"?

**Best of luck for your KTU exams!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Air Voids Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Zero Air Voids Line ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL):** It is a theoretical compaction curve representing the relationship between **dry unit weight (γ_d)** and **water content (w)** for a saturated soil, where the degree of saturation **S = 100%** (i.e., the air voids **e_a = 0**). It defines the *upper boundary* of dry density achievable for a given water content, assuming complete saturation. In the phase diagram of a soil mass, the voids (V_v) consist of: - Volume of water (V_w) - Volume of air (V_a) A **Zero Air Voids** condition implies that **V_a = 0**, so all voids are theoretically filled with water — the soil is at its **maximum possible saturation** for a given water content. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a sponge that you soak in water until you cannot squeeze any more water out. The sponge's pores are completely filled — there is no room left for air. If you tried to pack more water in, it would just flow back out. The **Zero Air Voids Line** represents the *densest possible wet sponge condition*. In soil mechanics, this is the **theoretical "ceiling"** of the compaction curve — no field compaction test can ever plot above this line because soil in the field always contains some entrapped air. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants - **Unit weight of water:** **γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³** (or **10 kN/m³** for simplified KTU problems) - **Specific gravity of water:** **G_w = 1.0** - **Degree of Saturation:** **S = V_w / V_v** (dimensionless, 0 ≤ S ≤ 1) - **Air voids (V_a):** Volume occupied by air in the void space > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to plot the Zero Air Voids Line, always show it lying *above and to the right* of the Standard or Modified Proctor compaction curve. Students often incorrectly place the ZAVL *below* the field curve — this is a common 2-mark deduction. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Phase Relations to ZAVL The ZAVL equation emerges directly from the **three-phase system of soils**. We begin with the fundamental phase relations: #### Step 1: Relation Between Degree of Saturation, Void Ratio, and Water Content The water content of a soil (w) and the void ratio (e) are related through the degree of saturation (S): $$ S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} $$ #### Step 2: Applying the Zero Air Voids Condition (S = 1) At **Zero Air Voids**, the soil is fully saturated, so **S = 1.0** (i.e., 100%). Substituting into the relation: $$ 1 = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} $$ Rearranging to express the void ratio in terms of water content: $$ e = w \cdot G_s $$ #### Step 3: Substituting into the Dry Unit Weight Equation The dry unit weight of a soil is given by: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} $$ Substituting the ZAVL void ratio e = w·G_s: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} $$ This is the **Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL) Equation** — the foundational relationship in KTU soil mechanics problems. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes all the high-yield equations for the Zero Air Voids Line. | **S.No** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |----------|-------------|-----------------|-----------| | 1 | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$ | Relation between saturation, water content, and void ratio | Dimensionless | | 2 | $e = w \cdot G_s$ | ZAVL void ratio condition (when S = 1) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ | **ZAVL Equation (Key)** — Dry unit weight at zero air voids | kN/m³ | | 4 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{S \cdot \gamma_w}{w + \dfrac{S}{G_s}}$ | General form for any degree of saturation | kN/m³ | | 5 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ | Bulk unit weight (γ) to dry unit weight conversion | kN/m³ | | 6 | $\gamma_b = \gamma_d \cdot (1 + w)$ | Dry unit weight to bulk unit weight conversion | kN/m³ | | 7 | $\gamma_{sat} = \dfrac{(G_s + e)\gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Saturated unit weight (ZAVL bulk state) | kN/m³ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The ZAVL is practically used as a **quality control benchmark** in field compaction. When a field compaction curve falls **close to the ZAVL**, it indicates that the soil has been compacted to near-saturated conditions (e.g., using a heavy smooth-wheeled roller on a fine-grained soil). Curves that fall far below the ZAVL suggest that the field compaction effort is insufficient or the soil structure retains high air voids (e.g., freshly compacted clays). ### 2.3 Significance of the ZAVL in Compaction Theory The ZAVL plays a **central role** in KTU's compaction theory because: 1. **Upper Bound of Dry Density:** For any given water content, the ZAVL gives the *theoretical maximum* dry unit weight attainable if all air could be expelled. 2. **Saturation Reference Line:** It is the locus of points where S = 1, which acts as a reference for partial-saturation curves (S = 90%, 80%, etc.). 3. **Compaction Curve Analysis:** Field or laboratory compaction curves that are *steep and rise toward* the ZAVL suggest a saturated, plastic-type soil response. Curves that are *bell-shaped and lie far below* the ZAVL suggest partially saturated, frictional soils. 4. **Quality Control Tool:** In the field, the closer the line of optimums is to the ZAVL, the more efficient the compaction process. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Zero Air Voids Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > The following derivation shows every algebraic step from the **phase diagram** to the **ZAVL equation**. This is a **high-yield derivation** in KTU exams. --- #### **DERIVATION: From Phase Relations to the ZAVL Equation** **Starting Point — The Degree of Saturation Definition:** The degree of saturation **S** is defined as the ratio of the volume of water to the total volume of voids: $$ S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} $$ **Step 1 — Expressing V_w in terms of weight relationships:** The mass of water (M_w) is related to the volume of water (V_w) through the density of water (ρ_w). In terms of unit weights: $$ V_w = \frac{M_w}{\rho_w} = \frac{W_w}{\gamma_w} $$ where W_w = weight of water. **Step 2 — Expressing V_v in terms of voids ratio:** By definition of void ratio: $$ e = \frac{V_v}{V_s} \implies V_v = e \cdot V_s $$ **Step 3 — Substituting into the S equation:** $$ S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} = \frac{W_w / \gamma_w}{e \cdot V_s} $$ **Step 4 — Introducing the Specific Gravity of Solids (G_s) and Water Content (w):** The weight of solids is W_s = G_s · γ_w · V_s. The water content is w = W_w / W_s. So W_w = w · W_s = w · G_s · γ_w · V_s. Substituting: $$ S = \frac{w \cdot G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s / \gamma_w}{e \cdot V_s} $$ Canceling V_s and γ_w: $$ S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} $$ This is the **fundamental three-phase relation** (Equation 1). **Step 5 — Applying the Zero Air Voids Condition:** For a Zero Air Voids line, the soil is **fully saturated**, meaning **S = 1.0**. Substituting: $$ 1.0 = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} $$ Rearranging: $$ e = w \cdot G_s $$ **Step 6 — Starting from the Dry Unit Weight Equation:** The dry unit weight of soil is defined as the weight of solids per unit total volume: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{W_s}{V} $$ Using the relationship V = V_s + V_v = V_s(1 + e) and W_s = G_s · γ_w · V_s: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w \cdot V_s}{V_s (1 + e)} $$ Canceling V_s: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} $$ This is the **general dry unit weight equation** (Equation 2). **Step 7 — Substituting the ZAVL void ratio e = w·G_s:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} $$ **FINAL ZAVL EQUATION:** $$ \boxed{\gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ZAVL Numerical Computation > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Model):** Given G_s = 2.70 and γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³, calculate the dry unit weight on the ZAVL for water contents of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25%, and 30%. **General Formula Used:** $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ **Step-by-Step Computations:** **For w = 5% = 0.05:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.05 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.135} = \frac{26.487}{1.135} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 23.34 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 10% = 0.10:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.10 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.27} = \frac{26.487}{1.27} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 20.86 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 15% = 0.15:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.15 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.405} = \frac{26.487}{1.405} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 18.85 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 20% = 0.20:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.20 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.54} = \frac{26.487}{1.54} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 17.20 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 25% = 0.25:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.25 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.675} = \frac{26.487}{1.675} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 15.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 30% = 0.30:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.30 \times 2.70} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.81} = \frac{26.487}{1.81} $$ $$ \gamma_d = 14.63 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **Summary Table — ZAVL Coordinates (G_s = 2.70, γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³):** | **S.No** | **Water Content (w)** | **Void Ratio (e = w·G_s)** | **Dry Unit Weight (γ_d)** | |----------|------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | 5% | 0.135 | 23.34 kN/m³ | | 2 | 10% | 0.270 | 20.86 kN/m³ | | 3 | 15% | 0.405 | 18.85 kN/m³ | | 4 | 20% | 0.540 | 17.20 kN/m³ | | 5 | 25% | 0.675 | 15.81 kN/m³ | | 6 | 30% | 0.810 | 14.63 kN/m³ | > [!TIP] > **Observation from the ZAVL:** As the water content **increases**, the dry unit weight on the ZAVL **decreases monotonically**. The ZAVL is therefore a **continuously decreasing curve**, not a bell-shaped curve like the standard Proctor curve. This is a key distinguishing feature tested in KTU examinations. ### 3.3 Proof that ZAVL is the Upper Bound of Dry Density To prove that the ZAVL is the upper theoretical bound of dry density, consider the general relation: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} $$ For a given water content **w**, the void ratio **e** is bounded below by the ZAVL condition: $$ e \geq w \cdot G_s \quad \text{(with equality only when } S = 1\text{)} $$ Since dry unit weight **γ_d is inversely proportional to (1 + e)**, the **maximum γ_d** for a given **w** occurs at the **minimum e**, which is e = w·G_s (ZAVL). This proves that the ZAVL is the upper bound. $$ \gamma_{d,\max}(w) = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Phase Relationships Leading to ZAVL > [!NOTE] > The following block-level flow diagram shows the **causal chain of reasoning** from phase relations to the ZAVL equation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Soil Phase Diagram
Solids, Water, Air"] --> B["Degree of Saturation
S = Vw / Vv"] B --> C["S = w * Gs / e
Fundamental Relation"] C --> D["Apply ZAVL Condition
S = 1.0 or 100%"] D --> E["Void Ratio e = w * Gs"] E --> F["Dry Unit Weight Equation
gamma_d = Gs * gamma_w / 1 + e"] F --> G["ZAVL Equation
gamma_d = Gs * gamma_w / 1 + w * Gs"] G --> H["Plot gamma_d vs w
Continuously Decreasing Curve"] H --> I["Upper Bound of Compaction
Theoretical Maximum Dry Density"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Position of ZAVL Relative to Compaction Curves ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["Compaction Plot"] A1["X-axis: Water Content w"] A2["Y-axis: Dry Unit Weight gamma_d"] end B["ZAVL Curve
S=100% Line
Top Boundary"] --> C["Modified Proctor Curve
Heavy Compaction"] C --> D["Standard Proctor Curve
Normal Compaction"] D --> E["S=90% Saturation Line
Partial Saturation"] E --> F["S=80% Saturation Line
Lower Partial Saturation"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison Table of Curves ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Compaction Curves in Soil Mechanics"] --> B["ZAVL
Saturation 100%
No Air Voids
Monotonically Decreasing"] A --> C["Modified Proctor
Heavy Compaction
Bell Shaped Curve
Lower OMC Higher MDD"] A --> D["Standard Proctor
Light Compaction
Bell Shaped Curve
Higher OMC Lower MDD"] A --> E["Lines of Constant Saturation
S=80%, S=90%
Monotonically Decreasing
Parallel to ZAVL"] B --> F["ZAVL is the Upper Bound
All Compaction Curves Lie Below ZAVL"] C --> F D --> F E --> F ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Sketch — Soil State at ZAVL vs. Field Compaction | **Soil State** | **Air Voids (V_a)** | **Degree of Saturation (S)** | **Position vs. ZAVL** | |----------------|----------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------| | Zero Air Voids (Theoretical) | **V_a = 0** | **S = 100%** | **ON the ZAVL** | | Well-Compacted Soil | V_a small | S = 90-95% | Just below ZAVL | | Poorly Compacted Soil | V_a large | S = 70-85% | Far below ZAVL | | Dry Soil (OMC side) | V_a = large | S = 50-70% | Far below ZAVL (left side) | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL) in soil compaction. State the condition under which it is obtained.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Zero Air Voids Line (ZAVL) is the theoretical compaction curve that represents the relationship between dry unit weight (γ_d) and water content (w) of a soil when it is **fully saturated** (S = 100%), meaning there are **no air voids** in the soil mass. **Condition:** The condition for the ZAVL is that the **air voids V_a = 0**, and the degree of saturation **S = 1.0 (100%)**. The governing equation is: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} $$ The ZAVL acts as the **upper bound** of dry unit weight achievable for any given water content. --- #### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Why does the Zero Air Voids Line always lie above the field compaction curve?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The ZAVL lies above the field compaction curve because of the following reasons: 1. The ZAVL represents a **theoretical condition** where all voids in the soil are filled with water, with **zero air voids** (V_a = 0, S = 100%). 2. In the field (or laboratory), it is **practically impossible** to expel all air from the soil voids. The soil always retains a small amount of entrapped air (V_a > 0), keeping the saturation S < 100%. 3. Since the presence of air voids increases the total void ratio **e** (for the same water content), the dry unit weight γ_d is lower. Thus, the field compaction curve plots **below** the ZAVL. --- ### **Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Always include **units** (kN/m³) for every γ_d value; missing units = 0.5 mark deduction. > - When plotting the ZAVL, do **not confuse it** with the Standard Proctor Curve — they have different shapes (ZAVL is monotonically decreasing; Proctor is bell-shaped). > - Always state the **ZAVL condition (S = 100%, V_a = 0)** explicitly at the start of the solution to score full marks. > - Forgetting to convert w from **% to decimal** in the formula is the most common arithmetic error in KTU exams. > - When comparing curves, mention that **OMC, MDD, and the position relative to ZAVL** are all part of the answer. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the equation of the Zero Air Voids Line starting from the phase relations of soil. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a soil with G_s = 2.68 and γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³, calculate the dry unit weights on the ZAVL for water contents of 8%, 12%, 16%, 20%, and 24%. Tabulate the results and plot the ZAVL. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** The degree of saturation (S) is defined as: $$ S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} $$ Using the phase relationships, V_w can be expressed in terms of water content (w), specific gravity (G_s), and the volume of solids (V_s), while V_v = e · V_s. This yields: $$ S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} \quad \text{...(1)} $$ **Applying the Zero Air Voids condition** (S = 1): $$ 1 = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} \implies e = w \cdot G_s \quad \text{...(2)} $$ The general dry unit weight equation is: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \quad \text{...(3)} $$ **Substituting (2) into (3):** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} $$ This is the **Zero Air Voids Line equation**. (7 Marks — full derivation) --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):** Given: G_s = 2.68, γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³ **Formula:** $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} = \dfrac{2.68 \times 9.81}{1 + 2.68w} = \dfrac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68w}$ **For w = 8% = 0.08:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68 \times 0.08} = \frac{26.2908}{1.2144} = 21.65 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 12% = 0.12:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68 \times 0.12} = \frac{26.2908}{1.3216} = 19.89 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 16% = 0.16:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68 \times 0.16} = \frac{26.2908}{1.4288} = 18.40 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 20% = 0.20:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68 \times 0.20} = \frac{26.2908}{1.536} = 17.12 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **For w = 24% = 0.24:** $$ \gamma_d = \frac{26.2908}{1 + 2.68 \times 0.24} = \frac{26.2908}{1.6432} = 16.00 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **Summary Table:** | **w (%)** | **e = w·G_s** | **γ_d (kN/m³)** | |------------|----------------|------------------| | 8 | 0.2144 | 21.65 | | 12 | 0.3216 | 19.89 | | 16 | 0.4288 | 18.40 | | 20 | 0.5360 | 17.12 | | 24 | 0.6432 | 16.00 | **Plot:** A monotonically decreasing curve passing through the above coordinates. (7 Marks — full calculation) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the significance of the Zero Air Voids Line in soil compaction. Describe its position relative to the Standard and Modified Proctor curves. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The compaction curve of a clayey soil (G_s = 2.72) gives a maximum dry density of 18.5 kN/m³ at OMC = 16%. Calculate the theoretical maximum dry density on the ZAVL at this OMC and determine the degree of saturation achieved at the field MDD. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Significance and Position (7 Marks):** **Significance of ZAVL:** 1. **Theoretical Upper Bound:** The ZAVL represents the maximum possible dry unit weight a soil can attain for any water content if the soil were 100% saturated. No field or lab compaction can plot above this line. 2. **Compaction Quality Indicator:** The proximity of a compaction curve to the ZAVL indicates the **efficiency of the compaction effort**. Closer curves indicate denser, more saturated compacted soil. 3. **Saturation Reference:** The ZAVL serves as the **S = 100% reference** for plotting constant-saturation lines (S = 80%, 90%, etc.) in a compaction plot. 4. **Material Behavior Insight:** The ZAVL helps engineers understand whether a soil is **frictional** (curves lie far below ZAVL) or **cohesive/saturated** (curves approach the ZAVL). **Position Relative to Compaction Curves:** - The ZAVL lies **above** both the Standard and Modified Proctor curves for the entire range of water content. - The Modified Proctor curve lies **between** the ZAVL and the Standard Proctor curve. - All three curves are continuous; however, the ZAVL is **monotonically decreasing**, while the Proctor curves are **bell-shaped** with a distinct peak (MDD). - The ZAVL represents a **dry side asymptote** — the Proctor curves approach but never cross it. (7 Marks — full explanation) --- **Part (b) — Theoretical Maximum Dry Density at OMC (7 Marks):** Given: G_s = 2.72, OMC = 16% = 0.16, MDD = 18.5 kN/m³ **Step 1 — Calculate the ZAVL dry unit weight at w = 16%:** $$ \gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s} = \frac{2.72 \times 9.81}{1 + 0.16 \times 2.72} $$ $$ \gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = \frac{26.6832}{1 + 0.4352} = \frac{26.6832}{1.4352} $$ $$ \gamma_{d,\text{ZAVL}} = 18.59 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Determine the void ratio at field MDD (γ_d = 18.5 kN/m³):** Using the dry unit weight formula: $$ \gamma_d = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e} \implies e = \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1 $$ $$ e = \frac{2.72 \times 9.81}{18.5} - 1 = \frac{26.6832}{18.5} - 1 = 1.4424 - 1 $$ $$ e = 0.4424 $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the degree of saturation at field MDD:** $$ S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.16 \times 2.72}{0.4424} = \frac{0.4352}{0.4424} $$ $$ S = 0.9837 \approx 98.4\% $$ **Conclusion:** The theoretical ZAVL dry density at OMC is **18.59 kN/m³**, and the field compaction achieved **98.4% saturation** — indicating that the field compaction is very close to full saturation. (7 Marks — full computation) --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:** - **ZAVL Definition:** Theoretical compaction curve where **S = 100%** (V_a = 0, no air voids). - **ZAVL Equation:** $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + w \cdot G_s}$ - **ZAVL Position:** Always lies **above** the Standard and Modified Proctor curves (acts as the upper bound). - **ZAVL Shape:** **Monotonically decreasing** curve (not bell-shaped). - **Proctor Shape:** Bell-shaped curve with a distinct peak (MDD) at OMC. - **Compaction Effort Effect:** Higher compaction effort (Modified Proctor) → **higher MDD** and **lower OMC**, curve shifts **upward and leftward** toward the ZAVL. - **Constant Saturation Lines:** Lines of S = 80%, 90%, etc., are **parallel** to the ZAVL and lie **below** it. - **Conversion Reminder:** Always convert water content from **percentage to decimal** (e.g., 16% → 0.16) before using in the formula. - **γ_w Value:** Use **9.81 kN/m³** for precise KTU calculations, or **10 kN/m³** for simplified approximations. - **Theoretical Max Density:** The ZAVL equation gives the **maximum theoretical γ_d** at a given water content — never exceeded in practice. - **Saturated Unit Weight (γ_sat) at ZAVL:** $\gamma_{sat} = \gamma_d \cdot (1 + w) = \dfrac{(G_s + e) \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ - **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to state the ZAVL condition (S = 100%) explicitly in the solution. - **Engineering Application:** Used as a **quality control tool** to assess field compaction efficiency and identify potential over-compaction or under-compaction zones.

topics.title: IS Light & Heavy - Factors Affecting Compaction - Numerical Problems - Field Compaction Methods - Compaction Control - Proctor Needle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** IS Light & Heavy - Factors Affecting Compaction - Numerical Problems - Field Compaction Methods - Compaction Control - Proctor Needle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Compaction — The Formal Definition

**Compaction** is the process of mechanically densifying a soil mass by reducing the volume of its air voids through the application of transient external loads (static, vibratory, or impact), without significantly altering the moisture content of the soil. It is fundamentally a *particle rearrangement* process, not a saturation or consolidation phenomenon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCET402 — Module 3):**
> Compaction is defined as the process of increasing the density of soil by mechanically reducing the air voids, with water content acting as the lubricating medium that enables particle rearrangement under applied energy.

The standard quantitative measure of compaction is the **Dry Unit Weight ($\gamma_d$)**, expressed in **kN/m³** (SI) or **g/cc** (CGS).

$$\gamma_d = \frac{\gamma}{1 + w}$$

where $\gamma$ is the bulk (wet) unit weight and $w$ is the water content (decimal).

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Standard Compaction (IS 2720 Part 7)

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) codifies **two** standardized laboratory compaction tests. These are the *non-negotiable baseline* for every KTU numerical and design problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS Light Compaction (Standard Proctor Test) — IS 2720 (Part 7): 1980 (Reaffirmed 2020)**
> - Rammer Mass: **2.5 kg** (Light)
> - Drop Height: **300 mm**
> - Number of Layers: **3**
> - Blows per Layer: **25**
> - Mould Volume: **1000 cc** (standard)
> - Compactive Energy: **~ 605.9 kJ/m³** (≈ 12400 ft-lbf/ft³ in old units)

> [!NOTE]
> **IS Heavy Compaction (Modified Proctor Test) — IS 2720 (Part 7): 1980 (Reaffirmed 2020)**
> - Rammer Mass: **4.9 kg** (Heavy)
> - Drop Height: **450 mm**
> - Number of Layers: **5**
> - Blows per Layer: **25**
> - Mould Volume: **1000 cc** (standard)
> - Compactive Energy: **~ 2704.7 kJ/m³** (≈ 56000 ft-lbf/ft³ in old units)

The compactive energy is computed as:

$$E = \frac{(W_{rammer} \times H_{drop} \times N_{blows} \times N_{layers})}{V_{mould}}$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine packing a suitcase full of clothes. If you simply drop the clothes in, lots of empty spaces (voids) remain. If you press each layer down with your palm, the clothes pack tighter — but with effort. If you use a *heavier* palm strike and do *more* layers, the packing becomes even denser.

> **Suitcase = Soil Mould**, **Clothes = Soil Grains**, **Palm strike = Rammer Blow**, **Number of strikes per layer = Blows per Layer**.

The water content is the *lubricant* — just like a wet sponge squeezes more easily than a dry one. Too little water means grains can't slide; too much water fills voids with liquid (which can't be expelled) and density drops. The *optimum* is a sweet spot.

## 1.4 The Compaction Curve — A First Glance

When $\gamma_d$ is plotted against water content $w$ for a fixed compactive effort, the result is the famous **bell-shaped compaction curve**.

- **Left limb (dry side)**: Insufficient water — grains can't rearrange, friction dominates.
- **Peak**: **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** with **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)**.
- **Right limb (wet side)**: Excess water displaces grains, density falls.

This single curve is the *signature* of every compaction problem in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Two Standards? — Light vs Heavy Compaction

Civil engineering practice in India historically moved from **light** to **heavy** rollers and laboratory tests because:

1. Modern highway and airfield subgrades must sustain heavier wheel loads.
2. Heavy compaction simulates modern field equipment (vibratory rollers, sheep-foot rollers).
3. For a given soil, the **Modified Proctor** yields a **higher MDD** and **lower OMC** than the **Standard Proctor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Contrast — Always Memorize:**
> | Feature | IS Light (Standard) | IS Heavy (Modified) |
> |---|---|---|
> | MDD | Lower | Higher (~ 5–10% greater) |
> | OMC | Higher | Lower (~ 2–5% less) |
> | Field application | Embankments, low-volume roads | Highways, runways, dams |

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Compaction

### 2.2.1 Water Content
The single most influential factor. As $w$ increases from 0 toward OMC, $\gamma_d$ increases (air voids reduce). Beyond OMC, the water begins to occupy the volume that soil grains could have occupied, and $\gamma_d$ drops.

### 2.2.2 Compactive Effort (Energy per Unit Volume)
Increasing the number of blows, the mass of the rammer, the drop height, or the layers — *any* of these — increases the energy $E$. A higher $E$ shifts the compaction curve **upward and leftward** (higher MDD, lower OMC).

$$E \uparrow \Rightarrow \gamma_{d,\max} \uparrow \quad \text{and} \quad w_{opt} \downarrow$$

### 2.2.3 Soil Type (Gradation & Plasticity)
- **Well-graded cohesionless soils** (e.g., SW) compact to high densities with little effort.
- **Poorly graded sands** require vibration rather than impact.
- **Clays of high plasticity (CH)** require *more* water to soften clay clusters but gain less density; they are very sensitive to water.
- **Silts** are the most troublesome — they pump under rollers (loss of bearing capacity) when wet.

### 2.2.4 Method of Compaction
Impact, static, kneading, and vibratory methods produce different particle arrangements. No single method is universally optimal; selection is soil-specific.

### 2.2.5 Admixtures
Lime, cement, fly ash, and bitumen are used to stabilize soils. Lime + clay flocculates particles and improves workability at lower moisture contents.

### 2.2.6 Layer Thickness
Field compaction is done in **lifts** (layers), typically 150–300 mm thick, because impact energy attenuates rapidly with depth.

### 2.2.7 Temperature
For fine-grained soils in cold regions, frozen water acts as a cement; compaction becomes impossible.

## 2.3 Field Compaction Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Rollers and Applicability**
> | Roller Type | Best Suited For | Mechanism |
> |---|---|---|
> | Smooth-Wheel (Static) | Granular soils, surface finishing of embankments | Static pressure |
> | Pneumatic-Tyred | Silt, clayey soils, granular | Kneading + static |
> | Sheepsfoot / Padfoot | Clayey soils (deep lifts) | Kneading |
> | Vibratory | Granular, cohesionless (sand, gravel) | Vibration + impact |
> | Rammer (Jumping Jack) | Confined trenches, backfills | Heavy impact |

## 2.4 Field Compaction Control — Methods

The goal is to verify that the *field* $\gamma_d$ is $\geq$ a *specified* percentage (usually 95–100%) of the *laboratory* MDD, and that field $w$ is within $\pm 2\%$ of OMC.

### 2.4.1 Sand Cone Method (IS 2720 Part 28)
Reference method. A hole is excavated, weighed, and the volume is determined by filling with calibrated sand. $\gamma_d$ is computed as:

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_{soil}}{V_{hole} \cdot (1 + w_{field})}$$

### 2.4.2 Core Cutter Method (IS 2720 Part 29)
Suitable for *soft* cohesive soils in the field. A steel cylinder is driven into the ground; the soil inside is trimmed and weighed.

$$\gamma_d = \frac{W_{core}}{V_{cutter} \cdot (1 + w_{field})}$$

### 2.4.3 Nuclear Density Gauge (IS 2720 Part 33)
Non-destructive, rapid. Uses gamma rays (for density) and neutrons (for moisture). Most modern highway projects use this.

### 2.4.4 Rubber Balloon Method
A thin balloon is inserted into a hand-excavated hole and inflated to fill the void. The displaced water gives the volume.

## 2.5 The Proctor Needle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proctor Needle** is a *rapid field control instrument* invented by R. R. Proctor (1933). It directly measures the **resistance** of a compacted soil to the penetration of a standard needle, without needing to determine water content. The penetration resistance is correlated to the percent compaction.

The needle has interchangeable tips of standard area. The needle is pushed into the soil, and the force required to drive it to a calibrated depth is read on a spring-loaded scale (in **lbf/in²** or **kg/cm²**).

A **calibration curve** is prepared in the lab: penetration resistance vs dry density (for the same soil at OMC, with known compactive effort). Field readings are then directly converted to $\gamma_d$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Print and Memorize**
> | # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + w}$ | Dry unit weight from bulk | kN/m³ |
> | 2 | $\gamma_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma_w}{1 + e}$ | Dry unit weight from void ratio | kN/m³ |
> | 3 | $e = \dfrac{G \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | Void ratio from dry density | — |
> | 4 | $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | Porosity from void ratio | — |
> | 5 | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{e}$ | Degree of saturation (S $\le$ 1) | — |
> | 6 | $E = \dfrac{W \cdot H \cdot N \cdot L}{V}$ | Compactive energy | kJ/m³ |
> | 7 | $\text{ZAV line: } \gamma_{d,zav} = \dfrac{G \gamma_w}{1 + wG}$ | Zero air voids density | kN/m³ |
> | 8 | $\rho_d = \dfrac{M}{V(1+w)}$ | Mass-basis dry density | g/cc or Mg/m³ |
> | 9 | $R_c = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,lab}} \times 100\%$ | Relative compaction (typically 95–100%) | % |

Note: In all KTU problem statements, $1\,\text{g/cc} = 10\,\text{kN/m}^3$ approximately. The exact conversion is $1\,\text{g/cc} = 9.81\,\text{kN/m}^3$ but for exams, the value **$9.81$** is the standard.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Compaction Concepts

In real engineering projects, compaction directly affects:

- **Settlement** of embankments, building pads, and runway subgrades.
- **Bearing capacity** of foundations.
- **Slope stability** of dams and embankments.
- **Pavement performance** — under-compacted subgrades lead to rutting and fatigue cracking.
- **Permeability** — denser soil $\Rightarrow$ lower permeability.
- **Resistance to erosion and frost heave**.

A field engineer who masters compaction control saves the project from expensive *post-construction* failures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Compactive Energy $E$

We begin from the definition of mechanical work:

$$W_{work} = \text{Force} \times \text{Distance} = (M_{rammer} \cdot g) \times H_{drop}$$

For one blow on one layer:

$$W_{one\,blow} = M \cdot g \cdot H$$

For one full layer in the mould:

$$W_{layer} = N_{blows} \cdot M \cdot g \cdot H$$

For the entire mould (with $L$ layers):

$$W_{total} = N_{blows} \cdot L \cdot M \cdot g \cdot H$$

Energy per unit volume:

$$E = \frac{W_{total}}{V_{mould}} = \frac{N_{blows} \cdot L \cdot M \cdot g \cdot H}{V_{mould}}$$

### 3.1.1 Numerical Verification — IS Light (Standard Proctor)

Given: $M = 2.5\,\text{kg}$, $H = 0.300\,\text{m}$, $N = 25$, $L = 3$, $V = 0.001\,\text{m}^3$ (1000 cc), $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

$$E_{light} = \frac{25 \times 3 \times 2.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.300}{0.001}$$

Step-by-step:

$$E_{light} = \frac{25 \times 3 \times 2.5 \times 0.300}{0.001} \times 9.81$$

$$= \frac{25 \times 2.25}{0.001} \times 9.81$$

$$= 56250 \times 9.81$$

$$= 551812.5\,\text{J/m}^3$$

$$= 551.81\,\text{kJ/m}^3$$

**Note:** IS code specifies the energy as approximately **605.9 kJ/m³** because the standard mould volume in older IS tables used **944 cc** (the Proctor mould). Using the 1000 cc mould, the energy reduces. KTU accepts the approximate figure cited in textbooks (~ 605 kJ/m³ or ~ 595 kJ/m³ depending on the reference).

### 3.1.2 Numerical Verification — IS Heavy (Modified Proctor)

Given: $M = 4.9\,\text{kg}$, $H = 0.450\,\text{m}$, $N = 25$, $L = 5$, $V = 0.001\,\text{m}^3$.

$$E_{heavy} = \frac{25 \times 5 \times 4.9 \times 9.81 \times 0.450}{0.001}$$

Step-by-step:

$$= 25 \times 5 \times 4.9 \times 0.450 \times 9810\,\text{J/m}^3$$

$$= 25 \times 5 \times 2.205 \times 9810$$

$$= 275.625 \times 9810$$

$$= 2703881.25\,\text{J/m}^3 \approx 2703.9\,\text{kJ/m}^3$$

This matches the textbook value of **2704.7 kJ/m³** closely.

> [!TIP]
> **Ratio check:** $E_{heavy} / E_{light} \approx 4.9 \cdot 4.9 / (2.5 \cdot 2.5) \cdot 5/3 \cdot 1.5 = 4.9$. Indeed, modified is ~**4.5 to 4.9 times** the standard — a key KTU expected fact.

## 3.2 The Zero Air Voids (ZAV) Line — Derivation

ZAV corresponds to the **theoretical maximum** dry density for a fully saturated state ($S = 1$, $e_a = 0$).

Starting from the phase relations:

$$V_s + V_w + V_a = V \quad \text{with} \quad V_a = 0 \Rightarrow V_w = V - V_s$$

$$S = \frac{V_w}{V_v} = 1 \Rightarrow V_w = V_v = e \cdot V_s$$

Water content relation:

$$w = \frac{W_w}{W_s} = \frac{V_w \cdot \gamma_w}{V_s \cdot G \cdot \gamma_w} = \frac{V_w}{G \cdot V_s} = \frac{e \cdot V_s}{G \cdot V_s} = \frac{e}{G}$$

Therefore: $e = wG$.

Substitute into $\gamma_d = G \gamma_w / (1 + e)$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{G \gamma_w}{1 + wG}$$

This is the **upper bound** of any compaction curve. A real compaction curve must always lie *below* the ZAV line.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem Set — KTU Pattern (Step-by-Step)

### 3.3.1 Problem A — Direct Calculation of $\gamma_d$

**Statement:** A sample of compacted soil has a wet mass of 1850 g and a volume of 1000 cc. The water content is 12%. Compute (i) bulk density, (ii) dry density, (iii) void ratio if $G = 2.70$, (iv) degree of saturation.

**Solution:**

(i) Bulk density:

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V} = \frac{1850}{1000} = 1.85\,\text{g/cc} = 18.5\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

(ii) Dry density:

$$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1+w} = \frac{1.85}{1 + 0.12} = \frac{1.85}{1.12} = 1.652\,\text{g/cc} \approx 16.52\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

(iii) Void ratio:

$$e = \frac{G \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1 = \frac{2.70 \times 9.81}{16.52} - 1$$

Step-by-step:

$$= \frac{26.487}{16.52} - 1 = 1.6034 - 1 = 0.603$$

(iv) Degree of saturation:

$$S = \frac{wG}{e} = \frac{0.12 \times 2.70}{0.603} = \frac{0.324}{0.603} = 0.537 \approx 53.7\%$$

### 3.3.2 Problem B — Compaction Curve from Proctor Data

**Statement:** In a Standard Proctor test, the following data were obtained (mould volume = 1000 cc):

| Trial | Mass of wet soil (g) | Water content (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1832 | 8.4 |
| 2 | 1936 | 11.2 |
| 3 | 1984 | 13.5 |
| 4 | 1968 | 16.0 |
| 5 | 1920 | 19.2 |

Determine the **MDD** and **OMC**.

**Solution — Step 1:** Compute bulk density $\rho_i$ for each trial:

$$\rho_1 = 1.832\,\text{g/cc}, \quad \rho_2 = 1.936, \quad \rho_3 = 1.984, \quad \rho_4 = 1.968, \quad \rho_5 = 1.920$$

**Step 2:** Compute dry density $\rho_{d,i} = \rho_i / (1+w_i)$:

$$\rho_{d,1} = \frac{1.832}{1.084} = 1.690\,\text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,2} = \frac{1.936}{1.112} = 1.741\,\text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,3} = \frac{1.984}{1.135} = 1.748\,\text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,4} = \frac{1.968}{1.160} = 1.697\,\text{g/cc}$$

$$\rho_{d,5} = \frac{1.920}{1.192} = 1.611\,\text{g/cc}$$

**Step 3:** Plot or tabulate:

| $w$ (%) | 8.4 | 11.2 | 13.5 | 16.0 | 19.2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_d$ (g/cc) | 1.690 | 1.741 | 1.748 | 1.697 | 1.611 |

**Step 4:** Identify peak. The maximum is at **trial 3**:

$$\boxed{\text{MDD} = 1.748\,\text{g/cc} \approx 17.48\,\text{kN/m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{\text{OMC} = 13.5\%}$$

In real practice, you'd draw a smooth curve and read MDD/OMC at the peak.

### 3.3.3 Problem C — Relative Compaction & ZAV Check

**Statement:** From Problem B, $G = 2.70$. (i) Plot the ZAV line for $w$ from 8% to 20%. (ii) A field compaction test gave $\gamma_d = 16.6\,\text{kN/m}^3$ at $w = 14\%$. Determine the relative compaction and check if the field is acceptable at 95% specification.

**Solution:**

(i) ZAV formula: $\gamma_{d,zav} = G \gamma_w / (1 + wG) = 2.70 \times 9.81 / (1 + 2.70w)$.

For $w = 8\% = 0.08$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1 + 0.216} = \frac{26.487}{1.216} = 21.78\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

For $w = 10\%$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1.27} = 20.86\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

For $w = 12\%$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1.324} = 20.00\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

For $w = 14\%$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1.378} = 19.22\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

For $w = 16\%$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1.432} = 18.50\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

For $w = 20\%$:

$$\gamma_{d,zav} = \frac{26.487}{1.540} = 17.20\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

(ii) Relative compaction:

$$R_c = \frac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,lab}} \times 100\% = \frac{16.6}{17.48} \times 100\% = 94.96\%$$

This is **just below** the 95% specification — the field compaction is **marginally unacceptable**. The contractor must add one more roller pass or adjust moisture.

### 3.3.4 Problem D — Field Compaction by Sand Cone

**Statement:** In a sand cone test, the following data were obtained:
- Mass of soil excavated from hole = 1240 g
- Mass of sand in cone (calibration) = 540 g
- Mass of sand in hole + cone = 2540 g
- Water content of soil = 11.4%
- Calibration: density of sand = 1.45 g/cc

Find field $\gamma_d$ and relative compaction if lab MDD = 1.78 g/cc.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Mass of sand that filled the hole:

$$M_{sand,hole} = 2540 - 540 = 2000\,\text{g}$$

Step 2: Volume of the hole:

$$V_{hole} = \frac{M_{sand,hole}}{\rho_{sand}} = \frac{2000}{1.45} = 1379.31\,\text{cc}$$

Step 3: Bulk density of field soil:

$$\rho_{field} = \frac{M_{soil}}{V_{hole}} = \frac{1240}{1379.31} = 0.899\,\text{g/cc}$$

Wait — this seems low. Let me recheck; a typical field compacted soil bulk density is 1.8–2.0 g/cc. The sand density of 1.45 g/cc is also low. Let me reconsider with a more realistic problem:

**Re-stated with realistic data:**
- Mass of soil excavated = 2540 g
- Mass of sand filling the hole = 1850 g (after cone correction)
- Sand density = 1.55 g/cc
- Water content = 11.4%

Step 1: $V_{hole} = 1850 / 1.55 = 1193.5\,\text{cc}$

Step 2: $\rho_{field} = 2540 / 1193.5 = 2.128\,\text{g/cc}$

Step 3: $\rho_{d,field} = 2.128 / 1.114 = 1.910\,\text{g/cc}$

Step 4: Relative compaction:

$$R_c = \frac{1.910}{1.78} \times 100\% = 107.3\%$$

This is **> 100%**, which is theoretically impossible (cannot exceed lab MDD). It indicates either (a) lab MDD was determined with Standard Proctor while the field used Modified Proctor-level effort, or (b) experimental error.

This is a **classic KTU pitfall** — students must check the reasonableness of their answer.

### 3.3.5 Problem E — Proctor Needle Calibration Use

**Statement:** A laboratory calibration of the Proctor needle on a soil at OMC = 14% gave the following:

| Compactive Effort (blows/layer) | $\gamma_d$ (g/cc) | Needle Resistance (kg/cm²) |
|---|---|---|
| 15 | 1.62 | 8 |
| 25 | 1.74 | 17 |
| 35 | 1.82 | 28 |

In the field, a Proctor needle reading of **22 kg/cm²** is obtained. Estimate the field $\gamma_d$ and relative compaction if lab MDD = 1.82 g/cc.

**Solution — Linear Interpolation:**

$\gamma_d$ between 1.74 and 1.82 g/cc; needle resistance between 17 and 28 kg/cm².

For needle = 22:

$$\gamma_d = 1.74 + \frac{22 - 17}{28 - 17} \times (1.82 - 1.74)$$

$$= 1.74 + \frac{5}{11} \times 0.08 = 1.74 + 0.0364 = 1.776\,\text{g/cc}$$

Relative compaction:

$$R_c = \frac{1.776}{1.82} \times 100\% = 97.6\%$$

This is **acceptable** for most specifications ($\geq$ 95%).

> [!TIP]
> In reality, the calibration is a smooth curve (often linear in the middle range), and field readings are read off the curve, not interpolated linearly in all cases.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Compaction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOIL SAMPLE COLLECTED] --> B[IS Light OR Heavy Proctor Test]
    B --> C[5-Point Compaction Curve]
    C --> D{Identify MDD and OMC}
    D --> E[Field Compaction Begins]
    E --> F[Roller Selected by Soil Type]
    F --> G[Lift Compaction 150-300 mm]
    G --> H[Field Density Test]
    H --> I[Sand Cone Method]
    H --> J[Core Cutter Method]
    H --> K[Nuclear Gauge Method]
    H --> L[Proctor Needle Check]
    I --> M[Compare Field gamma_d vs Lab MDD]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Rc Greater Equal 95 percent}
    N -->|Yes| O[Accept Compaction Layer]
    N -->|No| P[Add Roller Pass or Rework]
    P --> G
    O --> Q[Next Lift]
    Q --> R[Final Paved Surface]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block — Compaction Curve & ZAV Line Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Soil
    A1[Dry Side w less than OMC] --> A2[Peak Point MDD at OMC] --> A3[Wet Side w greater than OMC]
    end
    subgraph Limits
    B1[ZAV Line Upper Bound S equals 100 percent] --> B2[Saturation Line at 80 percent] --> B3[50 percent Saturation]
    end
    Soil --> Limits
    A2 --> X[Acceptance Zone 95 to 100 percent of MDD]
```

## 4.3 Field Compaction Equipment Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Soil Type Known] --> Q1{Plasticity Index less than 6}
    Q1 -->|Yes Cohesionless| Q2{Gradation}
    Q2 -->|Well Graded SW| R1[Smooth Wheel Roller]
    Q2 -->|Poorly Graded SP| R2[Vibratory Roller]
    Q1 -->|No 6 to 15| R3[Pneumatic Tyred Roller]
    Q1 -->|Yes Greater than 15 Cohesive| R4[Sheepsfoot Roller]
    R1 --> Done[Compaction Layer]
    R2 --> Done
    R3 --> Done
    R4 --> Done
```

## 4.4 Proctor Needle Calibration & Use Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lab Calibration on Same Soil] --> B[Plot Resistance vs Dry Density]
    B --> C[Calibration Curve]
    D[Field Compacted Lift] --> E[Insert Proctor Needle]
    E --> F[Read Resistance]
    F --> G[Match to Calibration Curve]
    G --> H[Get Field Dry Density]
    H --> I[Compute Relative Compaction Rc]
    I --> J{Rc Greater Equal 95 percent}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept]
    J -->|No| L[Rework]
```

## 4.5 IS Light vs IS Heavy Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISL[IS Light Standard Proctor]
    AL[2.5 kg Rammer]
    BL[300 mm Drop]
    CL[3 Layers]
    DL[25 Blows]
    EL[Energy 605 kJ per cubic m]
    end
    subgraph ISH[IS Heavy Modified Proctor]
    AH[4.9 kg Rammer]
    BH[450 mm Drop]
    CH[5 Layers]
    DH[25 Blows]
    EH[Energy 2705 kJ per cubic m]
    end
    EL --> Result[Heavy Gives Higher MDD Lower OMC]
    EH --> Result
```
