<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — CONSOLIDATION & SHEAR STRENGTH OF SOILS

## 1.1 Over Consolidated and Under Consolidated Soils

### 1.1.1 Core Technical Definition

In soil mechanics, the **Over Consolidation Ratio (OCR)** is defined as the ratio of the **preconsolidation pressure (σ'ₚ)** — the maximum effective vertical stress that the soil has experienced in its geological history — to the **present effective overburden pressure (σ'₀)**.

$$\text{OCR} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma'_p}{\sigma'_0}$$

Based on the magnitude of OCR, fine-grained soils are classified into three fundamental categories:

| Soil Class | OCR Value | Geological Interpretation |
|------------|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Normally Consolidated (NC)** | OCR = 1.0 | Soil has never been subjected to a stress greater than the present overburden. |
| **Over Consolidated (OC)** | OCR > 1.0 | Soil has been subjected to a higher stress in the past (e.g., glacier load, removed overburden). |
| **Under Consolidated (UC)** | OCR < 1.0 | Soil is still undergoing consolidation under its own weight; excess pore pressure remains. |

> [!NOTE]
> **OCR is a memory index of a soil.** It tells the geotechnical engineer what the soil "remembers" about its stress history. A high OCR indicates an aged, dense, stiff deposit with low compressibility, whereas a low OCR indicates a young, soft, compressible deposit.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Story

Imagine a wet kitchen sponge sitting on a table:

- A **Normally Consolidated** sponge has only the weight of the water inside pressing down on its base. It has only ever experienced *this much* pressure.
- An **Over Consolidated** sponge is one that previously had a heavy book pressing on it (the book has been removed). The sponge is still squeezed, deformed, and much stiffer than a fresh one — it "remembers" the higher pressure.
- An **Under Consolidated** sponge is one that was just placed on the table and is still actively losing water under self-weight. It has not yet finished settling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** OCR governs both the *magnitude of settlement* and the *rate at which it occurs*. OC soils settle less and faster, while UC soils settle more and over long durations.

### 1.1.3 Physical Significance of Preconsolidation Pressure

The **preconsolidation pressure (σ'ₚ)** is determined experimentally from a **Casagrande's graphical construction** applied to the laboratory e-log(σ') curve obtained from the **Oedometer Test**. It separates:

- The **recompression region** (σ' < σ'ₚ) — soil behaves as stiff, nearly elastic.
- The **Virgin compression region** (σ' > σ'ₚ) — soil structure yields and large plastic strains develop.

---

## 1.2 Terzaghi's Theory of One-Dimensional Consolidation

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

**Terzaghi's Theory of One-Dimensional Consolidation** is the foundational analytical model (1923) that mathematically describes the dissipation of excess hydrostatic pore-water pressure and the associated time-dependent volume change in a saturated clay layer subjected to an externally applied load, under the assumption that compression occurs only in the vertical direction.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Overview — The Squeeze-Out Analogy

When a load is suddenly applied over a saturated clay layer, the water within the voids momentarily carries the entire load as **excess pore water pressure (Δu)**. With time, this water is squeezed out through the permeable boundaries, transferring the load gradually to the soil skeleton. This **time-dependent transfer of stress from water to soil grains** is *consolidation*. Terzaghi's theory is the mathematical translation of this "squeeze-out" process.

### 1.2.3 Assumptions of Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation Theory (KTU Mandate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 directive, **no derivation is required**, but the *assumptions must be memorized verbatim* as they form a 3-mark favorite in Part A.

1. The soil is **homogeneous**, **saturated**, and **isotropic**.
2. The soil grains and pore water are **incompressible**.
3. **Darcy's Law** governs the flow of water through the soil.
4. The coefficient of permeability (k) and the coefficient of volume compressibility (mᵥ) remain **constant** during consolidation.
5. The applied load produces **one-dimensional (vertical) compression only**; lateral strain is zero.
6. The **time lag** of consolidation is entirely due to the low permeability of the soil.
7. Excess pore pressure is **zero at the drainage boundary** (pervious boundary) at all times.
8. The relationship between void ratio and effective stress is **unique and linear** within the increment considered.

---

## 1.3 Average Degree of Consolidation (U)

### 1.3.1 Definition

The **Average Degree of Consolidation (U)** at any instant *t* is defined as the ratio of the **amount of settlement that has already occurred** to the **total ultimate (final) settlement** that will eventually occur. It is, equivalently, the ratio of the volume of water expelled at time *t* to the total volume of water that must be expelled for full consolidation.

$$U \;=\; \dfrac{S_t}{S_{\infty}} \;=\; \dfrac{\int_0^{H} u(z,t)\,dz}{\int_0^{H} u_0\,dz} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{\int_0^{H} u(z,t)\,dz}{u_0 \cdot H}$$

where *u(z, t)* is the excess pore pressure at depth *z* and time *t*, and *u₀* is the initial excess pore pressure (assumed uniform).

### 1.3.2 Conceptual Analogy

Picture a full bucket of water with a small hole at the bottom. The fraction of water that has drained out, relative to the total water in the bucket, is the "degree of consolidation" — an indicator of how much work the squeeze-out process has done.

---

## 1.4 Time Factor (Tᵥ)

### 1.4.1 Definition

The **Time Factor (Tᵥ)** is a dimensionless number that scales real time (*t*) into the consolidation equation. It is defined as:

$$T_v \;=\; \dfrac{c_v \cdot t}{d^2}$$

where:
- **cᵥ** = Coefficient of consolidation (length²/time, typically cm²/s or m²/year)
- **t** = elapsed time
- **d** = drainage path length

For **double drainage (top + bottom permeable)**: $d = H/2$  
For **single drainage (one side permeable)**: $d = H$

---

## 1.5 Coefficient of Consolidation (cᵥ)

### 1.5.1 Formal Definition

The **Coefficient of Consolidation (cᵥ)** is a soil property that governs the **rate at which consolidation proceeds**. It combines permeability, compressibility, and the unit weight of water into a single diffusion-like parameter.

$$c_v \;=\; \dfrac{k}{m_v \cdot \gamma_w}$$

where:
- *k* = coefficient of permeability (cm/s)
- *mᵥ* = coefficient of volume compressibility (cm²/kg)
- *γw* = unit weight of water ≈ **9.81 kN/m³** (or 1000 kgf/m³)

Typical values of cᵥ range from **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² cm²/s** for clays.

### 1.5.2 Intuitive View

Think of cᵥ as the "speed knob" of consolidation. A high cᵥ (sandy clay) settles fast; a low cᵥ (highly plastic clay) settles over decades.

---

## 1.6 Laboratory Consolidation Test (Oedometer Test)

The **Oedometer Test** (also called the 1-D consolidation test) is the standard laboratory test to determine consolidation parameters. A disc-shaped soil specimen is confined in a rigid metal ring, sandwiched between two porous stones, and subjected to incrementally increasing vertical loads. Each load increment is held constant, and the resulting vertical deformation is recorded as a function of time. The output is:
- An **e vs. log σ'** curve (for σ'ₚ and compression index C꜀)
- A **settlement vs. √time (or log time)** curve (for cᵥ via Taylor's or Casagrande's method)

### 1.6.1 Determination of cᵥ

#### (a) Casagrande's Log-Time Method
- Plot settlement *S* vs. log(*t*).
- The **primary consolidation** branch is an S-shape; the **secondary consolidation** branch is nearly linear.
- Tangent intersection gives the time at **100% primary consolidation (t₁₀₀)**.
- **cᵥ = 0.198 · d² / t₅₀**, where *t₅₀* is the time for 50% primary consolidation, read at the point where the S-shape crosses *0.5 × S₁₀₀*.

#### (b) Taylor's √Time Method
- Plot settlement *S* vs. √(*t*).
- Draw the **straight-line portion** of the early curve and extend it back to *S = 0*; the intersection is the **corrected origin (t₀)**.
- Draw a line through this corrected origin with slope **1.15 times** the initial straight portion.
- The intersection of this second line with the experimental curve gives **t₉₀** (time for 90% consolidation).
- **cᵥ = 0.848 · d² / t₉₀**

---

## 1.7 Difference Between Consolidation and Compaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a perennial KTU 3-mark question. Master the contrast.

| Parameter | Consolidation | Compaction |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| **Driving mechanism** | Static (sustained) load from a structure | Dynamic (short-duration) mechanical effort |
| **Soil type** | Saturated fine-grained soils (clays) | Partially saturated soils (fills, embankments) |
| **Water role** | Pore water is **expelled**; volume change is permanent | Air is **expelled**; soil is densified to a target dry density |
| **Time scale** | Years to decades | Minutes to hours |
| **Stress path** | Increasing effective stress with time at constant total stress | Rapid mechanical kneading at near-constant water content |
| **Analytical model** | Terzaghi's 1-D consolidation theory | Proctor's compaction theory (Modified/Standard Proctor) |
| **Test apparatus** | Oedometer | Proctor mould + rammer |
| **Parameter measured** | C꜀, σ'ₚ, cᵥ | Maximum dry density (MDD), Optimum moisture content (OMC) |

---

## 1.8 Shear Strength of Soils — Practical Applications

### 1.8.1 Definition

**Shear strength of soil (τf)** is the maximum shear stress that a soil can sustain without undergoing failure. It is the most critical engineering property in geotechnical design, governing the stability of:

- Foundations (bearing capacity)
- Slopes and embankments
- Retaining walls (earth pressure)
- Underground excavations
- Pavement subgrades

### 1.8.2 The Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion (KTU High-Yield)

The shear strength of a soil at a plane is governed by the **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion**:

$$\tau_f \;=\; c' \;+\; \sigma' \cdot \tan\phi'$$

where:
- **τf** = Shear strength at failure (kN/m² or kPa)
- **c'** = Effective cohesion intercept (kPa)
- **σ'** = Effective normal stress on the failure plane (kPa)
- **φ'** = Effective angle of internal friction (degrees)
- **c'** and **φ'** are the **effective shear strength parameters**.

In terms of total stress, the criterion is written as:
$$\tau_f \;=\; c \;+\; \sigma \cdot \tan\phi$$
where *c* and *φ* are the **total stress parameters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandate:** Effective stress parameters (c', φ') are fundamental; total stress parameters (c, φ) are convenient short-hand for undrained analysis (e.g., φᵤ = 0 in saturated clays).

### 1.8.3 Physical Meaning

- **Cohesion (c')** — the "stickiness" or electrochemical bond between fine particles. Pure sand has c' = 0; saturated clay may have c' > 0.
- **Friction (tan φ')** — the mechanical resistance from grain-to-grain sliding. Dense sands have φ' = 35°–45°; loose sands φ' = 28°–35°.

### 1.8.4 Mohr Circle — Principal Stresses and Failure Envelope

A **Mohr Circle** is a 2-D graphical representation of the stress state on every plane passing through a point. With **major and minor principal stresses σ₁ and σ₃** (with σ₁ > σ₃), the circle has:
- **Center** at: $p = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$
- **Radius** at: $r = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$

A plane inclined at angle θ to the direction of σ₁ (measured from the major principal plane) has:
- **Normal stress:** $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \cdot \cos(2\theta)$
- **Shear stress:** $\tau = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \cdot \sin(2\theta)$

#### Condition for Failure
Failure occurs when the Mohr circle just **touches** the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope τf = c' + σ' tan φ'. The tangent point gives the **failure plane orientation**:

$$\theta_f \;=\; 45^\circ \;+\; \dfrac{\phi'}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the most-tested KTU facts: **the failure plane is inclined at (45 + φ'/2) to the major principal plane** and is NOT the plane of maximum shear stress (which is at 45°).

### 1.8.5 Relationship Between Shear Parameters and Principal Stresses

At failure, the relationship between principal stresses is derived by substituting the Mohr-Coulomb equation into the geometry of the tangent circle:

$$\sigma'_1 \;=\; \sigma'_3 \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \;+\; 2c'\tan\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right)$$

Using the common notation:
$$K_p \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \quad ; \quad K_a \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right)$$

The equation becomes:
$$\sigma'_1 \;=\; K_p \cdot \sigma'_3 \;+\; 2c'\sqrt{K_p}$$

For a **cohesionless soil (c' = 0)**:
$$\dfrac{\sigma'_1}{\sigma'_3} \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \;=\; K_p$$

This is the **Rankine passive coefficient** of earth pressure.

---

## 1.9 Triaxial Compression Test (Brief Discussion)

The **Triaxial Test** is the most versatile laboratory test for determining shear strength parameters. A cylindrical soil specimen is enclosed in a thin rubber membrane, placed inside a pressurized cell, and subjected to:
- **Confining pressure (σ₃)** — all-around cell pressure.
- **Axial load (σ₁ - σ₃)** — increasing deviator stress applied through a loading ram.

Three principal variants exist based on drainage conditions:

| Test Type | Drainage During Confining Stage | Drainage During Shear | Total Stress Parameter | Application |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Unconsolidated Undrained (UU)** | No | No | cᵤ, φᵤ | Quick, undrained strength (e.g., construction rate analysis) |
| **Consolidated Undrained (CU)** | Yes | No | c, φ | Construction-stage analysis, pore pressure measured |
| **Consolidated Drained (CD)** | Yes | Yes | c', φ' | Long-term, effective-stress analysis |

### 1.9.1 Unconsolidated Undrained (UU) Test — Detailed

- The specimen is **not allowed to consolidate** under the confining pressure.
- Shear is applied **rapidly** so that no drainage is permitted.
- Excess pore pressure builds up, but its magnitude is unknown (not measured).
- The test is run on **at least three identical specimens** at **different confining pressures** (e.g., 50, 100, 150 kPa).
- For saturated clays, the **Mohr circles at failure have the same diameter**, yielding **φᵤ = 0** and **cᵤ = qᵤ/2**, where qᵤ = (σ₁ - σ₃) at failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **φᵤ = 0 Concept:** For a saturated clay, the UU test indicates the soil has no "frictional" component in total stress terms because pore water carries the confining pressure increase without volume change. This is the famous *φᵤ = 0* concept (Skempton & Bishop, 1950).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Consolidation Theory — Layered Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Initial Condition (t = 0):**  
A load Δσ is suddenly applied. The soil skeleton cannot deform instantly. The entire increment is carried by **excess pore water pressure u₀ = Δσ**. Effective stress remains σ'₀.

**Step 2 — Pore Pressure Dissipation (0 < t < ∞):**  
Water is squeezed out of the void space. The excess pore pressure decreases. Effective stress increases. This transfer is governed by **Terzaghi's consolidation equation**:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} \;=\; c_v \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$$

> This is a parabolic partial differential equation, mathematically identical to the heat-conduction equation. Hence consolidation is often called the *"diffusion of pore pressure"*.

**Step 3 — Final Condition (t = ∞):**  
All excess pore pressure has dissipated. The entire applied load is now carried by the **soil skeleton** as effective stress: σ' = σ'₀ + Δσ.

**Step 4 — Settlement:**  
The volume of water expelled equals the volume change of the soil. The **final primary consolidation settlement** is:

$$S_{\infty} \;=\; m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot H \;=\; \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_0}\right)$$

where:
- *C꜀* = Compression index (from the virgin portion of the e-log σ' curve)
- *e₀* = Initial void ratio
- *H* = Thickness of the compressible layer
- *mᵥ* = Coefficient of volume compressibility = Δe / [Δσ(1 + e₀)]

**Step 5 — Average Settlement at Time t:**

$$S_t \;=\; U \cdot S_{\infty}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{OCR} = \sigma'_p / \sigma'_0$ | Over Consolidation Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_v = c_v t / d^2$ | Time Factor | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $c_v = k / (m_v \gamma_w)$ | Coefficient of Consolidation | cm²/s |
| 4 | $m_v = \Delta e / [\Delta\sigma(1+e_0)]$ | Coefficient of Volume Compressibility | m²/kN or cm²/kg |
| 5 | $S_\infty = m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot H$ | Ultimate consolidation settlement | mm or m |
| 6 | $S_t = U \cdot S_\infty$ | Settlement at time *t* | mm or m |
| 7 | $d = H/2$ (double drainage) or $H$ (single) | Drainage path | m |
| 8 | $c_v = 0.848 d^2 / t_{90}$ | Taylor's √t method | cm²/s |
| 9 | $c_v = 0.198 d^2 / t_{50}$ | Casagrande's log t method | cm²/s |
| 10 | $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'$ | Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion | kPa |
| 11 | $\sigma_n = p + r\cos(2\theta)$ | Normal stress on inclined plane | kPa |
| 12 | $\tau = r \sin(2\theta)$ | Shear stress on inclined plane | kPa |
| 13 | $\theta_f = 45^\circ + \phi'/2$ | Inclination of failure plane | degrees |
| 14 | $\sigma'_1 = \sigma'_3 K_p + 2c'\sqrt{K_p}$ | Principal stress relationship | kPa |
| 15 | $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi'/2)$ | Passive earth pressure coefficient | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.3 Mohr-Coulomb — Why It Works

The Mohr-Coulomb criterion is an **envelope-linear idealization** of the actual curved failure envelope of soil. It is remarkably accurate for the stress ranges encountered in routine civil engineering and is the backbone of:

- **Slope stability analyses** (e.g., Swedish circle method, Bishop's method)
- **Bearing capacity** (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Hansen)
- **Lateral earth pressure** (Rankine, Coulomb)
- **Retaining wall design**

The envelope is the **locus of all (σ', τf) pairs** obtained from multiple tests at different confining pressures. Each Mohr circle at failure should be tangent to this envelope.

---

## 2.4 Failure Plane Orientation — Geometric Proof

The failure plane makes an angle $\theta_f$ with the **minor principal plane** (plane on which σ₃ acts), so that the angle with the **major principal plane** is $90^\circ - \theta_f$. The most-tested KTU result:

$$\theta_f \;=\; 45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** As φ' increases, the failure plane rotates from vertical (φ' = 0) toward an angle approaching 90° (φ' = 90°). Always measure failure plane angle from the **major principal plane direction**, NOT from the horizontal in field coordinates.

---

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

| Engineering Problem | Parameter Used | KTU Application |
|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Building foundation design | cᵤ, φᵤ from UU test | Short-term stability |
| Long-term settlement | cᵥ, C꜀ from Oedometer | Serviceability |
| Earth-retaining structures | c', φ' from CU/CD | Earth pressure |
| Slope stability | c', φ' from CD | Long-term analysis |
| Embankment construction rate | cᵤ from UU | Stability during construction |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mohr-Coulomb Principal Stress Relation (c' - φ' Soil)

**Given:** A soil element at failure with major and minor effective principal stresses σ'₁ and σ'₃, shear parameters c' and φ'.

**To Prove:** $\sigma'_1 = \sigma'_3 \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi'/2) + 2c' \tan(45^\circ + \phi'/2)$

**Geometric Setup:**  
The Mohr circle representing failure has center at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_1 + \sigma'_3}{2},\,0\right)$ and radius $r = \dfrac{\sigma'_1 - \sigma'_3}{2}$. The Mohr-Coulomb envelope in the σ' - τ plane is a straight line $\tau = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'$.

**Step 1 — Tangency Condition:**  
The perpendicular distance from the circle's center to the failure envelope equals the circle's radius *r*. The equation of the envelope in line form is:

$$\tau \;-\; \sigma' \tan\phi' \;-\; c' \;=\; 0$$

The perpendicular distance from point $(x_0, 0)$ with $x_0 = \dfrac{\sigma'_1+\sigma'_3}{2}$ to the line $a\tau + b\sigma' + c = 0$ where $a = 1$, $b = -\tan\phi'$, $c = -c'$ is:

$$\text{dist} = \dfrac{\vert a(0) + b x_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \dfrac{\vert -x_0 \tan\phi' - c' \vert}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\phi'}}$$

Using $\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\phi'} = \sec\phi'$:

$$\text{dist} = \cos\phi' \cdot \vert -x_0 \tan\phi' - c' \vert = \cos\phi' \cdot (x_0 \tan\phi' + c')$$

(We drop the absolute value because both terms are positive at the tangent point on the right side of the circle.)

**Step 2 — Equate Distance to Radius:**

$$r \;=\; \cos\phi' \cdot \left( x_0 \tan\phi' + c' \right)$$

Substituting *r* and *x₀*:

$$\dfrac{\sigma'_1 - \sigma'_3}{2} \;=\; \cos\phi' \left[ \dfrac{\sigma'_1 + \sigma'_3}{2} \cdot \tan\phi' + c' \right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand and Rearrange:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma'_1 - \sigma'_3}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma'_1 + \sigma'_3}{2} \cdot \sin\phi' + c' \cos\phi'$$

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$\sigma'_1 - \sigma'_3 \;=\; (\sigma'_1 + \sigma'_3)\sin\phi' + 2c'\cos\phi'$$

**Step 4 — Collect σ'₁ and σ'₃:**

$$\sigma'_1 - \sigma'_1 \sin\phi' \;=\; \sigma'_3 + \sigma'_3 \sin\phi' + 2c'\cos\phi'$$

$$\sigma'_1 (1 - \sin\phi') \;=\; \sigma'_3 (1 + \sin\phi') + 2c'\cos\phi'$$

**Step 5 — Factor Using Half-Angle Identities:**

Using the trigonometric identities:
- $1 - \sin\phi' = 2\sin^2(45^\circ - \phi'/2)$
- $1 + \sin\phi' = 2\cos^2(45^\circ - \phi'/2)$
- $\cos\phi' = 2\sin(45^\circ + \phi'/2)\cos(45^\circ + \phi'/2)$ ... not directly used.

Divide both sides by $(1 - \sin\phi')$:

$$\sigma'_1 \;=\; \sigma'_3 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi'}{1 - \sin\phi'} \;+\; 2c' \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi'}{1 - \sin\phi'}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Identity:**

$$\dfrac{1 + \sin\phi'}{1 - \sin\phi'} \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \;=\; K_p$$

And:

$$2 \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi'}{1 - \sin\phi'} \;=\; 2\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \;=\; 2\sqrt{K_p}$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma'_1 \;=\; \sigma'_3 \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right) \;+\; 2c' \cdot \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}\right)\;}$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Failure Plane Inclination

The angle from the center of the Mohr circle to the tangent point, measured from the σ'-axis, is $2\theta_f$. By geometry of tangency:

$$2\theta_f \;=\; 90^\circ + \phi'$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_f \;=\; 45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2}$$

This is the angle measured from the **major principal plane** (the plane on which σ₁ acts, perpendicular to the σ₁ direction).

> [!NOTE]
> The angle of the failure plane in the field (measured from the horizontal) depends on the orientation of σ₁. For a typical triaxial test where σ₁ is vertical, the failure plane makes an angle of $(45 + \phi'/2)$ with the horizontal.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Time Factor (KTU 3-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A saturated clay layer is **8 m thick** and is sandwiched between two sand layers (double drainage). A surcharge load produces double drainage. The coefficient of consolidation cᵥ = **4 × 10⁻⁴ cm²/s**. Find the time required for **90% consolidation**.

**Given:**
- H = 8 m, double drainage → d = H/2 = **4 m = 400 cm**
- cᵥ = 4 × 10⁻⁴ cm²/s
- U = 90% → From standard tables, **Tᵥ = 0.848** at U = 90%

**Step 1 — Apply Time Factor Formula:**

$$T_v \;=\; \dfrac{c_v \cdot t}{d^2}$$

$$0.848 \;=\; \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot t}{(400)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for t:**

$$t \;=\; \dfrac{0.848 \times 160000}{4 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \dfrac{135680}{4 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 3.392 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Days:**

$$t \;=\; \dfrac{3.392 \times 10^8}{86400} \;\approx\; 3926 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 10.75 \text{ years}$$

**Answer: t ≈ 10.75 years**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Mohr Circle Stresses on Inclined Plane (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** The stresses at a point in a soil mass are: σ₁ = 400 kPa (major) and σ₃ = 100 kPa (minor). Determine the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at **30° to the major principal plane**. Also find the orientation of the failure plane if φ' = 20° and c' = 30 kPa. Check if failure occurs.

**Given:**
- σ₁ = 400 kPa
- σ₃ = 100 kPa
- θ = 30° (with major principal plane)
- φ' = 20°, c' = 30 kPa

**Step 1 — Compute Center and Radius:**

$$p \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{400 + 100}{2} \;=\; 250 \text{ kPa}$$

$$r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{400 - 100}{2} \;=\; 150 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2θ:**

$$2\theta \;=\; 2 \times 30^\circ \;=\; 60^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Normal and Shear Stresses on the Plane:**

$$\sigma_n \;=\; p + r \cos(2\theta) \;=\; 250 + 150 \cos(60^\circ) \;=\; 250 + 150(0.5) \;=\; 325 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau \;=\; r \sin(2\theta) \;=\; 150 \sin(60^\circ) \;=\; 150(0.866) \;=\; 129.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Shear Strength on this Plane:**

$$\tau_f \;=\; c' + \sigma_n \tan\phi' \;=\; 30 + 325 \tan(20^\circ) \;=\; 30 + 325(0.364) \;=\; 30 + 118.3 \;=\; 148.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Check Failure Condition:**

Since τ (= 129.9 kPa) < τf (= 148.3 kPa), the plane is **SAFE** — no failure occurs.

**Step 6 — Compute Orientation of Critical Failure Plane:**

$$\theta_f \;=\; 45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi'}{2} \;=\; 45^\circ + 10^\circ \;=\; 55^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Stresses on the Failure Plane (2θ_f = 110°):**

$$\sigma_{n,f} \;=\; 250 + 150\cos(110^\circ) \;=\; 250 + 150(-0.342) \;=\; 198.7 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_{f,\text{induced}} \;=\; 150 \sin(110^\circ) \;=\; 150(0.940) \;=\; 141.0 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau_{f,\text{strength}} \;=\; 30 + 198.7 \tan(20^\circ) \;=\; 30 + 72.3 \;=\; 102.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 8 — Check Failure on Critical Plane:**

Since τf,induced (141.0 kPa) > τf,strength (102.3 kPa), failure **DOES occur** on the critical plane oriented at 55° to the major principal plane.

**Conclusion:** The soil is stable on the 30° plane but fails on the 55° (critical) plane.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Principal Stress from Shear Parameters

**Problem:** A soil has c' = 20 kPa and φ' = 25°. A triaxial test on this soil gives σ'₃ = 150 kPa. Find the deviator stress (σ'₁ - σ'₃) at failure.

**Step 1 — Compute K_p:**

$$K_p \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{25^\circ}{2}\right) \;=\; \tan^2(57.5^\circ) \;=\; (1.5697)^2 \;\approx\; 2.464$$

**Step 2 — Compute Major Principal Stress:**

$$\sigma'_1 \;=\; K_p \cdot \sigma'_3 + 2c'\sqrt{K_p} \;=\; 2.464 \times 150 + 2(20)(1.5697) \;=\; 369.6 + 62.79$$

$$\sigma'_1 \;\approx\; 432.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Deviator Stress:**

$$\sigma_d \;=\; \sigma'_1 - \sigma'_3 \;=\; 432.4 - 150 \;=\; 282.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer: σd ≈ 282.4 kPa**

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem 4 — Settlement Computation

**Problem:** A 6 m thick saturated clay layer (single drainage at the bottom only) has cᵥ = 1.5 × 10⁻³ cm²/s. A structure imposes a uniform surcharge of 80 kPa. The initial void ratio e₀ = 0.9, C꜀ = 0.30, and initial effective stress σ'₀ = 60 kPa. Compute the settlement after 2 years.

**Step 1 — Final Settlement:**

$$S_\infty \;=\; \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma'_0}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{0.30 \times 6}{1.9} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{60 + 80}{60}\right)$$

$$S_\infty \;=\; 0.9474 \times \log_{10}(2.333) \;=\; 0.9474 \times 0.3680 \;\approx\; 0.349 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 349 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Time Factor:**

- t = 2 years = 2 × 365 × 86400 s = 6.31 × 10⁷ s
- d = H = 6 m = 600 cm (single drainage)
- cᵥ = 1.5 × 10⁻³ cm²/s

$$T_v \;=\; \dfrac{c_v \cdot t}{d^2} \;=\; \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.31 \times 10^7}{(600)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{94650}{360000} \;\approx\; 0.263$$

**Step 3 — Average Degree of Consolidation:**

For Tᵥ < 0.282 (small), use the approximation:

$$T_v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} U^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; U \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 T_v}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.263}{3.1416}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.3349} \;\approx\; 0.579$$

So **U ≈ 58%**.

**Step 4 — Settlement at t = 2 years:**

$$S_t \;=\; U \cdot S_\infty \;=\; 0.579 \times 349 \;\approx\; 202 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer: S₂ₐ ≈ 202 mm**
