# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Over Consolidation Ratio (OCR). Classify soils based on OCR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Over Consolidation Ratio is defined as the ratio of the **preconsolidation pressure (σ'ₚ)** to the **present effective overburden pressure (σ'₀)**:

$$\text{OCR} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma'_p}{\sigma'_0}$$

Based on this ratio, soils are classified as:
- **Normally Consolidated (NC):** OCR = 1 → Soil has never been loaded beyond its present overburden.
- **Over Consolidated (OC):** OCR > 1 → Soil was preloaded in the past (e.g., by glaciers or removed strata).
- **Under Consolidated (UC):** OCR < 1 → Soil is still consolidating under its own weight.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between consolidation and compaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Consolidation | Compaction |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **Soil state** | Saturated clay | Partially saturated soil |
| **Mechanism** | Expulsion of **pore water** under static load | Expulsion of **air** under dynamic/impact load |
| **Time** | Slow (years/decades) | Rapid (hours) |
| **Mechanism trigger** | Static structural load | Mechanical effort (roller/rammer) |
| **Parameter measured** | C꜀, cᵥ, σ'ₚ | OMC, MDD |
| **Test** | Oedometer | Proctor test |

### Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**List any six assumptions of Terzaghi's one-dimensional consolidation theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The soil is homogeneous, saturated, and isotropic.
2. Soil grains and pore water are incompressible.
3. Darcy's Law is valid.
4. Permeability (k) and compressibility (mᵥ) are constant.
5. Compression is one-dimensional (lateral strain = 0).
6. Excess pore pressure at the drainage boundary is zero.

### Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. What is the inclination of the failure plane to the major principal plane?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion is:
$$\tau_f \;=\; c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'$$
The failure plane is inclined at $\theta_f = 45^\circ + \phi'/2$ to the major principal plane.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) A saturated clay layer 6 m thick lies between two layers of sand. The coefficient of consolidation of the clay is 5 × 10⁻⁴ cm²/s. A uniformly distributed load is applied at the ground surface. Determine the time required for 50% and 90% of the consolidation to take place. Use double drainage. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Thickness of clay, H = 6 m
- Coefficient of consolidation, cᵥ = 5 × 10⁻⁴ cm²/s = 5 × 10⁻⁸ m²/s
- Drainage: Double, so d = H/2 = 3 m = 300 cm
- For U = 50%, Tᵥ = 0.197 (from standard table)
- For U = 90%, Tᵥ = 0.848 (from standard table)

**Step 1 — Time for 50% Consolidation:**

$$t_{50} \;=\; \dfrac{T_v \cdot d^2}{c_v} \;=\; \dfrac{0.197 \times (300)^2}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.197 \times 90000}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \dfrac{17730}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 3.546 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{50} \;\approx\; \dfrac{3.546 \times 10^7}{86400} \;\approx\; 410 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 1.12 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2 — Time for 90% Consolidation:**

$$t_{90} \;=\; \dfrac{0.848 \times (300)^2}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.848 \times 90000}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 1.5264 \times 10^8 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{90} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1.5264 \times 10^8}{86400} \;\approx\; 1767 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 4.84 \text{ years}$$

**Final Answer:** t₅₀ ≈ **410 days** and t₉₀ ≈ **4.84 years**.

---

**(b) At a point in a soil mass, the major and minor principal stresses are 450 kPa and 150 kPa respectively. The soil has c' = 20 kPa and φ' = 22°. Determine the normal stress, shear stress, and resultant stress on a plane inclined at 35° to the major principal plane. Comment on whether failure will occur on this plane. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- σ₁ = 450 kPa, σ₃ = 150 kPa
- θ = 35° (with major principal plane)
- c' = 20 kPa, φ' = 22°

**Step 1 — Mohr Circle Parameters:**

$$p \;=\; \dfrac{450 + 150}{2} \;=\; 300 \text{ kPa}$$

$$r \;=\; \dfrac{450 - 150}{2} \;=\; 150 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute 2θ = 70°:**

$$\sigma_n \;=\; 300 + 150 \cos(70^\circ) \;=\; 300 + 150(0.342) \;=\; 300 + 51.3 \;=\; 351.3 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\tau \;=\; 150 \sin(70^\circ) \;=\; 150(0.940) \;=\; 141.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Resultant Stress:**

$$\sigma_R \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + \tau^2} \;=\; \sqrt{351.3^2 + 141.0^2} \;=\; \sqrt{123411.7 + 19881} \;=\; \sqrt{143292.7} \;\approx\; 378.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Shear Strength on this Plane:**

$$\tau_f \;=\; c' + \sigma_n \tan\phi' \;=\; 20 + 351.3 \tan(22^\circ) \;=\; 20 + 351.3(0.404) \;=\; 20 + 141.9 \;=\; 161.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Failure Check:**

Since τ (= 141.0 kPa) < τf (= 161.9 kPa), **failure does not occur** on this plane.

**Step 6 — Failure Plane Orientation:**

$$\theta_f \;=\; 45^\circ + \dfrac{22^\circ}{2} \;=\; 45^\circ + 11^\circ \;=\; 56^\circ$$

**Final Answer:** σₙ = **351.3 kPa**, τ = **141.0 kPa**, σ_R = **378.5 kPa**, No failure occurs on this 35° plane (failure would occur on the 56° critical plane).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) The Oedometer test on a clay specimen gave the following observations: Initial void ratio e₀ = 1.10, thickness of specimen H = 20 mm, time for 50% consolidation t₅₀ = 9 minutes, drainage path = single drainage. If the coefficient of volume compressibility mᵥ = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ m²/kN, compute: (i) Coefficient of consolidation cᵥ, (ii) Coefficient of permeability k, (iii) Final settlement under a stress increment of 100 kPa. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- e₀ = 1.10
- H = 20 mm = 0.020 m
- t₅₀ = 9 min = 540 s
- Single drainage, so d = H = 0.020 m = 2.0 cm
- mᵥ = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ m²/kN
- Δσ = 100 kPa

**Step 1 — Compute cᵥ (Casagrande's Method):**

For U = 50%, Tᵥ = 0.197 (single drainage):

$$c_v \;=\; \dfrac{0.197 \cdot d^2}{t_{50}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.197 \times (2.0)^2}{540} \;=\; \dfrac{0.197 \times 4}{540} \;=\; 1.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert cᵥ to m²/s:**

$$c_v \;=\; 1.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} \;=\; 1.46 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute k:**

Using $c_v = k / (m_v \gamma_w)$, where γw = 9.81 kN/m³:

$$k \;=\; c_v \cdot m_v \cdot \gamma_w \;=\; (1.46 \times 10^{-7}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 9.81$$

$$k \;\approx\; 3.58 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Final Settlement:**

$$S_\infty \;=\; m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot H \;=\; (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 100 \times 0.020 \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \;=\; 0.50 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** cᵥ ≈ **1.46 × 10⁻³ cm²/s**, k ≈ **3.58 × 10⁻¹⁰ m/s**, S∞ = **0.50 mm**.

---

**(b) A soil specimen was tested in a triaxial apparatus under unconsolidated-undrained (UU) conditions with confining pressures of 100, 200, and 300 kPa. The deviator stresses at failure were 180, 220, and 260 kPa respectively. Determine the total stress shear strength parameters cᵤ and φᵤ. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate Data:**

| σ₃ (kPa) | σ_d (kPa) | σ₁ = σ₃ + σ_d (kPa) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| 100 | 180 | 280 |
| 200 | 220 | 420 |
| 300 | 260 | 560 |

**Step 2 — Plot Mohr Circles:**

| Circle | Center (kPa) | Radius (kPa) |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | (190, 0) | 90 |
| 2 | (310, 0) | 110 |
| 3 | (430, 0) | 130 |

**Step 3 — Determine Envelope:**

A common-sense approach: For saturated clays under UU, we expect φᵤ ≈ 0 and cᵤ = σ_d/2. The radii differ, so the circles have different diameters — they are NOT all the same, suggesting either partial saturation or testing inconsistency. Treating this as a CU-like result:

- Slope of envelope = sin φᵤ = (Δτ/Δσ') over two extreme circles.

Approximate φᵤ by comparing the maximum and minimum circles. For simplicity, take the **average** of σd/2:

$$c_u \;\approx\; \dfrac{180 + 220 + 260}{2 \times 3} \;=\; \dfrac{660}{6} \;=\; 110 \text{ kPa}$$

If we draw a best-fit line through the three circles:

$$\tan\phi_u \;\approx\; \dfrac{130 - 90}{430 - 190} \;=\; \dfrac{40}{240} \;\approx\; 0.167 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_u \;\approx\; 9.5^\circ$$

And the cohesion intercept from cᵤ = τ − σ tan φᵤ:

$$c_u \;\approx\; 130 - 430(0.167) \;=\; 130 - 71.8 \;=\; 58.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** cᵤ ≈ **58 kPa** and φᵤ ≈ **9.5°** (rounded). *Note: If the soil is a fully saturated clay, ideally φᵤ = 0 and cᵤ = 110 kPa; the slight variation suggests partial saturation or measurement variance.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing drainage path *d* with layer thickness *H***: Always remember d = H/2 for double drainage, d = H for single drainage.
> 2. **Mixing up Tᵥ = 0.197 (U=50%) with Tᵥ = 0.848 (U=90%)** — both must be memorized, and both appear in different questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit conversions** in consolidation problems: convert m to cm or vice versa, and seconds to years/days explicitly.
> 4. **Measuring the failure plane angle from the wrong reference** — always from the major principal plane, NOT from the σ₁ direction.
> 5. **In Mohr circle problems, students often forget to check failure on the critical plane** — the question may only ask about one plane, but you must comment on whether the *soil* as a whole is at failure.
> 6. **In UU tests on saturated clays, expect φᵤ = 0** — the Mohr circles all have the *same diameter*; if they differ, the problem is testing partial saturation or data interpretation.
> 7. **Skipping the stress-state to effective-stress check** — in real soils, OCR and consolidation parameters are defined in *effective* terms. Convert total stresses using u if pore pressure is given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OCR is a memory index** of the soil: NC (1), OC (>1), UC (<1).
- **Preconsolidation pressure (σ'ₚ)** is the maximum past effective stress a soil has experienced, identified via Casagrande's construction on the e-log σ' curve.
- **Terzaghi's 1-D consolidation theory** has 8 assumptions — memorize all of them; they are favorite 3-mark questions.
- **Time Factor** $T_v = c_v t / d^2$ is dimensionless and relates real time to the consolidation process.
- **Coefficient of Consolidation (cᵥ)** = k / (mᵥ γw) — the higher the cᵥ, the faster the consolidation.
- **Standard Tᵥ values to memorize**: U = 50% → Tᵥ = 0.197; U = 90% → Tᵥ = 0.848; U = 100% → Tᵥ = ∞ (theoretically).
- **Drainage path d** = H (single) or H/2 (double). This is the *most common error*.
- **Settlement** $S_t = U \cdot m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot H = U \cdot S_\infty$.
- **Consolidation vs Compaction**: Water expulsion vs air expulsion; static load vs dynamic effort; long-term vs short-term.
- **Mohr-Coulomb criterion**: $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'$.
- **Failure plane inclination**: $\theta_f = 45^\circ + \phi'/2$ measured from the **major principal plane**.
- **Plane of maximum shear stress is at 45°**, NOT the failure plane. They coincide only when φ' = 0.
- **Principal stress relation**: $\sigma'_1 = \sigma'_3 \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi'/2) + 2c' \tan(45^\circ + \phi'/2)$.
- **K_p** is the **passive** earth pressure coefficient: $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi'/2)$.
- **UU Test characteristics**: No drainage at any stage; for saturated clays, φᵤ = 0; the Mohr circles at failure have equal diameters.
- **Triaxial test advantages** over direct shear: pure stress state, control of drainage, ability to measure pore pressure, no predefined failure plane.
- **Always include units** in your final answer — KTU examiners explicitly check this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CU and CD Tests - Total and Effective Stress Strength Parameters - Unconfined Compression Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** CU and CD Tests - Total and Effective Stress Strength Parameters - Unconfined Compression Test # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Triaxial Shear Test – The Governing Concept The **Triaxial Shear Test** is the most versatile and widely used laboratory test in geotechnical engineering for determining the shear strength parameters of soils. A cylindrical soil specimen is subjected to a **confining pressure (σ₃)** all around and an **axial (deviator) stress (σ₁ − σ₃)** is gradually increased until failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The drained/undrained condition of the shearing stage — combined with whether consolidation is permitted before shearing — defines the three classical triaxial test variants: **UU (Unconsolidated Undrained)**, **CU (Consolidated Undrained)**, and **CD (Consolidated Drained)**. ## 1.2 CU Test (Consolidated Undrained Test) — The "With Pore Pressure" Test ### Formal Definition In the **CU Test**, the soil specimen is first allowed to **consolidate fully** under the applied cell (confining) pressure σ₃. After consolidation is complete, the drainage valve is **closed**, and the specimen is sheared by increasing the deviator stress at a **fast rate** so that **no further drainage is permitted**. Since drainage is prevented during shearing, **excess pore water pressure (Δu)** develops. This pore pressure is measured using a pore pressure transducer. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine squeezing a wet sponge inside a sealed, rigid plastic bottle. The sponge represents the soil skeleton, the water inside represents pore water, and the bottle represents the impermeable boundary. When you press quickly, water cannot escape — it gets compressed and pushes back. The CU test measures this "push-back" (pore pressure) plus the total push you applied. **Test Standard Speed:** Shearing rate in CU test is typically **0.5% to 1% axial strain per minute** (IS 2720 Part 12 / ASTM D4767). Saturated clays require slower rates so that **pore pressure equalizes throughout the specimen**. ## 1.3 CD Test (Consolidated Drained Test) — The "Slow and Steady" Test ### Formal Definition In the **CD Test**, the specimen is first consolidated under σ₃ (same as CU). After consolidation, the drainage valve remains **open**, and the deviator stress is applied at a **very slow rate** so that **no excess pore water pressure develops** (Δu ≈ 0). The entire applied stress is carried by the soil skeleton as **effective stress**. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Now take that same wet sponge, but this time, poke tiny holes in the bottle so water can freely squeeze out as you press. You press very slowly — gently — and the sponge just compresses steadily without any water "fighting back." The CD test is like this: water leaves, only the soil skeleton resists. **Test Standard Speed:** For clays, the CD test may take **6 to 8 hours to 2 weeks** per test depending on permeability. Rate chosen so that degree of consolidation during shearing ≥ **95%**. ## 1.4 Comparison Snapshot | Property | CU Test | CD Test | |----------|---------|---------| | Drainage during shearing | **Closed (None)** | **Open (Free)** | | Pore pressure (u) | **Develops & Measured** | **≈ 0 (dissipates)** | | Stress controlling strength | **Total + Effective** | **Effective only** | | Parameters obtained | **c, φ (total)** and **c', φ' (effective)** | **c', φ' (effective)** | | Test duration | Hours (with pore pressure measurement) | Very long (days) | | Best suited for | **Clayey soils**, short-term stability | **Clayey & Sandy soils**, long-term stability | ## 1.5 Unconfined Compression Test (UCC) — The "No Cell" Quick Test ### Formal Definition The **Unconfined Compression Test** is a special, simplified form of triaxial test where the **confining pressure σ₃ = 0**. A cylindrical clay specimen (typically **38 mm diameter, 76 mm height** with L/D = 2) is loaded axially between two platens until failure. The maximum axial stress at failure is the **Unconfined Compressive Strength, q_u**. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Take a stick of soft butter (chilled clay) and push down on it from the top with your thumb, with no surrounding support. It will bulge sideways and fail along an inclined plane. The "thumb pressure" at failure divided by the stick's cross-sectional area is q_u. This is essentially a "triaxial test without the cell" — the surrounding cell pressure is replaced by zero. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** UCC is **NOT applicable to sands or gravels** because they need lateral confinement to stand. It is strictly for **cohesive soils (clays)** that are self-supporting when unsupported. ## 1.6 Total vs Effective Stress Strength Parameters — The Fundamental Distinction | Parameter | Symbol | Stress Used | When to Use | |-----------|--------|-------------|-------------| | **Total Stress Parameter** | **c, φ** | Total stress σ | **End-of-Construction / Short-term** (Undrained condition) | | **Effective Stress Parameter** | **c', φ'** | Effective stress σ' = σ − u | **Long-term / Drained condition** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Truth (Terzaghi's Principle):** *All measurable effects of stress in a soil — strength, deformation, volume change — are governed exclusively by **effective stress**.* Total stress parameters are merely a **convenient engineering shortcut** for undrained (short-term) analysis where measuring pore pressure is impractical. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion — Universal Backbone The shear strength τ_f of a soil at failure on any plane is given by: $$ \tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi \quad \text{(Total Stress Form)} $$ $$ \tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi' \quad \text{(Effective Stress Form)} $$ where **σ' = σ − u** is the effective normal stress, and **u** is the pore water pressure at the failure plane. > [!NOTE] > **Why Two Forms?** Because in a CU test, the engineer usually does not know u at the failure plane in the field. So, for **practical short-term design**, total stress parameters (c, φ) are used. For **rigorous, long-term design**, effective stress parameters (c', φ') are mandatory. ## 2.2 Pore Pressure Coefficients — Skempton's A and B In a triaxial test, when the total stresses change by Δσ₁ (axial) and Δσ₃ (confining), the resulting **excess pore pressure Δu** is: $$ \Delta u = B \left[ \Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3) \right] $$ ### Coefficient B (Pore Pressure Coefficient under Hydrostatic Compression) $$ B = \frac{\Delta u_3}{\Delta \sigma_3} $$ | Soil Type | B Value | |-----------|---------| | **Saturated soil** (theoretical) | **1.0** | | Dry or partially saturated soil | **0 to 0.5** | | Fully dry soil | **0** | For a **saturated soil** (B = 1.0), under the consolidation stage of CU test: $$ \Delta u = \Delta \sigma_3 \quad \text{(equal to applied cell pressure)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** During consolidation, all the applied confining pressure initially goes into pore water, then slowly transfers to the soil skeleton as water drains. By the end of consolidation, **u returns to back-pressure / hydrostatic value** and **effective stress σ₃' = σ₃ − u₀** (u₀ = initial pore pressure). ### Coefficient A (Pore Pressure Coefficient under Deviator Loading) $$ A = \frac{\Delta u_d}{B(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)} = \frac{\Delta u_d}{B \cdot \Delta\sigma_d} $$ | Soil Behavior | A Value (at failure) | |---------------|----------------------| | **Loose sand, normally consolidated clay (contractive)** | **0.5 to 1.0** | | **Dense sand, overconsolidated clay (dilative)** | **−0.5 to 0** | | **Sensitive clays** | **> 1.0** (sometimes up to 1.5) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention for A:** A is **positive** if soil tends to **decrease in volume** (contractive — like loose sand or NC clay). A is **negative** if soil tends to **increase in volume** (dilative — like dense sand or OC clay). This sign tells you whether the pore pressure **rises** or **drops** during shearing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma' = \sigma - u$ | Effective stress (Terzaghi) | kPa | | 2 | $\tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi'$ | Mohr-Coulomb effective | kPa | | 3 | $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ | Mohr-Coulomb total | kPa | | 4 | $\Delta u = B[\Delta\sigma_3 + A(\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3)]$ | General pore pressure | kPa | | 5 | $B = \Delta u_3 / \Delta\sigma_3$ | Hydrostatic pore pressure coeff. | Dimensionless | | 6 | $A = \Delta u_d / [B \cdot \Delta\sigma_d]$ | Deviatoric pore pressure coeff. | Dimensionless | | 7 | $c = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ | Cohesion from UCC (φ = 0 condition) | kPa | | 8 | $S_t = \dfrac{q_u(\text{undisturbed})}{q_u(\text{remolded})}$ | Sensitivity | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\sigma_d = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Deviator stress | kPa | | 10 | $p = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$ | Center of Mohr's circle | kPa | | 11 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Radius of Mohr's circle | kPa | | 12 | $\sin\phi' = \dfrac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3' + 2c'\cot\phi'}$ | Mohr-Coulomb in principal stress form | — | | 13 | $q_u = \dfrac{P_{\text{fail}}}{A_c}$ | Unconfined compressive strength | kPa | | 14 | $A_c = A_0 \left(1 - \epsilon\right)$ | Corrected area (during loading) | mm² | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where CU Test is Used in Practice:** > - **Short-term stability of embankments on soft clay** during construction (e.g., Kochi Metro construction over marine clay). > - **Immediate bearing capacity** of footings on saturated clay. > - **End-of-construction analysis of earth dams** (using c, φ from CU tests on saturated specimens). > - **Pile design** in clays (c_u from CU/UCC). > > **Where CD Test is Used in Practice:** > - **Long-term slope stability** of natural hills (Kerala Western Ghats landslide analysis). > - **Final, long-term stability** of earth dams after pore pressures dissipate. > - **Drained bearing capacity** of foundations on sand. > > **Where UCC is Used in Practice:** > - **Rapid field/lab assessment** of clay strength for preliminary design. > - **Quality control** in earthworks. > - **Sensitivity classification** of clays. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between c, c' and φ, φ' in CU Test ### Setup In a CU test on a saturated soil (B = 1.0), the **effective** principal stresses at failure are: $$ \sigma_1' = (\sigma_1)_f - u_f $$ $$ \sigma_3' = (\sigma_3)_f - u_f $$ where **u_f** is the pore pressure measured at failure. ### Total Stress Mohr's Circle The **total stress** Mohr's circle is plotted using: - Center: $p = \dfrac{(\sigma_1)_f + (\sigma_3)_f}{2}$ - Radius: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{(\sigma_1)_f - (\sigma_3)_f}{2}$ The **total stress failure envelope** is tangent to this circle, giving total parameters c and φ. ### Effective Stress Mohr's Circle The **effective stress** Mohr's circle is plotted using: - Center: $p' = \dfrac{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3'}{2} = p - u_f$ - Radius: same as total (deviator stress unchanged): $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{(\sigma_1)_f - (\sigma_3)_f}{2}$ The **effective stress failure envelope** is tangent to this circle, giving c' and φ'. ### Relationship Derivation At failure, by Mohr-Coulomb in effective form: $$ \sin\phi' = \frac{\sigma_1' - \sigma_3'}{\sigma_1' + \sigma_3' + 2c'\cot\phi'} $$ The two envelopes have the **same deviator stress** at failure (i.e., $(\sigma_1)_f - (\sigma_3)_f$) but **different centers** because of u_f. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;c' < c\;\text{(in general)}\quad\text{and}\quad \phi' > \phi\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why φ' > φ?** Because the effective Mohr's circle is **shifted to the left** by u_f. A circle shifted to the left requires a **steeper envelope** to remain tangent. The steeper envelope has a higher friction angle. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unconfined Compression Test — q_u and c_u Relation ### Setup In UCC, **σ₃ = 0** (no confining pressure). Therefore, the only stress acting is the axial stress σ₁ applied by the loading frame. At failure: $$ (\sigma_1)_f = q_u $$ ### Mohr's Circle for UCC - Center: $p = \dfrac{q_u + 0}{2} = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ - Radius: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{q_u - 0}{2} = \dfrac{q_u}{2}$ ### φ = 0 Condition (Saturated Clay) For a **saturated clay in undrained loading**, the friction angle φ_u (undrained friction angle) = **0°. This is a fundamental result of the φ_u = 0 concept (Skempton & Bjerrum).** > [!NOTE] > **Why φ_u = 0 for saturated clay?** In undrained shearing of saturated clay, the volume cannot change (water is incompressible, soil particles rearrange but water content stays constant for a fully saturated specimen). The strength comes purely from **cohesion** (electrochemical bonding and packing). Friction requires sliding and volume change — but volume is locked, so no frictional component can mobilize. With φ = 0, the Mohr-Coulomb envelope is **horizontal**, tangent to the top of the circle: $$ \tau_f = c_u $$ Geometrically, the top of the Mohr's circle is at height = radius = $\dfrac{q_u}{2}$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;c_u = \dfrac{q_u}{2}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most important formula in UCC for KTU exams.** The unconfined compressive strength is **twice the undrained shear strength** for a saturated clay under the φ_u = 0 assumption. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pore Pressure Coefficient A (at failure, A_f) ### Setup For a saturated soil (B = 1.0), during the shearing (deviator) stage: $$ \Delta u = \Delta u_3 + \Delta u_d = B \cdot \Delta \sigma_3 + A \cdot B \cdot \Delta\sigma_d $$ In a **CU test**, σ₃ is held **constant** during shearing. Therefore Δσ₃ = 0, and: $$ \Delta u = A \cdot B \cdot (\Delta\sigma_1 - \Delta\sigma_3) = A \cdot B \cdot \sigma_d $$ For **saturated soil** (B = 1.0): $$ \boxed{\;u_f - u_0 = A_f \cdot (\sigma_d)_f\;} $$ where $u_0$ is the initial back-pressure (or initial pore pressure) and $(\sigma_d)_f$ is the deviator stress at failure. The **pore pressure coefficient A at failure** is computed as: $$ A_f = \frac{u_f - u_0}{(\sigma_d)_f} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** In CU test numerical problems, you will often be given the cell pressure σ₃, the deviator stress at failure σ_d(f), and the pore pressure at failure u_f. You must first compute A_f, then use it for the effective stress Mohr's circle. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns ### Pattern A: "Compute c' and φ' from CU Test Data" **Step-by-step procedure:** 1. For each of 3 specimens, identify σ₃, σ_d(f), u_f. 2. Compute total principal stress: $(\sigma_1)_f = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d(f)$. 3. Compute effective principal stresses: $\sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - u_f$ and $\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - u_f$. 4. Plot 3 **effective** Mohr's circles. The common tangent gives **c' and φ'**. 5. Plot 3 **total** Mohr's circles. The common tangent gives **c and φ**. ### Pattern B: "Find c_u from UCC" **Procedure:** 1. Read peak axial load P from the load–strain curve. 2. Compute corrected area: $A_c = A_0(1 - \epsilon)$ where $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. 3. $q_u = P / A_c$ at peak. 4. $c_u = q_u / 2$. ### Pattern C: "Classify Sensitivity" **Procedure:** 1. Compute $S_t = q_u(\text{undisturbed}) / q_u(\text{remolded})$. 2. Classification: | S_t Range | Classification | |-----------|----------------| | 1 | Insensitive (most clays) | | 1 – 2 | Low sensitivity | | 2 – 4 | Medium sensitivity | | 4 – 8 | High sensitivity | | 8 – 16 | Very high sensitivity | | > 16 | Quick clays (e.g., Leda clay, Scandinavian marine clays) | > [!NOTE] > **Kerala Context:** Marine clays of Kerala (e.g., around Kochi, Alappuzha backwaters) typically have **S_t between 2 and 5** (medium to high sensitivity), making them prone to strength loss upon disturbance — a major concern in pile driving and excavation. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (CU Test Analysis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A CU triaxial test was conducted on a saturated clay specimen with cell pressure σ₃ = **100 kPa**. The deviator stress at failure was σ_d = **220 kPa**, and the pore pressure at failure was u_f = **80 kPa**. Determine: (a) Total stress parameters, (b) Effective stress parameters, (c) Pore pressure coefficient A at failure. ### Solution (Step-by-Step) **Step 1: Compute total principal stresses at failure** $$ (\sigma_1)_f = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d = 100 + 220 = 320 \text{ kPa} $$ $$ (\sigma_3)_f = 100 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2: Compute effective principal stresses at failure** $$ \sigma_1' = (\sigma_1)_f - u_f = 320 - 80 = 240 \text{ kPa} $$ $$ \sigma_3' = (\sigma_3)_f - u_f = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3: For total stress parameters** (using only one circle, with assumed c and φ) Total Mohr's circle: - Center: $p = \dfrac{320 + 100}{2} = 210$ kPa - Radius: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{320 - 100}{2} = 110$ kPa **Step 4: For effective stress parameters** (using only one circle) Effective Mohr's circle: - Center: $p' = \dfrac{240 + 20}{2} = 130$ kPa - Radius: $\tau_{\max} = 110$ kPa (same — pore pressure shifts the center, not the radius) **Step 5: A single test gives only ONE point on the failure envelope — we need 3 tests for c, φ.** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Note:** With one CU test, you can only plot one circle. You need at least 3 circles (from 3 tests at different σ₃) to draw a common tangent. However, you CAN compute the pore pressure coefficient A from a single test: > > $$ > A_f = \frac{u_f - u_0}{(\sigma_d)_f} = \frac{80 - 0}{220} = 0.364 > $$ > > This value **0.364** indicates a **normally consolidated clay** behavior (NC clay typically has A_f between 0.5 and 1.0, while 0.364 is on the lower side, possibly lightly OC). **Step 6 (for 3 tests):** Suppose a second test at σ₃ = 200 kPa gave σ_d = 320 kPa, u_f = 130 kPa, and a third test at σ₃ = 300 kPa gave σ_d = 420 kPa, u_f = 180 kPa. For total stresses: - Test 1: (100, 320) - Test 2: (200, 520) - Test 3: (300, 720) Slope of failure envelope = tan φ. Using the relation $\sin\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3 + 2c\cot\phi}$ — solved by plotting common tangent to all three circles. For effective stresses: - Test 1: (20, 240) - Test 2: (70, 390) - Test 3: (120, 540) Common tangent through these gives c' and φ'. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture Flow: Decision Tree for Choosing Triaxial Test Type ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Need Shear Strength Parameters"] --> B{"Soil Type?"} B -- "SAND (cohesionless)" --> C["Use CD Test or Direct Shear Test"] B -- "CLAY (cohesive)" --> D{"Drainage Condition of Field Problem?"} D -- "Short-term / End-of-Construction" --> E["Use CU Test (c, phi) or UCC"] D -- "Long-term / Drained" --> F["Use CD Test (c-dash, phi-dash)"] D -- "Need quick field estimate" --> G["Use Unconfined Compression Test"] E --> H{"Pore Pressure Measurement Available?"} H -- "Yes" --> I["CU Test with pore pressure transducer"] H -- "No" --> J["UU Test or UCC"] I --> K["OUTPUT: c, phi (total) AND c-dash, phi-dash (effective)"] F --> K G --> L["OUTPUT: c_u = q_u / 2 (undrained)"] C --> M["OUTPUT: phi-dash only (c-dash approx 0)"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture Flow: CU Test Procedure (Stepwise) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Prepare cylindrical specimen
38 mm dia, 76 mm height
Saturate to B greater than 0.95"] --> S2["STEP 2: Place specimen in triaxial cell
with impermeable membrane"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Apply cell pressure sigma3
Open drainage valve
Allow consolidation to complete
Record volume change"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: CLOSE drainage valve
Set pore pressure transducer to zero"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Apply deviator stress
at controlled rate 0.5 to 1 percent per minute
Record load, deformation, and pore pressure"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Continue until failure
or 20 percent axial strain"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Plot deviator stress vs strain
Identify peak or ultimate failure"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Compute A_f = u_f minus u_0 / sigma_d at failure"] S8 --> S9["STEP 9: Plot total Mohr circles for 3 tests
Common tangent gives c and phi"] S9 --> S10["STEP 10: Plot effective Mohr circles
Common tangent gives c-dash and phi-dash"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture Flow: CD Test Procedure (Stepwise) ```mermaid flowchart TD T1["STEP 1: Prepare and saturate specimen"] --> T2["STEP 2: Place in triaxial cell"] T2 --> T3["STEP 3: Apply sigma3
Allow full consolidation
Drainage valve open"] T3 --> T4["STEP 4: KEEP drainage valve open
Apply deviator stress VERY SLOWLY
No excess pore pressure builds up
Rate based on coefficient of consolidation"] T4 --> T5["STEP 5: Record load, deformation,
AND volume change of pore water"] T5 --> T6["STEP 6: Continue until failure or 20 percent strain"] T6 --> T7["STEP 7: Plot sigma-d vs epsilon and
Volume change vs epsilon"] T7 --> T8["STEP 8: Plot 3 Mohr circles using
sigma1 equals sigma3 plus sigma-d
sigma3 prime equals sigma3 minus u_drained approx 0"] T8 --> T9["OUTPUT: c-dash and phi-dash
No total parameters meaningful in CD"] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram: Unconfined Compression Test (UCC) Setup ```mermaid flowchart TD U0["Unconfined Compression Test Setup"] --> U1["TOP Platen
(fixed)"] U0 --> U2["BOTTOM Platen
(movable)"] U0 --> U3["SPECIMEN
38 mm dia x 76 mm height
L by D equals 2
Saturated clay"] U1 --> U4["Loading Frame
with Proving Ring
or Load Cell"] U4 --> U5["Dial Gauge
for axial strain"] U3 --> U6["No lateral confinement
sigma3 equals 0
sigma1 equals q_u at failure"] U1 --> U3 U3 --> U2 U2 --> U4 U1 -- applies axial load --> U3 U3 -- fails along inclined plane --> U7["FAILURE: q_u equals P_peak divided by A_corrected"] U7 --> U8["c_u equals q_u divided by 2
Sensitivity S_t equals q_u undisturbed divided by q_u remolded"] ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: Pore Pressure Behavior — A Parameter Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["Starting state: Soil specimen under sigma3 effective"] --> A1["Apply deviator stress sigma-d"] A1 --> A2{"Volume change tendency of soil?"} A2 -- "CONTRACTIVE
NC clay, loose sand" --> A3["A positive 0.5 to 1.0
Pore pressure INCREASES
Effective stress decreases
phi-u less than phi-dash"] A2 -- "DILATIVE
OC clay, dense sand" --> A4["A negative 0.5 to 0
Pore pressure DECREASES
Effective stress increases
phi-u greater than phi-dash"] A2 -- "VERY SENSITIVE CLAY" --> A5["A greater than 1.0
Catastrophic pore pressure rise
Strain softening behavior"] A3 --> A6["Effective Mohr circle shifted LEFT
Smaller effective stresses at failure"] A4 --> A7["Effective Mohr circle shifted RIGHT
Larger effective stresses at failure"] A5 --> A8["Sudden strength loss
S_t greater than 8"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper] **Differentiate between CU and CD triaxial tests on saturated clay with respect to drainage conditions, pore pressure development, and parameters obtained.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | CU Test | CD Test | |--------|---------|---------| | **Drainage during shearing** | Closed (no drainage allowed) | Open (free drainage allowed) | | **Pore pressure (u)** | Develops excess pore pressure, measured by transducer | No excess pore pressure (u ≈ 0) | | **Shearing rate** | Fast (0.5%–1% strain/min) | Very slow (days to weeks) | | **Parameters obtained** | c, φ (total) AND c', φ' (effective) | c', φ' (effective) only | | **Application** | Short-term / end-of-construction | Long-term / fully drained | ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sample Paper] **What is the φ_u = 0 concept? Why does it apply to saturated clays in undrained loading?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **φ_u = 0 concept** (Skempton & Bjerrum) states that the undrained friction angle of a **saturated clay** is approximately **zero** under rapid loading conditions. **Reasoning:** 1. In undrained loading, the volume of a saturated soil cannot change (water is incompressible, degree of saturation = 100%). 2. Frictional strength requires particle sliding, which always involves some volume change (dilation/contraction). 3. Since volume is locked, only **cohesion** (electrochemical inter-particle bonds and packing) contributes to strength. 4. Therefore, the Mohr-Coulomb envelope becomes horizontal: **τ_f = c_u**, and **φ_u = 0**. 5. This gives the key UCC relation: **c_u = q_u / 2**. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Pattern] **(a)** *A consolidated undrained triaxial test was conducted on three identical saturated clay specimens. The test data at failure is:* - *Specimen 1: σ₃ = 100 kPa, σ_d = 280 kPa, u_f = 65 kPa* - *Specimen 2: σ₃ = 200 kPa, σ_d = 380 kPa, u_f = 110 kPa* - *Specimen 3: σ₃ = 300 kPa, σ_d = 470 kPa, u_f = 145 kPa* *Determine: (i) Total stress parameters c and φ. (ii) Effective stress parameters c' and φ'. (iii) Pore pressure coefficient A at failure for each specimen. [7 marks]* **(b)** *Describe the Unconfined Compression Test with a neat sketch. How is the undrained shear strength c_u determined from the test? Discuss the concept of sensitivity and its significance. [7 marks]* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (a) [7 marks] **Step 1: Compute total and effective principal stresses at failure** For each specimen: $$ (\sigma_1)_f = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d $$ $$ \sigma_1' = (\sigma_1)_f - u_f $$ $$ \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - u_f $$ | Specimen | σ₃ (kPa) | (σ₁)_f (kPa) | u_f (kPa) | σ₃' (kPa) | σ₁' (kPa) | |----------|----------|--------------|-----------|-----------|-----------| | 1 | 100 | 380 | 65 | 35 | 315 | | 2 | 200 | 580 | 110 | 90 | 470 | | 3 | 300 | 770 | 145 | 155 | 625 | **Step 2: Pore pressure coefficient A at failure** For saturated soil (B = 1.0): $$ A_f = \frac{u_f - u_0}{(\sigma_d)_f} = \frac{u_f}{\sigma_d} $$ (Assuming initial pore pressure u₀ = 0 at end of consolidation under back-pressure or hydrostatic conditions equalized.) - Specimen 1: $A_{f1} = 65 / 280 = 0.232$ - Specimen 2: $A_{f2} = 110 / 380 = 0.289$ - Specimen 3: $A_{f3} = 145 / 470 = 0.309$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** All A_f values are in the range **0.2 to 0.4**, indicating a **lightly overconsolidated clay** or a low-plasticity clay with mild contractive tendency. Pure NC clay would have A_f ≈ 0.5–1.0. Pure OC clay would have A_f near 0 or negative. **Step 3: Total stress parameters c, φ — Plotting total Mohr circles** For each specimen, total Mohr's circle: - Center: $p = [(\sigma_1)_f + \sigma_3] / 2$ - Radius: $\tau_{\max} = [(\sigma_1)_f - \sigma_3] / 2$ | Specimen | Center p (kPa) | Radius τ_max (kPa) | |----------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | 240 | 140 | | 2 | 390 | 190 | | 3 | 535 | 235 | **Step 4: Calculate sin φ from two circles and find c** For Specimens 1 and 3 (extremes): By the formula for common tangent to two circles of radii r₁, r₂ and centers p₁, p₂: $$ \sin\phi = \frac{r_3 - r_1}{p_3 - p_1} = \frac{235 - 140}{535 - 240} = \frac{95}{295} = 0.3220 $$ $$ \phi = \sin^{-1}(0.3220) = 18.79° \approx 18.8° $$ For c, using the y-intercept method (c = p sin φ · cos φ − r · cos² φ), or solving from Specimen 1: The total strength at σ = 240 kPa (center) on the failure envelope gives: $$ \sin\phi = \frac{r_1 + c\cot\phi}{p_1} \Rightarrow 0.3220 = \frac{140 + c \cdot \cot(18.8°)}{240} $$ $$ \cot(18.8°) = 1/\tan(18.8°) = 1/0.3404 = 2.938 $$ $$ 0.3220 \times 240 = 140 + c \times 2.938 $$ $$ 77.28 = 140 + 2.938\,c $$ $$ 2.938\,c = -62.72 $$ $$ c = -21.35 \text{ kPa} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Negative c?** This indicates the data has some scatter OR the assumed tangent is slightly off. In a KTU exam, slight negative c (e.g., -5 to -25 kPa) is acceptable — it just means **c ≈ 0** and the envelope passes near the origin. Engineering interpretation: the soil is essentially **cohesionless in total stress terms**, which is unusual for clay but possible if the clay is sensitive. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Exam:** If you get a small negative c, report it as c ≈ 0 or c = 0 (or take absolute value and call it a numerical artifact). The friction angle φ is the **reliable** answer. **Step 5: Effective stress parameters c', φ'** Effective Mohr's circles: | Specimen | Center p' (kPa) | Radius τ_max (kPa) | |----------|-----------------|---------------------| | 1 | 175 | 140 | | 2 | 280 | 190 | | 3 | 390 | 235 | For Specimens 1 and 3: $$ \sin\phi' = \frac{235 - 140}{390 - 175} = \frac{95}{215} = 0.4419 $$ $$ \phi' = \sin^{-1}(0.4419) = 26.24° \approx 26.2° $$ For c': $$ 0.4419 = \frac{140 + c' \cdot \cot(26.2°)}{175} $$ $$ \cot(26.2°) = 1/\tan(26.2°) = 1/0.4921 = 2.032 $$ $$ 77.33 = 140 + 2.032\,c' $$ $$ c' = \frac{77.33 - 140}{2.032} = \frac{-62.67}{2.032} = -30.84 \text{ kPa} $$ Again, c' ≈ 0 (essentially cohesionless in effective terms as well). **Final Answers for Q-A(a):** - **Total:** c ≈ 0 kPa, φ ≈ 18.8° - **Effective:** c' ≈ 0 kPa, φ' ≈ 26.2° - **A_f values:** 0.232, 0.289, 0.309 > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** As expected, **φ' > φ** (26.2° > 18.8°), confirming the theoretical result derived in Section 3.1. --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (b) [7 marks] **Unconfined Compression Test (UCC):** **Apparatus:** 1. Loading frame with proving ring (or load cell) and a hand-operated / motor-driven jack. 2. Two parallel, polished, hardened steel platens — top fixed, bottom movable. 3. Dial gauge (or LVDT) for measuring axial deformation. 4. Specimen: cylindrical, **38 mm diameter**, **76 mm length** (L/D = 2), trimmed from an undisturbed clay sample. **Procedure:** 1. The clay specimen is carefully trimmed and its initial dimensions (L₀, D₀) and weight are recorded. 2. The specimen is placed between the two platens. **No membrane or cell is used** — hence "unconfined." 3. A small seating load is applied to ensure proper contact. 4. Axial load is applied at a constant rate of strain (**0.5% to 1% per minute**), and readings of load (P) and deformation (ΔL) are taken at regular intervals. 5. Loading continues until **failure** (peak load) or **20% axial strain**, whichever comes first. 6. The axial stress σ₁ = P / A_c, where corrected area $A_c = A_0(1 - \epsilon)$ and $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. **Determination of c_u:** The maximum axial stress at failure is the **Unconfined Compressive Strength**: $$ q_u = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{A_c} $$ For saturated clay under the **φ_u = 0 condition**, the Mohr-Coulomb envelope is horizontal. The Mohr's circle has σ₃ = 0 and σ₁ = q_u, so: $$ c_u = \frac{q_u}{2} $$ **Sensitivity and its significance:** **Sensitivity (S_t)** is the ratio of undisturbed to remolded strength: $$ S_t = \frac{q_u(\text{undisturbed})}{q_u(\text{remolded})} $$ It quantifies the **strength loss upon remolding** (e.g., during pile driving, excavation, or disturbance by heavy machinery). **Significance:** 1. **Sensitive clays (S_t > 4)** can lose substantial strength when disturbed, leading to landslides, slope failures, and reduced pile capacity. 2. **Quick clays (S_t > 16)**, like Scandinavian marine clays, can liquefy upon disturbance. 3. Sensitivity is a key input in **choosing construction methods** for soft clays (e.g., in Kerala's Kuttanad region, Alappuzha). 4. Determines whether **pre-consolidation** or **careful excavation** is needed. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative ESE Choice **(a)** *Explain the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion for soils. Distinguish between total and effective stress strength parameters. When is each used in engineering practice? [7 marks]* **(b)** *A consolidated drained (CD) triaxial test on a saturated sandy clay gave the following failure data for three specimens:* - *Specimen 1: σ₃ = 50 kPa, σ_d = 130 kPa* - *Specimen 2: σ₃ = 100 kPa, σ_d = 220 kPa* - *Specimen 3: σ₃ = 200 kPa, σ_d = 380 kPa* *Compute the effective stress parameters c' and φ' (in CD test, u = 0 at failure). [7 marks]* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (a) [7 marks] **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion:** The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion is the most widely used shear strength criterion in geotechnical engineering. It states that **shear failure occurs along a plane when the shear stress τ on that plane reaches a critical value**, which is a linear function of the **normal effective stress σ'** on that plane: $$ \tau_f = c' + \sigma' \tan\phi' $$ The graphical representation is a **tangent line to the Mohr's circles** at failure, drawn in the τ-σ' plane. The line's intercept on the τ-axis is **c'** (effective cohesion), and the angle it makes with the σ'-axis is **φ'** (effective angle of internal friction). **Failure condition in terms of principal stresses:** $$ \sin\phi' = \frac{(\sigma_1')_f - (\sigma_3')_f}{(\sigma_1')_f + (\sigma_3')_f + 2c'\cot\phi'} $$ **Total vs Effective Stress Parameters:** | Aspect | Total (c, φ) | Effective (c', φ') | |--------|--------------|---------------------| | **Stress used** | Total stress σ | Effective stress σ' = σ − u | | **Pore pressure** | Ignored (lumped into parameters) | Explicitly subtracted | | **When valid** | Undrained, short-term analysis | Drained, long-term analysis | | **Source of data** | UU or CU test (without measuring u) | CU test (with u) or CD test | | **Theoretical basis** | Empirical shortcut | Rigorous (Terzaghi's principle) | **When each is used in practice:** 1. **Total stress parameters (c, φ):** - End-of-construction stability of embankments on clay. - Rapid drawdown analysis (initial condition). - Short-term bearing capacity of footings on saturated clay. - Initial stability of earth dams during construction. - Pile design in clay (using c_u = q_u / 2 from UCC). 2. **Effective stress parameters (c', φ'):** - Long-term slope stability of natural and cut slopes. - Long-term stability of earth dams (steady-state seepage). - Drained bearing capacity of foundations. - Liquefaction analysis. - Earth pressure problems (active/passive in long term). - Settlement and consolidation analysis (linked to effective stress path). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Point:** In **CU test**, BOTH (c, φ) AND (c', φ') are obtained. In **CD test**, only (c', φ') are obtained. In **UU test**, only (c, φ) are obtained (with c = c_u, φ = 0). --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (b) [7 marks] **Step 1: Compute (σ₁)_f for each specimen** $$ (\sigma_1)_f = \sigma_3 + \sigma_d $$ - Specimen 1: $\sigma_3 = 50$, $(\sigma_1)_f = 50 + 130 = 180$ kPa - Specimen 2: $\sigma_3 = 100$, $(\sigma_1)_f = 100 + 220 = 320$ kPa - Specimen 3: $\sigma_3 = 200$, $(\sigma_1)_f = 200 + 380 = 580$ kPa **Step 2: For CD test, u_f = 0, so effective stresses = total stresses** $$ \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_1' = (\sigma_1)_f $$ **Step 3: Plot Mohr's circles** | Specimen | σ₃' (kPa) | σ₁' (kPa) | Center p' = (σ₁' + σ₃')/2 | Radius r = (σ₁' − σ₃')/2 | |----------|-----------|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------------| | 1 | 50 | 180 | 115 | 65 | | 2 | 100 | 320 | 210 | 110 | | 3 | 200 | 580 | 390 | 190 | **Step 4: Calculate φ' from two extreme circles (Specimens 1 and 3)** $$ \sin\phi' = \frac{r_3 - r_1}{p_3 - p_1} = \frac{190 - 65}{390 - 115} = \frac{125}{275} = 0.4545 $$ $$ \phi' = \sin^{-1}(0.4545) = 27.04° \approx 27° $$ **Step 5: Calculate c' from one circle (e.g., Specimen 1)** Using the relation: $$ \sin\phi' = \frac{r_1 + c'\cot\phi'}{p_1} $$ $$ \cot(27.04°) = \frac{1}{\tan(27.04°)} = \frac{1}{0.5110} = 1.957 $$ $$ 0.4545 = \frac{65 + c' \times 1.957}{115} $$ $$ 0.4545 \times 115 = 65 + 1.957\,c' $$ $$ 52.27 = 65 + 1.957\,c' $$ $$ 1.957\,c' = 52.27 - 65 = -12.73 $$ $$ c' = -6.50 \text{ kPa} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** c' ≈ 0 kPa (taking absolute value or rounding to 0). The sandy clay is essentially **cohesionless** with respect to effective stress, which is typical for sandy clays and sands. **Step 6: Verify with Specimen 2** Using p' = 210, r = 110, predicted: $$ r_{\text{predicted}} = p' \sin\phi' - c'\cot\phi' = 210 \times 0.4545 - 0 = 95.45 \text{ kPa} $$ But actual r = 110. So 95.45 vs 110 — there's a small scatter, but the test data is consistent within typical experimental error. **Final Answers for Q-B(b):** - **c' ≈ 0 kPa** - **φ' ≈ 27°** --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:** > > **1. Forgetting Area Correction in UCC (–2 marks):** When calculating q_u, students often use the **original area A₀** instead of the **corrected area** $A_c = A_0(1 - \epsilon)$. The corrected area is **larger** than A₀, so q_u is **smaller** than P/A₀. Always use $A_c$. > > **2. Confusing Total and Effective Stresses in Mohr Circles (–3 marks):** When asked to "plot Mohr's circles from CU data," students sometimes plot only **one set** of circles. The question **always** requires **two sets**: total AND effective. Effective circle = Total circle shifted **left** by u_f. > > **3. Wrong Sign of A Parameter (–2 marks):** The A parameter is **positive** for NC clay / contractive soils and **negative** for OC clay / dilative soils. Students often write A as if it were always positive. > > **4. Forgetting that B = 1 for Saturated Soil (–1 mark):** In a CU test on saturated soil, B = 1.0 is implicit. Don't forget this when computing u. > > **5. Drawing the Envelope Wrong (–2 marks):** The Mohr-Coulomb envelope must be a **tangent to ALL three circles** (not secant). It must pass **above** the centers and be **tangent from above**. For φ' > 0, the envelope tilts upward to the right. > > **6. UCC vs UU Confusion (–1 mark):** UCC is **NOT** a UU test. UCC is a triaxial test with σ₃ = 0. UU is a triaxial test with σ₃ > 0 but no consolidation and no drainage. Different tests, different applications. > > **7. Not Stating Assumption of φ = 0 in UCC (–1 mark):** The relation c_u = q_u / 2 holds **only** when φ = 0. State this assumption explicitly. For partially saturated or sensitive clays, φ ≠ 0 and the relation is more complex. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 4 — Quick-Fire Recall Points:** > > 1. **CU Test:** Consolidated + Undrained shearing. **Measures pore pressure**. Gives **both** c, φ (total) and c', φ' (effective). Shearing rate ≈ 0.5%–1% strain/min. > > 2. **CD Test:** Consolidated + Drained shearing. **No pore pressure buildup** (u ≈ 0). Gives **only** c', φ' (effective). Shearing rate very slow (days/weeks). > > 3. **UU Test:** Unconsolidated + Undrained. **No consolidation**, no drainage. Gives only c, φ (total) with φ = 0 for saturated clay. c_u = (σ₁ − σ₃)_f / 2. > > 4. **φ_u = 0 Concept:** For **saturated clay** under undrained loading, friction angle = 0. Strength comes only from cohesion. Valid because volume is locked. > > 5. **UCC Relation:** $c_u = q_u / 2$. **Applies only to saturated clay** with φ = 0. Not applicable to sands, gravels, or partially saturated soils. > > 6. **Pore Pressure Coefficient B:** B = 1.0 for **saturated soil**, B = 0 for **dry soil**. Indicates fraction of hydrostatic stress taken by pore water. > > 7. **Pore Pressure Coefficient A:** A > 0 for **NC clay / contractive**; A < 0 for **OC clay / dilative**; A > 1 for **sensitive clays**. > > 8. **Effective Stress Principle:** $\sigma' = \sigma - u$. **All strength, deformation, and volume change are governed by σ'**, not σ. > > 9. **Sensitivity S_t:** $S_t = q_u(\text{undisturbed}) / q_u(\text{remolded})$. **S_t > 4** = sensitive clay; **S_t > 16** = quick clay. > > 10. **φ' > φ always** for CU tests, because effective Mohr's circles shift left by u_f, requiring a steeper envelope. > > 11. **Total stress parameters are empirical** (used for short-term undrained analysis). **Effective stress parameters are fundamental** (used for long-term / drained analysis). > > 12. **CD test is the "gold standard" for effective stress parameters** because u = 0 makes the test simple to interpret. But it's very slow. > > 13. **CU test is the practical compromise** — moderately fast, gives both total and effective parameters, but requires pore pressure measurement. > > 14. **Application shortcuts (Kerala & general):** > - **Pile design in clay** → c_u from UCC or UU test > - **Embankment on soft clay** → c, φ from CU test (short-term) > - **Hill slope stability** → c', φ' from CD or CU test (long-term) > - **Earth dam final design** → c', φ' from CD test > - **Earthquake / liquefaction** → c', φ' effective > > 15. **Specimen dimensions:** UCC uses 38 mm dia × 76 mm length. Triaxial uses 38 mm dia × 76 mm length (same). L/D ratio = 2. > > 16. **Rate of strain in CU test:** 0.5% to 1% axial strain per minute for typical clays. > > 17. **Rate of strain in CD test:** Chosen such that U (degree of consolidation) ≥ 95% throughout shearing; usually based on $t_{90}$ from consolidation test. > > 18. **Corrected area in UCC:** $A_c = A_0(1 - \epsilon)$, accounts for bulging of specimen during compression. > > 19. **For dry or partially saturated soil:** B < 1.0; effective stress buildup is less; CU test interpretation is more complex. > > 20. **Critical formula for KTU exams:** $c_u = q_u / 2$ — comes from Mohr's circle geometry with σ₃ = 0 and φ = 0. State the assumption explicitly when using this formula.

topics.title: Direct Shear Test and Vane Shear Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET402 - SOIL MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Shear Test and Vane Shear Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Direct Shear Test (DST)

**Formal Definition:**
The Direct Shear Test is a laboratory/deterministic shear strength test in which a soil specimen is sheared along a **predetermined horizontal plane** (the horizontal split of the shear box) by applying a controlled normal load and a steadily increasing horizontal (shear) load. The test is primarily used to determine the **Mohr-Coulomb shear strength parameters** — cohesion ($c$) and angle of internal friction ($\phi$) — for cohesionless as well as cohesive soils (with limitations for the latter).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCET402 – Module 4):**
> The Direct Shear Test and Vane Shear Test are core **laboratory-based strength determination** methods. They fall under CO-2: *Determine the engineering properties of soils and apply them to geotechnical design problems.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine two thick slices of buttered bread held between your palms. Your lower palm pushes horizontally while your upper palm pushes down. The bread slices start sliding past each other along a fixed horizontal line — exactly where the butter is. The Direct Shear Test mimics this exact mechanism: the **top half of the box** slides over the **bottom half**, and the soil gets sheared along a forced horizontal plane. The resistance offered by the soil is what we call **shear strength**.

- Standard box dimensions: **60 mm × 60 mm** (small) and **100 mm × 100 mm** (large) cross-section.
- Standard sample height in the box: **20 mm to 25 mm**.
- The plane of shearing is **forced** at the horizontal split of the box, NOT the actual weakest plane.

---

### 1.2 Vane Shear Test (VST)

**Formal Definition:**
The Vane Shear Test is a **rotational shear test** (conducted in the laboratory on undisturbed samples or in-situ in the field) used primarily to determine the **undrained shear strength ($c_u$) of soft, sensitive, or remoulded cohesive soils**. A four-bladed vane is pushed into the soil and rotated at a controlled rate; the torque required to rotate the vane is converted into shear strength using a geometric relationship between the vane dimensions and the cylindrical surface of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Variants of the VST:**
> 1. **Laboratory Vane Shear Test (LVST)** – Performed on undisturbed samples in the lab.
> 2. **Field Vane Shear Test (FVST)** – Conducted in-situ by pushing the vane directly into the ground at the desired depth.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a four-bladed kitchen beater (like an egg beater) plunged into a thick, sticky pudding. When you twist the handle, the blades cut a **cylindrical surface** through the pudding, and you feel resistance. The harder you twist, the more force the pudding exerts back. The Vane Shear Test works on the same principle — the **torque** you apply to rotate the vane is the "force" the soil exerts back along a cylindrical failure surface, and from this torque we calculate the soil's shear strength.

- Standard vane geometry: **Height-to-diameter ratio (H/D) = 2** (most common).
- Typical vane dimensions: D = 12.7 mm to 25.4 mm (lab); D = 50 mm to 100 mm (field).
- Rotation rate: **0.1°/second** to **0.5°/second** to ensure undrained conditions.

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Tests Together?

| Parameter | Direct Shear Test | Vane Shear Test |
|---|---|---|
| Best suited for | Granular / sandy soils | Soft to medium cohesive soils |
| Drainage condition | Drained (CD test) | Undrained (UU test) |
| Plane of failure | Predefined (forced) | Cylindrical (free to form) |
| In-situ capability | No (lab only) | Yes (FVST) |
| Cost & simplicity | Low cost, simple | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *DST = Drained Soil Test* (sands), *VST = Very Soft clay Test* (cohesive, undrained).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Direct Shear Test — Operational Concept

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A soil sample (disturbed or undisturbed) is carefully placed in the **shear box**, which is split horizontally into two halves.
2. **Box Assembly:** The top half is fixed to the upper loading platen, the bottom half rests on rollers or a low-friction surface to allow horizontal translation.
3. **Application of Normal Load (P):** A vertical load $P$ is applied through a loading cap. The corresponding normal stress is:
   $$\sigma_n = \frac{P}{A}$$
   where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the sample.
4. **Application of Shear Force (T):** A horizontal (shear) force $T$ is applied to the lower half of the box at a **constant rate of strain** (typically 0.5 mm/min to 1.25 mm/min for drained conditions).
5. **Data Recording:** The horizontal displacement $\Delta L$ and the corresponding shear force $T$ are continuously recorded (using a proving ring or load cell and a dial gauge).
6. **Failure Identification:** Failure is identified at the **peak shear force** (or at a strain of ~15–20% if no peak is observed for dense sands).
7. **Repetition:** The test is repeated on **3 to 5 identical specimens** under different normal loads ($\sigma_{n1}, \sigma_{n2}, \sigma_{n3}, \ldots$).
8. **Plotting:** A graph of $\tau$ vs. $\sigma_n$ is plotted. The slope gives $\tan\phi$ and the intercept gives $c$.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why a forced plane?** In the direct shear box, the failure plane is **fixed** at the gap between the two halves. This is a major limitation because real soils fail along their **weakest plane**, which is not necessarily horizontal.
- **Why repeat with multiple normal loads?** Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion requires at least 3 points to define the linear failure envelope $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$.
- **Why is DST ideal for sands?** Sands are cohesionless ($c \approx 0$) and free-draining. The DST imposes a slow, drained failure — perfect for measuring the **frictional resistance** between sand grains.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Direct Shear Test

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ = Normal load, $A$ = Sample area | kN/m² (kPa) |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{A}$ | $T$ = Shear force at failure | kN/m² (kPa) |
| 3 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta L}{H_0}$ | $\Delta L$ = Horizontal displacement, $H_0$ = Initial sample height | Dimensionless or % |
| 4 | Failure Envelope (Mohr-Coulomb) | $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$ | $c$ = Cohesion, $\phi$ = Angle of internal friction | kPa |
| 5 | Peak Friction Angle | $\phi_{peak} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\tau_{peak}}{\sigma_n}\right)$ | For $c = 0$ (sands) | Degrees (°) |
| 6 | Critical State Friction Angle | $\phi_{cv}$ (constant volume) | For loose sands at large strain | Degrees (°) |
| 7 | Dilation Angle | $\psi = \phi_{peak} - \phi_{cv}$ | Empirical relation (Bolton) | Degrees (°) |
| 8 | Residual Strength | $\tau_{res} = c_r + \sigma_n \tan\phi_r$ | For overconsolidated clays after large displacement | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Direct Shear Test data is **directly used in slope stability analysis, bearing capacity calculations, and earth pressure problems** (Rankine & Coulomb theories) by feeding the values of $c$ and $\phi$ into Mohr-Coulomb failure criteria.

---

### 2.3 Vane Shear Test — Operational Concept

**Step-by-Step Procedure (Field VST — FVST):**

1. **Site Preparation & Boring:** A borehole is advanced to a depth slightly above the test depth (about **5 times the vane diameter** above the test point) to avoid soil disturbance.
2. **Vane Insertion:** The vane is carefully pushed into the soil at the bottom of the borehole to the test depth (typically **3 to 4 times the borehole diameter** below the borehole bottom).
3. **Torque Application:** Rotation is applied to the vane rod at a controlled rate (≈ **0.1°/sec** for very soft clays, faster for stiffer clays).
4. **Recording Peak Torque:** The maximum torque $T_{max}$ required to shear the soil is recorded (peak undrained strength).
5. **Continued Rotation:** The vane is rotated rapidly (about 10 full revolutions) to **remould** the soil, and the reduced torque $T_{rem}$ is recorded (remoulded strength).
6. **Sensitivity Calculation:** Sensitivity $S_t = c_{u,\text{peak}} / c_{u,\text{remoulded}}$.
7. **Withdrawal:** The vane is withdrawn, and the test may be repeated at different depths.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why a four-bladed vane?** It creates a **well-defined cylindrical failure surface** comprising the cylindrical side surface plus the two end (top & bottom) conical/toroidal surfaces.
- **Why remould the soil?** This allows determination of the soil's **sensitivity** ($S_t$) — a measure of strength loss upon remoulding. Highly sensitive clays lose most of their strength.
- **Why correct the measured value?** VST measures $c_u$ at a higher strain rate than real loading. **Bjerrum (1972)** proposed an empirical correction factor $\mu$ to convert measured $c_u$ to field-realistic values.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Vane Shear Test

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vane Geometry Constraint | $\dfrac{H}{D} = 2$ (standard) | $H$ = Vane height, $D$ = Vane diameter | — |
| 2 | Total Failure Surface | $A_s = \pi D H + \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}$ | Side cylindrical + two semi-circular end caps | m² |
| 3 | Torque — General Form | $T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \dfrac{H}{2} + \dfrac{D}{6} \right)$ | $c_u$ = Undrained shear strength | N·m |
| 4 | Simplified Torque (for H/D = 2) | $T = c_u \cdot \dfrac{7 \pi D^3}{6}$ | Special case of the general formula | N·m |
| 5 | Undrained Shear Strength | $c_u = \dfrac{6T}{7\pi D^3}$ | Rearranged from above | kPa |
| 6 | Bjerrum's Correction | $c_{u,\text{design}} = \mu \cdot c_{u,\text{measured}}$ | $\mu$ = Correction factor | kPa |
| 7 | Bjerrum's Correction Factor | $\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(I_P)$ | $I_P$ = Plasticity index (%) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $S_t = \dfrac{c_{u,\text{peak}}}{c_{u,\text{remoulded}}}$ | Peak vs. remoulded strength | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The VST-derived $c_u$ is the primary input parameter for:
> - **Bearing capacity** of soft clay foundations (Skempton / Meyerhof equations).
> - **Stability of embankments** on soft soils.
> - **Pile design** in cohesive soils (total stress / $\alpha$-method).

---

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis: Direct Shear vs. Vane Shear

| Feature | Direct Shear Test | Vane Shear Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Drainage Condition** | Drained (CD) | Undrained (UU) |
| **Soil Type** | Granular soils (sands, gravels) | Soft to medium clays |
| **Failure Plane** | Forced horizontal | Free cylindrical surface |
| **Stress State** | Non-uniform | More representative |
| **Pore Pressure Measurement** | Not possible | Not possible (directly) |
| **Strain Rate Control** | Yes (constant rate) | Yes (constant rotation) |
| **In-Situ Capability** | No | Yes (FVST) |
| **Measures $c$ and $\phi$ separately** | Yes | Only $c_u$ |
| **Sample Disturbance** | Disturbed OK for sands | Must be undisturbed |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Direct Shear Test Stress Equations

**Starting Point:** A soil specimen of cross-sectional area $A$ sits in a shear box split into two halves.

**Step 1 — Normal Stress:**
The vertical load $P$ is distributed uniformly over the cross-sectional area $A$. By definition of stress:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{P}{A}$$

For a square box of side $b$ (e.g., 60 mm or 100 mm):

$$A = b^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_n = \frac{P}{b^2}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress:**
The horizontal (shear) force $T$ acts on the same cross-sectional area $A$ along the horizontal failure plane. Therefore:

$$\tau = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{T}{b^2}$$

**Step 3 — Shear Strain:**
The horizontal displacement $\Delta L$ occurs over the original sample height $H_0$. Thus:

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta L}{H_0}$$

This is a small-strain approximation (valid for $\Delta L \ll H_0$).

**Step 4 — Mohr-Coulomb Failure Envelope:**
After conducting $n$ tests (typically $n = 3$ to 5) at different normal stresses $\sigma_{n1}, \sigma_{n2}, \ldots, \sigma_{nn}$ and obtaining corresponding peak shear stresses $\tau_{f1}, \tau_{f2}, \ldots, \tau_{fn}$, the failure envelope is fitted by linear regression:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$$

Solving for the two parameters using **two representative points** (or least-squares for more points):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tau_{f2} - \tau_{f1}}{\sigma_{n2} - \sigma_{n1}}$$

$$c = \tau_{f1} - \sigma_{n1}\tan\phi = \tau_{f2} - \sigma_{n2}\tan\phi$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Direct Shear Test (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A direct shear test was conducted on a sandy soil using a 60 mm × 60 mm shear box. The following data were recorded at failure:

| Test No. | Normal Load $P$ (N) | Shear Force $T$ (N) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 180 | 120 |
| 2 | 360 | 240 |
| 3 | 540 | 365 |

**Determine the shear strength parameters $c$ and $\phi$.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Normal and Shear Stresses.**

Area of the box:
$$A = 60\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm} = 3600\,\text{mm}^2 = 3600 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$$

For Test 1:
$$\sigma_{n1} = \frac{P_1}{A} = \frac{180\,\text{N}}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2} = 50{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 50\,\text{kPa}$$

$$\tau_{f1} = \frac{T_1}{A} = \frac{120}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 33{,}333\,\text{Pa} \approx 33.33\,\text{kPa}$$

For Test 2:
$$\sigma_{n2} = \frac{360}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 100{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 100\,\text{kPa}$$

$$\tau_{f2} = \frac{240}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 66{,}667\,\text{Pa} \approx 66.67\,\text{kPa}$$

For Test 3:
$$\sigma_{n3} = \frac{540}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 150\,\text{kPa}$$

$$\tau_{f3} = \frac{365}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 101.39\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Fit the Failure Envelope.**

Using Tests 1 and 2 (linear interpolation):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tau_{f2} - \tau_{f1}}{\sigma_{n2} - \sigma_{n1}} = \frac{66.67 - 33.33}{100 - 50} = \frac{33.33}{50} = 0.6667$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 33.69°$$

Cohesion (interpolating from Test 1):

$$c = \tau_{f1} - \sigma_{n1}\tan\phi = 33.33 - 50 \times 0.6667 = 33.33 - 33.33 = 0\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Verification using Test 3:**

$$\tau_{f,\text{predicted}} = 0 + 150 \times 0.6667 = 100\,\text{kPa}$$

Measured: $101.39\,\text{kPa}$ → Excellent agreement (error $\approx 1.4\%$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sandy soil exhibits **purely frictional behaviour** with $c \approx 0$ and $\phi \approx 33.7°$, which is consistent with the typical behaviour of medium-dense sand.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Vane Shear Torque Equation

**Starting Point:** A four-bladed rectangular vane of diameter $D$ and height $H$ is rotated in a saturated clay. The soil fails along a **cylindrical surface** of diameter $D$ and height $H$, plus two **semi-spherical/toroidal end caps** at the top and bottom.

**Assumptions:**
- Soil is **saturated and undrained** (constant volume).
- Shear strength is **uniformly mobilized** along the entire failure surface.
- The **end resistance** is approximated by a uniformly sheared surface equal in area to a circle of diameter $D$ (the vane's projection).

**Step 1 — Identify the Two Resistance Surfaces:**

1. **Cylindrical side surface** (where the soil shears around the periphery of the vane):
   $$A_{\text{side}} = \pi D H$$

2. **Two end caps** (top and bottom of the cylinder): approximated as a disk of diameter $D$ for the vane tip:
   $$A_{\text{end}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \text{ (per end)}$$

   For **two ends**:
   $$A_{\text{ends}} = 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi D^2}{2}$$

3. **Total failure surface area:**
   $$A_s = A_{\text{side}} + A_{\text{ends}} = \pi D H + \frac{\pi D^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Set Up the Torque Equation:**

The torque is the integral of shear stress $\times$ lever arm over the failure surface. For the cylindrical side, the lever arm is constant at $D/2$:

$$T_{\text{side}} = c_u \cdot A_{\text{side}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = c_u \cdot \pi D H \cdot \frac{D}{2} = c_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2 H}{2}$$

For each end cap, the average lever arm is approximately $D/6$ (from the centroid of a uniform disk of radius $D/2$, measured from the centre of the vane, integrated over radial coordinates):

$$T_{\text{ends}} = c_u \cdot A_{\text{ends}} \cdot \frac{D}{6} = c_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{2} \cdot \frac{D}{6} = c_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Total Torque:**

$$T = T_{\text{side}} + T_{\text{ends}} = c_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2 H}{2} + c_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^3}{12}$$

Factor out $c_u \cdot \pi D^2$:

$$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D}{12} \cdot 2 \right) = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D}{6} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result (General Vane Shear Torque Equation):**
> $$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D}{6} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplification for Standard Vane Geometry ($H = 2D$):**

Substitute $H = 2D$:

$$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \frac{2D}{2} + \frac{D}{6} \right) = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( D + \frac{D}{6} \right)$$

$$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \cdot D \left( 1 + \frac{1}{6} \right) = c_u \cdot \pi D^3 \cdot \frac{7}{6}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{7 \pi D^3 c_u}{6}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Undrained Shear Strength $c_u$:**

Rearranging for $c_u$:

$$\boxed{c_u = \frac{6T}{7\pi D^3}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Vane Shear Test (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A field vane shear test was conducted in a soft clay deposit using a vane of diameter $D = 100\,\text{mm}$ and height $H = 200\,\text{mm}$. The peak torque recorded was $T_{\text{peak}} = 85\,\text{N·m}$. The remoulded torque (after 10 revolutions) was $T_{\text{rem}} = 28\,\text{N·m}$. The plasticity index of the clay is $I_P = 30\%$.

**Determine:**
1. Peak undrained shear strength $c_{u,\text{peak}}$.
2. Remoulded undrained shear strength $c_{u,\text{rem}}$.
3. Sensitivity $S_t$ of the clay.
4. Design undrained shear strength using Bjerrum's correction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify the standard geometry.**
$$H = 200\,\text{mm},\quad D = 100\,\text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{H}{D} = 2 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (standard vane)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Peak Undrained Shear Strength.**

Using the simplified formula (since $H/D = 2$):

$$c_{u,\text{peak}} = \frac{6 T_{\text{peak}}}{7 \pi D^3} = \frac{6 \times 85}{7 \pi \times (0.100)^3}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$7 \pi D^3 = 7 \times \pi \times (0.001) = 7 \times 3.1416 \times 0.001 = 0.02199$$

$$c_{u,\text{peak}} = \frac{510}{0.02199} = 23{,}192\,\text{Pa} \approx 23.19\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Remoulded Undrained Shear Strength.**

$$c_{u,\text{rem}} = \frac{6 T_{\text{rem}}}{7 \pi D^3} = \frac{6 \times 28}{0.02199} = \frac{168}{0.02199} = 7{,}640\,\text{Pa} \approx 7.64\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Sensitivity.**

$$S_t = \frac{c_{u,\text{peak}}}{c_{u,\text{rem}}} = \frac{23.19}{7.64} \approx 3.04$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bjerrum's Correction.**

Correction factor:
$$\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(I_P) = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(30)$$

Computing $\log(30) = \log(3 \times 10) = \log 3 + 1 = 0.4771 + 1 = 1.4771$:

$$\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \times 1.4771 = 1.7 - 0.7976 = 0.9024$$

Design undrained shear strength:

$$c_{u,\text{design}} = \mu \cdot c_{u,\text{peak}} = 0.9024 \times 23.19 = 20.93\,\text{kPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Summary:**
> - $c_{u,\text{peak}} = 23.19\,\text{kPa}$
> - $c_{u,\text{rem}} = 7.64\,\text{kPa}$
> - $S_t = 3.04$ (sensitive clay, since $2 < S_t < 4$)
> - $c_{u,\text{design}} = 20.93\,\text{kPa}$ (after Bjerrum's correction)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Vane Geometry Effect

The general torque equation is:

$$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^2 \left( \frac{H}{2} + \frac{D}{6} \right)$$

We can express this as:

$$T = c_u \cdot \pi D^3 \left( \frac{H/D}{2} + \frac{1}{6} \right) = c_u \cdot \pi D^3 \left( \frac{r}{2} + \frac{1}{6} \right)$$

where $r = H/D$ is the **aspect ratio** of the vane.

| Aspect Ratio $r = H/D$ | Geometric Term $\left(\frac{r}{2} + \frac{1}{6}\right)$ | Standard? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0.5 + 0.1667 = 0.6667$ | Not standard |
| **2** | **$1.0 + 0.1667 = 1.1667 = 7/6$** | **Most common (KTU-recommended)** |
| 3 | $1.5 + 0.1667 = 1.6667$ | Rare |

For the standard case ($r = 2$):

$$c_u = \frac{T}{\pi D^3 \cdot (7/6)} = \frac{6T}{7\pi D^3}$$
