## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Briefly explain the principle of the direct shear test. Why is the plane of failure considered forced in this test?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Direct Shear Test is a laboratory test used to determine the **shear strength parameters** ($c$ and $\phi$) of soils. A soil specimen is placed in a **shear box** split into two halves, and a **normal load ($P$)** is applied vertically while a **horizontal shear force ($T$)** is applied at a constant rate. The shear stress $\tau = T/A$ at failure is plotted against the normal stress $\sigma_n = P/A$ for different normal loads to obtain the **Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope**.

The plane of failure is termed **"forced"** because the box is split along a fixed horizontal plane, and the soil is constrained to fail along this **predefined gap** rather than along its natural weakest plane. This is the major limitation of the test, as real soil failure may occur on an inclined plane depending on stress state and anisotropy.

*(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for Mohr-Coulomb link, 1 mark for explaining "forced plane")*

---

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is a Vane Shear Test? Mention its two variants and the type of soil for which it is most suitable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Vane Shear Test (VST)** is a rotational shear test used to determine the **undrained shear strength ($c_u$)** of soft, sensitive, cohesive soils. A four-bladed vane is inserted into the soil and rotated; the **peak torque** required to shear the soil along a cylindrical surface is measured and converted to $c_u$ using the relation:

$$c_u = \frac{6T}{7\pi D^3} \quad \text{(for standard vane with } H/D = 2\text{)}$$

**Two Variants:**
1. **Laboratory Vane Shear Test (LVST):** Conducted on undisturbed soil samples in the laboratory.
2. **Field Vane Shear Test (FVST):** Conducted in-situ by pushing the vane into the ground at the desired test depth.

The test is **most suitable for soft to medium-sensitive clays** in the undrained condition.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for variants, 1 mark for soil type)*

---

### 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A direct shear test was conducted on a sandy soil using a 60 mm × 60 mm shear box. The following readings were recorded at failure:**

| Test No. | Normal Load $P$ (N) | Shear Force $T$ (N) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 80 |
| 2 | 240 | 160 |
| 3 | 360 | 245 |
| 4 | 480 | 320 |

**(a)** Plot the failure envelope and determine the **cohesion ($c$)** and **angle of internal friction ($\phi$)** of the soil. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If a similar test were performed on a saturated clay sample under the same normal loads, what changes would you expect in the failure envelope? Justify your answer with suitable reasoning. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Normal and Shear Stresses.**

Box area: $A = 60 \times 60 = 3600\,\text{mm}^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$

| Test | $P$ (N) | $T$ (N) | $\sigma_n = P/A$ (kPa) | $\tau_f = T/A$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 80 | 33.33 | 22.22 |
| 2 | 240 | 160 | 66.67 | 44.44 |
| 3 | 360 | 245 | 100.00 | 68.06 |
| 4 | 480 | 320 | 133.33 | 88.89 |

**Step 2: Determine the Slope (using two extreme points).**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tau_{f4} - \tau_{f1}}{\sigma_{n4} - \sigma_{n1}} = \frac{88.89 - 22.22}{133.33 - 33.33} = \frac{66.67}{100.00} = 0.6667$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 33.69° \approx 33.7°$$

**Step 3: Determine the Intercept (cohesion).**

$$c = \tau_{f1} - \sigma_{n1}\tan\phi = 22.22 - 33.33 \times 0.6667 = 22.22 - 22.22 = 0\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4: Verification (using Test 3):**

$$\tau_{f,\text{linear}} = 0 + 100 \times 0.6667 = 66.67\,\text{kPa}$$

Measured: $68.06\,\text{kPa}$ → Close match (deviation $\approx 2\%$).

> **Result:** $c \approx 0\,\text{kPa}$ and $\phi \approx 33.7°$. This is consistent with a **medium-dense sand** exhibiting **purely frictional behaviour**.

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks):**

**Expected Changes for Saturated Clay:**

1. **Non-zero Cohesion Intercept ($c > 0$):** Saturated clays exhibit **cohesion** due to electrochemical bonding between clay particles. The failure envelope will **not pass through the origin** — it will have a positive intercept on the $\tau$-axis.

2. **Lower Friction Angle ($\phi$ reduced):** Clays have a **much lower angle of internal friction** than sands (typically $\phi$ between $0°$ and $25°$, often around $20°$ to $25°$ for normally consolidated clays). This is because the platy clay particles slide over each other more easily than the rounded sand grains.

3. **Curved Failure Envelope:** The Mohr-Coulomb envelope for clay is often **non-linear** (curved) over a wide range of stresses. The linear approximation is only valid over a limited range.

4. **Drainage Considerations:** The DST on clay must be run as a **Consolidated Drained (CD) test** at a very slow strain rate to allow dissipation of excess pore water pressure. Otherwise, measured strength is the **undrained strength**, which is much lower.

5. **Sample Disturbance:** Undisturbed samples are required for clays; disturbed samples will underestimate the true cohesion.

> **Conclusion:** For the same normal loads, the failure envelope for clay will be **flatter** (lower $\phi$) and **shifted upward** (non-zero $c$), with the **linear approximation** being less reliable than for sand.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **field vane shear test**. Explain how the **undrained shear strength** and the **sensitivity** of a clay deposit are determined from this test. (7 Marks)

**(b)** In a field vane shear test on a soft clay, a vane of diameter 75 mm and height 150 mm was used. The peak torque was 60 N·m and the remoulded torque was 18 N·m. The plasticity index of the clay is 25%. Determine: (i) the peak and remoulded undrained shear strengths, (ii) the sensitivity of the clay, and (iii) the **design undrained shear strength** using Bjerrum's correction. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Description (7 Marks):**

**Field Vane Shear Test (FVST) — Apparatus & Procedure:**

1. **Apparatus:**
   - **Vane:** A four-bladed stainless-steel vane of standard aspect ratio $H/D = 2$.
   - **Extension Rods:** Connect the vane to the surface for torque transmission.
   - **Torque Measuring Head:** Sits at the top of the rod (e.g., torque wrench or proving ring).
   - **Rotation Mechanism:** Allows controlled rotation (geared handle or motor).
   - **Protective Sleeve:** Shields the rod from soil friction above the test depth.

2. **Procedure:**
   - Advance a borehole to about **5 vane diameters above** the test depth.
   - Push the vane into the soil at the bottom of the borehole to the test depth (about **3 to 4 borehole diameters** below the borehole bottom).
   - Allow 2 to 5 minutes for the soil to consolidate/equalize around the vane.
   - Rotate the vane at a controlled rate of **0.1°/sec** (very soft clay) to **0.5°/sec** (stiff clay).
   - Record the **peak torque ($T_{max}$)** at the point of failure.
   - Continue rotating rapidly for **about 10 full revolutions** to completely remould the soil.
   - Record the **remoulded torque ($T_{rem}$)**.
   - Withdraw the vane and check the soil profile.

**Calculations:**

- **Undrained Shear Strength (peak):**
  $$c_{u,\text{peak}} = \frac{6T_{max}}{7\pi D^3}$$

- **Undrained Shear Strength (remoulded):**
  $$c_{u,\text{rem}} = \frac{6T_{rem}}{7\pi D^3}$$

- **Sensitivity:**
  $$S_t = \frac{c_{u,\text{peak}}}{c_{u,\text{rem}}}$$

**Classification based on Sensitivity (Skempton & Northey, 1948):**

| Sensitivity $S_t$ | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1 | Insensitive |
| 1–2 | Slightly sensitive |
| 2–4 | Medium sensitive |
| 4–8 | Sensitive |
| 8–16 | Extra sensitive |
| > 16 | Quick (quick clays) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Vane diameter: $D = 75\,\text{mm} = 0.075\,\text{m}$
- Vane height: $H = 150\,\text{mm} = 0.150\,\text{m}$
- Check: $H/D = 150/75 = 2$ ✓ (standard vane)
- Peak torque: $T_{max} = 60\,\text{N·m}$
- Remoulded torque: $T_{rem} = 18\,\text{N·m}$
- Plasticity index: $I_P = 25\%$

**Step 1: Compute $D^3$ for use in the formula.**

$$D^3 = (0.075)^3 = 4.21875 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Peak Undrained Shear Strength.**

$$c_{u,\text{peak}} = \frac{6 \times 60}{7 \pi \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Denominator: $7 \pi \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-4} = 7 \times 3.1416 \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-4} = 9.275 \times 10^{-3}$

$$c_{u,\text{peak}} = \frac{360}{9.275 \times 10^{-3}} = 38{,}815\,\text{Pa} \approx 38.82\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3: Remoulded Undrained Shear Strength.**

$$c_{u,\text{rem}} = \frac{6 \times 18}{7 \pi \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{108}{9.275 \times 10^{-3}} = 11{,}645\,\text{Pa} \approx 11.65\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity.**

$$S_t = \frac{c_{u,\text{peak}}}{c_{u,\text{rem}}} = \frac{38.82}{11.65} \approx 3.33$$

**Step 5: Bjerrum's Correction Factor.**

$$\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(I_P) = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(25)$$

$\log(25) = \log(5^2) = 2\log 5 = 2 \times 0.6990 = 1.3980$

$$\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \times 1.3980 = 1.7 - 0.7549 = 0.9451$$

**Step 6: Design Undrained Shear Strength.**

$$c_{u,\text{design}} = \mu \times c_{u,\text{peak}} = 0.9451 \times 38.82 = 36.69\,\text{kPa}$$

> **Final Results:**
> - $c_{u,\text{peak}} = 38.82\,\text{kPa}$
> - $c_{u,\text{rem}} = 11.65\,\text{kPa}$
> - $S_t = 3.33$ (medium sensitive clay)
> - $c_{u,\text{design}} = 36.69\,\text{kPa}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the denominator of the vane formula — this is the #1 mistake. Always use $D$ in **metres** when computing $c_u$ in kPa.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula** (general $T = c_u \pi D^2 (H/2 + D/6)$ instead of the simplified $T = 7\pi D^3 c_u/6$) — both work, but the simplified form saves time. If $H \neq 2D$, you **must** use the general formula.
> 3. **Forgetting Bjerrum's correction** — KTU explicitly tests this in the 14-mark questions. Do not stop at $c_{u,\text{peak}}$; always compute $c_{u,\text{design}}$.
> 4. **Confusing the area** in the Direct Shear Test — for a 60 mm square box, area is $3600\,\text{mm}^2$ or $3.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$. Many students use $60 \times 60 = 3600$ in N/mm² instead of converting to m².
> 5. **Plotting conventions** — In KTU answer sheets, $\sigma_n$ goes on the **x-axis** and $\tau_f$ on the **y-axis** (not the reverse).
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — Always express the final answer in **kPa** (or kN/m²). Mixing kPa and Pa in one answer will lose marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the H/D = 2 verification** in VST problems — Always state this explicitly before applying the simplified formula.

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Direct Shear Test & Vane Shear Test (PCCET402 – Module 4):**

#### Direct Shear Test (DST)
- ☑ **Drained test (CD)**, ideal for **sands and gravels**.
- ☑ Two equations: $\sigma_n = P/A$ and $\tau = T/A$.
- ☑ Box sizes: **60×60 mm** or **100×100 mm**.
- ☑ Failure plane is **forced horizontal** (limitation).
- ☑ Mohr-Coulomb envelope: $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$.
- ☑ For pure sands: $c \approx 0$, $\phi \approx 28°$–$45°$.
- ☑ Cannot measure **pore water pressure** → no effective stress analysis.
- ☑ Peak vs. **residual strength** for overconsolidated clays.
- ☑ Dilation angle $\psi = \phi_{peak} - \phi_{cv}$ (Bolton's relation).

#### Vane Shear Test (VST)
- ☑ **Undrained test (UU)**, ideal for **soft, sensitive clays**.
- ☑ Standard vane: $H/D = 2$, four blades, rectangular.
- ☑ General formula: $T = c_u \pi D^2 (H/2 + D/6)$.
- ☑ Simplified (for $H/D = 2$): $T = 7\pi D^3 c_u / 6$.
- ☑ Solve for $c_u$: $c_u = 6T / (7\pi D^3)$.
- ☑ **Bjerrum correction**: $c_{u,\text{design}} = \mu \cdot c_{u,\text{peak}}$; $\mu = 1.7 - 0.54 \log(I_P)$.
- ☑ Sensitivity: $S_t = c_{u,\text{peak}} / c_{u,\text{remoulded}}$.
- ☑ $S_t > 4$ → **sensitive clay**; $S_t > 16$ → **quick clay**.
- ☑ Field version (FVST) is **in-situ**; lab version (LVST) is on undisturbed samples.
- ☑ Rotation rate: **0.1°/sec** (very soft) to **0.5°/sec** (stiff clay).
- ☑ Borehole depth: **5D above** test point; vane insertion: **3–4 borehole diameters** below borehole bottom.

#### Comparative Recall
- ☑ DST → Drained → Sands → Forces horizontal plane → Cheap & simple.
- ☑ VST → Undrained → Soft clays → Cylindrical failure surface → Measures $S_t$ → In-situ option.
- ☑ Both tests **cannot measure pore water pressure** (cannot compute effective stress directly).
